item.title: France says it will work with China on de-esclatating Iran war
item.description:
PARIS, March 3 (Reuters) - French Foreign Minister Jean-Noel Barrot held a call on Monday with his ŌĆŗChinese counterpart Wang Yi to discuss ŌĆīthe Iran war and both agreed to work on de-escalation, Barrot's office said in a statement.
Both ministers ŌĆŗcommitted to seeking a political solution ŌĆŗthat would guarantee collective security and take ŌüĀinto account the aspirations of the ŌĆŗIranian people, the statement said.
Barrot "reiterated the Iranian ŌĆŗregime's responsibility for the ongoing escalation, after it unjustifiably attacked several countries in the region," the statement ŌĆŗsaid.
He also criticised the Iranian regime for ŌĆŗrefusing to respect the resolutions from the United Nations' ŌĆīSecurity ŌüĀCouncil on the nuclear programmes, ballistic activities, support to non-state armed groups and rejecting good-faith multilateral negotiations.
The French minister reiterated that ŌĆŗFrance was ŌĆŗnot involved ŌüĀin the actions by the United States and Israel and ŌĆŗhad no prior knowledge of them. ŌĆŗCountries "must ŌüĀgive precedence to international institutions to solve disputes and, where needed, the use of force," ŌüĀhe ŌĆŗadded.
The two ministers agreed ŌĆŗto keep dialogue open on the issue.

item.title: Dollar gets its mojo back - but only by default
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - While it's tempting to assume the dollar's long-lost "safety" bid has returned since the weekend Iran attacks , it's not as clear-cut as it seems and owes more to relative energy plays. Yet the implications of the market response may be just as powerful.
Ever since Donald Trump's return to the White House last year, the dollar has waned even during periods of market anxiety and volatility, due in ŌĆŗlarge part to U.S. economic policy uncertainty and both domestic and geopolitical upheaval.
Reversing years of dollar over-valuation is a key tenet of the Trump administration's economic plan. But the greenback's diminished haven role in times of ŌĆīglobal political or financial stress suggests foreign investors - already up to their eyeballs in U.S. assets - have changed their behaviour.
So it was remarkable that the dollar (.DXY) , opens new tab jumped across the board after last weekend's extraordinary bombing campaign by U.S. and Israeli forces against Iranian targets, including the assassination of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and the wave of regional violence that's followed.
The crux of the move hinged more on the inevitable energy price dynamics rather than any dash for dollars per se. In fact, it was more a default move out of the currencies of economies ŌĆŗworst hit by an outsized and protracted energy price squeeze.
With the U.S. now a net exporter of total petroleum and energy products in general, the initial 10% surge in world oil prices on Monday hurt other ŌĆŗmajor currencies much more due to fears of a major demand hit if the supply hiatus persists for several weeks or even months.
That's why other traditional havens ŌüĀsuch as Japan's yen , caught no safety bid this time around and plunged over 1% against the dollar on Monday given Japan's big energy import bill and the fact that about a third of its energy imports comes through the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz.
China too is a big consumer of oil now stuck in those contentious waterways, particularly deeply discounted Iranian crude that's sanctioned in the West and now also in limbo. The recently high-flying yuan turned tail on Monday and dropped 0.8% as the ŌĆŗsituation unfolded.
"This isnŌĆÖt a friendly outcome for the Northern Asian currencies," said Societe Generale currency strategist Kit Juckes, adding that the most important indication from Trump so far has been that the U.S. action will take weeks , not days.
For Europe, the calculation is compounded by its exposure to natural gas after the shipping attacks effectively closed the Hormuz route, a conduit for 20% of worldwide liquefied natural gas shipments and up to 30% of crude oil.
Benchmark European gas prices surged by almost 50% at one point on Monday to their highest in more than ŌĆŗa year, closing up 35% and prompting the European Union's gas supply group to schedule an emergency meeting for Wednesday.
The U.S. supplied 58% of the European Union's LNG last year. Qatar, which accounted for 6% of the bloc's imports, shut down its ŌĆŗproduction plants on Monday after attacks from Iran.
The euro fell 1% against the dollar to its lowest in more than a month.
The Swiss franc's long-standing and often unwelcome haven status remains in play - but it's complicated by the Swiss National Bank's battle against deflation ŌĆīand its restated ŌüĀcommitment to intervene to sell francs to cap the unit.
As to the overall economic hit from an oil spike worldwide, Barclays economists assume every sustained $10 per barrel rise in crude prices takes up to 0.2 percentage point off global growth. And if a wave of forecasts of $100-plus per barrel were to prove accurate, then that could well bite.
As it stands, however, Monday's net Brent crude price rise of $5 to $77 per barrel will be a much more modest blow - and the moves so far would barely have any significant demand impacts on the U.S. itself.
Calculations then turn to whether oil price pressure becomes an economic depressant or inflation aggravator. With U.S. core inflation running above 3%, ŌĆŗthat could argue for more focus on the latter and ŌĆŗfor keeping U.S. interest rates high through the year - ŌüĀanother support for the dollar.
But, as so often with Middle East conflicts, the initial ready-reckoners on global economic hits all hinge on duration of conflict and the energy supply disruption.
Trump has indicated the military campaign will run for four or five weeks and, likely riffing off that, prediction markets such as Polymarket see a 63% chance Trump will ŌĆŗcall a halt by the end of this month.
And yet most of the thinking on currency reactions is not strictly calculations of dollar hoarding or cross-border dash ŌĆŗfor safety - rather they seem ŌüĀjust like relative economic assessments emanating from energy exposure.
But for all that, it can have a powerful and looping effect.
Barclays' rule of thumb for the dollar, for example, is that it gains between 0.5% and 1.0% for every $10 increase in oil.
If dollarŌĆædenominated energy prices rise and stay high, pushing the exchange rate up with them, that would both worsen the energy shock for overseas economies and drive the dollar even higher in a selfŌĆæreinforcing loop.
No one would want that scenario - least of ŌüĀall Washington.
(The opinions ŌĆŗexpressed here are those of the author , a columnist for Reuters.}

item.title: Israel prepared for weeks-long Iran campaign, unlikely to deploy ground forces, spokesperson says
item.description:
Tel Aviv, March 3 (Reuters) - The Israeli military has ŌĆŗprepared for a campaign against Iran ŌĆīthat could last several weeks but is unlikely to see the deployment of ground ŌĆŗforces, a military spokesperson told reporters ŌĆŗon Tuesday.
"We have prepared a general ŌüĀscope of weeks," Lieutenant Colonel Nadav ŌĆŗShoshani said in an online briefing, adding ŌĆŗthat the duration of the military campaign could change, depending on developments. He described progress ŌĆŗso far as positive.
When asked if ŌĆŗIsrael could deploy ground forces to Iran, Shoshani said ŌĆīthat ŌüĀwas unlikely.
"I don't think that's something very likely at the moment for Israeli forces. There's not a practical idea ŌĆŗat the ŌĆŗmoment that ŌüĀI know of," he said.
Israel and the U.S. have been ŌĆŗcarrying out air strikes against ŌĆŗIran ŌüĀsince Saturday, triggering Iranian retaliation .
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said on Monday that the U.S.-Israel ŌüĀwar ŌĆŗwith Iran could take "some ŌĆŗtime" but would not take years.

item.title: Progress on rules for lethal autonomous weapons urgently needed, says chair of Geneva talks
item.description:
GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - Progress on a potential international framework to prohibit and restrict Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems (LAWS) is urgently needed, as talks on the matter in Geneva enter a ŌĆŗcrucial phase, their chair said.
From this week to the mandate's end in September, ŌĆī128 states will discuss whether to agree by consensus a non-binding text that could pave the way for future negotiations on prohibitions and regulations on LAWS.
Since 2014, more than a hundred states party to the Convention on ŌĆŗCertain Conventional Weapons have met in the Swiss city to discuss banning LAWS that ŌĆŗdo not comply with existing international law while regulating others.
"If we wait then ŌüĀit almost gets to a stage where you're too late... We will be overtaken by ŌĆŗtechnological developments," Robert in den Bosch, the Dutch Disarmament Ambassador in Geneva and Chair of the CCW ŌĆŗGroup of Governmental Experts on LAWS, told Reuters.
There are mounting concerns over the role of AI-assisted semi-autonomous weapons that have been used in conflicts in Ukraine, Sudan, Gaza, and in Iran and the Gulf , opens new tab .
While states agree international humanitarian law (IHL) ŌĆŗapplies to LAWS, specific internationally binding standards for these systems remain virtually non-existent.
Russia and the ŌĆŗUnited States, among others, oppose new legally binding instruments, arguing existing laws suffice.
In den Bosch stated that others say ŌĆīnew ŌüĀrules are needed to bridge supposed accountability gaps in IHL, which place obligations on states and individuals, not on machines.
The Rolling Text under discussion in Geneva proposes "context appropriate human judgment and control" as a measure to ensure that systems that "identify, select and engage" targets without human intervention comply ŌĆŗwith IHL.
Despite growing calls for ŌĆŗurgent regulation, U.N. ŌüĀSecretary-General Antonio Guterres' deadline for agreeing a legally binding instrument on LAWS this year will realistically be missed, In den Bosch said.
While the deadline is ŌĆŗoutside the Geneva talks' remit, the challenge of securing consensus on even ŌĆŗnon-binding elements ŌüĀunderscores the difficulty of making progress, In den Bosch stated.
The talks are set within a challenging context of geopolitical tensions and recent European withdrawals from the landmine ban treaty over Russian threats.
The Review Conference of ŌüĀthe CCW ŌĆŗin November could decide to launch negotiations for a ŌĆŗbinding protocol after the Geneva talks end. However, in the absence of agreement, there is a risk some countries might ŌĆŗpursue a breakaway treaty elsewhere, In den Bosch stated.

item.title: Israeli military deploys additional forces to southern Lebanon
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 3 (Reuters) - The Israeli military deployed ŌĆīadditional forces to southern Lebanon overnight, to take up what a military ŌĆŗspokesperson on Tuesday described as ŌĆŗdefensive positions to protect Israeli ŌüĀcivilians and strategic sites from ŌĆŗany potential Hezbollah attack.
"We're only at ŌĆŗthe borderline area in a defensive manner to prevent attacks against civilians and ŌĆŗvery strategic important points," Lieutenant ŌĆŗColonel Nadav Shoshani said in an online ŌĆībriefing ŌüĀwith reporters.
Defence Minister Israel Katz said in a statement he had authorised the military to advance ŌĆŗand take ŌĆŗcontrol ŌüĀof additional positions in Lebanon after Hezbollah fired rockets ŌĆŗat Israel late on Sunday, ŌĆŗin ŌüĀreaction to U.S. and Israeli strikes against Iran .
Israeli forces have occupied ŌüĀfive ŌĆŗpositions in southern Lebanon ŌĆŗsince November 2024.

item.title: France ready to defend partners, Foreign Minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 3 (Reuters) - France is ready to defend its partners if ŌĆŗthey request help after Iranian ŌĆīretaliatory strikes and ireserves its right to intervene, Foreign Minister Jean-Noel ŌĆŗBarrot said on Tuesday.
"This ŌĆŗwar... is dragging a number ŌüĀof countries in the region ŌĆŗinto the conflict with which ŌĆŗwe have relations, defence agreements, and interests, including military bases," Barrot told ŌĆŗFrench broadcaster BFM TV.
He added ŌĆŗthat French Rafale fighter jets carried out ŌĆīair ŌüĀoperations to secure the skies above France's bases in the region. Around 400,000 French ŌĆŗnationals are ŌĆŗcurrently ŌüĀin the countries affected by the crisis in ŌĆŗthe Middle East and ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀFrench government is ready to fly back those most at ŌüĀrisk ŌĆŗusing both commercial ŌĆŗand military flights, Barrot said.

item.title: Drone hits fuel tank at Oman's Duqm port
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - A fuel tank at OmanŌĆÖs Duqm commercial port ŌĆŗwas hit on Tuesday when the facility came under ŌĆīattack from several unmanned aircraft, the state news agency said, citing a security source, on the fourth day of U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran , which ŌĆŗhas responded by targeting neighbouring countries.
The attack comes a ŌĆŗday after Qatar halted its production of liquefied ŌüĀnatural gas - about a fifth of global supply - while Saudi Arabia suspended ŌĆŗproduction at its largest domestic refinery.
Major Israeli gas fields, including Leviathan, ŌĆŗwere offline, and most output in the Iraqi Kurdish region had been shut down as Iran continued to target infrastructure, including energy facilities, ports ŌĆŗand airports, over recent days.
Oman had been acting as ŌĆŗmediator in talks between the U.S. and Iran over Tehran's nuclear programme before ŌĆīthe ŌüĀattacks on Iran.
The resulting damage at Duqm was contained and no casualties were recorded, Oman's news agency added. Duqm had also been targeted by two drones on Sunday, wounding one ŌĆŗworker. Oman had ŌĆŗbeen spared from ŌüĀattacks on Saturday, the day the U.S. and Israel launched their attack.
The Abu Dhabi government's ŌĆŗmedia office said on Monday a fire broke ŌĆŗout ŌüĀafter the Musaffah fuel tank terminal was targeted by a drone, without impacting its operations.
Abu Dhabi state oil giant ADNOC operates a ŌüĀfacility ŌĆŗin Musaffah from which fuels are ŌĆŗtransported by trucks and through a 1,600-kilometre (994 miles) pipeline network.

item.title: Trump says 'sad to see' US-UK relationship is not what it was
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said it was "sad to see" the relationship with Britain was "not what it was" after ŌĆŗPrime Minister Keir Starmer initially held off giving military support to the strikes ŌĆīagainst Iran.
Trump said the likes of France had been more supportive and said he had never expected to see the once "most solid of all" relationships change in ŌĆŗthis way.
"It's very sad to see that the relationship is obviously ŌĆŗnot what it was," Trump told the Sun newspaper on Tuesday, ŌüĀhis second interview to a British newspaper in as many days where ŌĆŗhe criticised the British leader.
Starmer said late on Sunday that he would ŌĆŗallow the U.S. to use British military bases for defensive strikes after they were not used in the initial attack on Iran.
Trump said the U.S. did not need Britain to ŌĆŗwage war in the Middle East but added: "It's not going to matter, ŌĆŗbut (Starmer) should have helpedŌĆ” he should have.
"I mean, France has been great. They've all ŌĆībeen ŌüĀgreat. The UK has been much different from others."
Senior British minister Darren Jones told Times Radio in response to the latest Trump comment's that the U.S.-UK relationship remained crucial but said the country had learnt lessons ŌĆŗfrom its involvement ŌĆŗin the 2003 ŌüĀIraq war.
"One of the lessons of Iraq was that it's better to be involved in these situations when ŌĆŗyou are aligned with international partners, and as I ŌĆŗsay, with ŌüĀa clear legal basis in the plan," he said.
Starmer said in parliament on Monday: "President Trump has expressed his disagreement with our decision not to get ŌüĀinvolved ŌĆŗin the initial strikes, but it is ŌĆŗmy duty to judge what is in BritainŌĆÖs national interest. That is what I have done, ŌĆŗand I stand by it."

item.title: France to send┬Āanti-drone systems to Cyprus after British base attack, report says
item.description:
NICOSIA, March 3 (Reuters) - France plans to send anti-missile and anti-drone systems to Cyprus after a British air base ŌĆŗon the island was attacked by drones, the semi-official Cyprus News ŌĆīAgency (CNA) said on Tuesday.
France would send anti-missile and anti-drone systems, as well as a frigate, CNA reported. It said French President Emmanuel Macron communicated the intention to Cypriot President Nikos ŌĆŗChristodoulides early on Tuesday.
A Cypriot government source, speaking on condition of anonymity, ŌĆŗconfirmed the report. The French defence ministry did not immediately respond ŌüĀto a request for comment.
The reported French aid to the island follows ŌĆŗGreece's offer of assistance hours after RAF Akrotiri, a British sovereign base, was targeted ŌĆŗin two separate drone incidents on Monday.
In the first instance an Iranian-made Shahed drone crashed into the runway of the base, causing limited damage. Hours later two other drones were intercepted.
Senior Cypriot ŌĆŗofficials say the attack was carried out by an Iranian Shahed drone most ŌĆŗlikely fired by Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah from Lebanon. The officials have stressed that the ŌĆībase - ŌüĀconsidered sovereign British territory - rather than Cyprus, was the target.
Britain said on Sunday that it had accepted a U.S. request to use its military bases for "defensive" strikes against Iran, a day after the launch of a U.S. and Israeli military campaign against ŌĆŗIran , which triggered ŌĆŗIranian retaliation. British ŌüĀPrime Minister Keir Starmer later said Britain's bases on Cyprus were not being used by U.S. bombers.
Cyprus has asked Britain that ŌĆŗthe bases only be used for humanitarian purposes.
Greece on Monday ŌĆŗdispatched four ŌüĀF-16 fighter jets to the island. Two frigates, one equipped with its anti-drone Centauros jamming system, were also sailing to Cyprus.
Centauros, which has been successfully deployed against Houthi ŌüĀrebels ŌĆŗattacking shipping lanes off Yemen, has the capability ŌĆŗof detecting and incapacitating low-flying drones, such as the one which crashed into RAF Akrotiri and evaded ŌĆŗradars, Cypriot defence sources said.

item.title: Russia says it has seen no evidence that Iran was developing nuclear weapons
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 3 (Reuters) - Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov said on Tuesday that Moscow had still seen no evidence that Iran ŌĆŗwas developing nuclear weapons as the U.S. and Israel ŌĆīpursue their attack.
U.S. President Donald Trump has offered various reasons for going to war with Iran. On Monday, in his most extensive public ŌĆŗcomments so far, he said he had ordered ŌĆŗthe attack to thwart Tehran's nuclear and ballistic ŌüĀmissile programmes.
Lavrov told Brunei's foreign minister during talks ŌĆŗin Moscow: "We still see no evidence that Iran was ŌĆŗdeveloping nuclear weapons, which was the main, if not the only, justification for the war."
He said the consequences of the attack ŌĆŗon Iran were being felt throughout the region, and ŌĆŗthat Arab countries were bearing economic costs and suffering casualties.
He reiterated ŌĆīRussia's ŌüĀcall for an immediate cessation of hostilities by all parties:
"As an unconditional first step, we must do everything possible to stop any actions that result in ŌĆŗcivilian casualties."
He pointed ŌĆŗto the ŌüĀreported bombing of a school in Iran.
Iranian state media have accused Israel and the ŌĆŗU.S. of a strike on a girls' ŌĆŗprimary ŌüĀschool in the southern Iranian town that Tehran said had killed more than 160 people.
U.S. Secretary of State ŌüĀMarco ŌĆŗRubio said U.S. forces would not ŌĆŗdeliberately attack a school.

item.title: Israeli military says striking Hezbollah command centers, arms depots in Beirut
item.description: March 3 (Reuters) - The ŌĆŗIsraeli military ŌĆīsaid early ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗTuesday that ŌüĀit ŌĆŗwas ŌĆŗcarrying out strikes ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗHezbollah command centers ŌĆīand ŌüĀweapons storage ŌĆŗfacilities ŌĆŗin ŌüĀBeirut.

item.title: IAEA confirms entrances to Iran's Natanz enrichment plant were bombed
item.description:
VIENNA, March 3 (Reuters) - Entrances to Iran's underground and ŌĆŗpreviously bombed uranium-enrichment plant at Natanz ŌĆīhave been struck as part of the U.S.-Israeli military attacks on the country, the U.N. ŌĆŗnuclear watchdog confirmed on Tuesday.
The underground ŌĆŗFuel Enrichment Plant (FEP) is one of Iran's ŌüĀthree uranium-enrichment plants that are known ŌĆŗto have been operating when Israel and ŌĆŗthe United States carried out strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities last June.
"Based on the latest available satellite ŌĆŗimagery, IAEA can now confirm some ŌĆŗrecent damage to entrance buildings of Iran's underground ŌĆīNatanz ŌüĀFuel Enrichment Plant," the International Atomic Energy Agency said on X.
"No radiological consequence expected and no additional impact detected at FEP ŌĆŗitself, which ŌĆŗwas ŌüĀseverely damaged in the June conflict," it added.
The IAEA's finding fits ŌĆŗwith that of U.S. think-tank ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀInstitute for Science and International Security published on Monday after Iran said Natanz was hit ŌüĀon ŌĆŗSunday and the IAEA responded ŌĆŗthat any military strikes were not major.

item.title: At least 30,000 displaced people in shelters in Lebanon, says UN refugee agency
item.description:
GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - At least 30,000 displaced ŌĆŗpeople have sought protection ŌĆīin shelters in Lebanon since hostilities ŌĆŗbetween Israel and ŌĆŗHezbollah began on Monday, the United ŌüĀNations refugee agency ŌĆŗsaid on Tuesday.
"Conservative ŌĆŗestimates suggest that nearly 30,000 people were hosted ŌĆŗand registered at ŌĆŗcollective shelters," said UNHCR spokesperson ŌĆīBabar ŌüĀBaloch.
"Many more slept in their cars on the side ŌĆŗof ŌĆŗroads ŌüĀor were still stuck in ŌĆŗtraffic jams ŌĆŗon ŌüĀthe roads," he added.

item.title: Key Gaza crossing will be reopened, says World Food Programme
item.description:
GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - A key aid crossing ŌĆīthat had been momentarily closed into Gaza will reopen imminently, the United Nations World Food Programme said ŌĆŗon Tuesday.
Humanitarian supplies will be able ŌĆŗto enter through the Kerem Shalom ŌüĀcrossing into the enclave between Tuesday and ŌĆŗWednesday, the WFP said.
"That is timely for ŌĆŗus, and we need to get in aid as fast as we can," Samer Abdel Jaber, ŌĆŗWFP Regional Director for the Middle ŌĆŗEast, North Africa, and Eastern Europe, told reporters in Geneva ŌĆīvia ŌüĀvideo link from Cairo.
Israeli government agency COGAT said it will reopen the Kerem Shalom crossing starting on Tuesday for the gradual ŌĆŗentry of humanitarian ŌĆŗaid ŌüĀinto the Gaza Strip.
COGAT said on Saturday that crossings into the ŌĆŗGaza Strip, vital for the ŌĆŗdelivery ŌüĀof humanitarian aid and the movement of patients in need of medical evacuation, were ŌüĀclosed ŌĆŗas Israeli and U.S. ŌĆŗforces attacked Iran.

item.title: World Cup ticket frenzy unfolds amid global unrest
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - With 100 days to go until the tournament kicks off, appetite for tickets to the ŌĆŗ2026 World Cup in the U.S., Mexico and Canada is reaching fever pitch, despite eye-watering prices that have fans crying foul amid global unrest ŌĆīfollowing the U.S. attack on Iran.
In addition to the U.S.-Israeli strikes against Iran -- a country scheduled to play its World Cup group stage games in the U.S. -- the heavy-handed crackdowns by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement personnel and the violence that erupted near host city Guadalajara after the death of Mexico's most-wanted cartel leader are causing concern for fans.
"I'm afraid I might not be allowed into the ŌĆŗcountry, I've decided to fly to Canada at most, but not to the USA," German soccer fan Tom Roeder told Reuters.
"I hope that at least the ŌĆŗissue of war with Iran does not reach North America. At least not in a way that affects us personally."
FIFA, which ŌüĀdid not immediately respond to a request for comment, has said nearly two million tickets were sold in the first two sales phases, with demand so intense that World ŌĆŗCup tickets were oversubscribed over 30 times.
Political and social tensions surrounding host nations are nothing new for the World Cup.
Mexico President Claudia Sheinbaum said there was " no risk " ŌĆŗto fans coming to the country and Adrian Nunez Corte, leader of Unipes, a fans' association in Spain, said it has not affected willingness to buy tickets.
"Obviously, it is causing concern, but some Spanish fans living in the area have helped to calm things down after the initial hours of alarm," Corte said.
"There is no alarm regarding U.S. immigration policy but people are taking preparation of ŌĆŗthe necessary visas seriously to avoid problems, especially since some fans will be travelling between the U.S. and Mexico due to the match schedule."
The ŌĆŗbuzz around the tournament in North America is unprecedented.
"The demand for the 2026 World Cup in the USA, Canada and Mexico is the strongest I've ever experienced," said Michael Edgley, director at ŌĆīAustralia's Green ŌüĀand Gold Army Travel.
"I think FIFA will make record amounts of money. There's no question.
"This World Cup will be a massive financial success and the beneficiaries will be the member federations."
But such popularity comes with a price.
Geography adds another layer of complexity as the tournament spans 16 host cities across three countries, making it more challenging and expensive for fans wanting to follow their teams.
"The price of tickets has been a major drawback, particularly affecting the number of matches each fan will attend, as well as the ŌĆŗdistances between venues and the costs involved," ŌĆŗsaid Corte.
The sticker shock ŌüĀis even more pronounced this year, especially with a huge resale market where tickets are sold at above face value, which is legal in the United States and Canada.
FIFA defended the secondary market model.
"Unlike the entities behind profit-driven third-party ticket marketplaces, FIFA is ŌĆŗa not-for-profit organisation," a spokesperson said.
"Revenue generated from the FIFA World Cup 2026 ticket sales model is reinvested into the global ŌĆŗdevelopment of football... FIFA ŌüĀexpects to reinvest more than 90% of its budgeted investment for the 2023-2026 cycle back into the game."
Mehdi Salem, vice-president of French football fans association Les Baroudeurs du Sport, said they are seeing more than a 200% increase on what they were told in 2018 by the French federation and by FIFA.
The pricing pain is so acute that Salem's association, which ŌüĀboasts around ŌĆŗ400 members, will have only 100 attend the tournament - a dramatic drop that he attributes to ticket ŌĆŗprices and the political landscape in the United States.
"We feel like this World Cup will not really be a people's World Cup, but rather an elitist World Cup," Salem added.
(This story has been refiled to add the first name, designation, and the tour operator, in paragraph 11)

item.title: Turkey eyes lifting U.S. CAATSA sanctions before U.S. midterms
item.description:
ANKARA, March 3 (Reuters) - Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said work was underway with the United States to lift sanctions imposed ŌĆŗon Ankara before the U.S. midterm elections in November, ŌĆībut added Israel was opposed to the potential move.
In 2020, Washington imposed CAATSA sanctions on fellow NATO member Turkey over its acquisition of ŌĆŗRussian S-400 systems. It also removed Ankara from ŌĆŗthe F-35 fighter jet programme, a move Turkey called ŌüĀunjust and illegal.
The sides are seeking a workaround to lift ŌĆŗsanctions and allow Ankara to buy the jets. Little progress ŌĆŗhas been made even though U.S. President Donald Trump has good ties with Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan, raising hopes in Turkey that a ŌĆŗsolution can be found this year.
Asked during a Ramadan fast-breaking ŌĆŗdinner on Tuesday with members of Turkish media if Israel - which Turkey ŌĆīhas ŌüĀcriticised fiercely over its assault on Gaza - opposed the U.S. lifting the sanctions, Fidan said Israel did not want regional countries to obtain certain capabilities.
"That is the mode they are ŌĆŗin now. Let's ŌĆŗsee, these ŌüĀbattles will continue. Everyone will play the cards in their hands somehow," he said, according ŌĆŗto a transcript of his comments on Wednesday.
"Work ŌĆŗis ŌüĀunderway to obtain a result on CAATSA before the midterms. We have taken the political steps. We'll see," Fidan added.
Fidan's comments ŌüĀcome ŌĆŗas the United States and Israel are ŌĆŗat war with Iran, Turkey's neighbour. Ankara has condemned the U.S. and ŌĆŗIsraeli offensive against Tehran.

item.title: Lebanon sucked deeper into war as Hezbollah, Israel trade blows
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, March 3 (Reuters) - Lebanon was pulled deeper into the war in the Middle East on Tuesday as the Iran-backed Lebanese group Hezbollah launched ŌĆŗmissiles at Israel for a second consecutive day and Israel sent troops into the south and carried out waves of air ŌĆīstrikes.
Israel's neighbour to the north and the theatre of numerous conflicts between Israel and Hezbollah, Lebanon had avoided spillover from the U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran until Monday, when Hezbollah opened fire with drones and missiles.
With dozens of people killed in retaliatory airstrikes, Hezbollah's move to enter the conflict has sharpened long-standing divisions in Lebanon over its status as an ŌĆŗarmed group - the only Lebanese faction to keep its weapons after the 1975-90 civil war.
The government on Monday took the unprecedented step of ŌĆŗoutlawing Hezbollah's military activities. The pro-Hezbollah al-Akhbar newspaper on Tuesday condemned this as a "capitulation to dictates, which could even lead ŌüĀto the outbreak of civil war".
Towers of smoke rose from the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of Beirut as Israel launched new airstrikes.
Many thousands of Lebanese have ŌĆŗfled homes in areas that bore the brunt of a major war between Israel and Hezbollah in 2024. The United Nations said that, by Monday, ŌĆŗan estimated 29,000 people, including 9,000 children, had fled.
The Israeli military said it had deployed additional forces to southern Lebanon overnight, saying this was to take up defensive positions to guard against any potential Hezbollah attack.
"We're only at the border line area in a defensive manner to prevent attacks against civilians and very strategic important points," Lieutenant Colonel ŌĆŗNadav Shoshani said.
Defence Minister Israel Katz said in a statement he had authorised the military to advance and take control of additional positions.
The Israeli military ŌĆŗon Monday night ordered residents of the entire border strip of southern Lebanon to evacuate.
A Lebanese security source said Israeli troops were carrying out incursions along some parts ŌĆīof the ŌüĀborder. Witnesses said the Lebanese army had pulled out of at least seven forward-operating positions along the border.
Israel kept some troops in the south at several hilltop positions following a ceasefire in the 2024 war.
The south, predominantly Shi'ite Muslim, has long been a key Hezbollah stronghold, where it has drawn political support and deployed weaponry ahead of the 2024 conflict. The Lebanese army has moved into the area and seized its weapons caches since that conflict, from which ŌĆŗHezbollah emerged greatly weakened.
Hezbollah announced three ŌĆŗseparate attacks on Tuesday using attack ŌüĀdrones and missiles and saying these had targeted military facilities in northern Israel.
A missile from Lebanon hit a house in northern Israel, Israeli media reported. Israel's ambulance service said a man was treated for ŌĆŗglass shrapnel injuries.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun said rockets fired at Israel on Monday from Lebanon came from outside ŌĆŗthe southern border zone ŌüĀwhere the army declared its control in January.
The Lebanese health ministry on Monday reported that 52 people had been killed in Israeli attacks in Lebanon and more than 150 wounded. It has not issued an update on Tuesday.
Overnight, an Israeli airstrike hit the headquarters of Hezbollah's al-Manar TV in Beirut.
The Israeli military reported more ŌüĀairstrikes in ŌĆŗBeirut on Tuesday, saying it had hit "command centres, weapons storage facilities, and satellite communication components ŌĆŗbelonging to HezbollahŌĆÖs intelligence headquarters in Beirut".
"These assets were operating under civilian cover," it said.
The Israeli military said it had taken steps to mitigate civilian harm, including the use of advanced warnings.

item.title: European capitals push back as Ukraine seeks fast-track EU membership
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 3 (Reuters) - UkraineŌĆÖs push for a fast track into the European Union as part of any ŌĆŗpeace deal has hit stiff resistance from EU governments, who do not want to open the PandoraŌĆÖs box of problems that they fear a quick accession would ŌĆīunleash.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy wants a 2027 date to join the EU as part of a settlement to end Russia's war , hoping this would anchor his country in EuropeŌĆÖs main political club and offer a path to greater prosperity, security and stability.
A clear route into the EU could be vital for Zelenskiy in selling any peace settlement to Ukrainians, particularly if - as is widely expected - Ukraine does not regain control of all of its territory or join ŌĆŗthe NATO military alliance, analysts say.
But EU governments - including heavyweights France and Germany - have privately voiced scepticism about a mooted reform of the accession process that would shorten Ukraine's path ŌĆŗto membership, diplomats say. Reuters spoke with eight European diplomats and officials, and many pointed to unease among EU capitals about the idea.
Among their ŌüĀconcerns is that Ukraine and others would not press on with reforms , such as cracking down on corruption, if it has already been granted EU membership.
Ukrainian Deputy Prime Minister and lead EU negotiator ŌĆŗTaras Kachka told Reuters that Kyiv was ready to address EU members' concerns . He proposed safeguards such as a monitoring system to check Kyiv was sticking to democratic standards and a transition period before it ŌĆŗreceived EU farm subsidies.
But he said a political commitment to a membership date would be important. "This is necessary for the peace process, for the establishing of long-lasting and just peace in Europe," he said.
The current process for joining the bloc is usually long and bureaucratic, even in straightforward cases, involving years of detailed negotiations and legal reforms to meet EU democratic and economic standards.
Every step of the accession process, ŌĆŗwhich is divided into chapters and clusters of policy issues, also requires approval from all EU members. Hungary has blocked Ukraine's path early in the process.
European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, head ŌĆŗof the blocŌĆÖs executive, has floated a revamp of the process behind closed doors, diplomats say.
Instead of carrying out all the required reforms first, a country could join the EU after meeting some minimum requirements ŌĆō but with ŌĆīlimited access to ŌüĀEU funds and decision-making until they meet all the membership benchmarks.
That could make it easier for Ukraine and others such as Moldova, Montenegro and Albania to join quickly ŌĆō although ZelenskiyŌĆÖs target of January 1, 2027, would still be unrealistic, not least as all 27 current EU members have to ratify any accession.
Kachka suggested Ukraine could at least sign an accession treaty with the EU next year, even if ratification and other steps might take longer to implement.
But analysts and diplomats see little appetite for such bold moves, including von der Leyen's mooted revamp, known as "reverse enlargement" ŌĆŗas it turns the process on its head ŌĆŗby letting a country join before it ŌüĀhas met all the current criteria.
"The growing support for populist and anti-enlargement parties in several capitals makes governments cautious about being seen to accelerate a process they have not yet built public consent for," said Anastasia Pociumban, a research fellow at the German Council on Foreign Relations.
"The concept of reverse ŌĆŗenlargement is dead," said one EU diplomat. "There is also no support for giving a concrete accession date."
A Western European official said: "Ukraine is just ŌĆŗnot ready and has rampant ŌüĀcorruption."
Von der Leyen tried to temper expectations in Ukraine last week. She told Zelenskiy publicly that "dates by themselves are not possible" even as she praised the countryŌĆÖs reforms in the midst of war.
Nevertheless, the Commission is expected to continue to push for ways to get Kyiv into the EU in the next few years, arguing that membership would strengthen the bloc militarily amid fears Moscow ŌüĀmay attack ŌĆŗan EU member next.
Some say a model of gradual integration, whereby aspiring members take part in more and more EU ŌĆŗprogrammes and meetings before joining the club, may be more feasible.
"I think a full accession within the next few years remains unlikely," Corina Stratulat, associate director at the European Policy Centre, said of Ukraine's prospects.
"What seems more likely to happen instead ŌĆŗis accelerated integration - single market access, energy, digital, transport - phased participation in EU programmes and policies."

item.title: Trump's Asian allies fear Iran war will sap defences against China
item.description:
TOKYO/TAIPEI/SYDNEY, March 3 (Reuters) - Japanese lawmakers reeling from attacks on Iran by the United States and Israel gathered on Monday at the ruling party's offices in Tokyo to question bureaucrats about evacuation plans, energy stocks and the legal ŌĆŗbasis for U.S. action.
But one query posed at the closed-door meeting, described to Reuters by a politician who attended, reflected a deeper fear haunting Asia's corridors of power since ŌĆīTrump's weekend attacks unleashed chaos in the Middle East.
How would the region respond to a hole left in its defences if Washington diverted ships and missiles it now uses to deter China?
The problem is urgent for Japan and South Korea, home to big U.S. military bases that help counter China's military flexing and nuclear-armed North Korea, as well as for democratic Taiwan, claimed by Beijing and armed by Washington.
"We hope this operation is fast, limited, and that resources can be promptly shifted back to Asia," said ŌĆŗChen Kuan-ting, a ruling party lawmaker in Taiwan who sits on its parliament's foreign affairs and defence committee.
A prolonged conflict could harm "stability and peace in the Indo-Pacific", said Chen, adding that Taipei ŌĆŗmust prepare for Beijing to step up "coercion" while the United States is distracted.
Trump, who has said U.S. operations in the Middle East will last four or five ŌüĀweeks, but could be sustained far longer, plans to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping at the end of March, though Beijing has not confirmed his visit.
Taiwan is an internal matter for China and Beijing firmly opposes ŌĆŗthe use of force to infringe on the sovereignty and security of other countries, Mao Ning, a spokesperson for China's foreign ministry said on Tuesday.
The U.S. State and Defense Departments did not immediately respond to a request ŌĆŗfor comment for this report.
The Japanese politician who recounted Monday's questioning said a top foreign ministry official replied that Tokyo had sought assurances from Washington that it would not shift U.S. military assets.
About 40% of U.S. navy ships ready for operations are currently stationed around the Middle East, the Center for Strategic and International Studies said in a report last month.
These include an aircraft carrier, the Abraham Lincoln, and at least six missile destroyers based in the Pacific ports of California, Hawaii ŌĆŗand Japan, the U.S. Naval Institute said on Monday.
The only U.S. carrier deployed in Asia, the George Washington, is undergoing maintenance at its base in the Japanese city of Yokosuka.
"The U.S. Navy is stretched thin," said ŌĆŗBryan Clark, a former U.S. defence official specialising in naval operations at the Hudson Institute.
If the war dragged on, there was a realistic possibility that the United States could draw down its naval strength in Asia to reinforce the Iran ŌĆīconflict, he added.
"The ŌüĀfleet ... is not sufficient to keep a steady presence in every theatre."
The Iran conflict is also depleting reserves of U.S. munitions, about which experts have long warned. The U.S. military has asked defence firms to step up production, but that could take several years.
That is a concern for the United States because rebuilding munitions reserves in the Indo-Pacific helps deter China from military action on Taiwan over the medium term, said a U.S. official, who sought anonymity as the matter is a sensitive one.
Japan has already faced delays in deliveries of hundreds of Tomahawk missiles ordered from the United States and could fall further behind schedule, said Jan van Tol, ŌĆŗa senior fellow at the Center for Strategic and ŌĆŗBudgetary Assessments.
It is just three months since ŌüĀWashington unveiled a new security strategy that framed the Indo-Pacific as the key "geopolitical battleground", and made a top priority of deterring a conflict over Taiwan.
Since then, Trump has captured the leader of Venezuela in an audacious military strike, threatened to annex Greenland and teamed with Israel to launch the air war against Iran.
But while allies ŌĆŗin Asia worry he may be taking his eye off the prize, some analysts say Beijing has little to cheer, at least for now.
By hitting ŌĆŗVenezuela and Iran, Trump weakened two ŌüĀof its allies that sent China streams of cheap oil, buoying its economy.
Some analysts have even suggested his military actions are part of a grand plan to enable the United States to focus on containing China.
But the longer Trump's entanglement in the Middle East persists, the more Beijing could start to benefit.
"The grand strategy is supposed to be 'contain Iran in the Middle East, then shift resources toward dealing with China,'" said a Japanese ruling party lawmaker, speaking ŌüĀon condition of ŌĆŗanonymity.
"But the question is whether there will be enough resources left to shift."
China has exploited previous episodes of U.S. distraction, said ŌĆŗJennifer Parker, a former warfare officer with the Royal Australian Navy, pointing to its rapid militarisation of South China Sea islands as the U.S. pursued the war in Afghanistan.
"Beijing will be watching closely," added Parker, a non-resident fellow at the Sydney-based Lowy Institute think tank.

item.title: US debuts suicide drone in Iran after fast-tracked Pentagon procurement
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - The U.S. successfully debuted a lowŌĆæcost suicide drone in combat ŌĆŗin Iran just eight months after its Pentagon unveiling, as the U.S. pushes to accelerate weapons programs.
The Low-Cost ŌĆīUncrewed Combat Attack System (LUCAS) drone, manufactured by Arizona's SpektreWorks, was showcased in July 2025 when Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth walked the Pentagon's inner courtyard with more than a dozen companies competing to supply the military with new equipment.
Drones have become central to modern warfare following their effective use in ŌĆŗthe Ukraine war , including Iran-made Shahed systems flown by Russia that closely resemble the LUCAS. The sector is also among ŌĆŗthe most fiercely competitive in the U.S. defense industry, with SpektreWorks vying for Pentagon contracts against major ŌüĀdefense primes and a wave of Silicon ValleyŌĆōbacked startups such as Anduril, Shield AI and AeroVironment (AVAV.O) , opens new tab .
U.S. Central Command said LUCAS drones ŌĆŗare modeled after the Shahed.
The rapid fielding of the LUCAS represents a departure from traditional Pentagon acquisition timelines, which typically ŌĆŗspan years from initial development to operational deployment. Defense officials said the compressed timeline reflects lessons learned from observing drone warfare in Ukraine, where both sides have employed thousands of low-cost unmanned systems.
The LUCAS deployment comes as the Pentagon pushes to rapidly expand American industrial capacity for producing inexpensive, attritable ŌĆŗdrones under the $1 billion Drone Dominance Program authorized in the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act of 2025."
The LUCAS drone uses an open ŌĆŗarchitecture that allows different payloads and communications systems, and can be deployed either for strikes or as a target drone, according to company materials. ŌĆīIt ŌüĀcan be launched from the ground or a truck. At about $35,000 each, it is far cheaper than the MQŌĆæ9 Reaper, which costs roughly $20 million to $40 million but is reusable and far more sophisticated.
The government owns the LUCAS design intellectual property, meaning multiple manufacturers could produce the system, though SpektreWorks currently holds manufacturing contracts.
SpektreWorks declined to comment for this story.
During ŌĆŗits development at the Pentagon, the ŌĆŗLUCAS drone was paired with ŌüĀsatellite communications systems including ViasatŌĆÖs MUSIC and SpaceXŌĆÖs Starlink or Starshield, according to two sources familiar with the program. Reuters could not determine what connectivity systems are being used during current Iran ŌĆŗoperations.
Neither SpaceX nor Viasat returned requests for comment.
A startup called Noda provides the software ŌĆŗto control the ŌüĀdrones, known as an "orchestrator" that allows warfighters to control multiple autonomous systems, one of the sources familiar with the program said. Noda declined to comment.
Drone experts told Reuters the LUCAS design shares similarities with Iran's Shahed drone, which Tehran has supplied to Russia for use in ŌüĀUkraine. The ŌĆŗShahed is believed to be a copy of Israel's Harpy loitering munition, ŌĆŗaccording to defense analysts. The Harpy design has been widely replicated by countries including China and Taiwan.
The LUCAS also bears resemblance to the Drone Anti-Radar (DAR), a loitering ŌĆŗmunition jointly developed in the 1970s and 1980s.

item.title: US homeland chief Noem to face scrutiny on immigration crackdown at Senate hearing
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - U.S. Department of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem is expected to face tough questions ŌĆŗover President Donald Trump 's immigration crackdown during a Senate hearing on Tuesday, with funding for her department still stalled ŌĆīdue to Democratic objections to its aggressive tactics.
Noem, appointed by the Republican president last year, also may field questions on other matters including possible threats to the United States after the U.S. attacks on Iran and reports of disorder within her department.
The former South Dakota governor has overseen Trump's immigration agenda, including ŌĆŗthe deployment of thousands of masked federal agents to U.S. cities, where they have swept through neighborhoods in search of ŌĆŗpossible immigration offenders and clashed with residents.
Noem is scheduled to testify before the Senate Judiciary Committee ŌüĀon Tuesday and the House of Representatives Judiciary Committee on Wednesday.
Trump has said mass deportations are needed after high levels of ŌĆŗillegal immigration under his Democratic predecessor, Joe Biden, and has defended Noem's job performance.
Criticism of Noem intensified after federal immigration agents killed ŌĆŗtwo U.S. citizens in Minneapolis in January. In both fatal shootings, Noem quickly called the victims "domestic terrorists" instead of waiting for a full investigation, leading to rebukes from both Democrats and Republicans as video evidence undercut her assertions about the events.
U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement is an agency within her department.
Democrats ŌĆŗin Congress have refused to agree to new funding for her department without changes to immigration enforcement practices. Funding for the ŌĆŗ260,000-employee department lapsed last month , but most of its immigration enforcement and national security operations are deemed essential and continue.
House Democrats launched an effort in January ŌĆīto ŌüĀimpeach Noem, saying immigration agents under her leadership have violated the civil rights of U.S. citizens, that she had blocked congressional oversight of detention centers for migrants, and that she gave government contracts to Republican-affiliated firms and people connected to close associates of hers.
The impeachment effort is likely to fail, with the House controlled by Republicans.
During her testimony, Noem also could face questions concerning the actions ŌĆŗof her top adviser, longtime ŌĆŗTrump ally Corey Lewandowski.
Citing sources, ŌüĀReuters reported last week that Lewandowski entered the cockpit uninvited on a U.S. government plane last year during a flight on which he later fired the pilot because Noem's blanket had been misplaced. ŌĆŗLewandowski said the facts as related by the sources were wrong but he did not respond ŌĆŗto certain questions ŌüĀabout the incident.
Trump's immigration policies, once an area of strength, could now be a liability for Republicans trying to retain control of Congress in November midterm elections. A Reuters/Ipsos poll in February found that while a majority of Americans support deporting immigrants without legal status, about ŌüĀ60% think ŌĆŗimmigration agents have gone too far.

item.title: Russia raises alarm over conflict's impact on Bushehr nuclear plant in Iran

MOSCOW, March 3 (Reuters) - The Bushehr nuclear power plant in Iran ŌĆŗis under threat as a ŌĆīresult of the escalating conflict, the head of Russian state nuclear corporation Rosatom ŌĆŗsaid on Tuesday, according ŌĆŗto the RIA state news agency.
"There ŌüĀis certainly a threat to ŌĆŗthe plant," Likhachev was cited as ŌĆŗsaying. "Explosions can be heard just kilometres away", he added, saying the plant itself was ŌĆŗnot being targeted.
Rosatom said on ŌĆŗSaturday it had evacuated nearly 100 people from ŌĆīIran, including ŌüĀemployees' children and non-essential staff, but other personnel have remained at the plant, which was built by ŌĆŗRussia in ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀIranian port city of Bushehr.
Likhachev said the next stage ŌĆŗof evacuations involving 150 ŌĆŗto ŌüĀ200 people from the plant would happen when the situation permits, according ŌüĀto ŌĆŗcomments cited by ŌĆŗthe Interfax news agency.

item.title: One in three EU women face violence, most cases unreported, survey finds
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 3 (Reuters) - Physical and sexual violence affects roughly a third of women in the European Union during their lifetime, but most incidents ŌĆŗgo unreported, a survey revealed on Tuesday.
The results of the ŌĆīsurvey, conducted by the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights (FRA) and the European Institute for Gender Equality, showed that only 11.3% of women reported physical or sexual abuse by ŌĆŗnon-partners to the police, and just 6.1% reported violence by intimate ŌĆŗpartners.
Common reasons for not reporting violence included shame, self-blame, fear, ŌüĀand distrust in law enforcement. Limited awareness or access to support ŌĆŗservices also emerged as contributing factors.
About 30.7% of women across the bloc said ŌĆŗthey had experienced violence, a slight decrease from the 33% recorded in the first such survey in 2012. The study also highlighted other widespread forms of abuse, including psychological, ŌĆŗeconomic, and online abuse.
"Violence against women is a fundamental rights violation," ŌĆŗFRA Director Sirpa Rautio said. "Member states have clear obligations to prevent violence, protect victims and ŌĆīensure ŌüĀaccess to justice, and these findings show there is still urgent work to do."
Prevalence rates varied significantly, ranging from 57.1% in Finland to 11.9% in Bulgaria.
Finland's Nordic neighbours, known for high gender equality, also showed high levels ŌĆŗof violence, with ŌĆŗ52.5% of women ŌüĀin Sweden and 47.5% in Denmark experiencing violence, while lower-equality countries to the south show more complex reporting ŌĆŗdynamics.
This disparity, termed the "Nordic paradox", could reflect actual variations in ŌĆŗwomen's ŌüĀexperiences or contrasting reporting behaviors and different perceptions of violence related to sexual encounters between countries, according to researchers.
FRA's first survey on violence against women in ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗEU in 2012 showed the same paradox.
The new ŌĆŗstudy drew on interviews with nearly 115,000 women aged 18 to 74 carried out between ŌĆŗSeptember 2020 and March 2024.

item.title: UN urges investigation into 'horrific' attack on Iran school

GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - The U.N. human rights office urged the "forces" ŌĆŗbehind an attack on ŌĆīa girls' school in Iran to investigate and share insights ŌĆŗinto the "horrific" incident, without ŌĆŗnaming them.
"The High Commissioner (Volker Turk) ŌüĀcalls for a prompt, ŌĆŗimpartial and thorough investigation ŌĆŗinto the circumstances of the attack. The onus is on the ŌĆŗforces that carried out ŌĆŗthe attack to investigate it," U.N. ŌĆīhuman ŌüĀrights office spokesperson Ravina Shamdasani told a Geneva press briefing, describing the incident as "horrific".
Secretary ŌĆŗof ŌĆŗState ŌüĀMarco Rubio said U.S. forces "would not deliberately ŌĆŗtarget a school", after ŌĆŗIranian ŌüĀstate media reported over 160 were killed on the first ŌüĀday ŌĆŗof the U.S. ŌĆŗand Israeli attacks on Iran.

item.title: UK planning to send warship to defend Cyprus military base, Times newspaper says

LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - Britain is planning ŌĆŗto send a warship to ŌĆīdefend its Royal Air Force Akrotiri base in Cyprus from Iranian ŌĆŗattacks, the Times newspaper said ŌĆŗon Tuesday.

item.title: Turkey says it's engaging with all sides to end Iran war, resume diplomacy
item.description:
ANKARA, March 3 (Reuters) - Turkey is engaging with all parties to find a way to end the war in Iran and return to negotiations, Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said, adding that Ankara ŌĆŗwas also in talks with Oman on the matter as the Gulf nation works ŌĆīfor the same goal.
The United States and Israel launched strikes against Iran on Saturday. The war quickly widened with Tehran striking Gulf states that host U.S. bases and Israel attacking Iranian-backed Hezbollah targets in Lebanon.
Turkey, a NATO member ŌĆŗand neighbour of Iran, had for weeks urged Washington and Tehran to reach an ŌĆŗagreement during their rounds of negotiations over Iran's nuclear programme and missile capabilities, ŌüĀwarning that the region could not handle any more destabilisation.
Fidan told members of Turkish media ŌĆŗduring a Ramadan fast-breaking dinner on Tuesday that Turkey was "sensitively carrying out necessary initiatives with all ŌĆŗour counterparts" to achieve peace in the region, and added it was critical to preserve the stability of Iran and the region.
"There isn't a single-layered negotiation, there is a multi-layered negotiation," Fidan said, according to a transcript ŌĆŗof his comments shared on Tuesday.
"We have been talking to the Europeans for a few days... ŌĆŗIf you want peace, let's work together. We are urging them to take action. The Gulf nations are ŌĆīnow ŌüĀfacing a serious situation," he said.
"We are talking to the Omani (foreign minister). Oman is also still trying to do something there. We are talking to the Americans."
In his strongest yet opposition to the attacks against Iran, Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan said on Tuesday that they were a "clear violation" ŌĆŗof international law .
Fidan warned against ŌĆŗthe conflict engulfing ŌüĀthe whole region and affecting energy supplies. He added that the closure of the Hormuz Strait, where one-fifth of global oil trade skirts Iran's ŌĆŗcoast, could "push the United States to obtain a quick result".
He said Iran ŌĆŗwas trying ŌüĀto "create costs" for the U.S. and its partners by attacking Gulf states and energy infrastructure, but added that he didn't believe Tehran could obtain the desired result that way.
"By bombing these places, Iran ŌüĀwill say ŌĆŗthey should pressure the United States and end the war, ŌĆŗbut that doesn't seem like it will happen. I don't know how much Iran has left," he said, adding that ŌĆŗTehran could instead "bother" Israel with missile strikes.

item.title: Putin will convey Arab concerns to Iran about attacks on oil infrastructure, Kremlin says
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 3 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin will convey to Iran concerns among Arab leaders about Tehran's strikes ŌĆŗon oil infrastructure in the region, the Kremlin ŌĆīsaid on Tuesday, as the Iran conflict continued to widen .
Putin held a flurry of phone calls on Monday with four Arab Gulf state ŌĆŗleaders, offering to use Moscow's ties to Tehran - with ŌĆŗwhich it has a strategic partnership - to try ŌüĀand defuse tensions in the region.
"Putin will certainly make every ŌĆŗeffort to contribute to at least a slight easing of ŌĆŗtensions," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters on Tuesday.
"Yesterday's discussions with virtually all of his interlocutors focused on Putin conveying his deep concern ŌĆŗabout the strikes on their infrastructure to our colleagues ŌĆŗin Iran, taking advantage of the dialogue we maintain with the Iranian ŌĆīleadership."
Oil ŌüĀprices rose on Tuesday for a third day as Iran responded to U.S. and Israeli attacks with strikes against energy infrastructure in Gulf countries and against tankers in the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz.
Putin offered his ŌĆŗcondolences to ŌüĀthe family of Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in a note to President Masoud ŌĆŗPezeshkian over the weekend, but the Kremlin ŌĆŗhas not ŌüĀpublicised any further contact with senior Iranian leadership since the strikes began on Saturday.
Putin has also not spoken with U.S. ŌüĀPresident ŌĆŗDonald Trump, and Peskov said there ŌĆŗwere no plans for such a conversation at this time.

item.title: Iran's strikes on Gulf states may widen war against Tehran, analysts say
item.description:
DUBAI, March 3 (Reuters) - Iranian airstrikes on Gulf states could push them into a broad coalition aligned with the United States and widen the war against Iran , Middle East analysts said, after attacks on ports, cities and oil facilities in a vital energy-producing region.
By striking at the GulfŌĆÖs economic lifelines ŌĆŗin its response to U.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran, Tehran may be driving wary Gulf states closer to Washington and towards coordinated action against the Islamic Republic, the ŌĆīanalysts said.
The aim of the strikes against six Gulf states, all of which are U.S. allies and host American military bases, was to ensure those countries put pressure on U.S. President Donald Trump to halt the war, but Iran appears to have miscalculated, they said.
"The Gulf states now face stark choices: to join the United States more openly in its war effort - allowing their skies and territory to be used and potentially participating in military operations - or risk further escalation on their own soil," Abdulaziz Sager, Chairman ŌĆŗof the Saudi-based Gulf Research Center think tank told Reuters.
The option of neutrality receded when Iranian missiles started landing and "forced us to be their enemies," pushing states that once hedged their position ŌĆŗinto open alignment with Washington and a readiness to defend their territory and interests, he said.
Iran unleashed its missile barrages after Supreme ŌüĀLeader Ali Khamenei was killed on Saturday, the first day of U.S. and Israeli military strikes that Trump said were intended to prevent a security threat to the U.S. and stop Iran developing nuclear arms.
The ŌĆŗGulf Cooperation Council (GCC) - Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Oman - held an emergency ministerial meeting on Sunday, invoking Article 51 of the U.N. Charter, setting "red lines" and signalling readiness for collective self-defence as energy disruptions ŌĆŗand security risks mount.
The GCC message to Tehran was unequivocal - that Iran's attacks had served to strengthen unity among the Gulf states.
It said continued strikes would risk transforming the Gulf from a defensive shield into an "active theatre of response", and the Gulf states have activated joint air-defence systems and reconnaissance flights across regional airspace.
The choice is stark: respond and risk a wider war, or hold back and accept the erosion of security, economic stability and international credibility under repeated attacks, analysts say.
Gulf officials ŌĆŗsay messages have been conveyed to Tehran, directly and indirectly, warning that further attacks would carry far greater consequences for the Islamic Republic.
"Practically, they're going to try and wait as much as possible. The Americans are ŌĆŗhitting them and the Israelis are hitting them," said one Gulf insider.
He said it was unclear who was directing events in Iran - whether strikes on oil facilities were centrally ordered or carried out by rogue units. Two scenarios appeared to be ŌĆīemerging: either command ŌüĀhas fractured, with units acting autonomously, or decisions are still being coordinated at the top.
The stakes extend far beyond the Gulf, with oil exports, shipping lanes and energy infrastructure threatened, a vital energy corridor endangered and global trade and markets suffering repercussions.
Global energy markets face one of their gravest shocks in decades. Missile threats have forced shutdowns at some Gulf energy assets, including QatarŌĆÖs LNG facilities - around 20% of global supply - and exposed the impact on energy markets .
"If the strikes continue at this pace, and the Gulf cannot withstand a prolonged conflict, disruption to oil shipping lanes or closure of the Strait of Hormuz, it would be natural for ŌĆŗother countries to step in, because global interests would ŌĆŗbe directly affected," Ebtesam Al-Ketbi, President of the ŌüĀEmirates Policy Center, told Reuters.
Mohammed Baharoon, director-general of the Dubai Public Policy Research Center, said the logic for broadening the coalition against Iran had been strengthened by the United Arab Emirates describing the Iranian strikes as acts of terrorism.
"Iran is pushing the Gulf into an expanding coalition against it," he said. "By attacking Gulf states, Iran ŌĆŗis turning them into enemies and risking a wider war no one wants."
Attacks on Western-linked sites - from a British base in Cyprus to facilities hosting ŌĆŗFrench forces in Abu Dhabi - also ŌüĀraised the possibility of NATO eventually being drawn into the conflict.
The UAE has borne the brunt of IranŌĆÖs assault, with 63% of strikes targeting its airports, ports and oil infrastructure. Officials estimate that 165 ballistic and cruise missiles, along with 600 drones, were launched in the first 48 hours.
Missiles that struck Doha, Dubai and Manama over the weekend also targeted the Gulf statesŌĆÖ image as thriving financial, investment and tourism hubs , hurting their reputation as stable and safe havens .
While the ŌüĀUAE is determined ŌĆŗto avoid full-scale war, it has moved swiftly through diplomatic channels, summoning IranŌĆÖs ambassador, withdrawing its own envoy and closing its ŌĆŗembassy in Tehran and pressing the issue at the U.N. Security Council.
Gulf officials say Iran has fundamentally altered the diplomatic landscape by unleashing ballistic missiles and drones on such a scale.
They say TehranŌĆÖs actions have made it harder for future negotiations with the U.S. to ŌĆŗtreat IranŌĆÖs missile programme as separate from its nuclear ambitions, a position they say is now shared by Sunni Gulf Arab neighbours and Western governments.

item.title: UK working with airlines to boost Oman evacuations
