LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - Foreign minister Yvette Cooper said on Tuesday ŌĆŗthat Britain was working with ŌĆīairlines on increasing capacity out of Muscat, Oman to prioritise the ŌĆŗevacuation of vulnerable British ŌĆŗnationals.
Cooper, addressing lawmakers in parliament, ŌüĀsaid she was in ŌĆŗclose contact with her counterparts ŌĆŗin the Gulf countries, where 130,000 British citizens have now registered their ŌĆŗpresence in the region.
"We ŌĆŗare also working with airlines on increasing ŌĆīcapacity ŌüĀout of Muscat for British nationals, with priority for vulnerable nationals," Cooper said.
"A government ŌĆŗcharter fight ŌĆŗwill ŌüĀfly from Muscat in the coming days, ŌĆŗprioritising vulnerable nationals, but ŌĆŗBritish ŌüĀnationals in Oman must wait to be contacted by the ŌüĀforeign ŌĆŗoffice regarding these ŌĆŗoptions."

item.title: Trump says US war supplies mean it can fight 'forever'
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - Donald Trump said the U.S. military has enough stockpiled weapons to fight wars "forever", as the Republican president ŌĆŗand his administration continued their push to justify a broad, ŌĆīopen-ended war on Iran with shifting aims and timeline.
In a social media post overnight, Trump said there was a "virtually unlimited supply" of U.S. munitions ŌĆŗand that "wars can be fought "forever," and very successfully, using ŌĆŗjust these supplies."
"The United States is stocked, and ready ŌüĀto WIN, BIG!!!" he wrote.
His comments late on Monday ŌĆŗcome as the conflict enters its fourth day following U.S. and ŌĆŗIsraeli air strikes on Iran on Saturday.
Trump, who began his second term last year, campaigned in part on not starting any wars and instead ŌĆŗfocusing on the economy and has long derided the ŌĆŗUnited States' wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. Some top MAGA influencers have spoken out ŌĆīagainst ŌüĀthe latest Iran strikes even as Republicans have been generally supportive despite potential political risks ahead of November's midterm elections.
Trump earlier on Monday offered no details on how long the campaign against ŌĆŗTehran would last, ŌĆŗbut said ŌüĀit had been projected to last four to five weeks.
"We're already substantially ahead of our time ŌĆŗprojections. But whatever the time is, it's ŌĆŗOK. Whatever ŌüĀit takes," he said in his first public event since the conflict began, speaking briefly about the war ahead of a ŌüĀMedal ŌĆŗof Honor ceremony at the White ŌĆŗHouse.
Trump has not given a televised address to the nation, as is customary ŌĆŗat times of military action.

item.title: Tunisia jails tycoon Mabrouk and ex-PM Chahed on corruption charges
item.description:
TUNIS, March 3 (Reuters) - A Tunisian court on Tuesday sentenced the country's richest businessman, Marouan Mabrouk, son-in-law of ŌĆŗformer president Zine El Abidine Ben Ali, to 20 years in ŌĆīprison, and former prime minister Youssef Chahed to six years, in corruption cases, lawyers said.
Mabrouk has been held in prison since late 2023. Chahed, who served as prime ŌĆŗminister from 2016 to 2020, is currently abroad.
Mabrouk is part of ŌĆŗan influential family with business interests in trade, banking, communications ŌüĀand car dealerships. Mabrouk also controls a major supermarket chain and owns ŌĆŗshares in BIAT Bank, French telecoms operator Orange and a biscuit company.
He ŌĆŗis one of Ben Ali's few relatives who did not flee Tunisia after a revolution in 2011 that toppled Ben Ali.
Mabrouk, however, has faced criticism that he received support ŌĆŗand protection from successive governments after 2011.
Mabrouk was charged with money ŌĆŗlaundering, stealing funds from state companies and obtaining illegal benefits from ChahedŌĆÖs government, while Chahed ŌĆīwas ŌüĀsentenced to six years for his cabinetŌĆÖs approval for lifting the freezing of MabroukŌĆÖs funds in European banks.
The court also sentenced six other former ministers to six years in prison on the same charges as Chahed.
President ŌĆŗKais Saied, who seized ŌĆŗcontrol of ŌüĀthe government and dissolved parliament in 2021 in a move that the opposition described as a coup, created a ŌĆŗcommittee in 2022 to collect money from business owners ŌĆŗallegedly involved ŌüĀin financial corruption cases, to reduce Tunisia's budget deficit.
Saied had said that these business owners must pay and that the state would not give up ŌüĀwhat ŌĆŗis owed to it.
He said the state would ŌĆŗcollect no less than $5 billion. However, after several years, the reconciliation committee has not announced ŌĆŗany amounts had been received.

item.title: Israel uses cargo ships to repatriate doctors stranded abroad by war
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 3 (Reuters) - Israeli authorities are using container ships to bring back dozens of essential medical staff stuck abroad after the air war ŌĆŗwith Iran broke out on Saturday, disrupting travel, according to Israel's ŌĆīcargo firm ZIM Shipping.
A ZIM official told Reuters on Tuesday that its cargo ships were shuttling between Limassol in Cyprus and the Israeli port of Haifa, in ŌĆŗan operation coordinated between the Transportation Ministry, major Israeli hospitals and ŌĆŗthe company.
So far, 40 doctors have returned in two sailings - including ŌüĀone on Tuesday - and more shuttles are planned this week to repatriate ŌĆŗwhat could be hundreds of doctors, to deal with Israelis injured by ŌĆŗIranian missiles.
"It's the easiest way to get them home when there are no flights," said the official, who asked not to be named.
The doctors were away either at conventions ŌĆŗor on holidays and now sought to "go back as fast as ŌĆŗpossible to their jobs in case of emergencies", he added.
ZIM was re-routing ships from usual ŌĆīshipping ŌüĀroutes between Israel and Mediterranean ports such as in Greece and Italy, and making makeshift arrangements on the cargo vessels to keep the doctors safe and comfortable during the 15-hour voyage from Cyprus.
Israel and the U.S. ŌĆŗbegan bombing Iran on ŌĆŗSaturday, triggering ŌüĀa wave of retaliatory strikes across the Middle East that have led to flight chaos, with hundreds of thousands of ŌĆŗpassengers stranded worldwide due to the closure of airspaces.
U.S. ŌĆŗand Israeli ŌüĀofficials said the campaign could last weeks.
Israeli airlines have begun repatriation flights via Taba in Egypt, which borders the Red Sea resort city of Eilat ŌüĀin ŌĆŗsouthern Israel. However, passengers must then travel north ŌĆŗto major cities, which takes hours by car or bus.
"This (cargo ship option) is far more ŌĆŗefficient and easy," the official said.

item.title: Greek shipping minister warns of alarming situation in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
ATHENS, March 3 (Reuters) - Greece's shipping minister on Tuesday called for the protection of global shipping and seafarers, amid an "alarming" situation that ŌĆŗhas left dozens of ships stranded in the Strait of Hormuz and ŌĆīthe wider area due to the widening Iran conflict .
Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz was closed for a fourth day on Tuesday, choking off a key artery accounting ŌĆŗfor about 20% of global oil and gas supply. An Iranian ŌĆŗRevolutionary Guards senior official said on Monday Iran would fire on ŌüĀany ship trying to pass through, Iranian media reported.
Greece is a ŌĆŗdominant force in global shipping, controlling one of the worldŌĆÖs largest merchant ŌĆŗfleets.
"This is alarming and worrying, and I wish that global shipping was left out of war conflicts," Vassilis Kikilias told Reuters, when asked about the safety of ŌĆŗseafarers and how they can be protected.
"Global shipping has to do ŌĆŗwith global commerce, which everybody needs. And sailors, of course, are not to blame."
He ŌĆīsaid ŌüĀthat at least 10 Greek-flagged ships were in the Gulf and another five outside, their crews including dozens of Greek seafarers. More than 325 ships of Greek interests are in the wider area.
The shipping ministry ŌĆŗhas advised vessels ŌĆŗto avoid the region ŌüĀsince Saturday and the safety of seafarers is a top priority, Kikilias added.
"We're in communication with them ŌĆŗday and night, 24/7, I'm happy they're okay, but ŌĆŗI'm worried, ŌüĀand we are worried, of course, because the area is in danger," he said. "We cannot dismiss the possibility of bigger problems."
Greece said on Tuesday ŌüĀthat ŌĆŗit has set up a plan to ŌĆŗrepatriate thousands of its stranded nationals but their return is difficult as the airspace over ŌĆŗthe region remains closed.

item.title: How the Middle East crisis has affected sporting events
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - Iran's ongoing conflict with the U.S. and Israel has led to sporting events being postponed in several countries, while competitions elsewhere have been hit by travel disruption, with thousands of flights cancelled in some of the world's busiest transit ŌĆŗhubs.
The International Paralympic Committee (IPC) said it is working to find solutions after ŌĆīseveral athletes were finding it difficult to travel to the Milano Cortina Winter Paralympic Games due to travel disruptions in several Middle Eastern airports.
"The closure of airspace in the Middle East is impacting the arrival of some stakeholders... we are working diligently with Milano Cortina 2026 to find ŌĆŗsolutions for those affected," the IPC said on Tuesday.
Play in the Fujairah ŌĆŗChallenger event was halted abruptly due to a security alert on Tuesday, before being cancelled for the ŌüĀremainder of the day.
Authorities in Fujairah extinguished a fire caused by debris following the interception of a drone by air ŌĆŗdefences in the oil industry zone.
All sporting events in ŌĆŗIran have been cancelled until further notice, including the country's top-flight Persian Gulf Pro League. All Asian Champions League Elite matches in the Middle East on Monday and Tuesday were also postponed .
The Qatar Football Association indefinitely postponed all soccer matches in the country, putting in doubt a clash between Spain ŌĆŗand Argentina set for Doha on March 27.
Russian players Daniil Medvedev and Andrey Rublev, who ŌĆŗparticipated in the Dubai Tennis Championships last week, are facing challenges to reach California for the Indian Wells Open.
They are among players facing travel ŌĆīdifficulties , and ŌüĀthe ATP is monitoring the situation.
Mohammed Ben Sulayem, who heads the global motorsport governing body FIA, said safety will be their main concern when taking a decision on next month's Formula One races in Bahrain and Saudi Arabia.
"Safety and wellbeing will guide our decisions as we assess the forthcoming events scheduled there for the FIA World ŌĆŗEndurance Championship and the FIA ŌĆŗFormula One world championship," he said ŌüĀon Monday .
Two-time Olympic medallist PV Sindhu was stranded for days at Dubai airport on her way to the All England Open, and returned to India ŌĆŗon Tuesday as the Badminton World Federation said the former world champion had withdrawn from the ŌĆŗtournament .
"Back home in ŌüĀBangalore and safe. The last few days have been intense and uncertain, but I'm truly grateful to be back to my house," Sindhu wrote on X.
The West Indies and Zimbabwe teams said on Monday they have delayed ŌüĀtheir return ŌĆŗfrom India after their exit from the Twenty20 World Cup.
Zimbabwe were scheduled to ŌĆŗreturn home via Dubai but remain safe and well in India, the country's cricket body said. Cricket West Indies said it was working with the sport's ŌĆŗglobal body (ICC) to make safe travel arrangements for its players and staff.

item.title: UN warns 200,000 more Afghan children face acute malnutrition in 2026
item.description:
GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - Hundreds of thousands more children face acute malnutrition ŌĆīin Afghanistan this year amid a hunger crisis exacerbated by foreign aid cuts and violence on the border with Pakistan, a U.N. official said on Tuesday.
International aid to Afghanistan has fallen sharply ŌĆŗsince 2021, when U.S.-led forces and . ŌĆŗThe crisis has been compounded by natural calamities including
"Acute malnutrition ŌüĀamong children is soaring. Last year we saw the highest surge ever ŌĆŗrecorded in Afghanistan, and this year, a staggering 3.7 million children will need ŌĆŗmalnutrition treatment," the World Food Programme's Country Director John Aylieff told a Geneva press briefing.
Some 200,000 additional children face acute malnutrition this year, he added.
Funding cuts mean the U.N. agency ŌĆŗonly has the resources to treat one in every four children needing ŌĆŗtreatment for acute malnutrition, Aylieff said.
Others do not even have the means to reach clinics, ŌĆīhe ŌüĀsaid, voicing concerns that some are trapped by snowfall in remote highland areas.
Most children who die in Afghanistan do so "during the winter... at home silently", he said.
"What I fear is when the snow is melted at the end ŌĆŗof March or ŌĆŗin April, we ŌüĀwill find there has been a very high toll of child deaths in the villages."
Expulsion policies in neighbouring Pakistan ŌĆŗand Iran have resulted in over 5 million returnees since late ŌĆŗ2023, ŌüĀfurther straining limited resources, Aylieff said.
Many of those returning to Afghanistan are close to areas where Pakistani and Afghan troops have clashed in recent days, forcing WFP to ŌüĀsuspend ŌĆŗsome services there.
"We foresee that acute malnutrition will ŌĆŗbe driven up further by the conflict as people are prevented from accessing health services," imperilling ŌĆŗtens of thousands of children, said Aylieff.

item.title: Israeli military says Iranian missiles hit central Israel
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 3 (Reuters) - Israel's ŌĆŗmilitary on Tuesday ŌĆīreported strikes in the Tel Aviv area ŌĆŗof Israel from ŌĆŗIranian missiles.
"Search and rescue ŌüĀforces, together with ŌĆŗnumerous emergency teams, ŌĆŗare currently operating at the impact sites in central ŌĆŗIsrael," the ŌĆŗmilitary said. "The circumstances of the ŌĆīimpact ŌüĀare under review."
Israel's ambulance service said it was treating three ŌĆŗpeople ŌĆŗat ŌüĀthe sites who were lightly ŌĆŗinjured.
Israeli police said ŌĆŗthere ŌüĀwere several impact areas involving munition ŌüĀfragments ŌĆŗwithin the ŌĆŗTel Aviv district.

item.title: UK's Reeves promises stability against backdrop of Middle East conflict
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - British finance minister Rachel Reeves ŌĆŗsaid she would provide stability for the economy in a budget update speech on Tuesday that contained no big policy surprises and ŌĆīwas overshadowed by alarm among investors over the conflict in the Middle East .
Reeves said fresh forecasts showed inflation and borrowing would be lower than previously thought by Britain's fiscal watchdog, although its economic growth projection for this year was cut to 1.1% from a previous estimate of 1.4%.
Those forecasts may soon be overtaken by the impact of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran which ŌĆŗhas sent British government borrowing costs soaring on worries about the global surge in energy prices.
"This government has the right economic plan for ŌĆŗour country, a plan that is even more important in a world that in the last few days has become ŌüĀyet more uncertain," Reeves said in her speech to parliament.
"It is incumbent on me and on this government to chart a course through that uncertainty, to ŌĆŗsecure our economy against shocks and protect families from the turbulence that we see beyond our borders."
Reeves stressed the need for predictability ŌĆŗin government policy as well as investment in infrastructure, accusing the previous Conservative administration of allowing inflation to soar and interest rates to rise to a 15-year high.
"Stability is the single most important precondition for economic growth," she said.
Reeves is hoping that a period of policymaking stability - after the political turmoil triggered by the Brexit vote 10 years ago - will ŌĆŗencourage businesses to invest.
Many employers say higher taxes and other costs imposed on them by Reeves are deterring them from hiring.
In her speech on Tuesday, Reeves ŌĆŗsaid she would set out proposals for closer post-Brexit trade ties with the European Union in the coming weeks and the government would set out reforms for reducing youth ŌĆīunemployment, which ŌüĀhas risen sharply.
However, her economic programme could be tested in the coming months if the financial fallout from the conflict in the Middle East is sustained.
Britain still has the highest inflation among the Group of Seven economies, which has prevented the Bank of England from cutting interest rates as quickly as other central banks.
Higher inflation also saddles the government with a bigger bill for its inflation-linked bonds.
Government bond yields surged for a second day in a row earlier on Tuesday ŌĆŗas investors worried that a doubling of ŌĆŗgas prices, if sustained, might prevent ŌüĀthe BoE from cutting borrowing costs this year.
Benchmark wholesale gas prices, which make up the largest single portion of Britain's domestic energy price cap, have doubled this week, which if sustained could push up the next pricing level for ŌĆŗthe July-to-September period.
Oil prices have risen 15%, leading to calls from some motoring groups for the government to reverse ŌĆŗan end to the ŌüĀfuel duty freeze currently expected in September.
Pressure has grown on Keir Starmer after his Labour Party suffered defeat in an election for a parliamentary seat last week. There are questions about how long Starmer will remain in Downing Street, with local elections in May seen as the next big test of his political future.
But Reeves stuck to ŌüĀher promise ŌĆŗnot to make major fiscal policy changes outside full budget statements, which take place in ŌĆŗthe autumn.
The Office for Budget Responsibility's forecasts showed Reeves had 23.6 billion pounds ($31.4 billion) of so-called headroom for meeting her main fiscal target.
At the time of her last full budget speech in November, ŌĆŗthat room for manoeuvre was judged to stand at almost 22 billion pounds.
($1 = 0.7510 pounds)

item.title: Conservative Anglicans meet in Nigeria to pick rival leader
item.description:
LAGOS, March 3 (Reuters) - A grouping of conservative Anglican churches will meet in Nigeria this week to elect a ŌĆŗleader to rival the Archbishop of Canterbury, an unprecedented ŌĆīstep that is likely to deepen a widening theological split within the Anglican Communion.
The Global Anglican Future Conference (GAFCON) ŌĆō which brings together conservative churches ŌĆŗmainly in Africa and Asia ŌĆō opposes liberal shifts in ŌĆŗparts of the Communion, including the ordination of women ŌüĀand greater inclusion of LGBTQ+ members.
The group protested the ŌĆŗChurch of EnglandŌĆÖs appointment last October of its first woman Archbishop of ŌĆŗCanterbury, Sarah Mullally.
In a statement, GAFCON said its Global Primates Council would elect a chairman, to be announced on Thursday, "to serve as primus inter ŌĆŗpares (first among equals) to replace the role traditionally held ŌĆŗby the Archbishop of Canterbury".
GAFCON, formed in 2008, says it now represents a ŌĆīmajority ŌüĀof the world's practising Anglicans. The Communion is made up of 46 autonomous churches, with the Church of England historically regarded as the "mother church".
Francis Aduroja, a priest from southwest Nigeria, ŌĆŗtold Reuters he ŌĆŗexpected bishops ŌüĀmeeting in Abuja "not to compromise the scripture" as they consider the future of global Anglican leadership.
"We ŌĆŗwant them to defend the faith of ŌĆŗour fathers... ŌüĀto come back with words to encourage priests and parishioners that we are still upholding the gospel with no backing out ŌüĀand ŌĆŗno compromise," he said.
GAFCON says it ŌĆŗhas not left the Anglican Communion but is asserting itself as its authentic ŌĆŗorthodox core.

item.title: Philippines, South Korea leaders pledge closer cooperation as geopolitical uncertainty mounts
item.description:
MANILA, March 3 (Reuters) - Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos met South Korean President Lee Jae Myung ŌĆŗin Manila on Tuesday, where ŌĆīthey discussed ways to deepen economic and security ties.
Lee was in Manila on a state ŌĆŗvisit.

item.title: Bombardment unleashes terror in Tehran with no sign of protests
item.description:
DUBAI, March 3 (Reuters) - Terrified residents of Iran's capital described it as a ghost town on Tuesday, its streets largely emptied by a U.S.-Israeli missile barrage apart from security checkpoints and Revolutionary Guards patrols that rove the city.
The airstrikes have killed hundreds of Iranians since Saturday ŌĆŗwhile Israeli and U.S. leaders have voiced hopes they would trigger an uprising , but Reuters found no evidence one was imminent in phone conversations with people around the country.
"There are ŌĆīcheckpoints on every street and alley," said Fariba Gerami, 27, who works for a company in north Tehran where her husband runs a small coffee shop.
Electricity and water cuts since the bombardment began have further raised her fears, and at night she and her friends fear thieves will burgle their apartments, she said.
The family plans to leave Iran as soon as it is safe to do so, but they worry about security on the roads out, she added.
Her account ŌĆŗwas backed up by those of two Iranian men arriving in Turkey through a border gate on Tuesday who described scenes of tension and fear in the capital.
"The kids were like ŌĆŗscreaming and crying," said one Iranian man, who declined to give his name, adding that civilian structures being hit by the strikes instilled fear in the ŌüĀcity's residents.
The second man said the destruction was widespread. "We saw a lot of buildings destroyed, especially on the way leaving the country. There were a bunch of buildings, a bunch of cars and streets ŌĆŗwere destroyed. People are panicking to leave the country. They don't know what to do," he said.
For those unable to leave the capital, the anxiety is immense, with strikes ŌĆŗon Monday hitting close to a Tehran hospital that was damaged and had to be evacuated.
Adding to fears of further civilian casualties is the example of the girls' school in southern Iran that was bombed in the first hours of the war, with a death toll that authorities have put at 150. Reuters has not been able to verify that toll.
At the girls' funeral on Tuesday, their small coffins draped with Iranian flags were passed from a truck across a large ŌĆŗcrowd, borne across a sea of upraised hands towards the grave site, video on state television showed.
"World, do you see? They are killing us. Hear our voice," said Firuzeh Seraj, speaking through tears from ŌĆŗTehran.
"My 10-year-old daughter is on dialysis and now we are trapped. I'm afraid to take her to the hospital. What if they bomb it? Why are you bombing us?" she said.
Iran said its death toll from the attacks had ŌĆīreached 787, ŌüĀciting the Red Crescent.
It has responded to the U.S.-Israeli attack with a blitz of drone and missile attacks on countries around the region, striking at both military and civilian targets in Israel, Jordan and Gulf monarchies.
Anger at the catastrophe unfolding in Iran was directed at the country's own leaders too.
News of Khamenei's death on Saturday prompted spontaneous celebrations in parts of Tehran, though supporters of the Islamic Republic's authorities also held mourning processions.
However, there has been no return to the major nationwide protests that convulsed Iran in early January, and which were put down with a spree of state violence in which ŌĆŗthousands were killed.
A retired army officer in a ŌĆŗnorthern Iranian city who gave only his first ŌüĀname, Hassan, blamed the late Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei , whose nuclear policy set Iran at odds with the West and who was killed on Saturday.
"Khamenei is dead but the consequences of years of his stubbornness are still killing the Iranian people," he said.
"Why so much hostility with the world? What have we gained ŌĆŗfrom this nuclear programme except bombardment, isolation and misery? Why are we living under bombs?" he added.
In Urmia, a city near the borders of ŌĆŗTurkey and Iraq, a woman ŌüĀwho asked to be identified only as Shahla, said the previous night's bombardment had been the heaviest yet.
"I was terrified. There are no shelters. No help. They are bombing everywhere. The internet cuts in and out. We are stocking up on food," she said.
Like other Iranians Reuters reached, she said food and medicine were still available in the shops, but she was worried supplies would start to run low and people were buying up goods ŌüĀin case of ŌĆŗa prolonged conflict.
One elderly woman in the Gulf coast city of Bushehr, home to Iran's one nuclear power plant, said ŌĆŗshe feared she would never again see her children who lived overseas.
"My children call me but even the internet does not work properly. I am afraid, very afraid, that I may never see them again and that I could die in these bombings," ŌĆŗsaid the 80-year-old, who gave only her first name, Fatemeh.

item.title: Iran has not contacted US about possible peace talks, says Tehran's UN envoy
item.description:
GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - Iran's ambassador to the United Nations in Geneva on Tuesday ruled out ŌĆŗfor now any negotiations with the United States, three days after ŌĆīthe U.S. and Israel launched joint strikes on his country .
Explosions rocked Tehran again on Tuesday, and financial markets around the world tumbled amid fears of a prolonged disruption to global energy ŌĆŗsupplies. U.S. President Donald Trump and Israel's Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu have ŌĆŗgiven open-ended answers when asked how long the war might last.
Ali ŌüĀBahreini, ambassador of the Iranian mission to the U.N. in Geneva, told ŌĆŗreporters that Iran had not contacted the U.S. either directly or indirectly about ŌĆŗholding talks to de-escalate the conflict or about resuming negotiations over Tehran's nuclear programme.
Asked about the prospects for any talks, Bahreini said: "For the time being we are very doubtful about ŌĆŗthe usefulness of negotiation... The only language for talking with the United States ŌĆŗis the language of defence."
"I don't think it is a time for having any kind ŌĆīof ŌüĀnegotiation from our side," he added.
Iranian and U.S. negotiators held talks in Geneva last Thursday which Oman, their mediator, said had produced progress, but the U.S. and Israel began their airstrikes on Iran two days later, killing Supreme Leader ŌĆŗAli Khamenei and other ŌĆŗsenior Iranian officials ŌüĀand triggering a regional crisis .
Iran has retaliated by firing missiles and drones at neighbouring Gulf Arab states and at Israel ŌĆŗand by strangling shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, where ŌĆŗa fifth ŌüĀof the world's oil and huge volumes of gas skirt its coastline.
Trump has suggested the war could take four or five weeks, while Netanyahu has said it ŌüĀis "not ŌĆŗgoing to take years".
A source familiar with Israel's ŌĆŗwar plan told Reuters on Tuesday that the Israeli campaign had been planned to last two weeks and ŌĆŗwas moving faster than expected.

item.title: China's foreign minister tells Israeli counterpart that attacks on Iran must end
item.description:
BEIJING, March 3 (Reuters) - China opposes the military strikes launched by Israel and the U.S. against Iran ŌĆŗand demands an immediate cessation of hostilities, Foreign Minister Wang Yi ŌĆītold his Israeli counterpart Gideon Saar on Tuesday, according to his ministry.
"Force cannot truly solve problems; instead, it often creates new ŌĆŗones and leaves serious long-term consequences. The real ŌĆŗvalue of military power lies not on the battlefield ŌüĀbut in preventing war," it quoted Wang as telling ŌĆŗSaar in a call.
Explosions tore through Tehran and Beirut on Tuesday ŌĆŗand financial markets around the world tumbled at the prospect of a prolonged disruption to global energy supplies from the U.S.-Israeli air war ŌĆŗagainst Iran .
Wang told Saar that China believes in resolving ŌĆŗinternational and regional issues through dialogue and negotiation and will continue ŌĆīto ŌüĀplay a constructive role in easing the situation.
Tuesday's call follows three calls the top Chinese diplomat held on Monday with the foreign ministers of Iran, Oman and France, in ŌĆŗwhich he urged ŌĆŗGulf countries ŌüĀto unite to oppose external interference.
Wang also spoke with Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov on ŌĆŗSunday about the Iran crisis.
However, Wang has ŌĆŗnot spoken ŌüĀto U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio since the launch of the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran on ŌüĀSaturday, ŌĆŗwhen Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ŌĆŗwas among a number of senior Iranian officials killed.

item.title: UK rapper Ghetts jailed for killing Nepali student in hit-and-run
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - British rapper Ghetts was jailed for 12 years ŌĆŗon Tuesday for killing a ŌĆīNepali student in a hit-and-run while driving dangerously and over the drink-drive limit.
The award-winning ŌĆŗartist, whose real name is ŌĆŗJustin Clarke-Samuel, pleaded guilty , opens new tab in December to ŌüĀcausing the death of 20-year-old Yubin ŌĆŗTamang in north London last year.
Clarke-Samuel, 41, also ŌĆŗadmitted an additional charge of driving his BMW M5 dangerously before the fatal incident.
Ghetts ŌĆŗwas nominated for the 2024 Mercury ŌĆŗPrize for his fourth studio album "On Purpose, with ŌĆīPurpose" ŌüĀand won best male act at Britain's MOBO Awards in 2021. The rapper has also collaborated with ŌĆŗhigh-profile artists ŌĆŗincluding Ed ŌüĀSheeran.
Clarke-Samuel sat in the dock at London's Old Bailey ŌĆŗas prosecutor Philip McGhee said ŌĆŗhe ŌüĀhad been drinking before driving home dangerously, running red lights and travelling ŌüĀat ŌĆŗnearly 70 miles ŌĆŗper hour (112.7 kilometres per hour).

item.title: Governments weigh repatriations as conflict grounds Middle East flights
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - Commercial flights across parts of the Middle East have been grounded due to the escalating conflict after U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, stranding foreign nationals and prompting governments to consider repatriation.
Below is what governments and officials have said about repatriation plans, in alphabetical order by country:AUSTRALIA
Foreign Minister Penny Wong said Australia was in discussions with airlines to help Australians stranded in the Middle East, but that evacuations would be difficult while ŌĆŗmuch of the region's airspace remains closed.
She said about 115,000 Australians were in the region and the most viable option would be to get people home when commercial airlines resume services.
Wong ŌĆīdeclined to say whether the government was planning repatriation flights. "Airspace is not open. So whether or not it is an Australian flight or a commercial flight, the flights are not able to occur," she said.FRANCE
A French government official said there are about 400,000 French nationals across roughly a dozen countries affected by the situation, including residents and dual nationals as well as travellers.
France urged travellers to register on the Foreign Ministry's "Ariane" system; more than 25,000 were identified as travellers who had registered, the official said.
France said it has ŌĆŗdeployed consular teams at Israel's borders with Egypt and Jordan to facilitate land exits so people can fly onward, and has put in place a similar mechanism in the UAE at borders with ŌĆŗOman and Saudi Arabia, where airspace has remained open.
France said it is preparing charter flights and prioritising vulnerable people, with embassies and consulates drawing up lists.GERMANY
The German government ŌüĀsaid the tourism industry is responsible for bringing home most of roughly 30,000 Germans stranded in the region and that military repatriation is only a last resort.
Berlin is planning to charter two Lufthansa flights, one from Riyadh ŌĆŗand another from Muscat, to bring home particularly vulnerable citizens such as children, pregnant women and people with disabilities.
Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul said crisis teams had been sent to evaluate opportunities for border crossings, amid uncertainty over how ŌĆŗpassengers would reach the charter flights.
Tour operator TUI said it was working to return thousands of cruise passengers, with initial groups heading home on Gulf carriers including Emirates, Etihad and Qatar Airways.GREECE
Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis said Greece had set up a plan to repatriate thousands of its nationals stranded in the Middle East, but that their return was difficult as airspace over the region remained closed.ITALY
A first charter flight carrying 127 Italian citizens stranded in Oman, or moved there from Dubai, landed in Rome's Fiumicino airport late ŌĆŗon Monday, March 2, according to a Reuters video report.
Passengers arriving in Rome credited the Italian embassy with helping them return. One passenger said the flight cost about 1,500 euros ($1,741.20).PHILIPPINES
President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. urged Filipinos in Israel ŌĆŗand other affected Middle East countries to move to safety, saying the government would arrange repatriation flights once it is secure to do so.
More than 2.4 million Filipinos live and work in the Middle East, including 31,000 in Israel and 800 ŌĆīin Iran, and ŌüĀmore than 1,000 migrant workers have requested repatriation, Marcos said.SPAIN
Spain has begun evacuating its citizens from the Middle East, Foreign Minister Jose Manuel Albares said on Tuesday.
Over 175 Spaniards will arrive on Tuesday evening on a flight from Abu Dhabi and further flights are expected from the United Arab Emirates via Istanbul, Albares said.
Spain was also reinforcing its embassies in the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Oman and Bahrain to provide support and facilitate further repatriations, he added.SLOVENIA
Slovenia has organised four buses on Tuesday, escorted by police, to take Slovenian citizens and families with children from Dubai to Muscat airport in Oman, the office of Prime Minister Robert Golob said in a statement.
The first flight for Slovenia will ŌĆŗbe organised on Tuesday evening, with arrival expected on ŌĆŗWednesday morning, and two more flights are scheduled ŌüĀfor Wednesday late afternoon and evening, the statement said.
The Swiss foreign ministry said on Monday there were at least 4,400 Swiss citizens travelling in the region and that it would not be organising evacuations for them.
It said there were also around 35,000 Swiss citizens resident in the region, most of them in Israel, followed by the UAE, ŌĆŗand that a helpline set up by the ministry had received about 2,000 inquiries since Saturday.UNITED ARAB EMIRATES
The UAE civil aviation authority will begin operating "special flights" ŌĆŗacross the country's airports to help ŌüĀsome of the tens of thousands of passengers stranded in the region leave, state news agency WAM reported.UNITED KINGDOM
The UK government has begun the initial stages of its repatriation effort, with the first flights carrying stranded British nationals having landed in the UK on Monday evening.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on Monday the UK is "working on all options" to support citizens, adding that an estimated 300,000 British nationals are in the region and urged them to follow local authority guidance ŌüĀand Home Office ŌĆŗtravel advice.
Foreign secretary Yvette Cooper confirmed that 102,000 Britons have registered their presence with the Foreign Office.
The U.S. Department of State ŌĆŗon Monday called on Americans to immediately exit more than a dozen countries in the Middle East, but has not yet announced repatriation flights.
Mora Namdar, the State Department's assistant secretary for consular affairs, said U.S. citizens should leave using available commercial transportation "due to safety risks."
Secretary of State ŌĆŗMarco Rubio urged U.S. citizens to register with the Smart Traveler Enrollment Program and monitor security updates from the State Department.
($1 = 0.8615 euros)

item.title: US urges Americans to leave Middle East amid air travel disruptions
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - The United States on Tuesday said it was pulling out non-essential personnel from several U.S. missions across the Middle East and advised American citizens to immediately leave the region even as air travel remains severely ŌĆŗdisrupted due to U.S.-Israeli military operation on Iran.
The State Department announced that it had ordered the departure ŌĆīof non-emergency U.S. government personnel and their family members from U.S. embassies in Bahrain, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, Jordan. Similar measures have already been taken for U.S. missions in Lebanon and Israel.
It has also on Monday urged American citizens across 16 countries in the Middle ŌĆŗEast to leave now using "available commercial transportation". The U.S. embassy in Jerusalem said it was unable to offer ŌĆŗassistance to Americans trying to leave.
"You told Americans to depart now via commercial means when you ŌüĀknow many airports/airspace are closed," Congressman Ted Lieu wrote in an X post and said Washington must immediately schedule ŌĆŗU.S. government evacuation flights for the stranded Americans.
"Maybe you should have thought of a frickin' plan first," Lieu wrote.
U.S.-Israeli air war ŌĆŗagainst Iran, which started on Saturday, has already sent shockwaves around the world , disrupting energy supplies and sending global air transport into chaos. Overnight, Iranian drones struck the U.S. embassy in Saudi Arabia.
As Washington presses ahead with one of the most consequential military actions in recent history, the ŌĆŗUnited States lacks Senate-confirmed ambassadors across many countries in the region including Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Iraq, Egypt, Kuwait, Algeria and ŌĆŗthe UAE.
Major Gulf aviation hubs, including the world's busiest international airport, Dubai - which normally handles over 1,000 flights a day - remained closed for ŌĆīa ŌüĀfourth day on Tuesday, leaving tens of thousands of passengers stranded. Ticket prices have soared.
"The U.S. Embassy is not in a position at this timeŌĆ» to evacuate or directly assist Americans in ŌĆ»departingŌĆ» Israel," the embassy said in an X post and said citizens can sign up to the shuttles being operated by Israeli Ministry of Tourism to the Taba border crossing with Egypt.
It ŌĆŗreiterated that Washington cannot ensure ŌĆŗthe safety of this route.
"The ŌüĀU.S. Embassy cannot make any recommendation (for or against) the Ministry of TourismŌĆÖs shuttle. If you choose to avail yourself of this option to depart, the U.S. government cannot guarantee your ŌĆŗsafety," it said.
The State Department did not immediately respond to questions on how exactly Americans ŌĆŗshould be departing ŌüĀin the absence of available commercial flights.
On Monday, a U.S. official said the department activated an inter-agency task force to manage the situation and had launched a dedicated WhatsApp channel, which it said has amassed 15,000 followers. It did not mention any ŌüĀgovernment assistance ŌĆŗfor evacuation of citizens.
Crude oil benchmarks rose about 7% on Tuesday, soaring ŌĆŗfor a third session as the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran widened. In a social media post overnight, Trump said there was a "virtually unlimited supply" of ŌĆŗU.S. munitions and that "wars can be fought "forever," and very successfully, using just these supplies."

item.title: Father of accused Atlanta-area school shooter convicted of second-degree murder trial
item.description: ATLANTA, March 3 (Reuters) - Colin Gray, the ŌĆŗfather of ŌĆīa boy charged with killing ŌĆŗfour people ŌĆŗat a school ŌüĀnear Atlanta ŌĆŗin 2024, was ŌĆŗfound guilty of second-degree murder on ŌĆŗTuesday in ŌĆŗa rare U.S. legal ŌĆīcase ŌüĀin which a parent was prosecuted ŌĆŗfollowing ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀactions of a child ŌĆŗin a ŌĆŗmass ŌüĀshooting.

item.title: Britain sends helicopters with counter-drone tech to Cyprus, deploys naval vessel
item.description: LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - British ŌĆŗPrime Minister ŌĆīKeir Starmer ŌĆŗsaid ŌĆŗon Tuesday that ŌüĀBritain ŌĆŗwas sending ŌĆŗhelicopters with counter-drone capabilities ŌĆŗto ŌĆŗCyprus, and was ŌĆīdeploying ŌüĀthe air defence ŌĆŗdestroyer ŌĆŗnaval ŌüĀvessel HMS ŌĆŗDragon ŌĆŗto ŌüĀthe region.

item.title: Bomb damage at Iran's Evin prison raises fears for detained Britons, family says
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - A British couple jailed in Iran have described explosions shaking Evin prison where they are being held and damage to their ŌĆŗwing as the conflict intensifies around Tehran, their son told Reuters after speaking to ŌĆīthem on Tuesday.
Joe Bennett, who is in regular contact with his mother, Lindsay Foreman, has reported worsening conditions inside the facility as bombing continues in the capital for a fourth day.
Lindsay and Craig Foreman ŌĆŗwere sentenced to 10 years in Iran on spying charges after Iran accused them ŌĆŗof gathering information in several parts of the country. The couple, arrested in ŌüĀJanuary 2025 while travelling through Iran on a global motorcycle journey, have denied the ŌĆŗcharges.
"They're hearing the jets going over. They're hearing the bombs hitting surrounding areas outside of ŌĆŗEvin," Bennett said. "One of the bombs was so close to the prison that it's actually... punctured the windows and the ceiling."
He said his mother and her husband, Craig Foreman, were scared and described the ŌĆŗatmosphere in the prison as increasingly tense, with more detainees being brought in after protests ŌĆŗin the city.
"You're worried for their safety - it's a genuine threat to their lives because the country ŌĆīis ŌüĀat war," Bennett said, adding that contact was limited to short calls from a communal landline that prisoners wait in line to use.
His warning comes as advocates estimate at least six U.S. citizens or permanent residents are being detained in Iran while possibly thousands of dual ŌĆŗU.S.-Iranian nationals remain in ŌĆŗthe country, raising ŌüĀfears they could be used as bargaining chips in the conflict.
The Foremans' family also fear for the ŌĆŗcouple's access to basic supplies inside Evin, with the prison shop ŌĆŗstill open but ŌüĀuncertainty over how long food and water will last as the conflict disrupts staffing and deliveries.
Bennett said the British government had been good at keeping in touch with the family but ŌüĀthat ŌĆŗthere had been no consular access for three months ŌĆŗand support had been "minimal", with no "plan, strategy" to secure the couple's release.
"The only support that matters to us is ŌĆŗtheir plan to get my parents home," he said.

item.title: Hillary Clinton tells congressional panel she has no information on Epstein
item.description:
Feb 26 (Reuters) - Former U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton told a congressional committee on Thursday that she did not remember ever meeting the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and had no information to share about his criminal activities.
"I do not recall ever encountering Mr. Epstein. I never flew on his plane or visited his island, homes or offices," Clinton said in a statement, which she released as she delivered a closed-door deposition to the House of Representatives Oversight Committee in Chappaqua, New York.
Following a seven-hour appearance before the panel, Clinton told reporters she repeatedly was asked the same questions throughout the day, but that she also offered some suggestions on conducting the investigation. She did not detail those ideas.
Clinton said that late into the procedures "it got quite unusual because I started being asked about UFOs and a series of questions about Pizzagate, one of the most vile bogus conspiracy theories."
She was referring to false, widely disseminated musings in 2016 that a Washington, D.C. pizzeria was a front for a child sex ring run by her and that New York City police had discovered a pedophilia ring linked to Democrats.
In her prepared testimony, Clinton, the 2016 Democratic presidential nominee, also accused the Republican-led panel of trying to shift focus away from Trump's ties to Epstein, who died by suicide in jail in 2019 while awaiting trial on federal sex-trafficking charges. She noted Trump's administration has "gutted" a State Department office focused on international sex trafficking.
Speaking to reporters after Clinton's testimony, House Oversight Committee Chairman James Comer dismissed the idea of having Trump appear before the panel.
"President Trump has answered hundreds if not thousands of questions from you all about Epstein and I think he's been very transparent in releasing the documents," said Comer, a Kentucky Republican.
Clinton and her husband, Democratic former President Bill Clinton, initially refused to testify before the committee, but relented when lawmakers moved to hold them in contempt of Congress.
Bill Clinton is scheduled to testify to the committee on Friday.
Hillary Clinton told reporters that the former president will tell the committee that the "vast majority" of people who had contact with Epstein before his criminal pleas in 2008 "did not know" about the sex trafficking. "That is exactly what my husband will testify to tomorrow," she said.
Before the hearing, Comer denied that the probe was a partisan effort, noting that several Democrats had pushed for the Clintons to testify.
"No one is accusing at this moment the Clintons of any wrongdoing but we do have a lot of questions," Comer said.
He said the committee would seek to find out about any interactions she might have had with Epstein, his involvement with the Clintons' charitable work, and any relationship she may have had with jailed Epstein associate Ghislaine Maxwell. He said transcripts and video of the Clintons' interviews will be made public.
Representative Robert Garcia of California, the top Democrat on the committee, told reporters that Trump and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick should also testify. Lutnick has admitted to visiting Epstein's private island years after he says he broke off ties. Comer said it was "possible" the committee might subpoena him.
Garcia and other Democrats are accusing Trump's Justice Department of selectively withholding material from 3 million Epstein-related documents it released to shield Trump from scrutiny. That includes records of a woman who accused Trump of sexually abusing her when she was a minor, Garcia said.
"Where are these files? Who removed them? These questions have to be answered," he said.
The Justice Department said it is reviewing whether any documents were improperly withheld and would publish them if appropriate. It has previously cautioned that the material it has released includes unfounded accusations and sensationalist claims about Trump.
Law enforcement authorities have not accused Trump of any criminal wrongdoing in connection with Epstein. Trump socialized extensively with Epstein in the 1990s and 2000s, before his 2008 conviction for soliciting prostitution from a minor. Comer said evidence gathered by the panel does not implicate Trump.
Bill Clinton flew on Epstein's plane several times in the early 2000s after he left office. He has denied wrongdoing and expressed regret for his association.
According to Comer, Epstein visited the White House 17 times while Clinton was in office.
The Justice Department sought to draw attention to photos of Bill Clinton in its document dump, but they also have revealed Epstein's ties to a long list of business and political leaders, including Lutnick and Tesla CEO Elon Musk.
Overseas, they have prompted criminal investigations of Britain's Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor , the former Duke of York, and other prominent figures.

item.title: Sudan accuses Ethiopia for the first time of involvement in its civil war
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - Sudan has accused Ethiopia of allowing drones to be launched from its territory into Sudan to carry out attacks in February and March, the first time it has directly ŌĆŗaccused its powerful neighbour of involvement in the three-year civil war .
The Sudanese foreign ministry ŌĆīstatement, released late on Monday, is the latest sign that one of the world's deadliest conflicts between the army and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) is drawing in regional powers from Africa and beyond.
A spokesperson for ŌĆŗthe Ethiopian prime minister's office did not immediately respond to a request for comment. ŌĆŗReuters has not independently verified the launching of attack drones from Ethiopia.
The ŌüĀSudanese army has been fighting the paramilitary RSF in a conflict that has threatened to ŌĆŗfracture the country and has driven millions of people to flee their homes.
The statement did not ŌĆŗspecify where the alleged attacks had taken place, but the southeastern Blue Nile state, which borders Ethiopia, has seen small skirmishes and drone attacks in recent weeks, according to eyewitnesses.
Parts of Blue Nile are controlled ŌĆŗby the SPLM-N rebel group which aligned with the RSF last year.
The alleged drone attacks ŌĆŗrepresent "a blatant violation of Sudanese sovereignty and an outright act of aggression against the Sudanese state," the foreign ŌĆīministry ŌüĀsaid in its statement.
"The Sudanese government warns the Ethiopian authorities against the consequences of these hostile acts and affirms its right to defend its sovereignty and territorial integrity, including the right to respond to such attacks by all available means," it added.
Last month, Reuters exclusively reported , opens new tab that Ethiopia ŌĆŗwas hosting a secret ŌĆŗcamp to train thousands ŌüĀof fighters for the RSF.
Ethiopia's government spokesperson, its army and the RSF did not respond to detailed requests for comment about the findings ŌĆŗof that story. The Sudanese Armed Forces also did not respond ŌĆŗto a ŌüĀrequest for comment on the story.
The RSF controls the Darfur region in Sudan's west, while the army maintains control of the country's eastern half.
The two sides are also fighting over the Kordofan region ŌüĀwhich ŌĆŗlies between their firm zones of control.
Drones have become a ŌĆŗdominant feature of the war, allowing the RSF to match the more established army's dominance in the air and ŌĆŗcontributing to mass civilian casualties.

item.title: Trump says U.S. has knocked out┬Āmany Iranian naval and air targets
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said ŌĆŗon Tuesday that the U.S. military ŌĆīhad been successful against many Iranian naval and air targets, saying "just about everything ŌĆŗhas been knocked out."
He made ŌĆŗhis comments in the Oval Office ŌüĀahead of a meeting with ŌĆŗGerman Chancellor Friedrich Merz.
Trump, answering questions from ŌĆŗreporters, said he ordered the attack against Iran because "I had a feeling" that Iran ŌĆŗwould attack the United States as ŌĆŗnegotiations over its nuclear program stalled.
He presented no ŌĆīevidence ŌüĀto support the view. Iran had been anticipating another round of talks with U.S. envoys after negotiations were ŌĆŗheld ŌĆŗin Geneva ŌüĀlast Thursday.
Trump said Iran was still lobbing missiles but ŌĆŗpredicted Tehran will eventually lose this ŌĆŗcapability ŌüĀdue to a sustained assault against them.
"They've shot a lot of them, ŌüĀand ŌĆŗwe're knocking out a ŌĆŗlot," he said.

item.title: No easy way out for tens of thousands looking to leave Gulf amid Iran conflict
item.description:
DUBAI, March 3 (Reuters) - As Middle East tensions rise ever higher, tens of thousands of people are finding themselves stranded across the Gulf with few options for leaving a region suddenly ŌĆŗengulfed by war .
Airspace remains closed in Qatar, where 8,000 travellers who had been in transit became marooned following the start of U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran . Only a limited number of ŌĆīflights were allowed to depart the United Arab Emirates starting from Monday, leaving tourists and some expatriates scrambling for a way out, as Iran launched strikes across the Gulf.
Dubai and neighbouring Doha sit at the crossroads of east-west air travel, funnelling long-haul traffic between Europe and Asia and handling tens of thousands of passengers every day
THIRTY-THREE HOURS TO DUSSELDORF
Dubai resident Sara, who asked her last name not be used, plans a roughly 33-hour journey to Germany on Wednesday to ensure she ŌĆŗdoes not miss being best-woman at her brotherŌĆÖs wedding next week.
ŌĆ£I was supposed to have a flight next week, but I just really cannot risk it being delayed whatsoever,ŌĆØ she said.
Sara ŌĆŗsaid a driver she found online ŌĆō the one that seemed the most reliable ŌĆō will pick her up at 5 a.m. on Wednesday and drive her ŌüĀacross the border to Muscat, Oman. From there she plans to fly to Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, and spend the night. She said then she flies to Amsterdam and then on to Dusseldorf.
ŌĆ£So itŌĆÖs a ŌĆŗvery long journey, but for me, it was just important,ŌĆØ she said.
U.S. President Donald Trump initially projected the conflict would last four to five weeks, but has since sought to justify a broad, . The ŌĆŗconflict has seen Iran launch missile and drone strikes against not only Israel and U.S. forces but also a host of countries across the region allied to the U.S.
U.S. embassies in Kuwait and Saudi Arabia came under fire and the U.S. State Department called on Americans to immediately leave more than a dozen countries in the region.
Other governments have started evacuations , including the United Kingdom, Spain, Italy and Germany, which chartered two flights - one in Riyadh and another in Oman's Muscat - ŌĆŗto bring home particularly vulnerable citizens initially out of the roughly 30,000 currently stranded in the region.
Slovenia said it had organised four buses escorted by police to take its citizens from Dubai to Muscat ŌĆŗairport, but it remained unclear how travellers from other countries would reach gateways to leave the region.
In the UAE, normally a bustling tourism destination and a business hub, many desperate to travel like Sara have resorted to shuttle ŌĆībuses and ŌüĀprivate car rides to cross into Oman.
Oman, which has so far suffered fewer attacks compared to its neighbours, has kept its airspace open, and while flights to other Gulf destinations have been cancelled in recent days, services to other regions remain largely unaffected.
State carrier Oman Air and budget airline SalamAir have arranged shuttle buses from a station in Sharjah, north of Dubai, to Oman's capital Muscat, which will run from Tuesday to Thursday. The journey takes about eight hours.
Facebook groups and Reddit threads online showed tens of queries from expatriates in the UAE looking for ways to reach Muscat's airport.
A tour operator ŌĆŗagent told Reuters he had received around 30 ŌĆŗcalls since Monday from people seeking a private ŌüĀride to the border in Hatta, a UAE mountain town an hour outside Dubai.
Until last week, the 150 km (93 mile) distance was generally covered by Omanis travelling to spend a few days in glitzy Dubai or by hikers and nature enthusiasts heading in the opposite direction to enjoy Oman's pristine mountain landscapes.
However, ŌĆŗthe agent said that he was now driving Ukrainian citizens looking to leave the UAE.
He noted there would be Omani taxis and private ride ŌĆŗproviders waiting on the other ŌüĀside of the border, as cars with a UAE plate cannot be driven in Oman unless they are fully owned by the driver.
Saudi Arabia is another route for people stranded in neighbouring countries seeking to fly out of the Middle East.
A British citizen living in the kingdom told Reuters he was in Dubai for the weekend when the strikes began and managed to return to Riyadh after an 11-hour journey late on Monday, ŌüĀwhich he described ŌĆŗas smooth.
"I got driven to the Saudi border (Al Ghuwaifat) from my hotel in Dubai...I had a driver lined up on ŌĆŗthe other side to take me back home in Riyadh," the British expatriate said, adding WhatsApp groups to coordinate ride shares had been set up to arrange trips via Saudi Arabia or Oman.
The Dubai-Riyadh trip cost over $1,000 in total, a steep increase from ŌĆŗthe average ticket of an economy class one-way flight between the two Gulf hubs of around $200.

item.title: Epstein estate's $35 million settlement with accusers wins preliminary judge approval
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 3 (Reuters) - A U.S. judge granted preliminary approval on Tuesday to an agreement for Jeffrey Epstein 's estate ŌĆŗto pay as much as $35 million to resolve a class ŌĆīaction lawsuit that accused two of the disgraced financier's advisers of aiding and abetting his sex trafficking of young women and teenage girls.
Boies Schiller Flexner, a law firm representing ŌĆŗEpstein victims, announced the settlement on February 19. On Tuesday, Manhattan-based U.S. ŌĆŗDistrict Judge Arun Subramanian said the agreement appeared fair. The ŌüĀjudge scheduled a hearing for September 16 to consider granting final approval.
The ŌĆŗdeal would bring an end to a 2024 lawsuit filed against Epstein's ŌĆŗformer personal lawyer Darren Indyke and former accountant Richard Kahn, who are co-executors of Epstein's estate.
Epstein's estate previously set up a restitution fund that paid out $121 million to victims. ŌĆŗThe estate also paid $49 million in additional settlements to victims.
Lawyers for Indyke ŌĆŗand Kahn did not immediately respond to requests for comment. One of their lawyers had ŌĆīpreviously ŌüĀsaid neither Indyke nor Kahn "made any admission or concession of misconduct" as part of the settlement.
Epstein died in a New York jail in August 2019. His death was ruled a suicide.
Millions of documents released this year by the ŌĆŗJustice Department from its ŌĆŗinvestigation into Epstein ŌüĀhave shed light on his social ties to wealthy and powerful people around the world .
In the 2024 lawsuit, lawyers ŌĆŗat Boies Schiller Flexner said Indyke and Kahn helped Epstein ŌĆŗcreate a ŌüĀcomplex web of corporations and bank accounts that let him hide his abuses and pay victims and recruiters, while leaving them "richly compensated" for their work.
The ŌüĀBoies law ŌĆŗfirm previously helped obtain $365 million of settlements ŌĆŗwith JPMorgan Chase and Deutsche Bank after accusing them of missing red flags about Epstein, once ŌĆŗa lucrative client.

item.title: Iranian missiles and drones fired at Gulf countries
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - Here are statistics released by Gulf defence ŌĆŗministries on drones and ŌĆīmissiles that Iran has fired at their territories ŌĆŗsince the start ŌĆŗof the U.S.-Israeli air war ŌüĀagainst Iran:
