Hong Kong-listed Tencent holds a stake in U.S. video game developer ŌĆŗEpic Games, the creator of "Fortnite", and ŌĆŗowns ŌüĀLos Angeles-based Riot Games, the developer of "League of Legends."
In 2016, it bought a majority ŌüĀstake ŌĆŗin Supercell, the Finnish mobile-game ŌĆŗmaker behind "Clash of Clans", for about $8.6 billion.

item.title: U.S., Kurds discuss potential Iran military operation, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the United States in recent days about whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in the ŌĆŗwestern part of the country, according to three sources with knowledge of the matter.
The Iranian Kurdish coalition of groups based on the ŌĆīIran-Iraq border in the semiautonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan has been training to mount such an attack in hopes of weakening the country's military, as the United States and Israel pound Iranian targets with bombs and missiles.
The goal would be to create room for Iranians opposed to the Islamic regime to rise up now that Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and other top officials ŌĆŗhave been killed since the U.S.-Israeli attack began on Saturday, two of the sources said.
A final decision has not yet been made on the ŌĆŗoperation and its possible timing, added the sources, who spoke on condition of anonymity to talk freely about sensitive military ŌüĀplanning.
The groups have requested U.S. military support and Iraqi leaders in Erbil and Baghdad have also been in touch with the Trump administration in recent days, ŌĆŗthey said.
The forces are in talks with the United States about CIA help to provide weapons, two of the sources said.
CNN was the first to report on ŌĆŗthe CIA's involvement with the groups and the potential ground operation. Axios said this week that President Trump held a telephone call with two of Iraqi KurdistanŌĆÖs top leaders.
Reuters could not independently confirm the extent of the CIAŌĆÖs involvement in the planning of the operation, if it had facilitated the weapons or if any U.S. forces planned to go into Iran ŌĆŗwith the Kurdish groups.
The CIA declined to comment. The White House and the Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Kurdish Regional Government ŌĆŗdid not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Any operation from Iraq would probably require significant U.S. military and intelligence support.
The Pentagon says the two ŌĆīU.S. bases ŌüĀin Erbil have supported the international coalition fighting Islamic State militants.
Kurdish groups in Iraqi Kurdistan have a long history of working with the U.S. but their shifting allegiances and ideologies have at times strained ties with Washington.
The United States worked with some of the Kurdish groups in Iraq both during the Iraq War and the battle against ISIS.
But it is unclear how successful the Iranian Kurdish groups would be in their fight inside Iran. The groupsŌĆÖ fighters have varying degrees of ŌĆŗexperience on the battlefield.
A source cited by ŌĆŗCNN said the plan would be ŌüĀfor Kurdish armed forces to take on Iranian security forces to make it easier for an uprising of unarmed Iranians in the countryŌĆÖs cities.
It is not clear exactly how such an operation in Iran by the Kurds will be received ŌĆŗby other countries in the region.
An armed uprising by the Iranian Kurds could have serious consequences for Iran's stability.
It ŌĆŗcould add fuel to ŌüĀan armed separatist movement among the country's ethnic Baluch minority that keeps close ties with separatists in Pakistan's neighboring restive province of Baluchistan.
It is unlikely that Islamabad would tolerate any move toward Baluch independence.
Turkey, a strong supporter of Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa, has seen the integration deal between Damascus and Kurdish forces as critical to restoring state ŌüĀauthority across ŌĆŗSyria.
It had threatened its own military operation against the Syrian Kurdish Democratic Forces (SDF) in the north, ŌĆŗif the group did not agree to come under central government control.
Ankara has been working to advance its long-running efforts towards securing peace with the outlawed Kurdistan Workers Party (PKK) and is unlikely to ŌĆŗbe sympathetic towards the arming of Kurdish groups close to its borders.

item.title: At least four killed in Israeli strike on building in Lebanon's Baalbek
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - An Israeli strike on a four-storey residential building has killed at ŌĆŗleast four people and wounded six others in the ŌĆīeastern Lebanese city of Baalbek with rescue teams working to pull families from beneath the rubble, state news agency NNA said on Wednesday.
The strike was ŌĆŗpart of a sharp escalation in fighting along the Lebanese-Israeli border ŌĆŗsince Monday after Hezbollah fired drones and missiles at ŌüĀIsrael. Since then, the Iran-aligned group has launched further rockets and ŌĆŗIsrael has carried out waves of air strikes across Lebanon and ŌĆŗsent troops into the south.
Shi'ite Muslim Hezbollah, established by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, opened fire on Israel to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme ŌĆŗleader in joint strikes by Israel and the United States.
Hezbollah said ŌĆŗon Wednesday its fighters fired a salvo of rockets at a gathering of ŌĆīIsraeli ŌüĀforces near the Metula area along the frontier in response to Israeli strikes on dozens of Lebanese towns and cities, including Beirut's southern suburbs.
The Israeli military on Wednesday warned residents of 16 villages ŌĆŗin Lebanon to ŌĆŗevacuate, saying ŌüĀHezbollah activity was forcing it to act against the group and cautioning that anyone near Hezbollah fighters, ŌĆŗfacilities or weapons would be putting their lives ŌĆŗat risk.
NNA ŌüĀalso reported several Israeli strikes early on Wednesday, including one on a hotel and another on an apartment in eastern Lebanon.
Lebanon's health ministry ŌüĀsaid ŌĆŗon Tuesday that Israeli strikes had ŌĆŗkilled at least 50 people and wounded 335 since the latest escalation began.

item.title: Taiwan indicts 62 over suspected scam centre operator Prince Group
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 4 (Reuters) - Taipei prosecutors on Wednesday indicted 62 people linked to the Prince Group, a multinational network accused of running a vast network of scam centres, ŌĆŗincluding the company's chairman who was arrested and deported to China from Cambodia earlier this year.
Chen ŌĆīZhi, who founded the conglomerate the U.S. alleges is a front for a multibillion-dollar online fraud and money laundering operation, was pictured by Chinese state media hooded and handcuffed as he was led off ŌĆŗa plane at a Beijing airport in January.
Taipei prosecutors said in a ŌĆŗstatement that Taiwan was one of the locations where Chen had ŌüĀfunnelled illicit funds via shell companies, buying luxury goods, sports cars and real ŌĆŗestate.
"This was done to conceal and disguise the source and flow of the criminal ŌĆŗproceeds," it said.
The funds remitted into Taiwan from overseas by members of the group for alleged money-laundering purposes totalled around T$10.8 billion ($339.12 million), where they bought 24 properties, 35 vehicles, and held T$55.53 ŌĆŗmillion in other assets such as cash, designer bags, and shoes, the statement ŌĆŗsaid.
More than T$5.5 billion in assets have been seized in Taiwan, the prosecutors' office added.
Chen's whereabouts ŌĆīare ŌüĀcurrently unknown and it was not possible to contact him for comment.
In November, the Prince Group denied any wrongdoing in a statement issued via a U.S. law firm.
On Monday, Taiwan put 33 luxury cars, including Ferraris, up for auction after they ŌĆŗwere seized as part ŌĆŗof the investigation.
"To ŌüĀconceal and disguise criminal proceeds, they exploited Taiwanese nationals to carry out money-laundering activities in Taiwan through online gambling and ŌĆŗunderground remittances," the prosecutors' office said.
"This not only seriously disrupted ŌĆŗTaiwan's financial ŌüĀorder and social stability, but also damaged Taiwan's international image."
Authorities across Asia, including Singapore and Hong Kong , have also seized assets or detained individuals linked to the Prince Group.
The transnational ŌüĀscam ŌĆŗindustry emerged in Southeast Asia during the pandemic. It ŌĆŗis believed to generate billions of dollars a year for organised crime using trafficked workers to defraud victims ŌĆŗfrom around the world.
($1 = 31.8470 Taiwan dollars)

item.title: Saudi Arabia to take all necessary measures to defend its security, cabinet says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia will take all ŌĆŗnecessary measures to ŌĆīdefend its security and protect ŌĆŗits territory, citizens ŌĆŗand residents, the ŌüĀstate news agency ŌĆŗreported early on ŌĆŗWednesday, citing a cabinet statement.
An Iranian attack ŌĆŗtargeted the ŌĆŗU.S. embassy in Riyadh on ŌĆīTuesday ŌüĀamid ongoing Iranian missile and drone strikes on ŌĆŗGulf ŌĆŗstates ŌüĀthat host U.S. bases, ŌĆŗfollowing U.S. ŌĆŗand ŌüĀIsraeli strikes on Iran on Saturday.

item.title: Hyundai Motor's robots herald hardware reboot
item.description:
HONG KONG, March 4 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Robots are hard at work for Hyundai Motor Co (005380.KS) , opens new tab . Although cars generate the profit, its Boston Dynamics unit is more responsible for the sudden rise ŌĆŗin market capitalisation. As investors increasingly value technological hardware as much as software, more companies across Asia will ŌĆībe rewarded.
The Hyundai conglomerate's automotive shares have roughly doubled this year, compared to a 40% rise in the domestic benchmark index. Vehicles, including its Tucson SUV, power the bottom line. Hyundai Motor Co is on track for $9 billion of net profit next year, according to Visible Alpha. A basket of international peers ŌĆŗtrades at about 7.5 times earnings on average, which would impute roughly $70 billion of equity value.
There's also a stake in ŌĆŗsister carmaker Kia (000270.KS) , opens new tab , worth $16 billion, and $17 billion of book value from investments in other related companies, as ŌüĀof September. Korea Investment analysts appraise the financing division at $15 billion. Add it all up, slap on a 40% conglomerate discount, ŌĆŗand the company is worth about $70 billion.
Hyundai Motor Co's market value is now closer to $100 billion, however. Its rally began in January after ŌĆŗBoston Dynamics showcased , opens new tab the Atlas humanoid cyborg at the annual consumer tech expo in Las Vegas and unveiled , opens new tab plans for mass production. Every scrap of related news since has stoked further excitement: the stock leapt 11% on Friday after Seoul said the Hyundai group would invest some $6 billion in an artificial intelligence data centre ŌĆŗand robot factory.
ItŌĆÖs more than just hype. Hyundai is well-positioned to sell autonomous machines like its four-legged Spot , opens new tab , wheeled Mobed , opens new tab and biped Atlas. The ŌĆŗgroup's decision to buy an 80% stake in Boston Dynamics from SoftBank in 2021 accelerated a multi-decade push into the technology; Hyundai Motor Co owns a ŌĆīroughly 28% ŌüĀindirect holding. The parentŌĆÖs broad portfolio also manufactures everything from ships to space exploration vehicles, enabling it to experiment and gather data for industrial applications. Essential components are also available internally.
ChinaŌĆÖs homegrown champions, such as Unitree, present formidable competition, but Hyundai is better positioned to tap the lucrative U.S. market, where higher labour costs make a compelling case for automating production. It's already introducing Atlas to production lines ŌĆŗin Georgia.
This dynamic will ripple ŌĆŗacross South Korea and Japan, ŌüĀwhere building a range of precision machines and components is a speciality largely overlooked while investors favoured coding experts selling subscriptions. Rainbow Robotics and gear-maker Nabtesco are among those picking up steam. It augurs ŌĆŗyet more promise for rebooted hardware.
Follow Katrina Hamlin on Bluesky , opens new tab and Linkedin , opens new tab .

item.title: Tanker heads to UAE port via Strait of Hormuz to load oil, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 4 (Reuters) - An oil tanker sailed through the Strait of Hormuz ŌĆŗon Tuesday, heading to a United Arab Emirates ŌĆīport to load crude in a rare voyage since the Iran war disrupted shipping in the Middle East, according to ŌĆŗindustry sources and shiptracking data.
Suezmax tanker Pola ŌĆŗswitched off its AIS tracker late on ŌüĀMarch 2, when it approached the Strait, and ŌĆŗre-appeared on March 3 off Abu Dhabi, LSEG data ŌĆŗshowed.
The vessel is heading to the port of Jebel Dhanna to load Abu Dhabi Murban crude for Thailand, said two ŌĆŗtrade sources, who spoke on condition of anonymity ŌĆŗas the matter is a sensitive one.
Dynacom Tankers which manages ŌĆīthe ŌüĀvessel could not be immediately reached for comment outside office hours.
The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran halted energy exports from the Middle East, with Tehran attacking ships ŌĆŗand energy facilities, ŌĆŗclosing navigation ŌüĀin the Gulf and forcing production stoppages in countries from Qatar to Iraq.
Crude ŌĆŗtanker transits through the strait fell ŌĆŗto ŌüĀfour vessels on March 1, the day after hostilities broke out, versus an average of 24 a day ŌüĀsince ŌĆŗJanuary, Vortexa vessel-tracking data showed.

item.title: US assault on Iran ahead of schedule, says US Middle East Commander
item.description:
DUBAI/TEL AVIV, March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S. assault on Iran was ahead of schedule, said the top American commander for the Middle East, as Israel ŌĆŗand the U.S. targeted sites deep inside the country , and Iran carried out strikes around the Gulf.
The five-day-old war continued to rattle global markets , as airline and tourism industries ŌĆīscrambled to deal with more than 20,000 flight cancellations and governments rushed to bring stranded travellers home from the Middle East.
The Israeli military said early on Wednesday it had begun a wave of strikes targeting Iranian launch sites, aerial defence systems and infrastructure.
In Israel, air raid sirens sounded early on Wednesday warning of Iranian missiles, and loud blasts as the missiles were intercepted shook buildings, said witnesses.
U.S. Admiral Brad Cooper, who leads U.S. ŌĆŗforces in the Middle East as the head of Central Command, said the first 24 hours of the 'Operation Epic Fury' bombardment of Iran were "nearly double the scale" of ŌĆŗthe first 24 hours of the 'Shock and Awe' campaign that opened the Iraq War in 2003.
"We are seeing that Iran's ability to hit ŌüĀus, and our partners, is declining, while our combat power, on the other hand, is building," Cooper said in a video briefing released on Tuesday evening. "My overall operational assessment is that ŌĆŗwe are ahead of our game plan."
Cooper said Iran's air defences had been badly degraded, its navy had no operational vessels on key waterways after 17 were sunk, and that more ŌĆŗthan 2,000 Iranian targets had been hit.
Some 50,000 U.S. troops were taking part in operations, and that "more capabilities" were on the way, he said.
The U.S. military on Tuesday identified four of the first American soldiers killed in the war, as the Trump administration warned the intensifying conflict would lead to more U.S. casualties. Trump has not ruled out using ground forces.
A source familiar with Israel's war plan told Reuters that the campaign had been planned ŌĆŗto last two weeks and was going through its target list faster than expected, with early success in killing Iran's leaders - including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in the opening ŌĆŗsalvos on Saturday.
Trump said on Monday initial U.S. projections were for the operation to last four to five weeks.
Iran said deaths from the attacks had reached 787 on Tuesday. That included 165 girls killed on ŌĆīthe war's ŌüĀfirst day when their school was bombed, the highest toll among several civilian sites reported to have been hit.
As Iranians have fled cities, the capital Tehran has become a ghost town.
ŌĆ£How long will this continue? Where are the shelters? Where is the government?ŌĆØ Bijan, 32, a bank employee, told Reuters by telephone from Tehran. ŌĆ£Every night my wife and I hide in the basement. The whole city is empty. There is smoke and blood everywhere.ŌĆØ
Israel continued to target the pro-Iran Hezbollah group in neighbouring Lebanon on Wednesday, after the militants fired on Israel in retaliation for the ŌĆŗdeath of Khamenei , 86, who had ruled Iran for ŌĆŗ37 years.
The Israeli military issued an ŌüĀevacuation warning on Wednesday to 16 villages in southern Lebanon, urging them to leave their homes, saying anyone near Hezbollah fighters, facilities or weapons would be putting their lives at risk.
Several people were killed in an Israeli air strike on a four-storey residential building in the eastern Lebanese ŌĆŗcity of Baalbek, the state news agency NNA reported early on Wednesday.
Trump has sought to justify the assault on Iran, saying he ŌĆŗhad ordered the campaign ŌüĀbecause he had "a feeling" Iran would attack after negotiations over its nuclear program stalled.
Iran has called the war an unprovoked attack.
"We have told the enemy that if you try to harm our main centres, we will hit all economic centres in the region," Revolutionary Guards adviser Ebrahim Jabari said in Iranian media.
Iran has fired missiles and drones at neighbouring Arab states that host U.S. bases, and strangled shipping ŌüĀthrough the Strait of ŌĆŗHormuz , where a fifth of the world's supplies of oil and liquefied natural gas travel past its coast.

item.title: Gauff worried about civilian loss of life in Middle East
item.description:
INDIAN WELLS, Calif., March 3 (Reuters) - World number four Coco Gauff expressed concern about the situation in the Middle East on Tuesday and said innocent lives were being ŌĆŗtaken in the wake of the U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran.
U.S. President ŌĆīDonald Trump's administration has given conflicting reasons for the attack, while Iran has said the U.S. assault was unprovoked.
Iranian state media has said that among the civilian casualties were more than 160 killed in a ŌĆŗstrike on a girls' school on Saturday. Administration officials have said they are ŌĆŗlooking into the incident and that the U.S. would not deliberately target ŌüĀschools.
"First off, what's happening is unfortunate, and my thoughts and prayers are out there ŌĆŗfor everyone affected and for the innocent lives being taken," American Gauff told reporters at ŌĆŗthe Indian Wells tournament.
"I think it's a lot of unnecessary violence going on."
The war in Iran has spilled over into the tennis world.
An ATP Challenger event in the United Arab Emirates was abruptly halted and ŌĆŗthen cancelled on Tuesday due to a security alert, which led to players and ŌĆŗpersonnel fleeing the courts to designated safe areas.
Meanwhile some players, ATP staff, and coaches, including Gauff's coach ŌĆīGavin ŌüĀMacMillan, have been stranded in Dubai following a tournament there as the conflict has led to widespread travel disruptions in the region.
The WTA has strong ties to the Middle East, with the season-ending WTA Finals being held in Riyadh last year and Saudi Arabia's Public Investment ŌĆŗFund (PIF) being the naming-rights ŌĆŗsponsor the WTA ŌüĀwomen's rankings.
Gauff, 21, added that she has always felt safe competing in the region, and expressed gratitude for having left the area ŌĆŗbefore the escalation of violence.
"I think it's just a lot of ŌĆŗunfortunate circumstances ŌüĀthat are happening right now," she said.
"I am thankful that I was able to get out of that before things turned to where it is now."
Reflecting on the broader implications of ŌüĀglobal ŌĆŗstrife, Italian player Jannik Sinner said: "There are certain scenarios ŌĆŗwe cannot control, so I tried to be focused.
"But you also realize that there are much more important things ŌĆŗin life than playing tennis."

item.title: Kevin Warsh has a point on AI and inflation
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Kevin Warsh claims artificial intelligence will justify lower interest rates. That is a mightily convenient view for the person nominated by President Donald Trump to be the next chair of the Federal Reserve. The former Morgan Stanley banker is still onto something, though. Letting economic orthodoxy constrain genuine growth would be a mistake.
The debate among economists over the likely impact of AI has been raging for years. ŌĆŗIn the United States, the technology may already be helping workers be more efficient. Revised data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics imply hiring slowed far more than initially thought in 2025 even as GDP ŌĆīstayed strong, meaning workers produced more per hour. Sectors that have embraced AI, notably telecom operators, broadcasters and publishers, have raised productivity more than hotels and restaurants, where the technology has fewer obvious applications. The Kansas City Fed cautions , opens new tab that direct AI use remains patchy, but industries that expect a big role for large-language models seem more willing to limit headcount .
Rapid adoption of AI would be different from the personal computer revolution, which took until the 1990s to deliver efficiency benefits. Back then, Warsh recently recalled in a YouTube interview , opens new tab with Aven CEO Sadi Khan, Fed Chair Alan Greenspan ŌĆ£sat on his handsŌĆØ in the ŌĆŗexpectation that a growth boost would not push up prices. He was right. Output per hour grew at an average rate of 2.7% between 1994 and 2004, even as inflation fell to 1.9%, echoing a similar innovative period in ŌĆŗthe 1960s.
Warsh thinks the U.S. is due for another technological windfall. AI will "be a significant disinflationary force,ŌĆØ he wrote in November , opens new tab in The Wall Street Journal. Investors are warming to that view. ŌüĀThe recent software selloff reflects anticipation that their clients will use tools like AnthropicŌĆÖs Claude Code to develop those functionalities themselves.
The problem with Warsh's belief is that it runs counter to mainstream economic theory. This holds that faster trend growth raises the ŌĆ£natural rate of ŌĆŗinterestŌĆØ as firms and households bring forward spending. If the central bank does not raise borrowing costs in lockstep, demand can overheat. In this scenario any disinflation caused by technological progress is temporary.
Indeed, Fed estimates depict the natural rate rising when productivity accelerates. Some ŌĆŗof the central bank's top officials, such as Lisa Cook , opens new tab , Michael Barr , opens new tab and Philip Jefferson , opens new tab , seem to be leaning the same way. Their views could limit Warsh's wiggle room, especially as his politically convenient thesis may make it harder to prove his commitment to defending the central bank's independence . Even Greenspan's dedication to tolerating technology-led growth had limits , opens new tab , since the Fed kept policy quite tight as the telecomŌĆæled investment boom got going in the mid-1990s and only cut interest rates after hedge fund Long Term Capital Management imploded in 1998.
Still, obsessing about an unobservable natural rate is a mistake. Surveys show companies rarely cite ŌĆŗborrowing costs as decisive for investment, and households base spending mainly on their income and age. Meanwhile, figures from the Bureau of Economic Analysis stretching back to 1977 show a clear historical correlation between sectors that experience faster productivity gains and those that ŌĆŗraise prices less. In the specific case of AI, fresh cross-sector research , opens new tab from the euro zone finds that, when the share of firms using it rises by 10 percentage points, producer inflation falls by up to 0.6 percentage points.
The catch is that, while clothes and smartphones have become ŌĆīcheaper, healthcare, education ŌüĀand housing have become more expensive. This is a pattern known as ŌĆ£Baumol disease.ŌĆØ The American economist William Baumol showed in the 1960s that as productivity rises in tradable industries ŌĆö those producing goods and services that can be consumed far from the point of production ŌĆö income flows to activities that are hard to automate or transport, so their prices and share of employment increase. In a paper , opens new tab he co-authored last year, Nobel laureate Michael Spence estimates that nontradable sectors now make up 77% of U.S. jobs and 68% of value added.
Using his approach to classify output and employment reveals the limits of the 1990s boom. Productivity in tradable industries surged, averaging 4.4% growth, but the impact on non-tradable sectors was small. The number of jobs in tradable industries grew only 6% from 1994 to 2004, ŌĆŗas tech firms shrank after the dotcom bubble burst, while ŌĆŗemployment in nontradable areas expanded 19%. In the former, workersŌĆÖ ŌüĀshare of value added fell from 62% to 57%, showing how investment-led growth skews gains toward owners of capital. It appears to support the view of the information technology wave as a one-off bump , opens new tab that mostly just benefitted the makers of hardware and software. Adopters of new digital systems like giant retailer Walmart gained in the U.S., but not in Europe.
A better takeaway, however, is that ŌĆŗthe macroeconomic environment matters. In the 1990s, the American labor market was far tighter than the European one, having quickly recovered from a recession, so U.S. firms had incentives to ŌĆŗsqueeze more output from existing workers. That ŌüĀtradable productivity growth plummeted to 1% in the 2010s suggests that it was the global financial crisis that killed the boom, not digital saturation.
Fast-forward to today, and hiring has also slowed after a growth period. Spending could weaken further if AI widens income inequality, since the wealthy consume a smaller proportion of their earnings. In that case, monetary policy should be looser. If stock market doomsters are right and industries like law, education and health care are able to automate key processes, the deflationary effect will be even more pronounced.
Of course there ŌüĀare big differences. ŌĆŗThe 1990s were a time when globalization weighed on prices. Now, tariffs are pushing up prices of tradable goods in the U.S. and the data center ŌĆŗinvestment boom is straining power, land and supply chains. All these factors place a floor on official borrowing costs.
Still, it would be a mistake for the Fed to try to restrain the AI boom to fit a textbook model, or to assert its independence from an interventionist president. In that sense, Warsh has ŌĆŗa point.
Follow Jon Sindreu on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Netanyahu's war alliance with Trump faces test as Iran crisis widens
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 4, (Reuters) - Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu delivered on a career-long ambition to topple Iran's leadership, but his lockstep alignment with U.S. President Donald Trump faces a test as their joint military campaign threatens to drag on, with its goals potentially shifting in the coming weeks.
At the outset of the bombing campaign on Saturday, both Trump and Netanyahu said regime change was the goal. But in ŌĆŗremarks at the White House on Monday, two days after Israeli air strikes killed IranŌĆÖs Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and much of his leadership, Trump did not mention overthrowing Iran's government as his top priority.
The U.S. goal, he said, was ŌĆīto destroy IranŌĆÖs missiles and navy, and to stop it from obtaining a nuclear weapon. His Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth said at a press conference that same day that the operation was not a ŌĆ£so-called regime-change war.ŌĆØ
Netanyahu, by contrast, has called on Iran's citizens to take to the streets and overthrow their rulers as recently as Monday night. "We're going to create the conditions, first, for the Iranian people to get control of their destiny," he told Fox News.
Asked about the U.S. and Israeli goals, a U.S. official familiar with the White House's objectives told Reuters that the two countries' military campaigns have different objectives. "Regime change is one of theirs," said the official, who spoke on the condition of ŌĆŗanonymity.
In the build-up to war, Netanyahu successfully convinced Trump that it was a now-or-never moment to prevent Tehran from acquiring nuclear weapons and destroy its ballistic missile capabilities. Trump has said the operation could take ŌĆ£four or five weeksŌĆØ or ŌĆ£whatever it takes.ŌĆØ
ŌĆ£I donŌĆÖt get bored, I ŌĆŗnever get bored,ŌĆØ he said at the White House on Monday in response to questions about his capacity for sustained focus.
But Israeli officials privately acknowledge that ultimately it will be Trump who decides when the war ŌüĀends. Dan Shapiro, a former U.S. ambassador to Israel under the Obama administration, said that Trump may decide to seek an "early off-ramp" from the war.
"If President Trump decides that he's reached the end of this operation before Netanyahu wants it to end, he's still going to end it," said ŌĆŗShapiro, of the Washington-based Atlantic Council think-tank.
President Trump faces domestic pressures that could affect his thinking as the war drags on and expands.
The operation is unpopular in the United States, with only one in four Americans saying they back U.S. strikes on Iran, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling. Primary votes began Tuesday ŌĆŗin the battleground states of Texas and North Carolina that may decide who controls Congress after the fall midterm elections.
With the crisis disrupting shipping and energy production, rising gas prices could become a daily reminder of the affordability crisis facing many Americans. Gas is up 11 cents per gallon in the U.S. this week, with much higher spikes in global markets suggesting more increases for American consumers.
Inside the U.S., support for Israel has become a partisan issue, with some 59% of Americans holding an unfavourable view of Israel's government, up from 51% a year ago, according to a Pew Research Center poll from October.
The White House and Netanyahu's office did not respond to requests for ŌĆŗcomment.
In power for most of the last three decades, Netanyahu has often clashed with American leaders, notably publicly criticizing former Democratic President Barack Obama for negotiating a nuclear deal with Iran. Democratic President Joe BidenŌĆÖs administration often clashed with Netanyahu and withheld some weapons from Israel during ŌĆŗits military assault in Gaza.
After Trump's return to office in 2025, Netanyahu met with the president seven times and repeatedly pushed in phone calls to focus his attention away from Israel's war in Gaza and toward Iran's ballistic missiles and nuclear ambitions, painting the clerical rulers in Tehran as a common enemy, a U.S. official ŌĆīwith direct knowledge ŌüĀof their conversations said.
The officials and others who shared details about U.S.-Israeli planning and objectives spoke on the condition of anonymity to describe sensitive military discussions.
Even as Trump dispatched envoys to nuclear talks with Iran in Geneva and Oman, the U.S. and Israel had been at work for months planning their military operation, and timing for the attack was decided weeks ago, an Israeli official said.
NetanyahuŌĆÖs last meeting with Trump was a hastily-arranged visit on February 11, 2026 which included a three-hour meeting at the White House, uncharacteristically closed to the press.
The day after that meeting, the USS Gerald Ford aircraft carrier, the worldŌĆÖs largest warship, departed the Caribbean where it was supporting U.S. military action in Venezuela, for the Mediterranean.
ŌĆ£I have tried to persuade successive American administrations to take firm action, and President Trump did,ŌĆØ Netanyahu told Fox News on Monday.
Trump rejected the notion that Israel might have forced his country into war, ŌĆŗtelling reporters at the White House on Tuesday: "Based on the way the ŌĆŗnegotiation was going, I think they were going to attack ŌüĀfirst, and I didn't want that to happen. So if anything I might have forced Israel's hand."
For the 76-year-old Netanyahu, his prosecution of a war that is supported by most Israelis represents an opportunity to seal his legacy ahead of elections, due by October, in which he faces formidable challenges.
His far-right coalition faces fissures, he's on trial for corruption he denies and Israelis are still reeling from a multi-front war that began in 2023 ŌĆŗand which Netanyahu has promised will transform the Middle East.
Israel's longest-serving leader has shown remarkable political skill in the past. Despite successive polls showing that he will lose the ballot in October, Netanyahu still has ŌĆŗa fair chance of victory if Israeli ŌüĀfatalities and the economic costs to Israel of the war remain low, said Udi Sommer, a political scientist at Tel Aviv University.
"If it succeeds, relatively quickly (like) in June 2025, it will work very much in his favour as Israel's protector and the one who had woven a particularly successful relationship with the administration in Washington," Sommer said.
Netanyahu's security credentials were shattered on October 7, 2023, when Iran-backed Hamas militants launched a surprise attack on Israel, killing more than 1,200 people and taking 251 hostages.
It was followed by a two-year military campaign against Hamas in Gaza, where Israel's longest war has killed at least 72,000 people, according to ŌüĀPalestinian health officials, left ŌĆŗmuch of the enclave in ruins, and exacted the highest Israeli military fatalities in decades.
Netanyahu has rejected responsibility for the security failures of October 7 and has pointed to Israel's ŌĆŗsubsequent gains in weakening Iran's proxies, Hamas and Hezbollah in Lebanon. Their ally Bashar al-Assad in Syria has also been ousted.
Even if Israel achieves its military objectives in Iran, that will not wash away the outrage of many Israeli voters, including among Netanyahu's own right-wing base, said political analyst Amotz Asa-el of the Jerusalem-based Shalom Hartman research institute.
"The past three years' ŌĆŗevents have been so traumatic and so dramatic and so revolting to that swing vote that I don't think any kind of salvation in Iran will offset this," he said.

item.title: OpenAI looking at contract with NATO, source says
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - OpenAI is considering a contract to deploy its AI technology on North Atlantic Treaty ŌĆŗOrganization's (NATO) "unclassified" networks, a person familiar with the matter said on Tuesday, days after the ŌĆīChatGPT-owner struck a deal with the Pentagon.
The Wall Street Journal first reported that OpenAI was considering an agreement with NATO.
The newspaper said the OpenAI CEO, Sam Altman, had initially said in a ŌĆŗcompany meeting that it was looking to deploy on all NATO classified ŌĆŗnetworks, but a company spokeswoman later clarified to the Journal that Altman misspoke and ŌüĀthe contract opportunity was for NATO's "unclassified networks."
NATO, a 32-member military alliance, did not ŌĆŗimmediately respond to a request for comment outside regular business hours.
OpenAI, which is backed by ŌĆŗMicrosoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab , Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab and others, announced a deal late last week to deploy its technology in the Pentagon's classified network, after U.S. President Donald Trump directed the government to stop working with rival Anthropic.
Anthropic's removal followed ŌĆŗa standoff in contract talks with the Pentagon over the use of the firm's ŌĆŗtechnology. Anthropic CEO, Dario Amodei, had stressed the company's opposition to the Pentagon using its AI models for ŌĆīmass ŌüĀdomestic surveillance or to power fully autonomous weapons.
The Pentagon has said previously it had no interest in using AI to conduct mass surveillance of Americans or using AI to develop weapons that operate without human involvement, but wanted any lawful use of AI to ŌĆŗbe allowed.
In an ŌĆŗupdated statement on Monday after ŌüĀstriking a deal on Friday, OpenAI said its AI systems "shall not be intentionally used for domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and ŌĆŗnationals," adding that the Pentagon also affirmed that AI services ŌĆŗwould not ŌüĀbe used by intelligence agencies such as the National Security Agency (NSA).
"I think this was an example of a complex, but right decision with extremely difficult brand consequences and very negative ŌüĀPR ŌĆŗfor us in the short term,ŌĆØ Altman said in ŌĆŗa company meeting on Tuesday, referring to the Pentagon deal, according to the Wall Street Journal.

item.title: Philippine lawmakers advance impeachment of Vice President Duterte
item.description:
MANILA, March 4 (Reuters) - Philippine lawmakers ordered Vice President Sara Duterte on Wednesday to respond to impeachment complaints against her after finding substance to allegations ŌĆŗinvolving unexplained wealth, the misuse of public funds and making public threats against ŌĆīPresident Ferdinand Marcos Jr.
The daughter of former President Rodrigo Duterte is seen as a frontrunner for the 2028 presidential elections, and has said she will run for the top ŌĆŗpost . She survived a similar impeachment bid last year, which the Supreme Court ŌĆŗstruck down for violating constitutional safeguards.
If convicted by the Senate ŌüĀat trial, Duterte would be removed from office and disqualified from ŌĆŗholding any future government position.
Duterte's defence team during the impeachment proceedings did not ŌĆŗimmediately respond to a request for comment.
Wednesday's vote in the House of Representatives' justice committee comes amid a bitter falling out between the two leaders, both scions of ŌĆŗpowerful political families, who swept to power in 2022 before becoming rivals.
Before any ŌĆŗvote on whether an impeachment trial should go ahead, the House will consider Duterte's response to the charges, ŌĆīif ŌüĀshe makes one, as well as any reply from the complainants.
The complaints against Duterte include the misuse of confidential funds, bribery and unexplained wealth, and publicly threatening to have Marcos killed, along with his wife and a cousin ŌĆŗwho was House ŌĆŗspeaker at the ŌüĀtime.
"This is a very good development that the process of accountability is now moving," Representative Leila de Lima ŌĆŗtold Reuters. De Lima endorsed one of the complaints ŌĆŗagainst Duterte.
Four ŌüĀimpeachment complaints were initially lodged against Duterte, but one complaint was dismissed over a rule that prohibits such complaints from being filed within a year after previously being ŌüĀsubmitted. Another ŌĆŗcomplaint was withdrawn to fast-track the proceedings ŌĆŗand because it was similar to another complaint.
Marcos survived a separate impeachment bid against him last month after his allies ŌĆŗin Congress voted to dismiss it.

item.title: South Africa's Lekota, who led breakaway from ANC, dies at 77
item.description: JOHANNESBURG, March 4 (Reuters) - South African anti-apartheid activist Mosiuoa Lekota, who broke away from the African ŌĆŗNational Congress to found a new ŌĆīpolitical party, died in the early hours of Wednesday morning, his party said in a statement. ŌĆŗHe was 77 years old.

item.title: Myanmar junta to ration fuel for private vehicles, blaming Middle East shipping disruptions
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Myanmar's junta announced the launch of a sweeping fuel rationing system for private vehicles on Wednesday, blaming disruptions to the global energy supply chain caused by escalating hostilities in the ŌĆŗMiddle East.
The country's National Defence and Security Council (NDSC) said the new regulations, effective ŌĆīMarch 7, 2026, were a response to "global political situations" and armed conflicts in the Middle East, which have obstructed oil shipments.
Under a new "even-odd" licensing scheme, even-numbered plates will only be allowed to drive on even dates, and ŌĆŗodd-numbered plates on odd dates, the announcement said. Electric vehicles and electric motorcycles are ŌĆŗexempt.
The NDSC warned businesses and individuals not to hoard fuel for resale at ŌüĀinflated prices, saying that violators would be prosecuted.
Global shipping costs have surged amid an ongoing U.S. and ŌĆŗIsraeli war against Iran and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, disrupting tankers bound ŌĆŗfor Asian ports.
Myanmar is heavily reliant on refined fuel imports from Singapore and Malaysia, which serve as regional processing hubs for Middle Eastern crude.
The disruptions have already caused shortages, though secondary supplies are available via Russia and ŌĆŗits neighbour Thailand.
Residents in Yangon, Myanmar's commercial hub, expressed concern that the rationing plan would drive ŌĆŗup the cost of living and further complicate daily life in a city already plagued by power outages.
"Operating ŌĆīvehicles ŌüĀon alternating even and odd days based on license plate numbers is an incredibly frustrating system for people in a city like Yangon, where we depend so heavily on cars," said one Yangon resident.
Skyrocketing prices have already made it difficult to meet demand, raising questions about the extent of ŌĆŗthe country's strategic reserves, ŌĆŗhe added.
Fuel supplies in ŌüĀthe border town of Myawaddy ran out as early as the evening of March 3, forcing local stations to temporarily shut down and residents ŌĆŗto queue up at gas stations in Thailand's Mae Sot, according to ŌĆŗa resident.
"Since ŌüĀyesterday, many people have been crossing over to the Mae Sot side to fill up their tanks. I went to stand in line myself and saw a massive number of vehicles queuing up at ŌüĀThai gas ŌĆŗstations," the resident said.
Myanmar has been in turmoil since ŌĆŗ2021, when the military ousted the elected government led by Nobel laureate Aung San Suu Kyi, sparking a wave of ŌĆŗanti-junta protests that have morphed into a nationwide civil war.

item.title: China says it seeks communications┬Āwith US but vows to hold its 'red lines'
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 (Reuters) - China is willing to work with the United States to promote communication on all ŌĆŗlevels, while upholding its "red lines" and principles, Lou ŌĆīQinjian, a spokesperson for its parliament, said on Wednesday.
The National People's Congress opens its annual session on Thursday, where it will unveil ŌĆŗthis year's economic targets and policy priorities.
The meeting comes ŌĆŗat a sensitive moment for China-US relations, as ŌüĀboth sides look to stabilise ties ahead of an expected ŌĆŗsummit of leaders Donald Trump and Xi Jinping in Beijing ŌĆŗat the end of March.
China and the United States should respect each other and coexist peacefully, Lou said.
"China has its own principles and ŌĆŗred lines, and as always, will resolutely defend its ŌĆŗsovereignty, security and development interests," he told a press conference.
Diplomacy between heads ŌĆīof ŌüĀstate has an "irreplaceable strategic role" in guiding the two countries' ties, Lou added, urging them to "expand the list of cooperation (areas) while reducing the list of problems".
He called on the ŌĆŗU.S. Congress to ŌĆŗview China "objectively", ŌüĀand do more to benefit ties.
A White House official has confirmed Trump will travel to ŌĆŗChina from March 31 to April 2, although ŌĆŗBeijing ŌüĀhas made no official announcement.
Top trade negotiators of both sides are expected to meet in Paris next week to discuss potential ŌüĀbusiness ŌĆŗdeals linked to the highly anticipated ŌĆŗmeet, Bloomberg News said on Tuesday.

item.title: Iranians to bid farewell to late Supreme Leader Khamenei at Tehran ceremony
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Iranians will bid farewell to late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ŌĆŗat a ceremony in Tehran late on Wednesday, ŌĆīa senior Iranian official told state media.
Hojjatoleslam Mahmoudi, head of IranŌĆÖs Islamic Propagation Council, ŌĆŗsaid the farewell ceremony would continue ŌĆŗfor three days and the funeral ŌüĀprocession will be announced later.
The official ŌĆŗsaid the public will be able ŌĆŗto pay their respects to the body of late Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei at TehranŌĆÖs ŌĆŗImam Khomeini Prayer Hall starting at ŌĆŗ10 p.m. (1830 GMT).
"The Mosalla (prayer hall) will be receiving ŌĆīvisitors ŌüĀand the dear people can attend and take part in the farewell ceremony and mark a strong presence once ŌĆŗagain," he ŌĆŗsaid in ŌüĀcomments carried by Iranian media.
Iran's Ali Khamenei, who based his ŌĆŗiron rule of Iran on ŌĆŗhostility ŌüĀto the U.S. and Israel, was killed on Saturday, aged 86, in air strikes ŌüĀby ŌĆŗIsrael and the U.S., ŌĆŗaccording to Iranian state media.

item.title: Sri Lanka rescues 30 people on board distressed Iranian ship, foreign minister says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - The Sri Lankan military ŌĆŗhas rescued at least ŌĆī30 people on board a sinking Iranian ship near Sri ŌĆŗLankan waters on ŌĆŗWednesday, the country's foreign minister ŌüĀtold parliament.
The Sri Lankan ŌĆŗnavy dispatched a rescue mission ŌĆŗafter a distress call from the Iranian ship, a defence ministry spokesperson said earlier ŌĆŗon Wednesday.
Foreign minister ŌĆŗVijitha Herath did not give further ŌĆīdetails ŌüĀbut said Sri Lanka would take appropriate action.
Local media reported the ship reported ŌĆŗdistress ŌĆŗoff ŌüĀthe coast of Galle in the southern ŌĆŗpart of the ŌĆŗcountry, ŌüĀand that the injured had been admitted to a ŌüĀhospital ŌĆŗin Galle.

item.title: Two senior Communist Party officials absent at top Chinese political event
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 (Reuters) - Two senior Chinese officials were absent on ŌĆŗWednesday from the ranks of ŌĆīChina's highest tier of power at the opening of the country's ŌĆŗtwo largest political events ŌĆŗof the year, with top ŌüĀCommunist Party cadres ensnared ŌĆŗin a wave of purges.
Zhang Youxia, ŌĆŗChina's highest-ranking general, was not among the party's 23-member politburo at ŌĆŗthe opening of the Chinese ŌĆŗPeople's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC), according to ŌĆīa ŌüĀReuters witness. Neither was Ma Xingrui, formerly the party's top official in the western Chinese ŌĆŗregion ŌĆŗof Xinjiang.
Zhang, ŌüĀalso a vice chairman on the Central ŌĆŗMilitary Commission (CMC), was put ŌĆŗunder ŌüĀinvestigation in January. Ma has not been in the public ŌüĀeye ŌĆŗsince late October.

item.title: Gold flubs its lines amid Middle East mayhem
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - The most peculiar market move in an alarming week of Middle East conflict was how the most obvious "safe haven" - gold - flubbed its lines. Instead of piling into gold, investors seemed to sprint for dollar cash, offloading ŌĆŗanything that caught a speculative head of steam before last weekend's attacks.
Three days after Saturday's attacks on Iran, the initial bid for ŌĆīprecious metals faded quickly. On Tuesday, there was a major reversal , with gold suddenly falling 4% and silver dropping as much as 10%.
A return of the dollar's long-lost "safety bid", which saw the greenback (.DXY) , opens new tab climb this week despite heavy losses in U.S. stocks and bonds, was cited as one basic reason for gold's reversal.
Both public and private funds in the Middle East region now ŌĆŗfacing Iranian retaliatory strikes may be opting for dollar liquidity. A spike in dollar-denominated oil and gas prices may also have spurred demand for cash in the ŌĆŗworld's reserve currency.
The main reason for the dollar rise, however, is most likely the hit to major European and Asian ŌüĀeconomies from a protracted energy supply disruption and price spike compared with the relatively insulated U.S.
Either way, a rebounding dollar takes the shine off gold.
But there are other possible reasons ŌĆŗfor gold's "no-show" so far this week.
One is gold's correlation with the Swiss franc . Both are traditionally the safest of safe havens in stressful times and get bid ŌĆŗup in tandem - especially now that others such as Japan's yen and U.S. Treasuries have been neutralised as such in recent years.
But an extraordinary warning of franc-selling intervention from the Swiss National Bank on Monday quickly reversed the currency's gains against both the dollar and the euro. The resulting unwind of haven trades may have added pressure on gold.
A ŌĆŗmore prosaic explanation is that investors who loaded up on gold in a speculative frenzy - one that almost doubled its price to fresh records over the past year - ŌĆŗare simply cashing out of their best-performing trades as risk and volatility rise.
That would tally, for example, with the sudden reversal of the year's best-performing stock market to date. South ŌĆīKorea's Kospi ŌüĀbenchmark (.KS11) , opens new tab fell more than 7% on Tuesday as Seoul returned from a holiday to reverse some of a near 50% year-to-date surge.
Gold and silver were the second- and third-best-performing major markets of 2026 before the strikes, behind the blockbuster Kospi, while Japan's Nikkei (.N225) , opens new tab ŌĆō up about 15% before the weekend - has since dropped more than 4%.
With volatility rising and another potential energy shock brewing for the global economy, many portfolios may simply be looking to increase cash and liquidity.
The fact that gold has not ŌĆŗyet performed as a haven in that ŌĆŗenvironment says a lot about ŌüĀthe nature of the buying and the rally over the past year - and not least how it hinged heavily on the ongoing reversal of more than a decade of one-way dollar strength.
As IMF First Deputy Managing Director Dan Katz said on Tuesday, ŌĆŗthe dollar's behaviour this week shows its role as a haven persists and the U.S. currency remains the "heart of ŌĆŗthe international monetary ŌüĀsystem."
Gold may yet rise for other reasons. But if its recent parabolic climb was fuelled by doomsday narratives about the dollar's demise, then this week's moves may prompt a rethink.
(The opinions expressed here are those of the author , a columnist for Reuters.)

item.title: Investors can still outwit AI, but only if theyŌĆÖre unpredictable
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - Investors know artificial intelligence is coming for their jobs. Machines can already run complex analyses of investments and portfolios, threatening fund managers' livelihoods.
But new studies show where AI falls short ŌĆō and it's in those blind spots where human managers still have the ability to generate "alpha" or excess returns.
For weeks now, stocks have been sorted ŌĆŗinto AI winners and AI losers . Share prices of companies with business models that may be disrupted by AI have sold off, while the makers of AI hardware and software ŌĆīhave kept rising. It is no secret that many academics and companies are testing the ability of AI to pick stocks and manage portfolios. So should fund managers fear for their jobs?
In recent weeks, I have come across several academic studies that examine both the capabilities and limitations of AI. The most comprehensive and interesting is a new NBER working paper , opens new tab by Boston University Assistant Professor Pietro Bini and his colleagues.
They ask four leading generative AI (genAI) models (GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Llama) to answer a set ŌĆŗof questions used to measure behavioural biases in finance and economics. They then evaluate whether these models give a rational answer or an answer that is biased in the same manner as most ŌĆŗhumans.
What emerges from the data is an interesting split. When dealing with common cognitive biases, like the gamblerŌĆÖs fallacy , opens new tab or base-rate neglect, , opens new tab where genAI can ŌüĀfall back on well-established mathematical formulas, the answers are largely free from bias. One can therefore expect that in forecasting situations in which humans may be subject to such biases, genAI will likely outperform investors ŌĆŗmade of flesh and bone.
But when dealing with problems that have a large degree of qualitative uncertainty or where the answer requires a judgment call, genAI is just as biased as most humans. When the model canŌĆÖt ŌĆŗrely on a mathematical answer, it has to deduce a solution from its training data. And the training data is mostly man-made and thus codifies the same biases as humans. ItŌĆÖs a case of garbage-in, garbage-out.
Importantly, these results indicate where human investors might be able to outperform machines moving forward.
In another study, Lauren Cohen from Harvard Business School , opens new tab and her collaborators trained AI to learn the decision processes of thousands of U.S. equity fund managers. The goal was to predict which ŌĆŗstocks a fund manager would buy, sell, or hold next quarter or the following year.
If AI can do this consistently, fund managers really should be worried about their jobs.
The bad news is that the AI-equipped researchers ŌĆŗmanaged to correctly predict 71% of all future trades. The good news is that fund managers' alpha was rooted mostly in the other 29%.
Naturally, fund managers have different processes, so there is variability in how well AI can anticipate ŌĆītheir actions.
Funds that ŌüĀare run based on a prescriptive, inflexible process (or by an inflexible fund manager) are, unsurprisingly, more predictable. These funds tend to invest more in stocks that are clear-cut cases for a specific investment style.
For example, value funds with a rigid, prescriptive process tend to invest only in the most obvious value stocks and ignore the ones that are ambiguous cases.
But if everyone knows that company A is a value company, then the shares would already be in the portfolios of most fund managers following that investment style. And that leaves less room for new investors ŌĆō including AI models ŌĆō to gain any advantage by buying the stock.
Company B, meanwhile, may be a ŌĆŗvalue stock, but may struggle with a difficult competitive ŌĆŗenvironment, ineffective management or other factors that cannot ŌüĀeasily be quantified. A fund manager would need to use judgment to determine whether this was truly a value stock or something that was "cheap" for a reason. This is where fund managers become unpredictable to AI.
If a fund manager "sees something" in company B that isnŌĆÖt in the data, an AI model will struggle to ŌĆŗpredict the purchase. Yet, if that judgment turns out to be correct, the shares of company B will likely rally much more as new investors flock ŌĆŗto buy the stock.
The result ŌüĀis that fund managers whose actions are harder for AI to predict, who appear more random and are better at handling qualitative factors, tend to outperform both their peers and the broader market.
Of course, these human advantages may prove fleeting. As AI models learn from ever richer data sets and from the very managers they now struggle to predict, today's blind spots could narrow, shifting the frontier of truly human alpha yet again.
The lesson for investors is ŌüĀthus that in ŌĆŗthe age of AI, a fundŌĆÖs alpha will increasingly come from being able to predict the unpredictable.
(The views expressed here are ŌĆŗthose of Joachim Klement , an investment strategist for Panmure Liberum.)

And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest ŌĆŗnews in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Canada PM Carney says Iran conflict a failure of the international order
item.description:
SYDNEY/TORONTO, March 4 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime ŌĆŗMinister Mark Carney said on Wednesday the ongoing conflict in the Middle East was a "failure" of the international order, and ŌĆīthe United States had not consulted its allies before striking Iran.
U.S. and Israeli forces launched strikes against Iran on Saturday after negotiations over Tehran's nuclear programme stalled.
The war has since spread beyond Iran's borders, with Iranian attacks hitting Gulf states including Bahrain, the United Arab Emirates and Qatar, as well as ŌĆŗU.S. embassies in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, prompting Washington to close diplomatic missions across the region.
"The current conflict is ŌĆŗanother example of the failure of the international order, despite decades of UN Security Council resolutions, ŌüĀthe work of the International Atomic Energy Agency in a succession of sanctions and diplomatic frameworks," Carney said during a visit ŌĆŗto Australia in a speech at the Lowy Institute think tank in Sydney.
"Iran's nuclear threat remains, and now the United States and ŌĆŗIsrael have acted without engaging the UN or consulting with allies, including Canada."
Earlier, Carney called for calm in the Middle East and said all countries engaged in hostilities, including the U.S. and Israel, must respect international rules of engagement.
"Canada calls for a rapid de-escalation of hostilities and is prepared ŌĆŗto assist in achieving this goal," Carney told reporters.
When asked about previous comments he made supporting the UN Charter's prohibition of ŌĆŗthe use of force, Carney said "it appears that these actions are inconsistent with international law," but said it was up to the U.S. and ŌĆīIsrael ŌüĀto make the case whether they were consistent with international law.
"It's for others more expert than me ... to make that determination," he said.
Carney said Canada was not informed in advance of the strikes and was not asked to participate.
"We would not have been in a position ... to take a judgement that met our standards if we had been asked to participate," Carney said.
After the strikes began ŌĆŗat the weekend, Carney said ŌĆŗCanada supported the U.S. "acting to ŌüĀprevent Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon and to prevent its regime from further threatening international peace and security."
Carney is on a multi-leg trip across the Asia-Pacific ŌĆŗregion also taking in Japan and India, with his stop in Australia aimed at bolstering relations ŌĆŗbetween the two ŌüĀso-called "middle powers".
"Canada is focused on building a dense web of connections, ad hoc coalitions that work issue by issue, with partners that share enough common ground on those issues to act together," Carney said on Wednesday.
"In contrast, great powers can afford to go it alone. ŌüĀThey have ŌĆŗthe market size, the military capacity to leverage, to dictate terms. Middle powers ŌĆŗlike Canada do not."
Australia and Canada are expected to deepen cooperation in areas such as defence and maritime security, critical minerals, trade and artificial intelligence, Carney's office said ŌĆŗahead of the visit.

item.title: Spain's Sanchez to Trump: 'You cannot play Russian roulette with the destiny of millions'
item.description:
MADRID, March 4 (Reuters) - Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez on Wednesday doubled down on his opposition to the attack ŌĆŗon Iran by the U.S. and Israel, warning ŌĆīthat the conflict risked playing "Russian roulette" with the lives of millions.
Sanchez was responding after U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to cut trade with Madrid over its ŌĆŗposition on the conflict.
"This is how humanity's great disasters ŌĆŗstart ... You cannot play Russian roulette with destiny ŌüĀof millions," Sanchez said in a televised address to the ŌĆŗnation.
Tensions between the two NATO allies increased after Sanchez denounced the ŌĆŗU.S. and Israeli bombings of Iran as reckless and illegal, and later banned U.S. aircraft from using naval and air bases in southern Spain for ŌĆŗthe offensive against Tehran.
Sanchez said the world could not ŌĆŗsolve its problems with conflicts and bombs.
"The position of the Spanish government ŌĆīcan ŌüĀbe summarised in four words: 'No to the war,'" he said, adding the stance was not disingenuous but coherent.
"We're not going to be complicit in something that's bad for the ŌĆŗworld nor contrary ŌĆŗto our ŌüĀvalues and interests simply to avoid reprisals from someone," Sanchez said, appearing to reference Trump's ŌĆŗtrade threats.
Sanchez highlighted the negative knock-on effects ŌĆŗof the ŌüĀIraq war, from a rise in jihadist terrorism to soaring energy prices, to argue that the consequences of this attack ŌüĀon ŌĆŗIran were just as nebulous and ŌĆŗthat it would not lead to a more just international order.

item.title: Hungary's opposition leads in polls, far-right party gains support
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 4 (Reuters) - Hungary's centre-right Tisza party leads Prime Minister Viktor Orban's governing Fidesz before an April 12 election, while the far-right Our Homeland is gaining support, two ŌĆŗopinion polls showed on Wednesday.
Nationalist Orban faces the biggest challenge to ŌĆīhis rule in 16 years although the outcome of the vote remains highly uncertain, with opinion polls showing that many voters are still undecided.
The poll by Zavecz Research, conducted between February 22 ŌĆŗand 28, shows that Tisza widened its lead to 12 points among decided ŌĆŗvoters from 10 points in a January poll.
The poll, published by ŌüĀnews site 24.hu late on Tuesday, showed 50% of decided voters supported Tisza, ŌĆŗup from 48% in January, while 38% backed Fidesz, down from 39% a month ŌĆŗearlier.
Zavecz showed 38% support for Tisza among all voters, while Fidesz was backed by 32%.
According to their data, some 20% of respondents said that they still did not know who to back, down ŌĆŗfrom 23% in February.
Tisza is led by former government insider Peter Magyar who ŌĆŗhas said his party would curb corruption, unlock billions of euros in frozen European Union funds ŌĆīto boost ŌüĀthe economy, and firmly anchor Hungary in the EU and NATO.
A second poll, published by newspaper Nepszava on Wednesday and conducted by Publicus Institute, showed 47% of decided voters supporting Tisza and 39% backing Fidesz, as support for both parties was down ŌĆŗ1 percentage point ŌĆŗsince a January poll.
While ŌüĀmost polls have shown a Tisza lead, Fidesz points to other surveys that still show it on course to victory, though ŌĆŗits opponents say these have mainly been conducted by institutes ŌĆŗwith financial ŌüĀor personal ties to the ruling party.
Both polls showed that the far-right Our Homeland (Mi Hazank) would be the only other party to pass the 5% threshold required for entry ŌüĀinto parliament.
Zavecz ŌĆŗResearch put support for Our Homeland at 7% ŌĆŗamong decided voters, up from 5% a month ago. Publicus said that 6% of decided voters back the ŌĆŗfar-right party, up from 5% in January.

item.title: Russian drone hits empty passenger train in Ukraine's south, deputy PM says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A Russian drone hit an empty passenger train in Ukraine's southern Mykolaiv ŌĆŗregion early on Wednesday, injuring a ŌĆīrailway worker, Deputy Prime Minister Oleksiy Kuleba said.
He added that there was also an ŌĆŗattempted Russian drone attack on a ŌĆŗtrain operating between the eastern-central city ŌüĀof Dnipro and Kovel in the ŌĆŗnorthwest late on Tuesday, but it was ŌĆŗstopped by railway workers and the drone hit a few metres away from the locomotive.
Ukrzaliznytsia, Ukrainian ŌĆŗnational railways, said that Russia had ŌĆŗintensified drone attacks on railway infrastructure, and that rolling ŌĆīstock ŌüĀwas among the main targets.
It reported that 18 strikes had been recorded since the start of March, damaging 41 ŌĆŗfacilities.
Locomotives, freight ŌĆŗcars and ŌüĀspecialised equipment used to repair infrastructure have also been targeted, ŌĆŗthe operator said, and railway depots ŌĆŗand ŌüĀbridges had also come under attack this month.
A Russian drone attack on a commuter ŌüĀtrain ŌĆŗin Dnipropetrovsk region killed ŌĆŗone person and wounded seven more people on Monday.

item.title: South Africa offers to mediate in Middle East conflict if asked
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, March 4 (Reuters) - South Africa is willing ŌĆŗto play a mediating role ŌĆīin the Middle East conflict if asked, President Cyril Ramaphosa told local media on the ŌĆŗsidelines of an energy conference ŌĆŗin Cape Town.
"South Africa is always ŌüĀready to play a contributing role, ŌĆŗeither in mediation or whatever. And ŌĆŗif a gap opens or if we are asked, we always live up to our ŌĆŗobligations," Ramaphosa said, according to ŌĆŗcomments broadcast on local broadcaster Newzroom Afrika.
"If the ŌĆīopportunity ŌüĀwere to open, we would talk and say: there must be a ceasefire. Dialogue is always the best ŌĆŗway of ŌĆŗending conflict ŌüĀand then ending the war. And we want this ŌĆŗwar to come to an ŌĆŗend ŌüĀimmediately," he said.
Ramaphosa added that the South African government was doing everything ŌüĀit ŌĆŗcould to bring home ŌĆŗits citizens stranded in the region.

item.title: Mojtaba Khamenei, seen as possible next Supreme Leader, has survived assault on Iran, sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Mojtaba Khamanei, the ŌĆŗson of ŌĆīIran's late Supreme Leader, has ŌĆŗsurvived the ŌĆŗassault on the country ŌüĀby U.S. ŌĆŗand Israeli ŌĆŗair strikes that killed Ayatollah Ali ŌĆŗKhamenei and ŌĆŗseveral military and other ŌĆīinfluential ŌüĀfigures, two Iranian sources told Reuters on ŌĆŗWednesday.
He ŌĆŗis ŌüĀseen by the establishment ŌĆŗas ŌĆŗa ŌüĀpossible successor to his father, ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗsources ŌĆŗsaid.

item.title: Appeal hearings begin in ex-South Korea President Yoon's obstruction case
item.description: SEOUL, March 4 (Reuters) - A South Korean court held an appeals hearing on Wednesday in a case involving ŌĆŗformer South Korean President Yoon Suk Yeol, who ŌĆīwas sentenced to five years in jail by a lower court on charges including obstructing arrest after his attempt to impose ŌĆŗmartial law.

item.title: Russia says France's plan to expand its nuclear arsenal is destabilising
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 4 (Reuters) - Russia said on Wednesday that France's plan to expand its nuclear arsenal was a highly ŌĆŗdestabilising move that posed a potential threat to ŌĆīMoscow.
French President Emmanuel Macron announced the plan on Monday, saying other European countries would also be able to take part ŌĆŗin French nuclear exercises. France and Germany said ŌĆŗthey had set up a nuclear steering group ŌüĀto discuss deterrence issues.
Macron first said in March ŌĆŗ2025 that he would launch a strategic dialogue on extending ŌĆŗthe protection of France's nuclear umbrella to European allies that have until now relied on the United States.
Russian Foreign Ministry spokeswoman ŌĆŗMaria Zakharova told reporters that Macron's announcement this ŌĆŗweek was "an extremely destabilising development".
It represented "a significant strengthening and expansion of ŌĆīNATO's ŌüĀnuclear potential, which, in the event of a direct military conflict with Russia, could be used in a coordinated manner against our country," she said.
Kremlin spokesman ŌĆŗDmitry Peskov, speaking ŌĆŗseparately to ŌüĀreporters, said the French move vindicated Moscow's position that French and British nuclear ŌĆŗweapons should be part of any future ŌĆŗnegotiation on ŌüĀthe global nuclear balance.
Russia says it is open to such talks following the expiry last month of New START , ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗlast bilateral treaty that limited ŌĆŗthe numbers of Russian and U.S. strategic nuclear warheads and missiles.

item.title: Drone attacks Iranian Kurdish opposition group's HQ in Iraq's Kurdistan, security sources say
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A drone struck an arms ŌĆŗdepot in an attack ŌĆīon the headquarters of an Iranian Kurdish opposition group in the ŌĆŗtown of Dekala in ŌĆŗIraq's Kurdistan region on Wednesday, wounding ŌüĀtwo fighters, security sources ŌĆŗsaid.
Iran has long accused Iraq's ŌĆŗautonomous northern Kurdish region of sheltering militant groups involved in attacks against ŌĆŗthe Islamic Republic, with Iran's ŌĆŗRevolutionary Guards in turn repeatedly targeting ŌĆītheir ŌüĀbases.
Reuters reported this week, as the United States and Israel pounded Iranian targets, that Iranian Kurdish ŌĆŗmilitias have ŌĆŗconsulted ŌüĀwith the U.S. in recent days about whether, ŌĆŗand how, to attack ŌĆŗIran's ŌüĀsecurity forces in the western part of the country.

item.title: At least 101 people missing after submarine attack on Iranian ship off Sri Lanka, sources say
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A submarine attack on an Iranian ship off the coast of Sri Lanka has left at least ŌĆŗ101 people missing, one dead and 78 injured, sources ŌĆīin Sri Lanka's navy and defence ministry told Reuters on Wednesday.
It was unclear who attacked the ship, which the sources said has now sunk.
A ŌĆŗSri Lankan navy spokesman said the report of 101 people ŌĆŗbeing missing was not true, and that 32 people ŌüĀinjured in the incident had been rescued by Sri Lankan ŌĆŗnavy and were under treatment in hospital.
Sri Lanka's foreign minister told ŌĆŗparliament earlier in the day that the navy had responded to a distress call from the ship and launched rescue operations at 6:00 a.m. (1230 GMT).
"What ŌĆŗwe know at this point is that 79 people were ŌĆŗrescued and brought to the hospital and one of them was seriously injured. ŌĆīAnother ŌüĀ101 are believed to be missing and the vessel has sunk," a Sri Lankan navy source, who declined to be named as he was not authorised to speak to the media, said.
The ŌĆŗsources said one ŌĆŗof the people ŌüĀbrought to the hospital had died.
Foreign minister Vijitha Herath did not give further details in parliament ŌĆŗbut said the military had rescued at least ŌĆŗ30 people ŌüĀwho were on board and said Sri Lanka would take appropriate action.
Local media reported the ship had signalled it was in distress ŌüĀoff ŌĆŗthe coast of Galle in southern Sri ŌĆŗLanka, and that the injured had been admitted to a hospital in the city.

item.title: Uncertainty faces China Inc's projects in Iran after USŌĆōIsraeli strikes
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 (Reuters) - Weeks before U.S.ŌĆōIsraeli strikes in Iran, wide swathes of state-backed Chinese companies were eyeing projects in areas from energy and heavy industry to trade promotion, data showed, spotlighting ŌĆŗthe risks the war presents for business in China.
One of Iran's closest allies and the biggest buyer of its oil, China has so far kept ŌĆīsilent about the business and trade impact of the attacks, though it condemned the U.S.-led operation as "unacceptable" and called for restraint.
Chinese government procurement and tender records on Iran show contracts issued in recent months, pointing to the vibrant commercial engagement of the two nations, though the total size of China's investment in the projects was not immediately clear.
But the inclusion of many state-backed firms suggests efforts by the world's second largest economy ŌĆŗto boost ties with Iran could transmit the business impact of the deepening Middle East crisis beyond just the private sector .
Beijing has long backed U.S.-sanctioned Tehran in ŌĆŗefforts to deepen its strategic and economic heft in the Middle East, signing a 25-year cooperation deal in 2021, though full details were never ŌüĀdisclosed.
China and Iran have drawn closer politically through participation in regional blocs, but economic cooperation has stagnated in recent years in the absence of big benefits to China in ŌĆŗnon-oil trade, an adviser to the foreign ministry said.
"China viewed the Iran protests as a cautionary tale, due to Tehran's domestic economic mismanagement, governance failures and corruption," added the expert, who ŌĆŗspoke on condition of anonymity.
None of the entities identified in the documents responded to requests for comment on the status of projects and whether recent events had affected operations.
The Chinese records show contracts, some issued or active in January and February, in areas such as steel fabrication, transmission-level grid equipment, hydropower commissioning, overland freight corridors and Tehran trade exhibitions.
Shanghai Baoye, a major engineering and ŌĆŗconstruction contractor within state-owned China Metallurgical Group Corp, issued a tender last month for structural steel supplies for a project based in Iran, they showed.
It also awarded a subcontract worth ŌĆŗ7.7 million yuan ($1.1 million) to a local firm for project-related equipment.
Pinggao Electric, widely associated with China's State Grid network, published a procurement result last month that referred to an Iran project involving a ŌĆīmobile substation, ŌüĀaccording to documents reviewed by Reuters.
Iran is also embedded in China's Belt and Road Initiative, a multi-trillion-dollar infrastructure scheme intended to stretch from East Asia to Europe.
In December, China Railway Container Transport, a state-owned subsidiary of China State Railway Group, published a shortlist of overseas service providers for westbound Central Asia routes, including Iran.
Filings reviewed by Reuters showed a two-way flow of Chinese equipment and engineering services into Iran, while Iranian raw materials and petrochemicals run back to Chinese industry.
Steelmaker Henan Fengbao Special Steel published notices in August 2025 for the purchase ŌĆŗof iron ore pellets labelled as being ŌĆŗof Iranian origin.
In petrochemicals, Jiangsu Sopo Group ŌüĀissued procurement notices last year for polyethylene specified as "Iran Petrochemical LFI 2119," with delivery to its plant in Zhenjiang.
The same resin appeared in multiple inquiries, suggesting repeat sourcing.
Chinese provincial authorities were also actively organising market access to Iran.
Procurement records from the ŌĆŗcommerce department of the eastern province of Zhejiang show contracts for organisational services tied to Tehran trade exhibitions across pharmaceuticals, electronic ŌĆŗcomponents and automotive parts.
Zhejiang ŌüĀis one of China's most export-oriented provinces, home to thousands of private manufacturers in machinery, electronics and automotive supply chains.
A February open tender sought contractors for an international exhibition of auto parts to be held in Iran later this year, with a bid deadline of March 2.
In northwestern Shaanxi and northeastern Heilongjiang, provincial commerce departments and state-linked oilfield equipment firms similarly announced participation in ŌüĀIran's international oil ŌĆŗand gas exhibitions.
Still there may yet be a silver lining for China.
"The crisis will certainly damage all ŌĆŗforeign direct investment into Iran, not just China's," said Michael Feller, chief strategist at consultancy Geopolitical Strategy.
"The advantage for China, however, is that should the war end, its firms will have the pick of reconstruction contracts or ŌĆŗa better risk appetite than Western firms if there is a US-backed regime put in charge."

item.title: Cyprus calls off alert after┬Ā suspect object spotted close to Lebanon
item.description:
NICOSIA, March 4 (Reuters) - Cyprus said a suspect object had been detected ŌĆŗclose to Lebanese airspace on Wednesday, ŌĆīand a government source said two Greek F-16 fighter jets had been scrambled to intercept it.
The alert was ŌĆŗcalled off more than an hour ŌĆŗlater.
"The incident for which there was relevant ŌüĀinformation has been investigated and is considered ŌĆŗclosed. The competent authorities are monitoring the situation ŌĆŗon an ongoing basis and all appropriate measures are being taken by the available means within the framework ŌĆŗof established procedures," government spokesperson Konstantinos Letymbiotis ŌĆŗsaid in a post on X.
No further details were immediately ŌĆīavailable.
Two ŌüĀaircraft that were preparing to land at Cyprus' Larnaca airport were diverted while the jets were scrambled, the government source said.
The incident followed ŌĆŗa strike by ŌĆŗan Iranian-made ŌüĀdrone on a British Royal Air Force base in Akrotiri in ŌĆŗCyprus on Monday as the U.S. ŌĆŗand ŌüĀIsraeli air war against Iran continued. Two other drones were intercepted by Cyprus later that day.
Cypriot officials ŌüĀsaid ŌĆŗthey believed the drones on ŌĆŗMonday were fired by Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon.

item.title: Discussions with Board of Peace 'on hold' due to Iran war, Indonesia says
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 4 (Reuters) - Indonesia's foreign minister said talks on U.S. President Donald Trump's Gaza " Board of Peace ," of which the Southeast Asian nation is a key troop-contributing ŌĆŗmember, were on hold due to the Middle East war.
The U.S. ŌĆīand Israeli air war against Iran has killed scores of civilians, thrown global air transport into chaos and sent oil prices surging after the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
"All BoP discussions are ŌĆŗon hold as all attention has shifted to the situation in Iran," Minister ŌĆŗSugiono, who goes by one name, said late on Tuesday in ŌüĀresponse to a question on calls for Indonesia to exit the peace board ŌĆŗin the aftermath of the fresh conflict in the Middle East.
"We will also consult ŌĆŗwith our friends and colleagues in the Gulf because they are also under attack," Sugiono told reporters after attending an event alongside President Prabowo Subianto.
Indonesia's participation on the board has drawn criticism from ŌĆŗexperts and Muslim groups at home, who say it compromises the worldŌĆÖs largest ŌĆŗMuslim-majority nationŌĆÖs longstanding support for the Palestinian cause.
Indonesia backs a two-state solution.
The Indonesian Ulema Council, a ŌĆīleading clerical ŌüĀbody, said on March 1 that Indonesia should leave the board, citing Trump's attack on Iran as rendering the initiative ineffective.
Meanwhile, Nahdlatul Ulama, Indonesia's largest Muslim organisation, said Jakarta should use its position to press Israel and the United States to halt ŌĆŗthe violence.
Trump first ŌĆŗproposed the board in ŌüĀSeptember when he unveiled a plan to end IsraelŌĆÖs war in Gaza, later expanding its remit to address other global conflicts ŌĆŗtypically handled by the United Nations.
Sugiono also said Prabowo is ŌĆŗwilling to ŌüĀbe a mediator in the Iran war in a bid "to cool down and de-escalate the situation in the region."
Indonesia is readying 1,000 troops for potential deployment in Gaza by early ŌüĀApril as ŌĆŗpart of a proposed multinational peacekeeping force, its ŌĆŗarmy said, as part of the UN-mandated International Stabilization Force. It has also been given the deputy commander ŌĆŗrole of the force.

item.title: Saudi Aramco's Ras Tanura struck again by unknown projectile, four sources say
item.description:
RIYADH, March 4 (Reuters) - Saudi Aramco's Ras Tanura, which houses its largest domestic ŌĆŗrefinery and a key crude export terminal, was struck again on ŌĆīWednesday by an unknown projectile, four sources said, two days after a source said a drone attack on the complex shut the refinery.
Initial indications show an attack ŌĆŗon the Ras Tanura refinery was carried out by a drone ŌĆŗand resulted in no damage, Saudi Arabia's defence ministry spokesman ŌüĀsaid, according to the state news agency.
The agency also cited an energy ŌĆŗministry source as saying that there was no disruption to supplies.
Saudi Arabia ŌĆŗand other regional Gulf oil producers, such as the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Iraq, have been unable to move oil through the Strait of Hormuz since the ŌĆŗU.S. and Israel launched attacks on Iran on Friday.
Hundreds of ships have anchored ŌĆŗon either side of the Strait as a precaution, and Iran has said it ŌĆīwould ŌüĀfire on any vessel that attempted to transit the shipping choke point.
Ras Tanura sits on Saudi Arabia's eastern coast on the Gulf. Aramco is attempting to reroute some of its crude exports to the Red Sea to ŌĆŗavoid the Strait, ŌĆŗsources have said.
Aramco ŌüĀdid not immediately respond to an emailed request for comment.
