Additionally, the dollar's share of international payments is about 50%. If you include intraŌĆæeuro zone payments in the calculation, that share rises to around 60%, according to a Federal Reserve study. Some 55% of international and foreign currency bank claims, and 60% of liabilities are ŌĆŗdenominated in dollars.
Looking at oil, it is estimated that as much as 20% of the world's crude trade is now priced in currencies other than ŌĆŗthe dollar, such as the ŌüĀeuro or Chinese yuan. Of course, that means around 80% is still denominated in dollars.
Eichengreen says he has long believed that a multi-polar global monetary and financial system would be good for the world, just as a more diverse ecosystem is good for the planet.
"But we're not yet at a point where other sources of global liquidity could step up and substitute for the dollar. So we are at a very delicate point in ŌüĀtime," Eichengreen ŌĆŗsays.
At a time when trade wars and real wars are flaring up, that seems something of an understatement.
(The opinions expressed here ŌĆŗare those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the ŌĆŗbiggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Hormuz reopening is not solely in TrumpŌĆÖs gift
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Donald Trump is waking up to the fact that cutting off the world's largest oil and gas shipping route has dire consequences. That's clear from his scramble to restart flows through the Strait of Hormuz ŌĆŗby offering insurance , opens new tab for tankers and dispatching U.S. Navy escorts. The idea evokes the late-1980s ŌĆ£Tanker WarŌĆØ, when Washington protected oil shipments during the Iran-Iraq ŌĆīWar. But this time a quick fix is unlikely.
Traders rely on the narrow channel at the end of the Arabian Gulf to transport a fifth of the worldŌĆÖs oil and gas. Just days after an audacious attack on Iran that effectively halted trade flows, Trump rushed to reassure them of U.S. help. Firstly, he is offering to underwrite risks that private insurers no longer ŌĆŗwant to bear via the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation (DFC). The entity is authorised to provide $1 billion in political risk insurance per entity, well ŌĆŗabove the value of a typical new tanker costing up to $120 million, per Evercore analysts. It currently has a total ŌüĀmaximum liability of $205 billion.
In theory, such coverage could coax reluctant tanker owners back into the Gulf. In practice, the opposite looks likely. Freight rates for very large ŌĆŗcrude carriers have nearly doubled since January, while many war-risk insurers are pulling back entirely. A government guarantee might cover financial losses, but it does little to counter ŌĆŗthreats of drones, sea mines and Iranian-led forces. A subsidiary of ChinaŌĆÖs state-owned COSCO Shipping, hours after the DFC announcement, suspended , opens new tab all new booking services for relevant routes.
Operational snags loom as well. The DFC's insurance product may still require lengthy approvals, Evercore reckons. Tanker operators, meanwhile, must decide now whether it is worth the danger.
And even if financial cover materialised quickly, military protection ŌĆŗmay be even trickier to pull off. During the 1980s Iran-Iraq war, the U.S. led escort operations but had a larger fleet and could present itself as ŌĆŗa semi-neutral guardian of shipping lanes. Today the U.S. Navy has fewer available assets, according to AXSMarine analysts, while Washington is a direct participant in the confrontation. That makes escorted ŌĆītankers more ŌüĀlikely to become targets.
The strategic backdrop is even less forgiving. Attacks on export infrastructure in the United Arab Emirates have shown even backup routes are vulnerable. Saudi Aramco is exploring alternatives through the Red Sea, but that route itself faced threats and harassments from Iran-aligned Houthi militants and has been effectively closed since 2024. In fact, the risk is so high that container shipowners stayed away even after Trump brought the Houthis to a peace deal last May and they have only recently ŌĆŗstated their intention to return.
If the chokepoint ŌĆŗstays disrupted, oil prices will rocket ŌüĀfurther. A full closure without any offsets could remove roughly 20 million barrels a day from global supply, Goldman Sachs analysts reckon. Apply GoldmanŌĆÖs rule of thumb that every million barrels per day of lost oil supply for a year adds $8 ŌĆŗto oil prices, based on data since 1998. If the Hormuz disruption lasts three months, that formula suggests crude ŌĆŗcould jump $40 per barrel. ŌüĀIn that scenario, $100 oil would become the new normal.
Follow Yawen Chen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran crisis jumbles the Gulf state pecking order
item.description:
LONDON, March 2 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The six countries of the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) have entered a new and disturbing world. Amid sustained Iranian rocket and drone attacks on most members of the group ŌĆō which includes Bahrain, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman ŌĆŗand the United Arab Emirates ŌĆō Western investors and tourists may now cool on a region that saw foreign direct investment more than quadruple to $83 ŌĆībillion in the decade to 2024. At the very least, a reshuffling of the pecking order in Saudi's favour seems possible.
With UAE airspace closed at the weekend, Saudi probably looks immediately more appealing to expats and foreign financiers, who have flocked en masse to the Gulf for years. Some companies have been driving staff across the border , opens new tab to Riyadh, where flights out of the region have remained in ŌĆŗoperation.
Global banks like JPMorgan, HSBC and Morgan Stanley arguably have financial reasons to move staff to the Saudi capital and away from the UAE, not least ŌĆŗthe promise of more advisory mandates from state-linked corporate giants like the Public Investment Fund. But bankers still find Dubai more ŌüĀlivable, partly because alcohol is available. Even though local defences have downed most of the 689 drones thrown at the UAE, the shocking spectacle of the Fairmont Hotel in ŌĆŗDubai catching fire on Saturday may change attitudes.
The bigger strategic issue worrying Western corporations with a big presence in the Gulf, according to a person familiar with one firm's ŌĆŗthinking, is what sort of Iran emerges next. A scenario referenced by U.S. President Donald Trump, in which Iranians rise up to overthrow the Islamic Republic and embrace democracy, is one possibility. But corporate leaders are well aware that an Iran that remains in the grip of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) could be an even greater threat than the status quo.
Thus far, GCC states have ŌĆŗlargely restricted themselves to a joint statement , opens new tab on Sunday, which highlighted their right to defend themselves. But the news on Monday that QatarEnergy had been forced by drone attacks ŌĆŗto halt production may now shift Gulf countries further towards a military response ŌĆō Qatar itself said later in the day it had shot down two Iranian aircraft. The risk of sustained conflict ŌĆīacross the ŌüĀregion might also further complicate the movement of fossil fuel shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, which carries a fifth of the world's daily oil consumption.
ItŌĆÖs hard to disentangle where this would leave the relative economic health of Saudi and the UAE, who are comfortably the GCCŌĆÖs biggest economies and who have of late been in a well-publicised spat . JPMorgan analysts on Monday cut estimates for 2026 UAE non-oil GDP growth by 0.5 percentage points, while only reducing SaudiŌĆÖs by 0.3 percentage points. Though the Emirati economy is starting ŌĆŗfrom a healthier fiscal position, Riyadh has more ŌĆŗscope to move oil exports by ŌüĀpipeline to avoid the disrupted Gulf. Equally, the energy sectors of both economies would benefit from higher prices .
The big picture, however, is that the UAE has outshone Saudi in attracting foreign money in the past few years, as reflected in an inflated property market. If ŌĆŗit continues to suffer Iranian attacks, that trend may not continue.
Follow George Hay on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Turkey has prepared plans for possible migrant flow from Iran, minister says
item.description:

Speaking in ŌüĀAnkara, Ciftci said three contingency plans had ŌĆŗbeen drawn up by authorities: managing any potential migrant ŌĆŗflow on the Iranian side of the border; creating buffer zones along the frontier if movement cannot be stopped; ŌĆŗand letting people into Turkey under controlled conditions.
He ŌĆŗadded that Turkey had prepared initial capacity to host up ŌĆīto ŌüĀ90,000 people in the event of a sudden inflow, including tent camps and temporary accommodation sites.
On Monday, a Reuters witness saw hundreds of Iranians crossing ŌĆŗthe border into ŌĆŗTurkey. Others ŌüĀwere reportedly waiting to cross. Ciftci said authorities had been informed that ŌĆŗIran was restricting its own citizens from ŌĆŗleaving ŌüĀthe country, while allowing Turkish nationals and third-country citizens to exit.
According to data provided by the ŌüĀminister, some ŌĆŗ5,010 people entered Turkey between ŌĆŗMarch 1 and 3, while 5,495 people exited.

item.title: New Zealand deploys two defence planes to the Middle East in preparation for evacuations

ŌĆ£The events in the Middle East are fast-moving, dangerous ŌĆŗand complex - and our primary focus is on ŌĆŗassisting New Zealanders in the region to the extent possible in ŌĆīthis ŌüĀvery challenging security environment,ŌĆØ Foreign Minister Winston Peters said in a statement.

item.title: Milei appoints chief Buenos Aires prosecutor as new justice minister

item.title: Exclusive: US carried out strike on Iranian warship off Sri Lanka coast, officials say

item.title: Top Vatican cardinal calls US-Israeli strikes 'truly alarming'
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 4 (Reuters) - The Vatican's top diplomat warned on Wednesday that the ongoing U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran undermine international law and ŌĆŗsaid nations do not have a right to launch "preventive wars", ŌĆīin an unusually direct criticism of the military campaign.
"If states were to be recognised as having a right to 'preventive war' ŌĆ” the entire world could risk going ŌĆŗup in flames," Cardinal Pietro Parolin, the Vatican's Secretary of ŌĆŗState, said in an interview with Vatican News.
Asked about ŌüĀthe U.S.-Israeli strikes, which have pressed on for the fifth day, ŌĆŗParolin said they had caused a "weakening of international law (that) is truly alarming".
"The ŌĆŗrule of force has replaced the force of law, with the conviction that peace can arise only after the enemy has been annihilated," the cardinal told ŌĆŗthe Vatican's media outlet.
It is unusual for Vatican diplomats to openly ŌĆŗcritique specific military campaigns. Vatican officials usually prefer to avoid press coverage and operate ŌĆībehind ŌüĀthe scenes, leaving open the possibility of the Church serving as mediator in conflicts.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said the strikes against Iran were needed to prevent the country from developing a nuclear weapon, ŌĆŗwhich Tehran denies ŌĆŗseeking, and to ŌüĀthwart its long-range ballistic missile program.
Parolin, the Vatican's lead diplomat since 2013 and a front-runner in the ŌĆŗ2025 conclave of cardinals that eventually elected Pope Leo, ŌĆŗis known ŌüĀas someone who is usually very cautious with words.
Leo did not address the ongoing conflict in his weekly audience on Wednesday with pilgrims ŌüĀin St. ŌĆŗPeter's Square.
The pope made an impassioned ŌĆŗappeal on Sunday for an end to the conflict. He called for a stop what ŌĆŗhe termed a "spiral of violence".

item.title: Chinese Communist Party elder Song Ping dies aged 109

BEIJING, March 4 - Chinese Communist Party elder and the longest-lived member of ŌĆŗthe elite Politburo Standing Committee, ŌĆīSong Ping, died on Wednesday at the age of 109, the official Xinhua ŌĆŗnews agency reported.

Born in 1917, Song was ŌĆŗat the core of the ŌĆŗcommunist party's second generation of leadership, an era when reform-minded Deng Xiaoping remade China ŌĆŗafter the 27-year rule of ŌĆŗMao Zedong.

item.title: Hungary foreign minister hopes Russia will free two ethnic Hungarian prisoners of war during talks
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 4 (Reuters) - Hungary's Foreign Minister Peter Szijjarto said on Wednesday that he hopes two ethnic Hungarian prisoners of war will be freed ŌĆŗby Russia during his talks in Moscow, where he is scheduled to meet Russian ŌĆīPresident Vladimir Putin later.
Szijjarto travelled to Moscow a day after Hungary's Prime Minister Viktor Orban held a phone call with Putin where they discussed the situation in the ŌĆŗMiddle East, Ukraine and the availability of crude oil and natural ŌĆŗgas for Hungary.
Orban's government has made Russia's war on ŌüĀUkraine a key topic in his campaign for the April 12 parliamentary election, ŌĆŗadding to tensions between Budapest and Kyiv.
Szijjarto said in a broadcast ŌĆŗon his Facebook page from Moscow that two ethnic Hungarian prisoners of war have recently asked Hungary for help.

ŌĆŗUkraine is home to around 150,000 ethnic Hungarians, most of them in the Transcarpathia ŌĆīregion. ŌüĀOrban's government and Kyiv have long clashed over the community's language rights.
Orban's government has also accused Kyiv of conscripting ethnic Hungarians, who Budapest said should not have been called up. Last Friday the foreign minister summoned Kyiv's ŌĆŗambassador to Budapest ŌĆŗto protest the ŌüĀconscription of two men.
Hungary's Orban has maintained warm relations with Moscow even after the start of the war ŌĆŗin Ukraine, and is not willing to give up ŌĆŗits purchases ŌüĀof Russian oil and gas, which has led to tensions with the European Union. Last month Hungary said that it will block the European Union's next ŌüĀpackage of ŌĆŗsanctions against Russia as well as a ŌĆŗ90 billion euro ($105 billion) EU loan for Ukraine to fund its defence against Russia until ŌĆŗshipments on the Druzhba pipeline resume.

item.title: US extends flight restrictions to Haiti capital until early September

WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S. Federal Aviation Administration said on ŌĆŗThursday it was extending its prohibition on U.S. ŌĆīflights landing in Haitian capital Port-au-Prince through September 3, citing risks from armed groups to civil aviation.
In November 2024, the FAA halted ŌĆŗall flights to Haiti after three U.S. ŌĆŗcommercial jetliners were struck by gunfire. It later ŌüĀallowed flights to resume to six other airports in ŌĆŗnorthern Haiti, but kept the ban on Port-au-Prince.
That restriction ŌĆŗhad been set to expire on Saturday, but the FAA said on Thursday it was extending the bar in response to ŌĆŗsecurity forces' continued inability to prevent attacks against aircraft ŌĆŗin Port-au-Prince and surrounding regions.
U.S. planes can transit over Port-au-Prince above ŌĆī10,000 ŌüĀfeet (3,048 m).
Armed gangs control much of the Haitian capital amid a political limbo, with no president in place or elections scheduled and a U.N.-backed expanded security force pending .
Since ŌĆŗSeptember 2025, ŌĆŗHaitian groups have ŌüĀused small-arms fire to attack at least three aircraft in the area where ŌĆŗU.S. civil aviation is prohibited, according to ŌĆŗthe FAA.
The ŌüĀFAA said an "expanded and shifted gang operating area raises the risk from small arms fire to civil aviation ŌüĀoperations ŌĆŗat lower altitudes."
The agency continues to ŌĆŗallow flights to six airports in Haiti: Port-de-Paix, Cap-Haitien, Pignon, Jeremie, ŌĆŗAntoine-Simon and Jacmel.

item.title: Russian LNG tanker sinks in Mediterranean, Moscow blames Ukrainian drones
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 4 (Reuters) - A Russian tanker carrying liquefied natural gas (LNG) sank in ŌĆīthe Mediterranean on Wednesday after what Moscow described as an attack by Ukrainian drones launched from Libya.
The Arctic Metagaz sank in waters between Libya and Malta after catching fire a ŌĆŗday earlier, Libya's maritime rescue agency said in an advisory seen ŌĆŗby Reuters.
"Information indicates that the tanker experienced sudden explosions followed ŌüĀby a massive fire, which ultimately led to its complete sinking," the ŌĆŗagency said.
Ukraine has frequently targeted Russian oil refineries and other energy infrastructure in ŌĆŗan attempt to deprive Russia's war machine of funding, but if confirmed, this would be its first attack on an LNG tanker.
The Security Service of Ukraine did not respond ŌĆŗto a Reuters request for comment.
Russia's transport ministry said the tanker, which ŌĆŗhad been carrying LNG from the Arctic port of Murmansk, was attacked by Ukrainian naval drones ŌĆīlaunched ŌüĀfrom the coast of Libya.
It said the 30 crew members, all Russian nationals, were safe, and thanked Maltese rescue services.
"We qualify what happened as an act of international terrorism and maritime piracy, a gross violation of the fundamental ŌĆŗnorms of international maritime ŌĆŗlaw," the ŌüĀministry said.
Libya's National Oil Corporation said it had no involvement with the tanker, adding that it was en route ŌĆŗto Port Said, Egypt.
The ship's Russia-based manager, LLC SMP Techmanagement, ŌĆŗand Russian ŌüĀLNG producer Novatek (NVTK.MM) , opens new tab have not commented on the incident.
The Libyan agency warned vessels to avoid the site and asked them to report any pollution in the ŌüĀarea ŌĆŗwhich one industry source said would likely be ŌĆŗlimited to the fuel oil on board.

item.title: 'Everyone cheered': stranded Gulf travellers gamble on their route home
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Stranded passengers in the Gulf states are waiting for one thing: a phone call confirming their flight home will depart.
Even then, few celebrate until the plane has taken off and cleared Emirati airspace.
"There was just ŌĆŗthis eerie feeling on the plane. Everyone was just dead quiet. No one really spoke," said Zoe Jackson, ŌĆīwho was on one of the first flights out of Dubai on Tuesday.
She said it was not until lunch that passengers finally began to relax, sensing the ordeal might be over.
Now safely home in Britain, Jackson said she received confirmation she could fly only hours before departure, when her hotel rang at 1 ŌĆŗa.m. to say she had to leave "now" if she wanted a seat.
Airports across the Gulf have begun operating at very ŌĆŗreduced capacity as airlines and governments hurry to bring tens of thousands of stranded citizens and residents home amid the escalating U.S.-Israeli conflict ŌüĀwith Iran .
Dubai, which normally handles more than 1,000 flights a day , and nearby Doha and Abu Dhabi sit at the crossroads of eastŌĆæwest ŌĆŗair travel, funnelling long-haul traffic between Europe and Asia through tightly scheduled connecting flights.
Many of the stranded passengers had expected to spend no ŌĆŗmore than a few hours on the ground and have been marooned - often without luggage - since Saturday.
Dozens of repatriation flights were due to leave Dubai on Wednesday.
Securing a seat remains uncertain.
In hotels, strangers swap updates on whether flights have been booked and the odds they will actually depart. In Facebook groups, travellers ŌĆŗdebate whether to attempt overland journeys rather than risk further cancellations at major hubs.
"The biggest decision is whether we stay put or try ŌĆŗto cross a land border," said Deirdre Amola, an American travel blogger stranded in Dubai. "Then it's: where should I even try to fly?"
James Gaskin was ŌĆīflying home ŌüĀto Britain from India on Saturday when he got stranded in Dubai. By Wednesday, he had got as far as Istanbul, with one final leg still to Manchester.
His journey has been far from smooth.
At Mumbai, on little sleep, Gaskin found he did not have an onward flight and had to corral two travel agents and use his corporate credit card to get a seat home.
Then, after finally boarding a ŌĆŗconnecting flight in Dubai, it was ŌĆŗdelayed for more than two ŌüĀhours. When the plane finally began taxiing, it was turned around and delayed again.
"When we got out of UAE airspace, everyone kind of cheered," Gaskin said, adding he felt guilty when so many people ŌĆŗwere still unable to get out.
Those stranded include Grzegorz Markiewicz ŌĆŗand his wife Malgorzata, ŌüĀa Polish couple, and one of their three daughters who got stuck in Doha, Qatar, on their way back from a wedding in Australia.
They have received no update on when their flight might depart.
Now they are considering a more than six-hour drive across the desert to Riyadh, Saudi ŌüĀArabia, where ŌĆŗsome fellow hotel guests have already headed in hopes of flying out.
"We are ŌĆŗwaiting to hear what they're going to say about the road, about safety on the road," Malgorzata said. "And then we will decide."

item.title: Hardline cleric Arafi joins wartime leadership as Iran juggles conflict, succession
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Ayatollah Alireza Arafi has emerged as a central figure in IranŌĆÖs power structure at a moment of unprecedented ŌĆŗturmoil , propelled into a leadership role from the ranks of top clerics after the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei .
Once known largely as a scholarŌĆæadministrator, ŌĆīthe hardline cleric is now one of three senior officials entrusted with steering the state through its most volatile period since the 1979 revolution.
Arafi was appointed to an interim three-member leadership council to manage state affairs alongside President Masoud Pezeshkian and Chief Justice Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei, following Khamenei's killing at the start of U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran on February 28.
The leadership's top priority, alongside responding to U.S.-Israeli attacks, is managing the leadership succession , a ŌĆŗprocess subject to extraordinary pressure at a time of war : If a supreme leader is not named quickly, the clerical establishment risks being seen as weak and unable ŌĆŗto guarantee continuity.
Arafi, in his late 60s, is lesser known abroad than other heavyweight clerics, but successive promotions by the fiercely anti-Western Khamenei ŌüĀhave shaped his career and given him moments in the spotlight. Some clerics have even viewed Arafi as a potential successor to Khamenei.
However, ŌĆŗfor Arafi to ascend to the role, he would need to clear several procedural hurdles. A committee within the 88-member Assembly of Experts ŌĆö the body tasked with selecting the next ŌĆŗleader ŌĆö would first have to put his name forward at a session attended by at least two-thirds of its members.
From there, he would still require the backing of two-thirds of those present ŌĆö roughly 40 senior clerics. There are no guarantees at any stage of the process.
Although he lacks hands-on political experience, Arafi, born in 1959, is widely seen as firmly aligned with Khamenei and his ideological vision. He has ŌĆŗconsistently argued that governance should be rooted in the comprehensive application of Shi'ite jurisprudence.
His clout is rooted chiefly in the powerful posts he has held: head of Al-Mustafa International ŌĆŗUniversity and dean of the seminary at the Iranian holy city of Qom. He is also a member of the Guardian Council, which approves all candidates for elected office, and a member of ŌĆīthe Assembly of ŌüĀExperts ŌĆö positions he secured with KhameneiŌĆÖs backing and trust.
His credentials as a heavyweight within Iran's religious establishment date back to his early years as an 11-year-old student of Islam when he moved to the city of Qom, the epicenter of Shi'ite theological scholarship.
Over the years Arafi, the son of an Ayatollah, completed advanced studies in jurisprudence and philosophy.
Arafi's career kicked off after Khamenei became supreme leader in 1989. He began his work in his hometown of Meybod as a Friday prayer leader at the young age of 33, one of the ŌĆŗfirst signs that he had won the ŌĆŗconfidence of Khamenei, who strategically promoted loyalists.
He would later fill that same role in Iran's holy Shi'ite city of Qom, gaining religious authority and political clout in Iran's clerical system.
Arafi also became chairman of Al-Mustafa International University , which has affiliated religious seminaries and Islamic colleges in over 50 ŌĆŗcountries and seeks to promote the Islamic Republic's ideology overseas.
With over 50,000 foreign students enrolled, according to Al-MustafaŌĆÖs Persian-language website, students ŌĆŗon its Iran campuses receive ŌüĀfree housing, home loans and health care for themselves and their families.
He stepped down from the post in 2018. Two years later, the U.S. State Department formally labeled the Quds Force a foreign terrorist organization, highlighting its recruitment networks.
It said the force had enlisted Pakistani and Afghan students from Al-Mustafa International University into the Zaynabiyoun Brigade and the Fatemiyoun Division ŌĆö militias deployed in ŌüĀSyria under the ŌĆŗcommand of the Revolutionary Guards' Quds Force and previously sanctioned under U.S. counterterrorism and human rights measures.
During ŌĆŗhis tenure, Arafi claimed that approximately 50 million people converted to Shi'ite Islam within eight years through the institutionŌĆÖs outreach. Critics questioned this assertion.
In 2016, he was appointed head of Iran's national seminary system.
Arafi advanced his political ŌĆŗstanding three years later when he was handpicked by Khamenei to join the Guardian Council, a powerful election watchdog body that reviews legislation and qualifies candidates for elections.

item.title: Iranian government making case for demise with indiscriminate attacks, EU's Kallas says
item.description:
WARSAW, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran's government is making a strong case for ŌĆŗits demise by indiscriminately attacking its ŌĆīneighbours, EU's top diplomat Kaja Kallas told reporters on Wednesday.

Asked ŌĆŗabout the incident, Polish Foreign Minister Rados┼éaw Sikorski said: "Iran is broadening the war to countries ŌĆŗthat did not attack it ... there ŌĆŗis a well known saying it's worse than a ŌĆīcrime, ŌüĀit's a mistake."
Kallas said the war in the Middle East is diverting attention from Ukraine, but it was important ŌĆŗnot to ŌĆŗlet ŌüĀthe war in Ukraine recede into the background. She added ŌĆŗthat the conflict in Iran ŌĆŗhas ŌüĀcost Russia an ally, but cautioned that Russia could benefit from a spike ŌüĀin ŌĆŗoil prices stemming from ŌĆŗthe crisis.

item.title: Northern European nations agree to draw up joint evacuation plans in event of crisis or military conflict
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 4 (Reuters) - Ten nations across northern Europe have agreed to prepare for possible cross-border evacuations of civilians in the event of a ŌĆŗcrisis or military conflict in the region, in a bid ŌĆīto draw lessons from the war in Ukraine, Sweden said on Wednesday.
The 10 will jointly prepare plans covering transport, border controls, travel corridors and other matters.
Germany ŌĆŗand Poland, along with fellow NATO members Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, ŌĆŗSweden, Norway, Finland, Iceland and Denmark, have ramped up their ŌüĀplanning in recent years for a possible future armed conflict ŌĆŗwith Russia.
"Experience from Ukraine has shown that temporary movements of the population ŌĆŗenable the continued defence of the country while protecting civilians," Sweden's ministry of defence said in a statement announcing the northern European agreement.
Millions of people have fled ŌĆŗUkraine in the four years since Russia's full-scale invasion in February ŌĆŗ2022, most of them seeking refuge in other European nations while the conflict ŌĆīat ŌüĀhome continues.
Sweden said that in addition to transport and travel corridors, the planning for cross-border evacuations will include the reception and registration of people and the protection of vulnerable groups.
"The purpose of the agreement is ŌĆŗto improve the ŌĆŗprotection of the ŌüĀcivilian population in the event of major crises or, in the worst case, war," it said.
The Kremlin ŌĆŗhas repeatedly said that Russia does not want to invade ŌĆŗNATO ŌüĀcountries.
Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania last year made a similar agreement between themselves, drawing up contingency plans to deal with the possibility of hundreds of thousands ŌüĀof ŌĆŗpeople fleeing a Russian troop buildup or ŌĆŗattack.
Finland, which shares a 1,340 kilometre-long border with Russia, in 2024 signed a similar deal ŌĆŗwith Sweden.

item.title: Iran FM says strikes targeted US, not Qatar; Doha rejects claim
item.description:
DOHA, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi ŌĆŗtold his Qatari ŌĆīcounterpart Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman al-Thani that Iranian ŌĆŗmissile attacks were ŌĆŗdirected at U.S. interests ŌüĀand not at ŌĆŗQatar.
Qatar's top diplomat "categorically rejected" ŌĆŗthat claim and called for an immediate halt to ŌĆŗIranŌĆÖs attacks, the ŌĆŗQatari Foreign Ministry said on ŌĆīX.
Sheikh ŌüĀMohammed bin Abdulrahman said his country would confront any aggression ŌĆŗwith ŌĆŗits "right ŌüĀto self-defence," stressing that Doha has ŌĆŗalways been inclined ŌĆŗtowards ŌüĀdialogue and diplomacy conducted in good faith.

item.title: Russian gas tanker sinks near Libya after explosions, maritime authority says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Russian gas tanker Arctic Metagaz (IMO number 9243148) sank near ŌĆŗLibya's coast following explosions and ŌĆīa fire, Libya's maritime rescue agency said in an advisory on ŌĆŗWednesday seen by Reuters.
"A distress ŌĆŗcall was received on Tuesday ŌüĀevening, March 3, 2026, from ŌĆŗthe commercial tanker named Arctic ŌĆŗMetagaz. Information indicates that the tanker experienced sudden explosions followed by a massive ŌĆŗfire, which ultimately led ŌĆŗto its complete sinking", the agency said ŌĆīin ŌüĀthe note.
"The tanker has been confirmed to have completely sunk at the site of the ŌĆŗincident. The ŌĆŗwreckage ŌüĀlies in the maritime area between Libya and ŌĆŗMalta (within the Libyan search and ŌĆŗrescue ŌüĀzone) ...", the note said.
Russia's transport ministry said on Wednesday that Ukrainian ŌüĀnaval ŌĆŗdrones were responsible ŌĆŗfor on the Arctic Metagaz.

item.title: Mexico court orders army to hand in missing documents on students that disappeared
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 4 (Reuters) - A Mexican court ordered the army to hand over long-awaited documents that could advance the landmark investigation into the 2014 ŌĆŗdisappearance of 43 students in southern Guerrero state at the alleged ŌĆīhands of organized crime and local security forces.
The ruling, seen by Reuters on Wednesday, rejected the army's prior assertion that 853 missing pages of information generated by the CFRI, an ŌĆŗarmy intelligence agency, did not exist, and ordered that these must now ŌĆŗbe handed over.
The government has for over a decade promised to ŌüĀfind those responsible for one of the country's worst human rights atrocities, ŌĆŗbut no one has been convicted, though over 100 have been arrested and face ŌĆŗongoing prosecutions.
The case of the disappearance of the students from Ayotzinapa Rural Teachers College has been marred by missteps and interference. International probes have ruled that they were killed by ŌĆŗorganized crime members in cahoots with police.
The documents may not be considered classified ŌĆŗdue to the "overriding interest on the part of the victims' families, as well as society ŌĆīin ŌüĀgeneral, to know the truth," the ruling said.
The families' legal representative, the Agustin Pro Human Rights Centre, known as Centro Pro, hailed the order.
"This ruling recognizes the right to the truth of the families and of society as a whole, ŌĆŗunderlining the obligation ŌĆŗof SEDENA (defense ministry) ŌüĀand other military bodies to provide key information for the search for the students," it said in a statement.
Only the ŌĆŗremains of three of the 43 students have been positively ŌĆŗidentified. The ŌüĀlocations of the others remain unknown.
Mexico's defense ministry did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the ruling, dated February 19.
Asked about the case in a ŌüĀmorning press ŌĆŗconference on Wednesday, President Claudia Sheinbaum said she ŌĆŗhad no knowledge of the ruling but would meet with the victims' parents at the end ŌĆŗof this month.

item.title: Portuguese leader, in contrast with Spain, defends decision to let US use base in Iran campaign
item.description:
LISBON, March 4 (Reuters) - Portuguese Prime Minister Luis Montenegro defended his decision to let the United States use the Lajes ŌĆŗairbase on Portugal's Azores Islands during the Iran bombing campaign, a ŌĆīposition at odds with that of neighbour Spain which refused such requests.
A longstanding agreement with the United States allows Washington to use the base without prior ŌĆŗauthorisation during peacetime, but required Portugal to give approval once ŌĆŗhostilities began.
Montenegro told parliament on Wednesday that U.S. use of ŌüĀthe base was in compliance both with Portuguese law and ŌĆŗagreements with the United States. Authorisation was granted for defensive purposes, based on ŌĆŗnecessity and against military targets, following international law, he said.
"Portugal objectively has a close relationship with our ally, the United States," Montenegro told lawmakers.
"Iran repeatedly violated international norms ŌĆŗwith its nuclear ambitions and long-range missile capabilities," he said, describing ŌĆŗTehran as a state sponsor of international terrorism.
Portugal's stance starkly contrasted with that ŌĆīof ŌüĀSpain, which angered President Donald Trump by refusing permission to use its bases. Montenegro did not directly address the difference in policy between the neighbours but noted that Portugal was a founding member of the ŌĆŗNATO alliance in ŌĆŗ1949, whereas Spain ŌüĀjoined only in 1982.
He said the government believed diplomacy must be the priority, but since the start ŌĆŗof the conflict, Iran had attacked other countries that ŌĆŗhad ŌüĀnot previously engaged in any hostilities.
The government will also implement an "extraordinary and temporary" reduction of the tax on petroleum and energy products to offset ŌüĀany ŌĆŗfuel price increase above 0.10 euros ($0.12) of ŌĆŗthis week's price due to the conflict, he added.
($1 = 0.8595 euros)

item.title: Iran conflict may divert US weapons from Ukraine
item.description:
KYIV/WASHINGTON March 4 (Reuters) - With the United States focused on its conflict with Iran , Ukraine could face a critical shortage of U.S. air defence missiles at a time when Russia shows no signs of easing its campaign of striking Ukrainian cities.
Since the ŌĆŗU.S. and Israel began strikes on Iran on Saturday, Iran has fired hundreds of ballistic missiles and drones at Gulf countries. Most have been intercepted, including with the PAC-3 Patriot missile interceptors that Ukraine relies ŌĆīon to defend its energy and military infrastructure from ballistic missiles.
The roughly 600 PAC-3s produced yearly by Lockheed Martin are already insufficient to cover U.S. needs and those of its allies in the Gulf, let alone Ukraine's, said Serhii Kuzan, head of the Kyiv-based Ukrainian Security and Cooperation Center think tank.
"It's the very simple mathematics of war," Kuzan said, adding the Franco-Italian SAMP/T air-defence system - which has similar capabilities - had not ramped up production fast enough to offer an alternative.
Fabian Hoffmann, a doctoral research fellow at Oslo University who specialises in missiles, said stockpiling of Patriots ŌĆŗby Gulf states meant they were unlikely to run out completely, particularly as the intensity of Iranian missile bombardments appeared to be decreasing, but they might need to become more selective in their use as time ŌĆŗgoes on.
A wider shortage could be avoided if the U.S. and Israel are successful at destroying Iran's missile stockpiles and launchers in the coming days, according to Mykola Bielieskov of ŌüĀthe state-run National Institute for Strategic Studies in Kyiv.
Russia, which has invested heavily in military production, has fired more than 700 missiles during this year's winter campaign against Ukraine's energy infrastructure, Kyiv says. Last month, it launched 32 ballistic missiles ŌĆŗin a single night.
The vast majority of Patriots supplied to Ukraine are provided by European nations under the Prioritised Ukraine Requirements List (PURL), a NATO-led initiative launched last year to purchase U.S.-made weapons for Ukraine.
Ukraine's allies have committed to send 37 PAC-3 missiles ŌĆŗsince their last meeting in mid-February, a source with knowledge of the matter told Reuters. Italy has ruled out taking away from Kyiv's air defences to support the Gulf states, according to a separate source with knowledge of the matter.
But there is concern that, if the Iran war drags on, delays to supplies under PURL could worsen as the U.S. depletes its own stockpiles, two European diplomats said.
A senior U.S. defense official confirmed production-related delays in supplies to PURL in the past, and said the logjams could get worse if the war against Iran ŌĆŗbecomes protracted. "We can only crank out so much at a time," the official said.
The U.S. can assert its right to preempt deliveries to other countries at any time, for any reason.
Although Lockheed Martin is scaling up production of PAC-3s to 2,000 ŌĆŗannually under a deal announced in January, that will come too late to address any shortages this year.
The Pentagon did not respond to a request for comment on weapons supplies to Ukraine.
Zelenskiy said on Monday that ŌĆīa prolonged and ŌüĀintense war in Iran could reduce the air defence systems available to Ukraine and that Russia was preparing a new wave of attacks on infrastructure, logistics and water supplies.
He has been an outspoken supporter of the U.S. attack on Iran, striking a contrast with Moscow, which has condemned the attacks on its ally.
U.S. President Donald Trump, who has pressured Ukraine for a quick peace deal that could involve ceding land to Russia, said on Tuesday that ending the Ukraine war remained high on his priority list.
But a new round of U.S.-mediated peace talks will not be taking place in Abu Dhabi on Wednesday and Thursday as expected due to Iran's attacks on Gulf states in response to the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, and no new location has been ŌĆŗannounced.
If talks do continue, Russia could try to convince a ŌĆŗdistracted U.S. to further pressure Ukraine into accepting bad terms, ŌüĀsaid Yevhen Mahda of the Institute of World Policy in Kyiv.
Ukraine has ruled out giving up land and has retaken territory in recent weeks, gaining more in February than it lost for the first time since 2023 , opens new tab , according to Black Bird Group, a Finland-based security and intelligence analysis team.
Spring temperatures may also bring relief to Ukrainians whose power supplies have been depleted by ŌĆŗRussian drones and missiles, while mud may slow any advances on the battlefield.
But Emil Kastehelmi of Black Bird Group said that, if there was a severe decline in Ukraine's ŌĆŗair defences, it would force the ŌüĀmilitary to make difficult decisions about what to defend.
"Ukraine has to be able to protect not just energy infrastructure, but its industry and its military bases," he said.
Hoffmann said Ukraine needed to develop its ability to strike at Moscow's missile production facilities deep inside Russian territory with its own missiles.
"Missile defense is supposed to be a temporary measure until you can degrade the adversary's offensive capabilities," he said. Ukraine and its allies need to invest in its missile capabilities because the payload on Ukraine's long-range drones was ŌüĀtoo small to ŌĆŗinflict significant damage, he said.
Trump's suggestion in October that the U.S. could supply long-range Tomahawk cruise missiles to Ukraine never materialized, following warnings from ŌĆŗMoscow that this would seriously harm relations.
Ukraine said last month it carried out a strike using its domestically produced FP-5 Flamingo cruise missile on a Russian missile factory - the Votkinsk Machine Building Plant in the remote Udmurtia region, around 1,400 km (870 miles) from the Ukrainian border.
"In the end, Ukraine is going ŌĆŗto have to invest in offensive capabilities," Hoffmann said. "This is the only way."

item.title: White House: Spain has agreed to cooperate with US military

item.title: Blackout hits most of Cuba amid US oil chokehold
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A power outage struck most of Cuba, including the capital of Havana, the country's national electricity union ŌĆŗUNE said on Wednesday, as the island's communist-run ŌĆīgovernment faces increasing pressure from the Trump administration.
Cuba has experienced a series of major blackouts in recent years, even before ŌĆŗthe U.S. cut off oil shipments to the ŌĆŗCaribbean's largest island.
Cuba's government has attributed its ŌüĀeconomic crisis to decades of economic sanctions from the ŌĆŗU.S.
A more recent scarcity of oil from Venezuela and ŌĆŗMexico due to U.S. pressure has worsened existing shortages.
The UNE electricity union said it was working to restore services, and ŌĆŗthat the blackout affected the island from the central ŌĆŗprovince of Camaguey to Pinar del Rio in the far west.
The ŌĆīFelton ŌüĀ1 thermoelectric power plant, located in Holguin province in eastern Cuba, remains online and recovery protocols have been activated, Cuba's energy ministry said.

item.title: Trump weighing US role in Iran after conflict, White House says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump is discussing with his advisors what role the U.S could have in Iran after ŌĆŗthe military campaign while U.S. intelligence is monitoring reports that Mojtaba Khamenei, the ŌĆīson of Iran's slain supreme leader, has emerged as a frontrunner to succeed him, the White House said on Wednesday.
"We've seen those reports as well, of course, and this is something that our intelligence agencies ŌĆŗin looking at. We The truth is, we'll have to wait and see," ŌĆŗpress secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters.
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba has emerged as the frontrunner ŌüĀto succeed his late father as Iran's supreme leader after years spent forging close ŌĆŗties with the elite Revolutionary Guards and building influence in the clerical establishment.
Leavitt added that Trump ŌĆŗwas actively considering and discussing with his national security team what role Washington may have in Iran's future once the operation is over but the primary focus at the moment was the success of the ŌĆŗmilitary operation.
Leavitt also defended the U.S. goals of the joint Israeli-U.S. air war against Iran amid criticism ŌĆŗthat Washington has failed to provide evidence for the imminent threat that Tehran posed directly for the ŌĆīUnited States, ŌüĀwhile adding that Trump believed that the American people supported the war.
"This decision to launch this operation is based on a cumulative effect of various direct threats that Iran posed to the United States of America," Leavitt said.
"Again, this is a rogue terrorist regime that has ŌĆŗbeen threatening the United ŌĆŗStates, our allies ŌüĀand our people for 47 years and the American people are smart enough to know that," Leavitt said.
Trump has rejected suggestions that Israel ŌĆŗpushed the U.S. into the conflict, as his administration gave varying accounts ŌĆŗand faced ŌüĀcriticism from some supporters and Democrats who accused him of launching a "war of choice."
A Reuters/Ipsos poll released earlier this week showed that only one in four Americans approves of U.S. strikes on ŌüĀIran that ŌĆŗhave plunged the Middle East into chaos, while about ŌĆŗhalf ŌĆö including one in four Republicans ŌĆö believe Trump is too willing to use military force.

item.title: US Interior Secretary meets with Rodriguez, mining companies in Venezuela visit
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum met with ŌĆŗVenezuelan acting President Delcy Rodriguez on Wednesday afternoon after arriving in the South American country earlier in the ŌĆīday, in an encounter filmed briefly by media outlets.
The two met with the U.S. representative in Venezuela, Laura Dogu, in Caracas' Miraflores presidential palace. Burgum met earlier with executives from foreign mining companies, two sources said, as the interim Venezuelan government weighs changes to mineral regulations.
The visit is part of ŌĆŗU.S. efforts to open Venezuela to American investment, especially in oil, gas and minerals, as the Trump administration tries to exert ŌĆŗmore control over the country following a January U.S. raid that captured President Nicolas Maduro. It is the ŌüĀsecond visit by a U.S. cabinet secretary since the ouster of Maduro, who courted the likes of China and Russia as ŌĆŗallies.
In public, Trump has heaped praise on Rodriguez for cooperating with the U.S. and hailed Venezuela as ŌĆ£our new friend and partnerŌĆØ in ŌĆŗhis annual State of the Union address. Behind the scenes, the Trump administration has been applying pressure, building a legal case against Rodriguez that could include corruption and money laundering charges, Reuters reported Tuesday, citing four people familiar with the matter.
Burgum, who also heads the U.S. Energy Dominance Council, which focuses ŌĆŗon boosting U.S. energy production, is in Venezuela to discuss supply chains for critical minerals, the Venezuela Affairs Unit, as the U.S. ŌĆŗdiplomatic mission in Venezuela is known, said in a post on X.
Venezuela's national assembly is preparing a reform of the country's main ŌĆīmining law, ŌüĀincluding provisions that would allow foreign companies to exploit gold, diamonds and rare earths, Rodriguez's brother Jorge Rodriguez, the assembly's head, said this week. Venezuela's current mining legislation dates to 1999.
The South American country owes billions of dollars to industrial conglomerates, oil and mining companies after deep waves of nationalizations two decades ago, including to Crystallex, Gold Reserve and Rusoro Mining (RML.V) , opens new tab .
Burgum is paying attention to the ŌĆŗmining law proposal, the sources told ŌĆŗReuters, and met on Wednesday ŌüĀmorning with several mining companies. He is expected to meet with oil and gas companies on Thursday to discuss expansion and investment, the sources added. U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright visited last ŌĆŗmonth.
Exploration has not yet taken place in Venezuela to confirm reserves of rare earths, a grouping ŌĆŗof 17 minor ŌüĀmetals used to make magnets that turn power into motion. Rare earths are a subset of critical minerals, much of which is produced globally by China.
A report from the Venezuelan government in 2018 on mineral deposits used key mining industry terms like reserve and resource interchangeably, making ŌüĀit difficult ŌĆŗto ascertain what the true measurements are. An official map published in 2021 ŌĆŗshowed reserves of antimony, copper, nickel, coltan, molybdenum, magnesium, silver, zinc, titanium, tungsten and uranium, but did not list volumes.
As part of bilateral agreements, Iranian companies in past ŌĆŗyears have explored for mining resources in the country, but the work did not lead to investments.

item.title: White House says Trump using preventative skin treatment for neck rash, offers no other details

item.title: Iran denies Axios report that messages were sent to US
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - An Iranian official said the country ŌĆŗhad not sent any ŌĆīmessages to the United States, in response to an Axios ŌĆŗreport, Iran's Tasnim news ŌĆŗagency said early on Thursday.
"No ŌüĀmessage has been sent ŌĆŗfrom Iran to the ŌĆŗUS, nor will any response be given to US messages. Iran's ŌĆŗarmed forces have prepared ŌĆŗthemselves for a long war," the ŌĆīofficial, ŌüĀwho was not named, was quoted as saying by Tasnim.
On Wednesday, Axios reported the Iranians had ŌĆŗsent messages ŌĆŗto ŌüĀthe United States over the last few ŌĆŗdays but the U.S. ŌĆŗdid ŌüĀnot respond, citing an American official and a second ŌüĀsource.

item.title: Australia says more flights from Middle East expected, situation still volatile
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 5 (Reuters) - Australia said three more commercial flights were scheduled to depart the United Arab Emirates on Thursday as the first group of Australians stranded ŌĆŗin the Middle East returned home overnight following the outbreak of ŌĆīthe Iran conflict.
Commercial air traffic remained largely absent across much of the region, with major Gulf hubs - including Dubai, the world's busiest airport for international passengers - largely shut for a fifth ŌĆŗstraight day, in the biggest travel disruption since the COVID-19 pandemic.
Australian Foreign ŌĆŗMinister Penny Wong said she hoped the scheduled three flights ŌüĀwould proceed, but warned the situation remained volatile after NATO air defences destroyed an ŌĆŗIranian ballistic missile fired towards Turkey.
"This could move very quickly if there is ŌĆŗfurther military activity that makes it unsafe for people to fly, obviously then these flights will not proceed," Wong told ABC News on Thursday.
"This is a conflict zone and we're operating ŌĆŗin an environment which is highly unpredictable."
About 200 Australians arrived in an ŌĆŗEmirates flight from Dubai on Wednesday night, with emotional scenes at arrival gates as families embraced ŌĆīreturning ŌüĀtravellers, including children on a school trip, television footage showed.
Sam Brown, who had recently moved to Dubai with her husband and four-year-old daughter, told ABC News the family had been left "just totally shocked by the situation" but were relieved ŌĆŗto make it home.
"We ŌĆŗjust couldn't believe ŌüĀwe got on that flight. We're so grateful. We felt nervous, but in very safe hands."
According to airport information, ŌĆŗan Emirates flight to Sydney from Dubai is scheduled to ŌĆŗleave at ŌüĀ9 a.m. (2200 GMT Wednesday), with another flight to Melbourne set for 5 p.m. (0600 GMT). An Etihad Airways flight to Sydney from Abu Dhabi scheduled for Thursday has ŌüĀbeen delayed.
About ŌĆŗ115,000 Australians remain in the Middle East, with ŌĆŗ24,000 in the United Arab Emirates. Australian officials view commercial flights as the most viable option ŌĆŗto bring stranded travellers home.

item.title: Colorado Governor Polis signals he is open to clemency for Tina Peters
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Colorado Governor Jared Polis signaled on Tuesday he was willing to consider clemency for Tina Peters , a former state county clerk convicted of illegally tampering with voting machines as ŌĆŗshe pursued claims that the 2020 presidential election had been stolen.
Polis, citing the case ŌĆīof another public official who was given a lighter penalty, said clemency may be appropriate to avoid sentencing disparities, although Colorado's secretary of state said Peters had committed a more grave offence.
Polis has drawn attention to the ŌĆŗrelatively light sentence - probation and community service - handed down to State Senator Sonya Jaquez ŌĆŗLewis, who was convicted of attempting to influence a public official and other charges ŌüĀafter prosecutors said she forged letters from former aides.
"It is not lost on me that she ŌĆŗwas convicted of the exact same felony charge as Tina Peters ŌĆö attempting to influence a public ŌĆŗofficial ŌĆö and yet Tina Peters, as a non-violent first-time offender got a nine-year sentence," Polis, a Democrat, wrote on X. He said that justice needed to be applied evenly.
He extended the clemency application deadline until April 3, allowing ŌĆŗhim to review the case.
Colorado Secretary of State Jena Griswold, the state's top election official, said ŌĆŗon Wednesday that it was inaccurate, however, to suggest that the actions of Lewis and Peters had the ŌĆīsame impact.
"Peters ŌüĀorganized the breach of the election equipment, broke the public trust and attacked the very foundations of our democratic process," Griswold said in a statement.
Peters, a Republican, was an outspoken supporter of U.S. President Donald Trump 's unsubstantiated claims that he lost the 2020 U.S. presidential election because of widespread fraud.
She was indicted ŌĆŗin 2022 following an ŌĆŗelection security breach at ŌüĀher office that led to voting equipment passwords getting posted on a right-wing blog. She denied wrongdoing.
Peters, 70, was convicted in 2024 on seven charges, ŌĆŗincluding three counts of attempting to influence a public servant. She is ŌĆŗnot eligible ŌüĀfor release until November 2028.
Peter Ticktin, a lawyer for Peters, said he hopes the governor commutes the former county clerk's sentence this week, saying her nine-year sentence was "too harsh."
Trump has repeatedly called for Peters to ŌüĀbe ŌĆŗreleased from the La Vista Correctional Facility, a state prison ŌĆŗin Colorado, and has criticized Polis for not granting her clemency.
Trump pardoned Peters in December in a move that was considered ŌĆŗsymbolic since Peters is not in federal custody.

item.title: Trading Day: Respite, but for how long?
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. and European stock markets registered solid gains on Wednesday, on hopes that conflict in the Middle East might soon cool , while stabilization in oil and energy markets also helped lay the ground for a rebound in other beaten-down assets.
More on that below. In my column today , ŌĆŗI look at why geopolitical crises and the risk of a dollar liquidity shock are reminders that the transition away ŌĆīfrom a dollar-centric financial universe toward a more fractured, multi-polar world could be very rocky.
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* And ... breathe?
Call it profit-taking, position-squaring, tactical trading, ŌĆŗa technical bounce, or hope that the war may end soon. Either way, some of the outsized market moves sparked by the Middle East ŌĆŗturmoil stalled or reversed a bit on Wednesday, as investors took a figurative breather.
That meant oil, the dollar and volatility all fell, while Wall Street, European stocks, emerging ŌĆīFX and ŌüĀsilver rebounded strongly. Not only did Asia miss out, stocks there got slammed on Wednesday. Catch-up Thursday, perhaps?
* Underlying economic strength
Health warning - purchasing managers' index surveys for February pre-date the Middle East crisis. Still, figures on Wednesday showed that business activity around the world was humming along at a surprisingly solid clip last month.
Service sector PMIs show that U.S. activity surged to more than a 3-1/2-year high in February, Chinese activity was the strongest in three years, and European growth ŌĆŗpicked up pace too. This follows ŌĆŗfairly upbeat manufacturing PMIs too.
* ŌüĀWarsh nomination goes to the Senate
The ball to make Kevin Warsh the new Chair of the U.S. Federal Reserve is officially rolling, after President Donald Trump on Wednesday submitted his nomination of the former Fed governor to the Senate.
The confirmation ŌĆŗprocess won't be plain-sailing. Some Senators object to the administration's investigations into current chair Jerome Powell and Governor ŌĆŗLisa Cook, and ŌüĀothers say Warsh will be merely a puppet for Trump, further eroding the central bank's independence.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, ŌĆŗwhich, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Nepal goes to the polls; voters seek change after youth-led protests
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 5 (Reuters) - Nearly six months after a wave of unprecedented youth-led protests and the deaths of 77 people forced Nepal's then ŌĆŗprime minister to quit, people began voting on Thursday in a general election that will choose a new parliament in ŌĆīthe Himalayan nation.
Perched between China and India, the country of 30 million people has been plagued for decades by political instability , crippling a largely agrarian economy and worsening unemployment - structural issues compounded by rampant corruption.
The long-festering, deep-seated malaise erupted into street demonstrations last September, triggered by a social media ban, that brought thousands on the streets, leading ŌĆŗto clashes and fatalities that forced the resignation of Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli.
Oli, who leads the moderate Communist Party of ŌĆŗNepal (Unified Marxist-Leninist, UML), is once again in the fray on Thursday, along with more than 3,400 other candidates ŌüĀfrom 65 parties.
They include the country's oldest party, the Nepali Congress led by 49-year-old Gagan Thapa, and the Nepali Communist Party (NCP) comprising former ŌĆŗMaoist insurgents who joined mainstream politics.
Together with UML, these parties have dominated Nepali politics for the last three decades, although the country has ŌĆŗseen 32 government changes in the past 35 years.
But the frontrunner for these polls is the three-year-old Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP), which has fielded the charismatic rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah as its prime ministerial candidate.
The 35-year-old former mayor of Nepal's capital Kathmandu is drawing large crowds, connecting with legions of young voters clamouring for change on ŌĆŗthe ground and online, even as he takes on Oli , 74, on his home turf along the Indian border.
"The election is critical to address ŌĆŗthe aspirations of the youths expressed during the Gen Z protests," said political analyst Puranjan Acharya.
"If the newly elected leaders are seen as unfit to do ŌĆīso, there ŌüĀis a risk of further trouble."
Some 19 million voters are eligible to cast their ballot for 275 members of parliament through a mixed electoral system ŌĆō 165 seats in direct first-past-the-post elections and 110 through proportional representation.
Voting started at 7 a.m. local time (0115 GMT) and closes at 5 p.m., with counting scheduled to start soon after, according to the country's election commission.
Early trends are likely to emerge by ŌĆŗFriday but complete results could take ŌĆŗa week or more as ŌüĀcounting of proportional representation votes would take time, election commission officials said.
"Voting is not just about sending someone to victory," Interim Prime Minister Sushila Karki, who took over after Oli, said in a public broadcast ŌĆŗthis week.
"It's a decision you make about your future and that of your children."
Promises of jobs, ŌĆŗreining in corruption and ŌüĀimproving governance - all demands raised during the September protests - have dominated much of the election campaign, which officially ended on Monday ahead of a two-day cooling-off period.
In Kathmandu, many candidates, wearing marigold garlands and vermillion smeared on their forehead walked the streets, rode on trucks and appeared from the ŌüĀsunroof of ŌĆŗcars, to woo voters one last time before the polls.
But some voters are still ŌĆŗon the fence.
"Old parties did nothing except corruption," said Ramkrishna Pandey, 50, discussing politics with half a dozen others at a newspaper kiosk on the outskirts of the ŌĆŗcapital.
"New ones don't seem any better. I have not decided who to support."

item.title: China to build 'birth-friendly society', refine social security system
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China said on Thursday that it would build a "childbirth-friendly society" in the next five years as it pledged to ŌĆŗaddress concerns over employment, education, medical care, health and income, according to ŌĆīan official government report.
Authorities will improve population services and respond proactively to population ageing, including "promoting high-quality, full employment, improving the income distribution system, and refining the social security system."
The announcement comes after China's ŌĆŗpopulation fell for a fourth consecutive year in 2025, as the birth rate ŌĆŗplunged to a record low, official data showed in January, with ŌüĀexperts warning of further decline.
China's population has been shrinking since 2022 and is ageing rapidly, ŌĆŗcomplicating Beijing's plan to boost domestic consumption and rein in debt.
New policies will be ŌĆŗintroduced to promote "high quality development of silver economy", targeted at those aged 60 and older, with elderly care services to be increased, particularly in rural areas, the report said.
Authorities will also draw ŌĆŗup measures to hone supportive policies designed for seniors including pension finance, wellness and ŌĆŗcare, it said.
By 2035, the number of people aged over 60 is set to hit 400 ŌĆīmillion - ŌüĀroughly equal to the populations of the United States and Italy combined - meaning hundreds of millions of people are set to leave the workforce at a time when pension budgets are already stretched .
China has already increased retirement ages, with men now expected to ŌĆŗwork until they are ŌĆŗ63 rather than ŌüĀ60, and women until they are 58 rather than 55.
China will foster "positive attitudes toward marriage and childbearing," the report said, adding ŌĆŗthat it would boost housing support for families with children.
Services for ŌĆŗwomen in ŌüĀearly stages of pregnancy as well as reproductive health would be improved while authorities aimed to better prevent and treat birth defects.
Authorities will also refine policies on free preschool ŌüĀeducation ŌĆŗand increase the supply of regular senior secondary school ŌĆŗplaces, with government spending on education mandated to be higher than 4% of GDP, the report said.

item.title: Who is contesting Nepal's polls and what is at stake?
item.description:
March 5 - Nepal votes in a general election on Thursday, its first since deadly youth-led anti-graft protests toppled the government ŌĆŗof then-Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli in September.
Here is a look at ŌĆīthe key contenders, and what is at stake.
Nearly 19 million of Nepal's 30 million people are eligible to vote in the for the 275-member assembly.
About one million of the voters - most ŌĆŗof them youth - were added after last year's protests , which killed 77 people ŌĆŗand injured more than 2,000.
While direct contests will decide 165 seats, ŌüĀwhich means the person who gets the most votes will win, the rest ŌĆŗwill be filled through proportional representation, where seats are allocated to parties in proportion ŌĆŗto their vote share.
Election authorities say 65 political parties are in the fray.
Apart from corruption, job creation is among the main issues, analysts say, with about a fifth of the population ŌĆŗliving in poverty, and high youth unemployment.
Ties with India and China, which border Nepal ŌĆŗand are among its major trade partners, will also be a factor in the election as the ŌĆīlandlocked ŌüĀnation negotiates a balance between the Asian powers.
While India accounts for two-thirds of Nepal's international trade, China accounts for 14% and has also lent the country - among the world's poorest - more than $130 million, according to the World Bank.
Rapper-turned-politician and former Kathmandu ŌĆŗmayor Balendra Shah, ŌĆŗ35, of the centrist ŌüĀRastriya Swatantra Party is among the frontrunners for prime minister.
Facing him in the Jhapa 5 constituency is four-time prime minister Oli , 74, of ŌĆŗthe Communist Party of Nepal (Unified MarxistŌĆōLeninist), vying for the top post again ŌĆŗbut facing ŌüĀan uphill battle to win back young voters who ousted him barely six months ago.
Other contenders include the centrist Nepali Congress party's 49-year-old Gagan Thapa and three-time prime ŌüĀminister Pushpa ŌĆŗKamal Dahal, 71, who now leads the Nepali Communist ŌĆŗParty.
Oli has been a liberal communist since the 1990s while Dahal led a bloody Maoist insurgency for a ŌĆŗdecade before joining mainstream politics in 2006.

item.title: Australia, Canada sign new deals on critical minerals
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 5 (Reuters) - Australia and Canada said on Thursday they had signed new agreements ŌĆīon critical minerals as Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney made a landmark address to the Australian parliament, a sign of the developing bond between the "middle powers".
Carney is on a multi-leg trip across the Asia-Pacific region also taking in Japan and India. His ŌĆŗstop in Australia included the first address to Australia's parliament by a Canadian leader since 2007.
"In ŌĆŗa world of great power rivalry, middle powers have a choice: compete for favour ŌüĀor combine for strength," he told lawmakers.
Introducing Carney in parliament, Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said his address ŌĆŗrepresented the closeness of the ties between the two nations.
"Australia and Canada are middle powers in a world ŌĆŗthat is changing. We cannot change it back, but we can back ourselves, back our citizens, and back each other," he said.
Albanese told a press conference that Australia would join Canada's G7 critical minerals production alliance.
"We have agreed to deepen ŌĆŗour relationship across several areas, building on our joint declaration of intent on critical minerals that we signed ŌĆŗlast year," he told a press conference.
The G7 alliance is a Canada-led initiative to diversify and secure global critical minerals ŌĆīproduction ŌüĀand supply.
Canada and Australia together produce about a third of global lithium and uranium, as well as more than 40% of global iron ore.
Western nations have been attempting to diversify their supply chains away from China, which still controls the majority of production and processing of critical minerals, essential for semiconductors and defence applications.
Canada believes ŌĆŗthat the best way ŌĆŗto address the issue ŌüĀof concentrated supply of critical minerals is through a production alliance or a buyers' club rather than just a price floor, Energy and Mining Minister Tim Hodgson ŌĆŗtold Reuters on Tuesday.
Australia has already allocated A$1.2 billion ($850 million) to build a ŌĆŗcritical minerals stockpile, ŌüĀbeginning with antimony, gallium and rare earths.
That will now be more closely aligned with Canada's defence stockpiling regime that has a similar aim, Albanese said.
"There's a lot Canada and Australia can do together on critical minerals as producer ŌüĀnations," ŌĆŗAustralian Resources Minister Madeleine King told Reuters ahead of Carney's visit.
Australia ŌĆŗand Canada will also deepen cooperation in areas including defence and maritime security, trade and artificial intelligence, the two leaders said.
($1 = 1.4122 Australian ŌĆŗdollars)

item.title: China's defence spending to rise 7% in 2026 vs 7.2% set for 2025
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China's 2026 defence spending will rise 7%, a copy of an official government ŌĆŗreport reviewed by Reuters showed on Thursday, slightly ŌĆīlower than the 2025 increase of 7.2% but roughly in line with rises over the last decade.
Regional military attaches and security analysts ŌĆŗare closely watching the budget as China's military ŌĆŗmodernisation effort continues apace and its deployments increase ŌüĀacross East Asia amid regional tensions.
The on-going rise in ŌĆŗspending also reflects President Xi Jinping's goal of completing ŌĆŗthat modernisation by 2035, with China's military developing new advanced missiles, ships, submarines and surveillance technologies.
The budget announcement comes amid an on-going ŌĆŗcorruption crackdown that was sparked by the highest-profile ŌĆŗpurge of upper military ranks in decades.
China's two most senior generals ŌĆīhave ŌüĀbecome ensnared in disciplinary investigations, with Zhang Youxia - a veteran military ally of Xi - placed under investigation in January and He Weidong expelled in October last year.
The International ŌĆŗInstitute of ŌĆŗStrategic Studies said ŌüĀin a report last month that rises in Chinese military spending were consistently ŌĆŗoutpacing the rest of Asia amid a global ŌĆŗsurge ŌüĀin defence budgets.
China's share of the total amount spent on the military in Asia grew to almost 44% in ŌüĀ2025, ŌĆŗup from an average of ŌĆŗ37% between 2010 and 2020, the report said.

item.title: China to support Hong Kong's integration with country's overall development
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China will ensure ŌĆŗHong Kong ŌĆīis more effectively governed in ŌĆŗaccordance with ŌĆŗthe law, in ŌüĀa copy ŌĆŗof an official ŌĆŗgovernment report reviewed by Reuters on ŌĆŗThursday.
The ŌĆŗreport also said China ŌĆīwould ŌüĀsupport Hong Kong and Macau in better ŌĆŗintegrating ŌĆŗand ŌüĀcontributing to the ŌĆŗcountry's overall ŌĆŗdevelopment.

item.title: Australia to join G7 critical minerals alliance, Canada's Carney says
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 5 (Reuters) - Australia and Canada on Thursday signed a series of new agreements on critical ŌĆŗminerals, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said, including Australia ŌĆījoining the G7 minerals alliance.
Western nations have been attempting to diversify their supply chains away from China, which still controls the majority of production and ŌĆŗprocessing of critical minerals, essential for semiconductors and defence applications.
"Earlier ŌĆŗtoday, we signed a series of new agreements on ŌüĀcritical minerals, including Australia joining the G7 minerals alliance ŌĆō the largest ŌĆŗgrouping of trusted democratic mineral reserves in the world," Carney said ŌĆŗin a speech to Australia's parliament, on his final day of a three-day visit to the country.
The two countries produce around a third of global ŌĆŗlithium and uranium, as well as over 40% of global iron ŌĆŗore.
Canada believes that the best way to address the issue of concentrated supply ŌĆīof ŌüĀcritical minerals is through a production alliance or a buyers' club rather than just a price floor, Energy and Mining Minister Tim Hodgson told Reuters on Tuesday.
Australia has already allocated funds to build ŌĆŗa critical minerals ŌĆŗstockpile, beginning ŌüĀwith antimony and gallium.
Carney is on a multi-leg trip across the Asia-Pacific region also taking in Japan ŌĆŗand India, with his stop in Australia aimed ŌĆŗat bolstering ŌüĀrelations between the two so-called "middle powers".
As well as critical minerals, Australia and Canada are also expected to deepen cooperation in areas including defence ŌüĀand ŌĆŗmaritime security, trade and artificial intelligence, ŌĆŗCarney's office said ahead of the visit.
(Reporting by Alasdair Pal in Sydney, editing by Jamie ŌĆŗFreed)
