item.title: Philippine resupply mission data leaked to Chinese intelligence, security official says
item.description:
MANILA, March 5 (Reuters) - A spokesman for the National Security Council (NSC) of the Philippines on Thursday said some information on the country's resupply missions in ŌĆŗthe South China Sea was compromised and passed on to Chinese intelligence agents.
His statement came a day ŌĆīafter the Philippine government said it apprehended some of its citizens on suspicion of spying for China in a "serious national security matter."
NSC spokesman Cornelio Valencia called the breach "alarming" but limited in scope, adding that the channels used to transmit the information had been shut down.
"Rotation and resupply data fall under operational ŌĆŗsecurity because disclosing it can endanger personnel, and that has been compromised," Valencia said.
Philippine and Chinese vessels have frequent ŌĆŗrun-ins during Manila's resupply missions to its occupied features in the South China Sea. And Chinese diplomats, ŌüĀPhilippine lawmakers, and a Coast Guard spokesperson have recently engaged in heated public exchanges over the disputed waterway.
Valencia said three Filipinos had ŌĆŗbeen apprehended and "there could be more." He declined to say whether charges would be filed.
Reuters spoke with the three accused people last month under ŌĆŗan agreement with security sources on the condition that their identities not be revealed.
One of the accused, who obtained information on deployments, resupply runs and personnel rotations through a connection with a Philippine Coast Guard staffer, said he passed the details to his contact using a phone issued to him.
The accused showed ŌĆŗReuters the phone, which contained a Tetris game that, when a code was entered, opened a hidden messaging platform that he said he ŌĆŗused to communicate with his handler.
"What I can say is that they're cooperating with our security services," Valencia said. "They have confessed and admitted to it. They ŌĆīdisclosed the ŌüĀdata that they were able to get and share, the assessments they made and how they were recruited."
China's Foreign Ministry rejected the Philippines' accusations, saying on Thursday: "We object to it, the case is not clear and there is no conclusive evidence."
Another accused person told Reuters that he was initially approached by a Filipina acquaintance when he was a junior staffer at the Department of National Defence with an offer ŌĆŗto write opinion articles in ŌĆŗreturn for money.
That later expanded to ŌüĀproviding information related to the South China Sea and the defence ministry's bilateral engagement with Philippine allies, including the U.S., he said.
He did not immediately realise he was working for Chinese agents and only became suspicious ŌĆŗlater, but it was hard to stop as he needed the money, he said, adding that ŌĆŗhe did this ŌüĀwork between 2023 and 2025.
The recruitment pattern mirrors methods seen in other foreign intelligence operations, where seemingly innocuous offers of consultancy or writing work are used as entry points before escalating into requests for sensitive material.
Last year, Reuters reported that networks linked to Chinese actors have used consulting ŌüĀfirms and ŌĆŗcovert job offers to lure targets, often exploiting financial vulnerabilities and gradually seeking ŌĆŗmore sensitive information, a pattern analysts say follows techniques used by previous Chinese intelligence operations.
Philippine lawmakers from both the ruling party and the opposition are seeking to overhaul ŌĆŗdecades-old espionage laws that would expand their traditionally wartime focus to cover peacetime and cyber-enabled threats.

item.title: Crude tanker reports suspected hull breach after blast near Iraq port

LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - The Bahamas flagged crude oil tanker Sonangol Namibe's hull ŌĆŗwas likely to have been breached after ŌĆībeing hit by a blast while anchored near Iraq's Khor al Zubair port, U.S. representative company Sonangol Marine Services said on ŌĆŗThursday.

The tanker had a contract with ŌĆŗIraq's state ŌĆŗoil marketer ŌüĀSOMO and was heading to an Iraqi terminal to load 80,000 METRIC ŌĆŗtons of Iraqi fuel, an Iraqi ŌĆŗenergy ŌüĀsource and a shipping agent said.

item.title: Exclusive: NATO allies will keep supporting Ukraine despite situation in Iran, Rutte says

BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO allies will keep supporting ŌĆŗUkraine despite the ŌĆīsituation in Iran, NATO Secretary General Mark ŌĆŗRutte told Reuters ŌĆŗon Thursday.

item.title: Exclusive: NATO's Rutte does not see need to invoke Art. 5 after missile incident
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - The shooting down of a ballistic missile headed for Turkey by NATO ŌĆŗair defences does not provide immediate reason to trigger ŌĆīthe alliance's Article 5 mutual defence clause, its Secretary General Mark Rutte told Reuters on Thursday.
"Nobody's talking about Article 5," Rutte ŌĆŗsaid. "The most important thing is that our adversaries have ŌĆŗseen yesterday that NATO is so strong and ŌüĀso vigilant, and even more vigilant, if possible, since ŌĆŗSaturday."
Turkey said NATO air defence s destroyed an Iranian ballistic missile as it headed into ŌĆŗTurkish airspace, marking the first time the alliance member has been drawn into the Middle East conflict and raising the possibility of an expansion ŌĆŗinvolving its bloc allies.
The Iranian Armed Forces General Staff ŌĆŗon Thursday denied it had fired missiles at Turkey, saying the Islamic ŌĆīRepublic ŌüĀrespected the sovereignty of "friendly" Turkey, according to a statement carried by Iranian media.
NATO's alliance's Article 5 specifies that an attack on one of the alliance's members is an attack on ŌĆŗall the ŌĆŗothers.
Rutte said NATO ŌüĀsupports the United States in its strikes against Iran as the country was "close to ŌĆŗbecoming a threat to Europe as well".
As ŌĆŗthe U.S.ŌĆōIran ŌüĀwar entered its sixth day , the conflict has widened beyond Gulf states and into Asia, convulsing global markets and prompting thousands ŌüĀof ŌĆŗstranded tourists and residents to try ŌĆŗto flee the Middle East.

item.title: For Mexico's security chief, El Mencho killing was personal
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 5 (Reuters) - MexicoŌĆÖs security chief, the man who helped lead the operation that killed the drug lord known as ŌĆ£El Mencho,ŌĆØ spends his days and nights inside fortified office buildings, including a one-bedroom apartment in the security ministry built for him.
His quarters ŌĆō inside a modern complex beside a busy thoroughfare ŌĆō include a bedroom, gym, kitchen and a conference room that seats 25. From the living room, guests can hear the crack of gunfire ŌĆŗfrom a firing range within the building complex, according to a high-ranking government official who has visited the apartment. A red telephone on his desk provides a direct line to the president.
Omar Garcia Harfuch, 44, has lived this way since 2020, when on his ŌĆīcommute to work a truck cut off his armored Suburban and gunmen disguised as road workers sprayed his vehicle with more than 400 bullets. Harfuch returned fire and survived with three gunshot wounds. Two of his bodyguards and a bystander were killed.
The security chief blamed the assassination attempt on Nemesio Oseguera, 59, better known as El Mencho, leader of the brutal Jalisco New Generation Cartel, one of MexicoŌĆÖs largest and bloodiest crime groups. Six years later, bringing down the cartel leader was a deeply personal moment for Harfuch, who friends say was devastated by his security guardsŌĆÖ deaths.
Harfuch declined to comment for this story. The account is based on interviews with a dozen friends, colleagues and security analysts.
Those close to Harfuch say that he's unlikely to ŌĆŗlet his guard down now that El Mencho is gone. But the death of the kingpin has lifted the profile of Mexican President Claudia SheinbaumŌĆÖs Minister of Security and Citizen Protection, whoŌĆÖs credited with spearheading her more muscular approach to fighting cartels, so much so heŌĆÖs considered an ŌĆŗearly frontrunner for the presidency when her six-year term ends in 2030.
ŌĆ£Omar Garcia Harfuch is the number one presidential candidate today,ŌĆØ said Armando Vargas, the top security expert at the think tank M├®xico Evalua. ŌĆ£He is the most visible leader ŌüĀof this new strategy.ŌĆØ
The approach is not without risks: El MenchoŌĆÖs death triggered a wave of violence across Mexico that killed 25 National Guard members and could fuel deadly feuds as rival cartel factions fight for control.
ItŌĆÖs also a marked departure from former President Andres Manuel Lopez ObradorŌĆÖs ŌĆ£hugs not bulletsŌĆØ philosophy, under which cartels ŌĆŗgrew in power and reach to control vast swaths of territory and diversified from drugs into extortion, human trafficking, and contraband fuel.
Harfuch rose to prominence within the Mexico City government when now-President Sheinbaum was the capitalŌĆÖs mayor.
Rodrigo Canales, who advised Sheinbaum on her security strategy, said Harfuch helped her navigate a difficult ŌĆŗperiod early in her mayoral tenure when high-level police officers were accused of corruption.
ŌĆ£He has ClaudiaŌĆÖs absolute trust and earned it by being extremely loyal and effective in key moments early in her mayorship,ŌĆØ Canales said.
Sheinbaum promoted Harfuch to head of the cityŌĆÖs police in 2019 after sacking his predecessor over a money laundering scandal.
Harfuch had been in the job for under a year when assassins made the attempt on his life. After initially returning fire, he scrambled into the back seats of his armored SUV and crouched down until reinforcements could arrive, he recalled in interviews after the attack. Twelve alleged members of the Jalisco cartel were arrested and given life sentences.
Following the ambush, he moved out of his house and into ŌĆŗMexico CityŌĆÖs police headquarters. His inner circle, already small, became even tighter. He sees his children in fleeting moments.
"He went from someone who could go to a restaurant, meet friends, attend a colleague's birthday party, to being guarded in an office, spending practically 90 percent of his life inside police buildings," said a friend ŌĆŗwho has known Harfuch for 20 years.
Like the cartel bosses he hunts, one misstep could cost him his life.
Harfuch comes from a line of Mexican top brass.
His grandfather, Marcelino Garcia Barragan, was defense minister in the 1960s while his father, Javier Garcia Paniagua, was a senator and presidential contender who led a federal security agency ŌĆīin the 1970s.
That mix ŌüĀof police and military heritage is rare in Mexico and puts Harfuch in a unique position to lead the countryŌĆÖs heavily militarized public security structure, two sources whoŌĆÖve worked with him say.
ŌĆ£Garcia Harfuch was sort of destined to follow in his father and grandfatherŌĆÖs footsteps,ŌĆØ said Gladys McCormick, a professor and historian of U.S.-Mexican relations at Syracuse University.
But that same legacy is regarded with suspicion in parts of the ruling leftist Morena party. Both his grandfather and father oversaw periods of military abuses and repression of social movements by security forces.
Critics also highlight HarfuchŌĆÖs links to the infamous disappearance of 43 students from the Ayotzinapa teaching college in 2014. A 2022 truth commission report cited himŌĆöthen a mid-level federal police officerŌĆöas having attended meetings where officials crafted a version of events that masked the role of security forces in the disappearances.
Harfuch, who was never accused of wrongdoing, has previously said he only attended meetings to help coordinate the search for the missing students. No local or ŌĆŗfederal officials have been sentenced in the case.
For the United States, Harfuch has ŌĆŗemerged as the linchpin in security collaboration with Mexico at a ŌüĀtime when U.S. President Donald Trump is piling pressure on his southern neighbor to aggressively combat cartels, and threatening U.S. military force if Mexico canŌĆÖt show results.
Derek Maltz, former acting administrator of the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration, said he met Harfuch last year shortly after Mexico transferred 29 suspected high-level cartel members to the U.S. in what was then the largest such handover in history.
ŌĆ£He looked me in the eyes, and he said, ŌĆśThis is only the beginning,ŌĆØ Maltz recalled.
Maltz had his doubts, ŌĆŗbut in the coming months Mexico handed over another 63 suspected cartel leaders and took down the countryŌĆÖs most-wanted drug lord. ŌĆ£I'm very impressed with what I'm seeing,ŌĆØ he said.
The prisoner transfers underscore what officials from both countries ŌĆŗsay is an unprecedented level of cooperation and ŌüĀintelligence sharing as they seek to dismantle cartels through military confrontations, money laundering investigations, and drug and firearm seizures.
The hunt for El Mencho gained urgency in November, when the Jalisco cartel kidnapped two of HarfuchŌĆÖs investigators in the city of Zapopan, a cartel stronghold, according to the high-ranking Mexican official.
Soldiers raided houses of suspected cartel members and interrogations produced information that helped tighten the net around El Mencho. Reuters is the first outlet to report the role of the kidnappings in the search for El Mencho. The agents were released after a week.
The breakthrough came when authorities tracked one of El MenchoŌĆÖs multiple girlfriends to his villa, MexicoŌĆÖs Defense Minister Ricardo Trevilla has ŌüĀsaid. A new U.S.-military-led ŌĆŗtask force confirmed the exact location of the house, Reuters reported.
But the government official said the real slip wasnŌĆÖt the romance; it was that the 59-year-old cartel leader wanted to see his ŌĆŗtwo children with the woman. Mexican troops swooped in after the girlfriend and El MenchoŌĆÖs children left.
After a shootout, El Mencho died on a military helicopter en route to a hospital. Eight of his bodyguards were also killed. Two soldiers died during the raid; another two died later from injuries.
Harfuch received a confirmation text with an image of El MenchoŌĆÖs body, still clad in a flak ŌĆŗjacket, the Mexican official said.
ŌĆ£I spoke with him Sunday morning after El Mencho was killed,ŌĆØ said Eduardo Clark, a top health official in Mexico who is close to Harfuch. ŌĆ£He told me, ŌĆśthis is a huge relief.ŌĆÖŌĆØ

item.title: Gulf food strategy tested as Iran war snarls shipping routes
item.description:
DUBAI, March 5 (Reuters) - Wealthy Gulf states are facing their biggest food security challenge since the 2008 global food crisis, as the Iran conflict threatens ports and disrupts shipping through the Strait of Hormuz , opens new tab .
The war is testing strategies adopted after ŌĆŗ2008, when soaring food prices prompted Gulf nations to shift toward import-dependent policies reliant on pouring cash into agricultural investments abroad.
That strategy did away with prior expensive programmes that sought to ŌĆīraise domestic production of strategic grains but ran up against the region's brutal climate and lack of water. Saudi Arabia, for example, began to scale back a domestic wheat-growing programme in 2008 to become almost exclusively reliant on imports.
Now with global shipping disrupted and airspace closed in many countries in a region that is 80%-90% dependent on food imports, price surges and scarcity of some goods are expected.
"With over 70% of GCC foodstuffs being imported through the Strait of Hormuz, Gulf states face shortages if the war persists," said Neil ŌĆŗQuilliam, associate fellow at think tank Chatham House.
"While GCC countries have taken steps to diversify suppliers and ensure sufficient stores to withstand disruption, this can only last several months. At this point, price increases ŌĆŗand longer lead times will start to hit the markets."
Analysts warn that even temporary blockages in Hormuz that force rerouting from major ports to smaller ones will ŌüĀcreate strains.
Most major Gulf ports, including Dubai's Jebel Ali and primary ports in Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar and the Saudi Gulf coast, are located where most incoming traffic would have to pass through the Hormuz waterway.
Iranian attacks struck ŌĆŗmany of those lifelines including Jebel Ali, the region's largest container port, this week, suspending operations for hours.
"The biggest immediate effect will be due to the blockade of Jebel Ali, serving about 50 million people," Ishan Bhanu, lead agricultural ŌĆŗcommodities analyst at Kpler, said about the Dubai port that also serves as a re-export hub to the region and beyond.
UAE ports outside the strait have limited capacity. Khorfakkan can handle 5 million twenty-foot equivalent units (TEUs) and Fujairah less than 1 million and would be hard-placed to make up for capacity lost at Jebel Ali or Abu DhabiŌĆÖs Khalifa Port.
"Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain and Iraq effectively become landlocked and will depend on overland routes through Saudi Arabia," Bhanu added, warning of costly congestion.
Those bottlenecks are yet to show and the UAE has said its strategic reserves of vital goods cover four to six months of needs. It urged residents to report unjustified price increases through a dedicated hotline.
Supermarket staff ŌĆŗtold Reuters shelves remain largely stocked, though suppliers are taking longer to replenish certain products. Dubai this week temporarily relaxed truck-movement restrictions to maintain the flow of goods.
The start of Iran's strikes on the Gulf on Saturday prompted many to hoard and ŌĆīcaused a ŌüĀtemporary dip that further fueled panic, a dry run for what could come.
"It is worth noting that perception risk matters and even if stocks are sufficient now, public runs on supermarkets can spook the public," Quilliam said.
A few residents already noticed some price rises.
"Is it just me whose groceries cost three times as of yesterday?" one shopper wrote in a local Facebook group on Tuesday. "Even the bananas cost have gone crazy."
Goods like bananas that spoil quickly are particularly vulnerable to any shipping rerouting that makes journeys longer. Flying in perishable food when airspace opens to cut the journey will raise prices.
"If food is flown in or brought in overland, that is going to be more expensive ŌĆŗthan shipping," said Justin Alexander, Gulf analyst at GlobalSource Partners ŌĆŗand director at Khalij Economics.
"It may be that ŌüĀgovernments choose to absorb some of that cost through subsidizing the food. And they've certainly done that in previous crises."
Apart from investing abroad to secure access to major food production bases, Gulf states have also been constructing modern silos capable of storing hundreds of thousands of tons of strategic grains in the past two decades. These ŌĆŗsilos offer a buffer for staples that can be stored for months like wheat, rice and edible oils.
The UAE opened its Fujairah grain silos in 2016 on ŌĆŗthe Indian Ocean coast outside ŌüĀthe Strait of Hormuz, with roughly 300,000 metric ton capacity. The location was chosen strategically to bypass Hormuz with Iran having already threatened to close the strait whenever tensions rose with the West.
"Fujairah's grain silos act as a strategically important pressure valve providing routing flexibility and risk diversification when the Gulf's maritime environment tightens," said Sudhakar Tomar, president of India Middle East Agri Alliance Ecosystem.
The initiative originally envisioned emergency stocks to be shared by the entire Gulf region. But practical obstacles, ŌüĀincluding long distances ŌĆŗand limited road or rail links between countries, meant it stayed domestic. Other Gulf states have since built their own storage, including Qatar's ŌĆŗFood Security Terminal at Hamad Port with 51 climate-controlled silos.
Regional cooperation among Gulf Cooperation Council members will be critical to averting food shortages, but the six-nation bloc has long struggled with coordination.
"It will require close cooperation amongst GCC states to manage complex logistics and ensure that ŌĆŗall six states and Iraq are sated," Quilliam said.

item.title: Trump undertakes sweeping makeover of White House and Washington
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump is pursuing an ambitious remaking of Washington, from a $400 million White House ballroom to a 250-foot arch and a renovated Kennedy Center.
Trump's sweeping redesign efforts would be arguably the most dramatic by a U.S. president since Theodore Roosevelt championed a structural overhaul of the National Mall in the early 1900s. In the 1950s, Harry Truman gutted and rebuilt the White House.
Here are some of Trump's passion ŌĆŗprojects, which have drawn sharp criticism from Americans concerned about pocket-book issues and the preservation of historic landmarks.
Trump has said his planned 90,000-square-foot ballroom will be "the Greatest of ŌĆīits kind ever built," matching the existing White House in height and scale. Estimated to cost $400 million, he said it will be funded by wealthy individuals and corporations.
But the president's enthusiasm about the project, which he says will seat 1,000 guests, has not been widely shared. He has faced public backlash since he demolished the East Wing to make room for the structure, despite earlier assurances that he would not.
A Washington Post-ABC News-Ipsos poll in October found 56% of Americans opposed the project, with 28% supporting it.
Preservationists and opponents of ŌĆŗthe project criticized the loss of the East Wing - which housed the offices of the first lady and the White House movie theater - and raised concerns that it could visually overpower the main mansion.
The work ŌĆŗon the project provides a daily cacophony of construction noise between the White House and the Treasury Department, a disruption likely to continue for much of Trump's three ŌüĀremaining years in office.
The National Capital Planning Commission, whose chairman is a top White House aide, is scheduled to hear public comments on the project and potentially vote on it on Thursday.
Congress authorized the John ŌĆŗF. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts to honor the Democratic president who was assassinated in 1963. That name went unchallenged for decades ŌĆö until a TrumpŌĆæappointed board voted last year to rename it the TrumpŌĆōKennedy Center.
The storied cultural center ŌĆŗsaw a flurry of show cancellations and slumping ticket sales after Trump's takeover.
On February 1, Trump announced that the property will close for two years beginning July 4 for a major overhaul. Trump insists the structure needs a retool, citing problems with the plumbing and crumbling masonry.
He has said he does not plan to tear down the Kennedy Center but instead will renovate it, a claim that critics note he also made about the East Wing before it was destroyed. He estimated the renovations will cost $200 million.
Across the ŌĆŗPotomac River from the Lincoln Memorial lies a nondescript highway roundabout upon which Trump wants to build what he calls the Independence Arch - an arch reminiscent of the Arc de Triomphe in Paris, except much bigger.
The height of the ŌĆŗarch, with eagle statues and a Lady Liberty-type figure on top, has been estimated at 250 feet. That is higher than the Lincoln Memorial and not far off the size of the U.S. Capitol, which at 288 feet can be seen across ŌĆīmuch of Washington.
By ŌüĀcontrast, the Arc de Triomphe in Paris is 164 feet high.
Trump told reporters on January 31 that he wanted a large arch because "we're the biggest, most powerful nation."
Whether the arch will actually be built with a 250-foot height remains unclear, as there is a possibility it could interfere with the flight path of southbound planes on the final approach to Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport a few miles away.
Trump embarked on his first major redecoration project at the White House when he moved back into the Oval Office in January 2025.
He transformed the storied room with gold accents and statuettes, portraits of famous Americans pulled from storage - including some who are not easily identifiable - ŌĆŗand a copy of the Declaration of Independence hanging on ŌĆŗthe wall behind a black sheet.
Busts of Abraham ŌüĀLincoln and Benjamin Franklin are now situated on tables near his desk. The level of bric-a-brac can give the room a cluttered, old library feel compared to the styles of previous presidents, but Trump is pleased with it and likes to give tours to visitors.
Outside the Oval Office, Trump replaced the Rose Garden's iconic grass ŌĆŗlawn , opens new tab with a white stone patio and umbrella-covered tables to give it a patio style much like Trump has by his pool at his Mar-a-Lago Club in Palm Beach, ŌĆŗFlorida. He said the stone ŌüĀwas needed because women wearing high heels would sink into the soil.
Along the nearby colonnade walkway, Trump placed portraits of America's 47 presidents on the wall, with a plaque beneath each inscribed with Trump's view of that person.
Bitter at his loss to Democrat Joe Biden in the 2020 election, Trump replaced BidenŌĆÖs portrait with an image of an autopen, a device he and other Republicans have criticized Biden for using for signing documents despite it being a fairly standard practice for recent presidents.
Elsewhere ŌüĀon the White ŌĆŗHouse grounds, Trump has placed large flagpoles on both the North and South Lawns.
Inside the White House, Trump tore out the green-tiled, retro-styled ŌĆŗbathroom in the Lincoln Bedroom and replaced it with a modern version featuring white and black marble.
The green-tiled Lincoln bathroom dated back to Truman's White House renovation in the 1950s, according to a Washington Post article from 2007.
At the time, then-President George W. Bush and first lady ŌĆŗLaura Bush undertook a renovation of the Lincoln Bedroom, which Lincoln used as an office. The Post article describing the Bush renovation said they had left alone the "well-preserved" bathroom.

item.title: The Kurdish struggle for rights and land
item.description:
March 5 - Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the U.S. about whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in western Iran, sources say, as the U.S. and Israel pursue their military campaign against Tehran.
The Kurds are an ethnic group that was left stateless a century ago when the borders of the modern Middle East emerged from the collapsing Ottoman Empire.
Kurdish nationalism stirred in the 1890s ŌĆŗwhen the Ottoman Empire was on its last legs. The 1920 Treaty of Sevres, which imposed a colonial carve-up of Turkey after World War One, promised them independence.
Three years later, ŌĆīTurkish leader Mustafa Kemal Ataturk rendered that accord obsolete by winning the Turkish War of Independence. The Treaty of Lausanne, ratified in 1924, divided the Kurds among the new nations of the Middle East.
They speak a language related to Farsi and are concentrated in a mountainous region straddling the borders of Armenia, Iraq, Iran, Syria and Turkey. Kurds are mostly Sunni Muslims.
Kurds form about 10% of the Syrian population.
Under deposed President Bashar al-Assad, the Baathist state deprived thousands of them of citizenship rights, banned their ŌĆŗlanguage and clamped down on Kurdish political activity.
As Syria descended into civil war in 2011, with Assad focused on trying to crush Sunni Arab rebels in western Syria, the Syrian Kurdish PYD group ŌĆŗand an affiliated militia, the YPG, established self-rule in Kurdish-majority areas of the north.
The area under Kurdish control expanded as the YPG, under the umbrella of the ŌüĀSyrian Democratic Forces, partnered with the United States in the campaign against Islamic State.
Following Assad's ouster by Islamist rebels led by Sharaa in 2024, the Kurdish groups have aimed to preserve their autonomy, concerned that the new ŌĆŗrulers in Damascus aim to dominate the new order.
But their hand has been weakened as Washington has built close ties to Sharaa, echoing his calls for the Kurds to integrate with Damascus.
Sharaa, who has vowed to protect ŌĆŗthe rights of all Syrian groups, issued a decree on January 16 formally recognizing Kurdish as a national language alongside Arabic, and permitting it to be taught in schools, among other steps.
Since then, government advances have forced the SDF to retreat to Kurdish-majority areas.
The PYD is heavily influenced by the ideology of the Kurdistan Workers Party (PKK), long active in neighbouring Turkey.
Kurds form about 20% of the population of Turkey, mainly living in the southeast.
The PKK took up arms against the state in 1984, waging an ŌĆŗinsurgency, initially seeking a separate state but later moderating its goal to autonomy and greater rights for Kurds.
More than 40,000 people have been killed in the conflict.
Turkey has held PKK leader Abdullah Ocalan in prison since capturing him ŌĆŗin 1999.
In early 2025, Turkey launched a peace process with the PKK, and Ocalan called on the PKK to lay down arms. The process has, however, somewhat stalled since then.
President Tayyip Erdogan removed restrictions on using the Kurdish language during the ŌĆī2000s and early ŌüĀ2010s.
The United States, the European Union and Turkey classify the PKK as a terrorist organization.
Turkey's military has often struck targets in Iraq's Kurdish region near the PKK's stronghold in the Qandil mountains.
Turkey has also sent troops into northern Syria several times to counter the YPG, which Ankara deems an extension of the PKK.
Turkish officials have said recent events in Syria could kick-start the peace process in Turkey.
Kurds form 15-20% of the population, mainly inhabiting three mountainous northern provinces that make up Iraqi Kurdistan.
In the late 1980s, Iraq's authoritarian ruler Saddam Hussein targeted Iraqi Kurds with chemical gas, razed Kurdish villages and forced thousands of Kurds into camps.
After Saddam's forces were driven out ŌĆŗof the north in the first Gulf War in ŌĆŗ1991, Kurdish political parties gained control of Iraqi ŌüĀKurdistan.
Since the U.S.-led invasion that toppled Saddam in 2003, Iraq's central government has recognised autonomy for Kurdistan, which is led by a regional government with a budget apportioned from Baghdad under a formula for dividing oil income.
When Islamic State militants swept through much of northern Iraq in 2014, Kurdish fighters exploited the collapse of central authority ŌĆŗto expand their territory, including taking control of Kirkuk, an oil city they view as their ancient capital.
In September 2017, Iraq's Kurds held a referendum on ŌĆŗindependence, which backfired, eliciting fierce ŌüĀopposition from Baghdad and regional powers. The vote prompted military and economic retaliation from Baghdad, which retook Kirkuk and other disputed areas. Ties between the centre and the Kurdish regional government have since improved, though tensions remain over oil exports and revenue-sharing.
Kurds form about 10% of the population.
Rights groups say Kurds, along with other religious and ethnic minorities, face discrimination under the ruling clerical establishment. The Islamic Republic denies persecuting Kurds.
There are three main Iranian Kurdish separatist factions, all ŌüĀbased in Iraqi ŌĆŗKurdistan. Iran has demanded authorities in Iraq hand over separatist Kurdish dissidents stationed there and close their bases.
Iran's Kurdish regions have been ŌĆŗa focal point of unrest following anti-government protests that began in late December 2025 and led to thousands of people being killed in January 2026. During the unrest Reuters reported that armed Kurdish separatist groups had sought to cross the border into Iran from Iraq.
Kurdish areas ŌĆŗwere also a major flashpoint during the previous major wave of domestic unrest in 2022, when nationwide protests were triggered by the death in custody of an Iranian-Kurdish woman.

item.title: Mexico steps up investigations into fuel smuggling by drug cartels
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 5 (Reuters) - Mexico has expanded a probe into fuel smuggling at sea ports highlighted in a 2025 Reuters investigation into cartel-linked fuel crimes, according to a government document reviewed by the news agency outlining ŌĆŗactions taken by the administration.
The February document says the anti-corruption ministry is overseeing internal investigations ŌĆīwithin the ports of Guaymas, Tampico and Ensenada as well as in the Navy and customs agency.
The government previously had acknowledged probes into suspected fuel smuggling at the Port of Tampico and last year arrested 14 people allegedly involved, including customs agents and ŌĆŗNavy officials.
The government comments on the fuel smuggling investigations came in response to questions from the ŌĆŗopposition National Action Party (PAN) about suspected corruption within state agencies that purportedly allowed fuel smuggling ŌüĀto flourish in Mexico. The fuel smuggling section was part of a broader 217-page response to questions ŌĆŗfrom senators about the administration's performance on its policy priorities.
That section of the document, available on the Senate's website , opens new tab , was ŌĆŗfirst reported by local media.
The port administrations of Guaymas, Tampico and Ensenada didn't respond to requests to comment, nor did Mexico's Attorney General's office, Navy or customs agency.
Fuel smuggling in Mexico involves falsifying shipping and customs paperwork on fuel coming mainly from the ŌĆŗUnited States. Smugglers evade a steep Mexican tax on imported diesel and gasoline by declaring it to be ŌĆŗsome other type of petroleum product that's exempt from the duty. The savings can amount to more than half a cargo's ŌĆīvalue.
Illicit fuel ŌüĀand stolen crude are now the second-largest source of revenue for Mexico's cartels behind narcotics, according to the U.S. Treasury Department.
Emboldened smugglers have graduated from truck and rail shipments to using petroleum tankers, a development that U.S. and Mexican security officials say points to collusion between cartels and crooked port insiders.
In its 2025 investigation, Reuters tracked ŌĆŗa vessel that offloaded imported ŌĆŗdiesel in the ports ŌüĀof Ensenada and Guaymas that was declared in Mexico to be tax-free lubricants, costing the government $7 million in lost duties.
The importer was suspected to be a front company ŌĆŗfor the Jalisco New Generation Cartel, according to three Mexican security officials and ŌĆŗan undated government ŌüĀsecurity document viewed by Reuters.
PAN in its questions urged the federal government to explain how it would investigate, dismantle and punish anyone involved in the fuel smuggling network, including high-ranking government officials that may be part of it.
Mexican President ŌüĀClaudia Sheinbaum ŌĆŗhas been under intense pressure from U.S. President Donald Trump to ŌĆŗcrack down on drug cartels.
PAN and the president's office did not respond to requests for comment.

item.title: US-Israeli strikes on Iran in breach of international law, says Italian minister
item.description:
ROME, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran which sparked the latest Middle East crisis were ŌĆŗclearly in breach of international law, Italian ŌĆīDefence Minister Guido Crosetto said on Thursday.
It was the strongest criticism to date from Italy's right-wing administration, ŌĆŗwhich has sought to establish close ŌĆŗties with U.S. President Donald Trump since ŌüĀhe took office last year.
The decision to ŌĆŗlaunch the strikes last weekend "of course fell outside, ŌĆŗneedless to say, the rules of international law", Crosetto told the lower house of parliament.
He said Italy ŌĆŗwas being forced to respond to a ŌĆŗconflict that the United States and Israel had initiated without ŌĆīwarning ŌüĀtheir allies and partners.
"It is a war that was started without anyone in the world knowing. One in which we, like the ŌĆŗrest of ŌĆŗthe world, ŌüĀfind ourselves having to manage (the consequences)," said Crosetto, who is a ŌĆŗmember of Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni's ŌĆŗBrothers ŌüĀof Italy party.
Italy has said it will send air defence aid to Gulf countries and naval assets ŌüĀto ŌĆŗCyprus to help them ŌĆŗprotect against Iranian strikes launched in response to the U.S.-Israeli ŌĆŗattacks.

item.title: Russia and Ukraine to exchange 500 POWs over coming two days, Moscow says
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - Russia ŌĆīand Ukraine exchanged 200 prisoners of ŌĆŗwar on ŌĆŗeach side on Thursday ŌüĀand will swap another ŌĆŗ300 each on ŌĆŗFriday, the Russian Foreign Ministry said.
The two ŌĆŗsides have ŌĆŗperiodically traded POWs and ŌĆīreturned bodies ŌüĀof dead soldiers - most recently last week - ŌĆŗdespite ŌĆŗa ŌüĀlack of progress towards ŌĆŗa peace ŌĆŗagreement ŌüĀto end their four-year war.

item.title: French appeals court lowers charges against police officer facing trial for killing of teenager
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - A French police officer initially charged with murder in the deadly shooting of a teenager in 2023 will instead stand trial for ŌĆŗthe lesser charge of voluntary assault leading to death, the Versailles ŌĆīappeals court said on Thursday.
Police officer Florian M. had fired at Nahel Merzouk after the latter had failed to comply with an order to stop his car.
The seventeen year old had ŌĆŗlater died from his wounds, sparking violent protests across the country.
In 2025, the ŌĆŗNanterre prosecutor's office said one of two officers under investigation, ŌüĀidentified as Florian M., would face the Assize Court of Hauts-de-Seine on charges ŌĆŗof murder.
Florian M. appealed, and the court lowered the charges on Thursday.
His lawyer ŌĆŗLaurent-Franck Lienard said he was satisfied with that decision but said that his client should not be on trial at all.
"On this point, it is a real disappointment," he said. "We will ŌĆŗcontinue our efforts so that his shooting is recognized as legitimate."
Under French ŌĆŗlaw, voluntary assault leading to death by a person holding state authority carries a maximum sentence ŌĆīof ŌüĀ20 years, compared with up to 30 years for murder.
A charge of complicity in murder against the other officer was dropped in June.
"The court did not follow the investigating judges regarding the homicidal intent, considering that it had not ŌĆŗbeen established that Florian ŌĆŗM. intended, at ŌüĀthe moment he fired, to take the driverŌĆÖs life," the court said in a statement.
A video, widely circulated on ŌĆŗsocial media and verified by Reuters, had shown two police ŌĆŗofficers beside ŌüĀthe car, a Mercedes-AMG, with one firing his weapon as the driver pulled away.
"This is a rather scandalous and shameful decision," said Frank Berton, lawyer for the ŌüĀfamily of ŌĆŗNahel M.
"It is not for the Versailles appeals ŌĆŗcourt to rule on the intent to kill. With this decision, the court allows the police ŌĆŗofficer to avoid appearing before a jury."

item.title: UKŌĆÖs slow support for allies in Iran conflict deepens doubts about military effectiveness
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - Britain's cautious response to the escalating conflict in the Middle East and its hesitancy in defending its allies from attack have renewed doubts among partners about its military effectiveness when the U.S. ŌĆŗis demanding widespread rearmament.
Trump lashed out at Prime Minister Keir Starmer on Tuesday, suggesting he had helped to "ruin" the countries' historically close relationship after London blocked the U.S.'s use ŌĆīof British bases to launch pre-emptive strikes on Iran .
Cypriot officials have also criticised London after an Iranian-made drone hit one of Britain's military bases on the island, prompting France, Greece and others to send support . A British destroyer is not expected to sail until next week, and will likely take around a week to get to the region.
Starmer has defended his actions , saying British personnel would only get involved in military action that was legal and well planned. ŌĆŗBritish jets have since brought down Iranian drones, Britain has resupplied allies' air defence systems and its bases are now being used by the U.S. for defensive operations .
But the initial response ŌĆŗhas sparked alarm about the state of Britain's military preparedness after years of cuts.
Simon Diggins, a former British defence attach├® who worked with U.S. troops ŌüĀin Afghanistan, said if he had to summarise the situation he would say: "The Brits are not serious".
He said Trump was "vulgar, brash, rude, and undiplomatic" but Britain had made itself "operationally and strategically" irrelevant, despite ŌĆŗits nuclear weapons and advanced fighter jets such as F-35s.
The latest spat follows months of growing tensions between Washington and its traditional allies.
But the shrinking of Britain's armed ŌĆŗforces has undermined London's efforts to be seen as the U.S.'s most reliable military partner in Europe, when mounting geopolitical tensions mean Britain is facing requests to bolster positions in Ukraine, in the Arctic, and now in the Middle East.
With a little over 70,000 full-time trained soldiers, Britain's army is its smallest since the Napoleonic wars.
According to the International Institute for Strategic Studies think-tank, Britain has fallen behind the rearmament of other European countries with gaps in armoured ŌĆŗvehicles, ships and ground-based air defence systems.
Starmer, who has blamed previous Conservative governments for a lack of military investment, plans to spend 2.5% of GDP on defence by 2027 and aims for 3% after ŌĆŗ2029.
But his government has delayed the release of its defence investment plan for the next decade.
Trump has said that Germany and France are now challenging Britain as Washington's most valued partners in Europe.
Starmer's caution is ŌĆīexplained in ŌüĀpart by the spectre of the Iraq war, when previous Labour Prime Minister Tony Blair's decision to join the 2003 U.S.-led invasion proved deeply divisive in the country.
Analysts also said that, while Britain could defend its hesitancy on the use of its bases due to legal concerns, the lack of military preparation while the U.S. amassed warships and fighter jets in the region was a strategic failure.
Tom Sharpe, who commanded four British warships during a 27-year naval career, said the failure to have a warship in the region was one of the worst British military decisions he had seen.
"You have the wafer-thinness of our ŌĆŗforces and that increases the imperative to make ŌĆŗgood operational decisions and we can't seem to ŌüĀdo that either," he said.
That lack of readiness has been brought home by the drone attack on the British Royal Air Force's Akrotiri base in Cyprus.
Cypriot officials told Reuters they were infuriated that Starmer had not stated publicly that the U.S. would not use Akrotiri, shortly before it was hit by ŌĆŗa drone thought to have come from Hezbollah.
Former diplomats, a current official and analysts said although Trump was clearly ŌĆŗangry with Britain, they did ŌüĀnot see his latest outburst - when he said Starmer was "no Winston Churchill" - as a fundamental shift in policy.
Michael Martins, who worked at London's U.S. embassy in Trump's first term, said he did not expect the two countries to "decouple meaningfully" when they work closely on intelligence.
A U.S. official said he saw the criticism as "a moment, not a shift", and Starmer himself said the U.S.-British "special relationship" was reflected in their deep levels of cooperation, ŌüĀnot in the ŌĆŗlatest words of the president.
But the widening conflict in the Middle East means it is not just the U.S. which is ŌĆŗlooking for Britain to do more.
Bader Al-Saif, an associate fellow at Chatham House based in Kuwait, said European countries needed to show they could support their Gulf allies when needed.
"I'm not saying that they should join the war effort, but I ŌĆŗthink they have vested partners in the region, including the Gulf states, which they can offer support to," he said.

item.title: Iranian warship sunk by US torpedo participated in Indian drill
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 5 - The U.S. attack on an Iranian warship in the Indian Ocean this week was the first time since World War Two that the United States has sunk an enemy vessel ŌĆŗwith a torpedo.
The following details are based on accounts from Sri Lankan, U.S. and ŌĆīIranian officials of the March 4 incident, part of U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran and its military assets.
The Sri Lankan Navy launched a search-and-rescue operation after receiving a distress call from the Iranian warship IRIS ŌĆŗDena early on Wednesday.
By the time rescuers reached the area, the ship had already sunk, leaving ŌĆŗonly an oil slick on the surface. The vessel had about 130 people ŌüĀon board.
Sri Lanka recovered the bodies of 87 sailors and rescued 32, who were being treated for ŌĆŗminor injuries and were expected to be released from hospital on Thursday. Search operations were continuing for ŌĆŗan estimated 10 crew members who remain unaccounted for.
The ship sank within Sri LankaŌĆÖs exclusive economic zone, about 19 nautical miles off the southern port city of Galle, after coming under ŌĆŗattack from a U.S. submarine.
Dena was returning to Iran after taking part in a naval exercise ŌĆŗin India from February 16 to February 26, held off the southern Indian city of Visakhapatnam.
The Iranian vessel was among ŌĆī18 ŌüĀforeign warships participating in the biennial MILAN exercise, alongside ships from Sri Lanka, Australia, Japan and Russia. Officials from more than 70 countries attended, including representatives from the United States and Britain.
India described the exercise as one of the largest multilateral naval drills in the Indo-Pacific, aimed at strengthening interoperability, maritime domain ŌĆŗawareness and collective response ŌĆŗcapabilities.
IndiaŌĆÖs ŌüĀnavy had welcomed DenaŌĆÖs arrival as a reflection of the ŌĆ£long-standing cultural links between the two nationsŌĆØ. Since the sinking, videos have circulated of Iranian sailors marching ŌĆŗthrough the streets of Visakhapatnam during exercise events.
Iranian Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi ŌĆŗsaid on ŌüĀX that Dena was ŌĆ£a guest of IndiaŌĆÖs NavyŌĆØ and was struck without warning in international waters. He said the U.S. "will bitterly regret the precedent it has set".
New Delhi has not officially commented on the attack, though ŌüĀmany in ŌĆŗIndia have praised southern neighbour Sri Lanka for its role ŌĆŗin the rescue. IndiaŌĆÖs main opposition Congress party criticised the governmentŌĆÖs silence, saying the conflict had ŌĆ£reached our backyardŌĆØ and questioning New ŌĆŗDelhiŌĆÖs role as a ŌĆ£net security providerŌĆØ in the Indian Ocean region.

item.title: Joy of UK's Iranian diaspora turning to fear as conflict rocks homeland
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - In an area of London known ŌĆŗas Little Tehran, the killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei prompted spontaneous street parties, dancing and fireworks. Shoppers still smile ŌĆīas some stores hand out sweet pastries, traditional in Persian culture when there's cause for celebration.
But as the bombardment of their homeland enters its sixth day, the joy is beginning to fade.
Hundreds of civilians have been killed, major cities are being pummelled and there is growing concern for uncles, ŌĆŗaunts and cousins who cannot be reached in Iran.
"On Saturday night, people were happy, it was amazing," said Mostafa ŌĆŗZaryabi, 44, who works in an Iranian bakery in the Little Tehran area in Finchley, north ŌüĀLondon, explaining that with the death of Khamenei, who he called "a dictator," he was hopeful there could now be freedom in ŌĆŗIran.
At a gathering of about 200 mostly Iranian British citizens in central London on Wednesday, 38-year-old human rights activist Bita, who did ŌĆŗnot want to share her surname, spoke of the "happy mood" of Iranians in Britain.
"There's a sense of justice and I think, above all, there's a sense of hopefulness," she said.
Another activist, Haleh Blake, 39, said she felt much calmer in a world without Khamenei and while she did feel ŌĆŗfear about the route to change, she was optimistic about the future.
"We can actually dream about what Iran will be next," ŌĆŗshe said.
But Rajad Ali Shahabi, 69, is worried about the destruction of the country. He owns the bakery where the diaspora come to buy ŌĆītraditional ŌüĀhandmade flatbreads sprinkled with sesame seeds, and delicacies like green raisins, saffron ginger and sour cherry snacks.
"It's day and night, they don't stop," Shahabi said of the bombing, which he finds stressful to follow on the news.
Reflecting on the events of recent days, 52-year-old I.T. engineer Kamran Naderi said he felt "happy and sad" at the same time.
"Happy to see the government will hopefully disappear, but ŌĆŗsad to see some innocent ŌĆŗpeople as collateral damage could ŌüĀbe killed."
Naderi is one of 114,000 Iranian-born people living in Britain, according to the 2021/22 census. Between 2015 and 2024, tens of thousands ŌĆŗof Iranians applied for asylum in the UK, more than any other nationality, according to the ŌĆŗMigration Observatory at ŌüĀthe University of Oxford.
Like dozens of premises in the area, Shahabi's bakery displays the Lion and Sun flag, the symbol of Iran before the Islamic Revolution of 1979. It is often used to show support for the opposition figure Reza Pahlavi , the exiled son of Iran's ŌüĀlast shah.
Shahabi, ŌĆŗwho would like to see Pahlavi return to lead a secular democratic Iran, ŌĆŗsaid while there were apprehensions about the conflict, the majority of his customers have been smiling since Saturday.
"You can see joy on their faces," he said. "This ŌĆŗis the beginning of the end."

item.title: Conservative Anglicans set up a council to lead them, not Archbishop of Canterbury
item.description:
ABUJA, March 5 (Reuters) - A group of conservative Anglicans said ŌĆīat a conference in Nigeria on Thursday that they were setting up a council to lead the global Anglican Communion, in a ŌĆŗdirect challenge to the authority of the Archbishop ŌĆŗof Canterbury.
The council will include bishops, clergy and lay members, ŌüĀeach with voting privileges, the group announced.
It unanimously ŌĆŗelected Rwandan archbishop Laurent Mbanda as the chairman of the ŌĆŗnew council but said he would not be "primus inter pares" (first among equals) but rather share power.
"Believing the current instruments of communion no ŌĆŗlonger meet the needs of the majority of ŌĆŗAnglicans around the world, the global Anglican Communion is to be led ŌĆīby ŌüĀa conciliar structure," Bishop Paul Donison told the conference.
"I am also pleased to announce that Archbishop Laurent Mbanda was unanimously elected chairman of the Global Anglican Council," ŌĆŗDonison added.
The Global ŌĆŗAnglican Future ŌüĀConference (GAFCON) brings together conservative churches mainly in Africa and Asia and claims to now ŌĆŗrepresent the majority of Anglicans worldwide.
It opposes ŌĆŗliberal ŌüĀshifts in parts of the Communion, including the ordination of women and greater inclusion of LGBTQ+ members. The group ŌüĀstrongly criticised ŌĆŗthe Church of England's appointment ŌĆŗlast October of Sarah Mullally as its first female Archbishop of Canterbury.

item.title: Belarus leader Lukashenko pardons 18 prisoners, his administration says
item.description: MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - Belarusian ŌĆŗPresident Alexander ŌĆīLukashenko has pardoned ŌĆŗ18 prisoners, ŌĆŗof whom 15 ŌüĀwere ŌĆŗserving sentences ŌĆŗfor "extremism", a charge often ŌĆŗused ŌĆŗagainst opponents of ŌĆīhis ŌüĀgovernment, a Telegram channel linked ŌĆŗto ŌĆŗhis ŌüĀadministration said ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗThursday.

item.title: More than 20,000 Israelis return since start of Iran war, ministry says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 5 (Reuters) - More than 20,000 Israelis have returned to the country since the start of the Iran air war that began on Saturday, the Transportation Ministry said on Thursday, adding ŌĆŗthat about 120,000 more Israelis currently abroad are seeking to come home.
Israel began to open ŌĆīits airspace on Thursday and allowed a handful of flights to land at Ben Gurion International Airport near Tel Aviv.
Israel's airspace was closed on Saturday at the start of the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, which have triggered missile barrages ŌĆŗfrom Iran against Israel, leaving tens of thousands of passengers stranded abroad.
The ministry said its data ŌĆŗshow 120,000 Israelis are currently abroad who wish to return to Israel and ŌüĀthat the operation to repatriate them would likely take seven to 10 days.
Israel's Population and Immigration Authority ŌĆŗsaid nearly 300,000 Israelis have flown abroad in the last three months and airlines have said tens ŌĆŗof thousands of customers seek to return.
It added that it was working to expand options for arriving in and departing from Israel by air, land and sea crossings.
Most Israelis have so far arrived in Israel via land crossings into the ŌĆŗsouthern Red Sea resort city of Eilat.
Israeli airlines have been operating repatriation flights from European cities to ŌĆŗTaba, Egypt and Aqaba, Jordan - adjacent to Eilat.
These flights will continue but Israel's four airlines - flag carrier El Al (ELAL.TA) , opens new tab , Israir (ISRG.TA) , opens new tab , ŌĆīArkia ŌüĀand Air Haifa - have begun flights to Tel Aviv. While airspace is being gradually opened, only incoming flights are currently allowed, at a rate of just one landing per hour due to frequent missile fire from Iran.
"We are doing everything to return every Israeli home safely," said Transportation Minister Miri Regev, previously a ŌĆŗBrigadier General in the ŌĆŗmilitary.
"With the opening of ŌüĀairspace, the return of Israelis to Israel began, and we continue to work around the clock together with all parties to expand the options for returning ŌĆŗand leaving the country in accordance with security restrictions," she said.
Earlier, the first ŌĆŗplanes to ŌüĀarrive at Ben Gurion were Israir and Arkia flights from Rome, and an El Al flight from Athens. Flights from other cities in Europe, as well as from the United States and Asia, are also planned.
Ben Gurion ŌüĀsaid ŌĆŗoutgoing flights would resume on Sunday, but initially would be ŌĆŗlimited to 50 passengers per flight.
Israel's airlines have halted ticket sales between March 15 and March 21 to allow customers whose flights ŌĆŗwere cancelled to be accommodated when the airspace opens.

item.title: Israel to attack Iran's underground missile sites in second phase of war, sources say
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 5 (Reuters) - Israel's war in Iran is entering a second phase that will see its fighter jets attacking ballistic missile sites buried ŌĆŗdeep underground, two sources familiar with Israel's military campaign said.
The joint air assault with the U.S. in Iran is ŌĆīnearing the end of its first week after opening salvos killed the country's leaders and set off a regional war with Iranian attacks in Israel, the Gulf and Iraq, and Israeli attacks in Lebanon.
Israel's military says it has hit hundreds of Iranian missile launchers above ground that could target Israeli cities. The second phase will include ŌĆŗbunkers storing ballistic missiles and equipment, said the sources, who spoke on condition of anonymity because of the sensitivity of the ŌĆŗsubject.
One said Israel aimed to neutralise Iran's ability to launch aerial attacks at Israel by the end of the war, ŌüĀwhich was also focused on taking out the Islamic Republic's leadership.
A military spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment on its ŌĆŗattack plans. The military has previously asserted that it and the U.S. military took control of much of Iran's airspace in the opening days of ŌĆŗthe attacks.
In a statement on Thursday, the military said that, overnight, the Air Force struck "an underground infrastructure site used by the Iranian regime to store ballistic missiles and storage sites for missiles intended for use against aircraft."
The military has not previously announced attacks on underground missile facilities, according to a review of its ŌĆŗpublic statements since the start of the joint U.S.-Israeli attacks on Saturday.
Estimates of Iran's missile stockpile vary widely, from roughly 2,500 before the ŌĆŗwar, according to Israel's military, to around 6,000 according to other analysts. The extent of what remains could prove critical to how the war develops. Tehran ŌĆīhas continued ŌüĀto carry out missile attacks on Israel and across the region.
Douglas Barrie of the UK-based International Institute for Strategic Studies said on Wednesday the think tank assesses Iran still possesses some land-attack cruise missiles, precision-guided weapons that fly low to evade radar detection.
Israel's Air Force fighter jets have carried out near-constant sorties since Saturday, accelerating further in pace after Lebanon's Iran-backed Hezbollah militants fired rockets ŌĆŗat Israel, drawing heavy Israeli ŌĆŗairstrikes as far north as Beirut.
In ŌüĀsome cases, the same Israeli warplanes have struck both Iran and Lebanon in a single operation: bombing targets in Tehran or western Iran on the way out, and striking Hezbollah sites on the way ŌĆŗback, one of the sources familiar with the plans and an Israeli security source said.
Israeli and U.S. ŌĆŗofficials say ballistic ŌüĀmissile and drone launches from Iran have declined since Saturday, a decrease that they attribute in part to U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iranian launch sites and related military infrastructure.
The Israeli military has said that the decrease could also reflect an effort by Tehran to preserve its missile stocks as ŌüĀit prepares ŌĆŗfor a drawn-out war of attrition.
Eran Lerman, a former Israeli deputy national security adviser, ŌĆŗsaid the hope from the initial week of strikes was that Iran's ruling system would "begin to disintegrate earlier, more quickly".
"But this has yet to happen and as long ŌĆŗas it doesn't, the system needs to be further and further degraded," Lerman said.

item.title: More tankers come under attack as US-Iran conflict spreads in the region
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - More tankers came under attack in Gulf waters on Thursday as ŌĆŗthe U.S.ŌĆōIran war escalated , and Iranian drones entered Azerbaijan, threatening to spread the crisis to more oil producers in the region.
A ŌĆīBahamas-flagged crude oil tanker was targeted by an Iranian remote-controlled boat laden with explosives while anchored near Iraq's Khor al Zubair port, according to initial assessments. A second tanker at anchor off Kuwait was taking on water and spilling oil after a large explosion on its port side.
Nine vessels have come under attack since the conflict broke out between the ŌĆŗU.S., Israel and Iran on Saturday. Iran launched a wave of missiles at Israel early on Thursday and also sent drones into Azerbaijan , ŌĆŗinjuring four people.
The escalation comes after a motion to halt the U.S. attacks was blocked in Washington and as ŌüĀthe son of Iran's slain supreme leader emerged as a frontrunner to succeed him, suggesting Tehran was not about to buckle under the pressure.
Around ŌĆŗ200 ships, including oil and liquefied natural gas tankers as well as cargo ships, remained at anchor in open waters off the coast of major ŌĆŗGulf producers, according to Reuters estimates based on ship-tracking data from the MarineTraffic platform.
Hundreds of other vessels remained outside the Strait of Hormuz unable to reach ports, shipping data showed. The waterway is a key artery for around a fifth of the world's oil and LNG supply.
U.S. President Donald Trump offered U.S. navy escorts and insurance in ŌĆŗa bid to restart shipping flows and curtail energy prices. Insurance market Lloyd's of London said on Thursday it is engaging with the U.S. ŌĆŗgovernment on a plan .
BP (BP.L) , opens new tab evacuated foreign staff from Iraq's Rumaila oil field after two unidentified drones landed inside the field, according to Iraqi oil sources. Baghdad has cut its oil ŌĆīproduction by ŌüĀnearly 1.5 million barrels a day as the country ran out of storage and was unable to load tankers, officials told Reuters.
A refinery in Kuwait shut down and another reduced its processing rate. A third refinery in Bahrain also cut its output.
Oil prices extended their rally on Thursday, climbing around 2%. They have risen 15% since the war started on Saturday, as U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran disrupted Middle East supplies.
A benchmark European gas price also gained 2% on ŌĆŗThursday. It has surged around 50% ŌĆŗthis week so far.
Russia's President ŌüĀVladimir Putin said that Russia could halt gas supplies to Europe right now, amid the spike in energy prices from the Iran crisis.
Qatar, which provides 20% of the world's LNG supply, halted gas production earlier this week due to ŌĆŗthe conflict. Other major producers such as the U.S. and Australia have little spare capacity to offset this lost ŌĆŗsupply, according to ŌüĀReuters calculations and industry analysts.
The EU's task of refilling its gas storage in the coming months has become riskier and more expensive due to the Iran conflict and disrupted LNG supplies. It still imports some gas from Russia, planning to end pipeline supply by late 2027 and ban new short-term LNG contracts from late ŌüĀApril 2026.
Asian ŌĆŗimporters came under further pressure from the disruptions in supply from the Middle East. China ŌĆŗasked refiners not to sign new contracts to export fuel and to try to cancel shipments already committed, several people with knowledge of the matter said on Thursday.

item.title: Highlights of NATO chief's interview with Reuters
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte told Reuters on Thursday the alliance was vigilant and ready to defend every inch of its territory after Iran fired a ballistic missile towards NATO member Turkey.
Rutte also addressed France's shift in nuclear doctrine and responded to criticism from some over the way he lavishes praise on U.S. President Donald ŌĆŗTrump.
Here are highlights from the interview.
"NATO air missile defence was able to take it out. ŌĆīSo that was very important. It also shows you, I think, clear evidence that we, with a 360-degree approach, will defend every inch of NATO territory. Our military are constantly on it. But of course, this was serious and absolutely condemnable."
"Article 5, I think, is not in order here. Nobody's talking about Article 5. ŌĆŗAnd the most important thing is that our adversaries have seen yesterday that NATO is so strong and so vigilant, and even more vigilant, if ŌĆŗpossible, since Saturday because of the situation, of course, in the Middle East."
"We know that Iran ŌüĀwas close to getting its hands on a nuclear capability, a missile capability, which would be a threat, not only to the Middle East, and of course, ŌĆŗto Israel, and potentially it would be life-threatening to Israel, but also a threat to Europe."
"Clearly, NATO is not itself involved here. NATO allies are providing key enabling support. ŌĆŗWhat we see here is that NATO, in that sense, is also this power protection projection platform for the United States. Because without European allies, the U.S. would have found it very difficult to launch this campaign against Iran."
"I think there is widespread acknowledgement and agreement that at least we have to be sure that going forward, Iran, this ŌĆŗrepublic, is not able again, to pose (a) death threat to its neighbours, to Israel, the Middle East, to Europe."
"From my conversations with senior American military and political ŌĆŗleaders, it's my absolute sense that they know where they are going."
"Iran was one of the key supporters of the Russian war effort against Ukraine ŌĆö totally unprovoked, this ŌĆīcrazy war ŌüĀwhich started in 2014 and then the full-scale invasion in February 2022."
"What we also see now is that Ukraine is already, for some weeks, being able to recapture terrain, territory which the Russians got their hands on before, so that is really good news. We know that the Ukrainians are posing extreme losses on the Russians, months in, months out, many times more than the Russians lost in Afghanistan in the 1980s."
"Obviously I do believe that the United States is the most ŌĆŗpowerful and most quintessential ally within ŌĆŗthe Alliance. If the president of that ŌüĀcountry is able to get the whole of NATO at 2% last year, and then in The Hague, collectively agreeing to the 5% ŌĆö I would doubt it, without President Trump, we would have really reached it. And then him now taking this ŌĆŗdecisive action to take out this capability of Iran ... if a president of a country is providing that type of ŌĆŗleadership, some praise is ŌüĀwarranted."
"Spain is deployed all over NATO territory. Their troops are part of many forward land forces, many initiatives, many NATO missions. I really want to commend them for that ŌĆö there is a Spanish Patriot system in Turkey, defending key American interests. Yes, there are also discussions on spending. But this is an alliance of ŌüĀdemocracies. When ŌĆŗthere are debates between allies, I always try to stay a bit muted."
"I ŌĆŗreally welcome this. It is leveraging more of what France is doing. However, and we all agree on this, the ultimate, supreme guarantor of our way of life ... is, in the end, the United States nuclear ŌĆŗumbrella, and that is key."
"I'm absolutely convinced the U.S. is completely committed to NATO."

item.title: Four medics killed in Iran, ambulances damaged, WHO says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 5 (Reuters) - The World Health Organization chief said on Thursday ŌĆŗthat it has verified 13 attacks ŌĆīon health infrastructure in Iran amid a U.S.-Israeli campaign, killing four health care workers and injuring ŌĆŗ25 others.
"WHO has verified 13 attacks ŌĆŗon health care in Iran and one ŌüĀin Lebanon," World Health Organization Director-General ŌĆŗTedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus told a press conference, ŌĆŗwithout attributing blame.
Dr. Hanan Balkhy added at the same briefing that four ambulances in Iran were ŌĆŗalso affected and that hospitals and ŌĆŗother health sites suffered minor damage due to strikes ŌĆīnearby. ŌüĀOne of these hospitals in the capital Tehran was evacuated as a result, the U.N. health agency previously said.
Iran's ambassador to the ŌĆŗU.N. in ŌĆŗGeneva has ŌüĀalleged that 10 facilities have been hit by military strikes ŌĆŗin a letter to Tedros earlier ŌĆŗthis ŌüĀweek.
Balkhy says that the WHO logistics hub in Dubai which provides health supplies ŌüĀto ŌĆŗdozens of countries is temporarily ŌĆŗout of service because of transport restrictions in the ŌĆŗregion.

item.title: US Senator Warren targets US ammunition sales linked to Mexican cartels
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. Senator Elizabeth Warren will introduce legislation on Thursday to stop a U.S. Army-owned ammunition plant from selling military-grade bullets ŌĆŗto civilians, asserting that some are being diverted to arm Mexican drug ŌĆīcartels and have been used in more than a dozen American mass shootings.
The Stop Militarizing Our Streets Act, co-sponsored by Senator Andy Kim and Representatives Robert Garcia and Jamie Raskin, ŌĆŗwould prohibit Pentagon contractors from selling military-grade assault weapons and ammunition ŌĆŗto civilians.
It would also require that military contractors only sell firearms ŌüĀand ammunition to commercial dealers that follow minimum safety practices, such as ŌĆŗscreening customers and having a low history of gun sales that are later linked ŌĆŗto a crime.
The bill takes aim at the Lake City Army Ammunition Plant in Independence, Missouri, a facility built during World War Two to supply the U.S. military and the ŌĆŗlargest manufacturer of rifle ammunition for the U.S. armed forces.
The facility is ŌĆŗrun by Olin Winchester, part of Olin Corporation (OLN.N) , opens new tab . The Army has a deal with Winchester that ŌĆīwhatever ŌüĀammunition is not purchased by the service can be sold commercially on the civilian market.
High-powered .50-caliber cartridges that Mexican authorities have seized from cartels were traced to the Lake City plant, Warren said.
Neither Olin nor its subsidiary immediately responded to ŌĆŗa request for comment.
"Americans' ŌĆŗtax dollars ŌüĀshould not be used to fuel gun violence," Warren said in a statement. "Congress must step in to keep Americans ŌĆŗsafe, and that means stopping the U.S. military and giant ŌĆŗdefense contractors ŌüĀfrom selling weapons of war to cartels, criminal groups, and mass shooters that terrorize our communities."
A New York Times investigation in 2023 found that AR-15 ammunition produced at ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗLake City plant had been used in at ŌĆŗleast a dozen mass shootings since 2012, including the attacks in Aurora, San Bernardino, Las Vegas, Sutherland Springs, ŌĆŗParkland, Buffalo, and Uvalde.

item.title: Exclusive: Hezbollah elite fighters return to south Lebanon to fight Israeli troops
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Hezbollah has deployed elite fighters to confront Israeli forces in southern Lebanon, sending them back into the border ŌĆŗregion from which they withdrew after a war in 2024, three Lebanese sources familiar with the deployments said, as the ŌĆīIran-backed group wades deeper into the conflict in the Middle East .
Members of Hezbollah's Radwan force had received orders to join the battle and block advances by Israeli tanks, the sources said, citing the town of Khiyam - one of several areas where Israeli troops reportedly advanced on Wednesday - as an example.
Hezbollah, a Lebanese Shi'ite Muslim group established by Iran's ŌĆŗRevolutionary Guards in 1982, entered the war on Monday, opening fire to avenge the death of Iran's supreme leader, who was killed in ŌĆŗa surprise U.S.-Israeli attack on Tehran.
Israel has carried out heavy strikes in southern Lebanon and more widely since then, sending ŌüĀmore troops over the border, and ordering Lebanese to leave a swathe of territory in the frontier region, where the Israeli military had kept soldiers ŌĆŗat several positions since the 2024 war.
Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem said in a televised speech on Wednesday that the group would confront what he described ŌĆŗas an Israeli plan of "occupation and expansion".
"For us, this is an existential defence," he said.
The Hezbollah elite fighters deployed to the south after the group opened fire with rockets and drones on Monday, according to the sources, speaking on condition of anonymity because of the sensitivity of the topic.
They had left that part of Lebanon - specifically ŌĆŗthe area between the Litani River and the border with Israel - after a U.S.-brokered ceasefire in the 2024 war, moving north of the river, ŌĆŗthe sources said.
The sources did not say how many elite forces had been deployed.
Hezbollah's opening salvo on Monday was fired from north of the Litani River, Lebanese President ŌĆīJoseph Aoun ŌüĀhas said.
An Israeli military spokesperson said they were checking but didn't immediately provide comment on the sources' accounts. The Lebanese army declined to comment. The office of Prime Minister Nawaf Salam didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Israeli military said on Wednesday two soldiers were wounded as a result of anti-tank fire against troops in southern Lebanon, the first reported injuries among Israeli forces since the start ŌĆŗof the regional war.
Hezbollah has announced ŌĆŗseveral attacks targeting Israeli tanks ŌüĀthis week, along with numerous rocket and drone launches targeting Israel.
An Israeli military spokesperson said on Wednesday the military was "positioning troops a little farther" into Lebanon than before, "to prevent any attacks against the northern communities" in Israel.
In ŌĆŗMay, 2023, before the Hamas-led October 7 attack on Israel triggered a region-wide conflict, Hezbollah invited media to ŌĆŗwatch Radwan fighters ŌüĀsimulate an invasion of Israel.
Some 5,000 Hezbollah fighters were killed in the 2024 war with Israel, many of them members of the Radwan force. The war left Hezbollah severely weakened.
Israel has carried out near-daily strikes targeting Hezbollah since that conflict, including an attack in December on what it described as a training compound used ŌüĀby Radwan ŌĆŗforces in the south.
Following the 2024 truce, which demanded the Lebanese army confiscate all unauthorized ŌĆŗarms , beginning in the area south of the Litani, the Lebanese army seized many Hezbollah weapons caches. Hezbollah said it respected the ceasefire south of the Litani, but that it didn't ŌĆŗapply to the rest of Lebanon.

item.title: NATO's Rutte backs Macron's nuclear revamp but says US umbrella is ultimate┬Āguarantee
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte on Thursday welcomed French President Emmanuel Macron's pledge to expand his country's nuclear deterrent but said the U.S. nuclear umbrella would ŌĆŗremain the ultimate guarantor of European security.
Macron this week announced France would ŌĆīincrease its nuclear arsenal and potentially allow European partners to host French warplanes on deterrence missions, following concerns among allies about the reliability of the U.S..
Rutte said Macron's update to France's nuclear doctrine would make ŌĆŗit harder for Russia to assess European deterrence and defense and that he supported discussions ŌĆŗover nuclear cooperation between Paris and several European capitals.
But he said ŌüĀEurope could not do without U.S. protection and sought to dismiss concerns over Washington's commitment to ŌĆŗdefending Europe.
"The ultimate, supreme guarantor of our way of life ... is, in the end, the United ŌĆŗStates' nuclear umbrella, and that is key," Rutte told Reuters in an interview.
In his speech at a submarine base, Macron said the rethink of French nuclear doctrine had been done in transparency with Washington, and was complementary ŌĆŗto NATO's nuclear mission.
France and Germany have established a nuclear steering group to discuss ŌĆŗdeterrence issues and will start concrete cooperation this year. Greece, Poland, the Netherlands, Belgium, Denmark and Sweden could also ŌĆībe ŌüĀexpected to take part in French nuclear wargames, Macron said.
The Trump administration has at times over the past 14 months been scathing in its view of Europe's willingness and ability to defend itself.
In mistakenly shared comments by top Trump administration officials ahead of strikes on Yemen's Iran-aligned ŌĆŗHouthis last year, a ŌĆŗperson identified as Vice ŌüĀPresident JD Vance wrote "I just hate bailing out Europe", prompting a second official identified as U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth to lambast European "free-loading" in ŌĆŗresponse.
Under pressure from Washington, NATO's European members have raised their military spending ŌĆŗcommitments .
A ŌüĀstandoff over Greenland earlier this year added to European doubts about U.S. commitments to defending its allies. But Rutte told Reuters he did not doubt the United States' commitment to NATO and the ŌüĀsafeguarding of ŌĆŗEuropean security.
"I'm absolutely convinced the U.S. is completely committed ŌĆŗto NATO," he said.
"The United States knows that to stay safe itself (on) the U.S. mainland, you need a secure Atlantic, a ŌĆŗsecure Europe, a secure Arctic."

item.title: UK starts visa requirements for St. Lucians
item.description:
CASTRIES, March 5 (Reuters) - Britain has introduced a visa requirement for St. Lucians effective from ŌĆŗThursday citing a "notable increase" in nationals from the ŌĆīsmall Caribbean nation claiming asylum, St. Lucia's government said in a statement.
Immigration is one of Britain's most politically sensitive ŌĆŗissues, and Prime Minister Keir Starmer's government has ŌĆŗsought to show it is tightening the system ŌüĀas the populist Reform UK party gains ground ŌĆŗin opinion polls.

item.title: UK PM Starmer to address press conference on Middle East conflict at 1400 GMT
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer will address a ŌĆŗpress conference on Thursday afternoon ŌĆīto give an update on the escalating conflict in the Middle East , his ŌĆŗspokesman said.
Starmer will speak to ŌĆŗjournalists at 1400 GMT in ŌüĀLondon, his spokesman said.
Britain, which ŌĆŗwas not part of the assault ŌĆŗon Iran that began at the weekend, has announced it will start moving ŌĆŗmilitary resources to the region ŌĆŗafter an attack on one of its ŌĆībases ŌüĀin Cyprus.
Starmer's government said this week it would deploy a warship, as well as helicopters with ŌĆŗcounter-drone capabilities ŌĆŗto ŌüĀhelp blunt Iran's retaliatory strikes.
As the U.S.ŌĆōIran war ŌĆŗentered its sixth day, the ŌĆŗconflict ŌüĀhas widened beyond Gulf states and into Asia, prompting thousands of ŌüĀstranded ŌĆŗtourists and residents to ŌĆŗtry to flee the Middle East.

item.title: US jobs data forces gaze back from Mideast mayhem to AI 'doom'
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 5 (Reuters) - With war still raging in the Middle East , investors on Friday will, at least temporarily, turn their attention back to more familiar economic ground: U.S. jobs data .
Events since the joint U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran last Saturday have dominated market thinking so much that fears of artificial intelligence soon throwing millions of ŌĆŗwhite-collar workers onto the scrapheap have been pushed to the back burner.
Friday's U.S. non-farm payrolls and unemployment figures for February will bring those concerns to the forefront ŌĆīof investors' minds once again, and depending on the details, perhaps to the top of policymakers' agendas as well.
The median consensus in a Reuters poll of economists is for a net rise in non-farm payrolls last month of 59,000, less than half of January's rise. The unemployment rate is expected to remain stable at 4.3%.
While it may be too early to see concrete evidence of AI-related labor market disruption, the jobs ŌĆŗreport will still be closely scrutinized for warning signs, including weak job growth, or even net job losses, and an unwelcome rise in the unemployment rate.
Indeed, from this ŌĆŗpoint on, the monthly payrolls reports and other labor market indicators such as the "JOLTS" job openings, layoff figures and weekly jobless claims are ŌüĀlikely to be lightning rods for the "AI doom" debate over whether the technology will end up destroying jobs, demand and economic growth.
Markets last week were awash with talk of ŌĆŗthe coming AI "apocalypse." Stocks wobbled, as investors sought to identify AI winners and losers, and bets on multiple Federal Reserve rate cuts this year rose.
Helping to stoke fear was Jack Dorsey, CEO of ŌĆŗBlock Inc (XYZ.N) , opens new tab , who openly cited AI in his announcement on February 26 that he was firing nearly half of his workforce, even though the fintech company is "strong ... and profitability is improving."
There's a school of thought that Dorsey and other CEOs and chief financial officers may blame the expected disruptive power of AI for what are really just cost-cutting efforts ŌĆō especially given the labor hoarding that occurred after the pandemic.
But regardless, Dorsey's ŌĆŗstatement spooked investors because it came after a series of long-form research notes and blogs outlining the doomsday AI scenario had gone viral.
However, when assessing the impact of AI on the ŌĆŗlabor market, investors and policymakers must separate facts from noise. That means analyzing hard data, which are obviously often backward-looking. The challenge is using that to predict which way the wind is blowing.
So far, ŌĆīthe picture ŌüĀseems more balanced than the AI doomers would have you believe.
