A recent study co-led by Harvard Business School Professor Suraj Srinivasan analyzed nearly all U.S. job postings from 2019 through March last year. It found that following ChatGPT's launch in November 2022, openings for routine jobs most likely to be replaced by AI fell 13%, but demand for more analytical, technical, and creative roles rose 20%.
Economists at Goldman Sachs estimate that AI currently presents a headwind to job growth of 5,000-10,000 per month. In an economy that creates more than 30 million gross new ŌĆŗjobs per year, that's negligible.
Although only 2.5% of ŌĆŗworkers are at risk of being replaced ŌüĀby current AI use cases, Goldman's economists estimate that 11 million jobs - 6-7% of the workforce - will be displaced by AI going forward. But the technology will create new jobs.
"We therefore do not anticipate a job apocalypse," they wrote last week.
Other research points in the same ŌĆŗdirection. A Morgan Stanley survey of U.S. companies in January indicated that firms in industries most prone to AI adoption are more ŌĆŗlikely to hire or retrain ŌüĀworkers than eliminate or not fill positions.
And a Dallas Fed paper last week found that, so far at least, AI is both aiding and replacing workers.
The monthly U.S. employment reports are usually all about the headline figures. But with AI doomsday fears rife, the details beneath the headlines may now begin to take on much more significance and cut through the fog of war.
(The opinions ŌüĀexpressed here are ŌĆŗthose of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for ŌĆŗglobal financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Morning Bid: Global markets take a breath
item.description:
March 5 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
Asian markets snapped back on Thursday, with South KoreaŌĆÖs KOSPI rising by almost 10%, clawing back a chunk of its drubbing in ŌĆŗrecent days, and JapanŌĆÖs Nikkei adding nearly 2%.
The apparent respite amid the ongoing Middle East conflict wasnŌĆÖt confined to Asia, with U.S. ŌĆīand European stocks also pushing higher on Wednesday as investors took comfort from even the faintest hints that geopolitical tensions might ease.
IŌĆÖll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why Europe may be able to absorb the Middle East energy shock - for now.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast . Subscribe ŌĆŗto hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
Global markets appear ŌĆŗto be catching their breath. The Nasdaq jumped 1.29% on Wednesday in a tech-led rally, while the S&P 500 ŌüĀrose 0.78%, helped along by some upbeat macro prints.
A mix of factors seem to be fuelling this market breather. Traders seemed to take heart ŌĆŗin the New York TimesŌĆÖ report yesterday that Iranian intelligence had indirectly reached out to the CIA after SaturdayŌĆÖs attacks to try to reach a resolution. WashingtonŌĆÖs plan ŌĆŗto help reopen the embattled Strait of Hormuz via navy escorts and insurance coverage may also have raised some optimism.
There was also some positive news on the labour market front ahead of FridayŌĆÖs payrolls release. U.S. private payrolls printed a higher-than-expected rise for February. ISM's non-manufacturing PMI, meantime, hit a more than three-year high.
But uncertainty still reigns as ŌĆŗthe Middle East conflagration enters its sixth day and a swift resolution to a clogged-up Strait of Hormuz looks, for now, like a distant prospect.
Indeed, ŌĆŗanalysts have warned that Iran can keep up drone harassment in the narrow waterway for months, and it's far from clear that tankers would brave the strait even with ŌĆīU.S. naval ŌüĀand insurance support in place.
And other signs point towards this being more than a days-long conflict, as the U.S. Senate failed to block the administrationŌĆÖs campaign against Tehran, giving President Trump largely unbounded power to direct the war.
Oil marched higher on Thursday on the prospect of continued disruption. Both U.S. and Brent crude jumped over 2%, with the latter topping $83 per barrel.
Gold also edged up on safe-haven demand, and the dollar strengthened again after retreating from three-month highs.
Meantime, U.S. ŌĆŗstock index futures edged lower ahead of ŌĆŗthe bell and U.S. Treasuries eased. ŌüĀInvestors will have even more to chew on later today with the release of weekly jobless claims, ahead of tomorrow's non-farm payrolls.
Elsewhere, chip designer Broadcom on Wednesday reported a first-quarter revenue rise of 29% to $19.31 billion and projected its ŌĆŗAI chip revenue would top $100 billion next year. ItŌĆÖs emerging as a strong competitor in the space as ŌĆŗit signs deals ŌüĀto rival those of its dominant competitor Nvidia, including with Meta.
Chart of the day
Global stocks have rebounded in recent days, recovering some of their 2026 gains lost in the wake of the U.S.-Israel attack on Iran on 28 February.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. weekly jobless claims (8:30 AM EST), January import prices (8:30 AM EST), ŌüĀQ4 productivity (8:30 ŌĆŗAM EST)
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every ŌĆŗweekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect ŌĆŗthe views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Iran reveals stretched Trump tariff trades
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Financial markets have swung from fear to hope, pricing in the inflationary impact of the war in the Middle East but also seeming oddly blas├® about how long ŌĆŗthe disruption will last. Tellingly, in both bouts of optimism and pessimism this week, the winning trades of the past ŌĆīyear appear to have run their course.
At first glance, financial markets seem to be painting a coherent picture since Donald Trump began his Iran war. Brent hovered around $83 a barrel on Thursday ŌĆö up 21% on the month but far from the $100 plus levels expected under a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The implication ŌĆŗis that blocking this vital trade artery for more than a few weeks would be so devastating that the countries involved ŌĆŗwonŌĆÖt allow it.
Other markets reflect a similar view that the war will push up inflation a bit but ŌüĀnot be catastrophic. Equity indexes are slightly lower, energy stocks are higher, and the U.S. dollar has risen 1.3% since the attack on ŌĆŗIran thanks to AmericaŌĆÖs status as a net oil exporter. The S&P 500 has held up better than European and emerging market indexes, which ŌĆŗrely more on imported energy. Government bonds have edged down on the assumption that central banks will turn a little more hawkish .
But many other current trends make little sense. Gold, a traditional inflation hedge, is down about 3% since the attacks. Consumer staples shares, think food and cleaning products, have been beaten by consumer discretionary ŌĆŗones ŌĆö apparel, travel and cars ŌĆö despite being less sensitive to household spending. ŌĆ£GrowthŌĆØ stocks, normally hurt most by rising rates, have beaten cheaper ŌĆ£valueŌĆØ names, like ŌĆŗUniversal Health Services. This includes software firms such as Adobe (ADBE.O) , opens new tab , which had been battered by AI fears and had no obvious reason to do well. These movements ŌĆīare mostly ŌüĀthe opposite of what happened in 2022 after a similar energy shock: RussiaŌĆÖs invasion of Ukraine.
What ties this together is simple: assets that had been outperforming are now lagging, and vice versa. The yearŌĆÖs winning trades before the war were extensions of strategies that surged after Donald TrumpŌĆÖs tariff package last year. Many had become overstretched. So-called dedollarisation wagers were so popular that net short bets on the greenback using derivatives hit ŌĆŗa five-year high of $22.8 billion in ŌĆŗFebruary, according to the Commodity ŌüĀFutures Trading Commission. Gold seemed to have become an unbeatable all weather asset , which is unsustainable. Fund flows into U.S. staples were at record highs.
Some of those underlying trends could theoretically continue: central banks may keep buying ŌĆŗgold, and global investors look for alternatives to the dollar. But it is telling that, even as ŌĆŗmarkets rebounded Wednesday, ŌüĀthe old winning wagers kept underperforming ŌĆō staples, for instance, fell another 0.5%. Every investment playbook eventually tires, and this crisis has revealed how worn out some pages already were.
Follow Jon Sindreu on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Russia charges ex-deputy defence minister with corruption
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - Russia has charged a former deputy defence minister ŌĆŗwith corruption, investigators said on ŌĆīThursday, in the latest of a series of prosecutions against top ŌĆŗfigures in the military ŌĆŗestablishment.
The state Investigative Committee said ŌüĀRuslan Tsalikov was accused of ŌĆŗembezzlement, money laundering and bribe-taking.
Tsalikov ŌĆŗserved as first deputy defence minister from 2015 to 2024, when a ŌĆŗseries of corruption cases led ŌĆŗto the arrest of a string of ŌĆīgenerals, ŌüĀdeputy ministers and civilian defence civilians.
Another former deputy minister, Timur Ivanov, was jailed for 13 ŌĆŗyears in ŌĆŗJuly ŌüĀ2025.
Ivanov and Tsalikov served under Sergei Shoigu, a ŌĆŗlongtime ally of Russian ŌĆŗPresident ŌüĀVladimir Putin. Shoigu was removed as defence minister in 2024, ŌüĀand ŌĆŗappointed secretary of ŌĆŗRussia's Security Council.

item.title: UK sending four extra Typhoon jets to Qatar, PM Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on Thursday that four additional ŌĆŗTyphoon fighter jets would be sent to ŌĆīQatar as the conflict in the Middle East escalates, insisting that the UK has the right plan for defence.
Britain's ŌĆŗcautious response to the Iran crisis and ŌĆŗa drone attack on its key military base ŌüĀin Cyprus have led to doubts among ŌĆŗpartners about its military effectiveness.
U.S. President Donald Trump ŌĆŗhas also criticised Starmer for failing to provide sufficient support for his strikes on Iran.
Starmer told a press conference Britain ŌĆŗhad the right plan and had already ŌĆŗbeen pre-deploying military equipment across the region before the start ŌĆīof ŌüĀthe war.
"My focus is providing calm, level headed leadership in the national interest," Starmer said.
"That means deploying our military and diplomatic strength to protect our ŌĆŗpeople, and ŌĆŗit means ŌüĀhaving the strength to stand firm by our values and our principles, ŌĆŗno matter the pressure to do otherwise."
He ŌĆŗsaid ŌüĀBritain was sending four additional typhoon jets to join the squadron in Qatar to strengthen our defensive operations there and ŌüĀin ŌĆŗthe region.
"We have the right ŌĆŗplan for defence," he said.

item.title: Israel orders residents to leave southern Beirut
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Israel warned residents to leave Beirut's Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs on Thursday, sparking panic as people fled a swathe of the Lebanese capital on the fourth day of full-scale ŌĆŗhostilities between the Iran-backed group and Israel.
An Israeli military spokesperson, in ŌĆŗa post on X, ordered residents of the southern suburbs ŌüĀto move east and north, posting a map showing four sprawling districts of ŌĆŗthe capital he said they must leave, part of the area is adjacent ŌĆŗto Beirut airport.
"Save your lives, evacuate your homes immediately," Israeli military spokesperson Avichay Adraee posted, saying that any movement southwards may endanger their lives.
Roads out of the suburbs were clogged ŌĆŗas people fled by car and on foot, television footage showed. Gunfire ŌĆŗwas heard in the southern suburbs, warning residents to leave.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in ŌĆīthe Middle ŌüĀEast on Monday, when Hezbollah opened fire, sparking intensified Israeli airstrikes largely focused on the southern suburbs, southern Lebanon and eastern Lebanon.
On Wednesday, the Israeli military ordered residents to leave an area of southern Lebanon amounting to about 8% of its ŌĆŗterritory.
Israeli bombardment and warnings ŌĆŗhave already forced ŌüĀtens of thousands of Lebanese to flee homes in the southern suburbs and the south this week. The Lebanese ŌĆŗhealth ministry has said 77 people have been killed.
There ŌĆŗhave been ŌüĀno reports of fatalities in Israel as a result of Hezbollah attacks.
Beirut's predominantly Shi'ite Muslim southern suburbs are among the capital's most densely populated areas. The ŌüĀarea ŌĆŗwas pounded by Israeli airstrikes during a war ŌĆŗwith Hezbollah in 2024, and during a previous war with Israel in 2006.

item.title: Algerian military aircraft crashes after takeoff from Boufarik base, two crew killed
item.description: March 5 (Reuters) - A small ŌĆŗAlgerian military ŌĆītransport aircraft ŌĆŗcrashed ŌĆŗshortly after taking ŌüĀoff ŌĆŗfrom Boufarik ŌĆŗair base on ŌĆŗThursday, ŌĆŗkilling two crew ŌĆīmembers ŌüĀand injuring four others, ŌĆŗEnnahar ŌĆŗTV ŌüĀreported ŌĆŗciting the ŌĆŗdefence ŌüĀministry.

item.title: US opposes IAEA board resolution condemning attacks on Ukraine's power grid
item.description:
VIENNA, March 5 (Reuters) - The United States joined Russia, China, and ŌĆīNiger on Thursday in opposing a resolution adopted by the U.N. nuclear watchdog's board denouncing attacks on Ukraine's energy infrastructure as a threat to nuclear safety, diplomats said.
The resolution, passed by the ŌĆŗInternational Atomic Energy Agency's 35-nation Board of Governors, is the seventh on Ukraine ŌĆŗsince Russia invaded its neighbour four years ago.
This is the first time ŌüĀthe United States has opposed one.
"While we continue supporting the IAEA's work in-country, ŌĆŗwe do not support the Board's current consideration of an unnecessary resolution that does not ŌĆŗhelp achieve peace between Ukraine and Russia," the United States said in its statement to the board before the vote.
U.S. President Donald Trump has in the past year pressured Ukraine for a quick peace ŌĆŗdeal that could involve ceding land to Russia. Ukraine has ruled out giving up ŌĆŗterritory.
The IAEA board passed the resolution with 20 votes in favour, including France, Britain, Australia, Canada, South ŌĆīAfrica ŌüĀand Argentina, 10 abstentions and the four votes against, diplomats at the closed-door meeting said. Brazil, Egypt, Morocco and Saudi Arabia were among the abstentions.
The resolution's wording was not as strong as previous ones.
The text seen by Reuters said the board "reemphasizes that attacks ŌĆŗtargeting Ukraine's energy infrastructure ŌĆŗfor the off-site power ŌüĀsupply of nuclear power plants, including at the ZNPP (Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant), represent a direct threat to nuclear safety and security".
The ŌĆŗmove follows the United States' abstention at the U.N. General Assembly on ŌĆŗthe anniversary ŌüĀof the invasion in February when it passed a resolution supporting Ukraine, backing its international borders and voicing concern over intensifying Russian attacks on civilians and critical energy infrastructure.
That resolution ŌüĀpassed ŌĆŗwith 107 countries voting in favour, 12 against, and ŌĆŗ51 abstentions.
The United States said at the time that the General Assembly resolution included language likely to distract ŌĆŗfrom ongoing negotiations aimed at ending the conflict.

item.title: Cartels can only be defeated with military power, White House's Miller says
item.description:
MIAMI, March 5 (Reuters) - Cartels operating in the ŌĆŗWestern Hemisphere can ŌĆīonly be defeated with military power, ŌĆŗWhite House ŌĆŗDeputy Chief of Staff ŌüĀStephen Miller ŌĆŗsaid on Thursday.
"We ŌĆŗhave learned after decades of effort is that ŌĆŗthere is ŌĆŗnot a criminal justice ŌĆīsolution ŌüĀto the cartel problem," Miller told Latin American ŌĆŗdefense leaders ŌĆŗgathered ŌüĀat the U.S. Southern ŌĆŗCommand headquarters ŌĆŗfor ŌüĀa conference.

item.title: Greek seafarers strike over crews stranded in the Gulf by Iran war
item.description:
PIRAEUS, Greece, March 5 (Reuters) - Greek seafarers began a 24-hour strike on Thursday, halting local ferry services, as they protested over vessel crews stranded in the Gulf amid the escalating ŌĆŗMiddle East war, and demanded the area is declared a war risk ŌĆīzone to enable their repatriation.
The Iran conflict threatens Gulf ports and has already disrupted global trade through the Strait of Hormuz, a key artery accounting for about 20% of global oil and gas supply. ŌĆŗAlthough the Strait is not shut, Iran has warned that it will fire ŌĆŗon any ship trying to pass through.
Greece is a dominant force in ŌüĀglobal shipping, controlling one of the worldŌĆÖs largest merchant fleets. More than 325 ships of ŌĆŗGreek interests, their crews including dozens of Greek seafarers, are in the wider Gulf ŌĆŗarea.
"We demand that all of our colleagues, currently in the dangerous Gulf area, the Gulf of Oman and the Red Sea, are evacuated and safely repatriated," said Angelos Galanopoulos, head of Greece's lower engine ŌĆŗcrews' union Stephenson.
Dozens of protesters rallied outside the shipowners union near the port of ŌĆŗPiraeus and spray-painted: "No sacrifice for profits and wars" on the ground. A motorcycle convoy drove to ŌĆīthe shipping ŌüĀministry.
The International Maritime Organization says it is concerned for about roughly 20,000 seafarers in the region. At least nine ships have been damaged in strikes since the conflict began on Saturday.
Seafarers usually have the contractual right to refuse to sail in designated war zones ŌĆŗand demand repatriation at ŌĆŗthe shipowner's expense.
Dozens ŌüĀof ships remained at anchor in open waters off the coast of major Gulf producers and dozens of oil tankers were inside the ŌĆŗStrait, ship tracking data showed.
Risks include not only attacks but also ŌĆŗpotential food ŌüĀand supply shortages said Apostolis Kypraios, head of the marine engineers' union PEMEN.
"The government and shipownwers are responsible for the people trapped in war areas," Kypraios said.
"We demand that they ŌüĀfind ŌĆŗa solution for our colleagues to return home. Their ŌĆŗfamilies are worried and seafarers don't know if they will return alive, if they will be injured."

item.title: As Iran conflict rages, Pope Leo asks God to help leaders renounce war
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 5 (Reuters) - Pope Leo released a video on Thursday praying that God would help world leaders renounce war ŌĆŗas a means of resolving conflicts in an unusual ŌĆīappeal as the U.S.-Israeli bombing campaign against Iran pressed on for the sixth day.
"Lord, enlighten the leaders of the nations, so they may have the courage ŌĆŗto abandon projects of death," the pontiff said in the ŌĆŗvideo message.
"Today we lift up our prayer for peace ŌüĀin the world, asking that nations renounce weapons and choose the ŌĆŗpath of dialogue and diplomacy," he said.
Leo releases a video message ŌĆŗeach month to announce his prayer intentions for that month. The pope's intention for March is "for disarmament and peace".
It was unclear if Thursday's video was ŌĆŗcreated specifically to respond to the U.S.-Israeli military campaign, which has ŌĆŗset off a regional war with Iranian attacks in Israel, the Gulf and ŌĆīIraq, ŌüĀand Israeli attacks in Lebanon.
The Vatican did not immediately respond to a question about when the video was recorded.
The Vatican's top diplomat warned on Wednesday that the U.S.-Israeli strikes undermined international law and said ŌĆŗnations did not ŌĆŗhave a ŌüĀright to launch "preventive wars", an unusually direct criticism of the military campaign.
"If states were to be recognised ŌĆŗas having a right to 'preventive war' ... the entire ŌĆŗworld could ŌüĀrisk going up in flames," Cardinal Pietro Parolin, the Vatican's secretary of state, said in an interview with Vatican News.
In his video, the ŌüĀpope ŌĆŗasked God to help the world ŌĆŗunderstand "that true security does not come from control fuelled by fear but from trust, justice ŌĆŗand solidarity among peoples."

item.title: Diplomats in Riyadh ordered to shelter in place due to potential threat, sources say
item.description:
RIYADH, March 5 (Reuters) - Diplomats and staff at ŌĆīembassies in Riyadh's Diplomatic Quarter were told to shelter ŌĆŗin place on ŌĆŗThursday due to a potential ŌüĀbut unspecified threat, ŌĆŗfour people with direct ŌĆŗknowledge of the matter said.
They did not have further details, ŌĆŗand the Saudi government ŌĆŗmedia office did not immediately ŌĆīrespond ŌüĀto a request for comment.
The gates to the Diplomatic Quarter, a neighborhood on ŌĆŗthe western ŌĆŗedge ŌüĀof the Saudi capital that houses ŌĆŗmost foreign missions ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe country, were closed as of Thursday afternoon, ŌüĀtwo ŌĆŗof the people ŌĆŗsaid.

item.title: Exclusive: NATO's Rutte 'not deaf' to criticism of his Trump praise, offers more
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte said on Thursday he was "not ŌĆŗdeaf" to criticism that he goes overboard in praising Donald Trump but argued the U.S. president ŌĆīdeserved it.
However, in an interview with Reuters , Rutte also offered praise for Spain - a target of Trump's ire for refusing to allow U.S. military bases on its soil to be used in the American-Israeli assault on Iran .
Rutte, a Dutch former prime minister, was selected to lead NATO partly ŌĆŗdue to his strong bond with Trump , which diplomats say has helped keep the military alliance together despite the ŌĆŗU.S. president's criticism of the organisation and its members.
But some officials, analysts and commentators have ŌüĀaccused Rutte of going over the top in lavishing praise on Trump, arguing it is demeaning to Europe and ŌĆŗdownplays the continent's ability to defend itself.
"I hear the criticism, obviously. I'm not deaf," Rutte said in the interview at ŌĆŗNATO headquarters in Brussels.
Famously, at a NATO summit, Rutte likened Trump to a "daddy" intervening in last year's conflict between Israel and Iran.
Most recently, he has offered strong backing for the new U.S.-Israeli military action against Iran, even though leaders of ŌĆŗsome NATO nations such as Spain and Turkey have been critical of the intervention and some others have not endorsed ŌĆŗit.
In the interview, Rutte said Trump had played a crucial role in getting NATO allies to agree a new spending target of ŌĆī5% ŌüĀof GDP last year, composed of 3.5% on core defence and 1.5% on broader security-related spending.
The previous NATO target was to spend 2% of GDP on core defence.
Rutte also called the U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran "decisive action to take out this capability of Iran to pose a threat as an exporter of terrorism and chaos to the Middle East, to Europe, ŌĆŗto the world".
"I ŌüĀthink if a president of a country is ... providing that type of leadership, some praise is warranted," he said.
On Tuesday, Trump threatened to impose a full U.S. trade embargo on ŌĆŗSpain due to the row over the military bases and also renewed criticism of ŌĆŗMadrid for not ŌüĀsigning up to the 5% defence spending target.
Asked about the spat between Trump and Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez , Rutte offered praise for Spain's contribution to NATO.
"Their troops are part of many foreign land forces, many initiatives, many NATO missions. I really want ŌüĀto commend ŌĆŗthem for that," he said.
In response to a question about whether ŌĆŗhe had spoken to Trump and Sanchez to lower the tension between them, Rutte said: "When there are debates between allies, I always try to stay ŌĆŗa bit muted, and therefore being able, if necessary, to help a bit."

item.title: Zelenskiy taunts Hungary's Orban for blocking aid to Ukraine
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy taunted Hungarian leader Viktor Orban ŌĆīon Thursday for blocking a 90 billion euro ($104 billion) aid package from the European Union critical to Kyiv's survival as it fends off Russia.
Ukraine relies on financial aid from partners to cover its budgetary needs while it funnels most state funds ŌĆŗtoward defence. EU member Hungary, which has maintained cordial ties with Moscow, vetoed the package this ŌĆŗmonth amid a dispute over oil supplies.
"We hope a certain person in the EU will ŌüĀnot keep blocking the 90 billion... and Ukrainian soldiers will have weapons," Zelenskiy told reporters in Kyiv.
"Otherwise, we ŌĆŗwill give the address of this person to our armed forces, our guys. Let them call him, speak ŌĆŗwith him in their own language."
The remark will likely heighten the tension between the two leaders during Russia's four-year war on Ukraine, which Orban - who faces elections on April 12 - has been careful not to condemn.
Hungary's veto of the aid package, ŌĆŗas well as new EU sanctions on Russia, came in response to what it says was Ukraine deliberately ŌĆŗcutting supplies from the Druzhba pipeline carrying Russian crude to Europe.
Kyiv says the oil flows stopped after a Russian attack ŌĆīon ŌüĀpipeline infrastructure in January and it is fixing the damage as fast as it can. Zelenskiy said on Thursday that the Soviet-era pipeline could be operational within a month and a half.
"They (the Russians) are killing us, and we're supposed to give poor little Orban oil, because without it he won't win elections?" he said.
Orban has ŌĆŗmade the Ukraine war ŌĆŗa focal point in his ŌüĀcampaign for re-election.
Ukraine's troops are fending off grinding Russian assaults along several parts of the 1,200-kilometer front line, as Kyiv faces U.S. pressure to secure peace while ŌĆŗfending off Russian demands to cede land.
Despite being outgunned and outmanned, Kyiv's army ŌĆŗhas retaken territory ŌüĀin recent weeks, gaining more in February than it lost for the first time since 2023, according to the Finland-based Black Bird Group analysis team.
European foreign ministers had tried unsuccessfully this month to persuade Budapest not to punish ŌüĀUkraine ŌĆŗfor delays restarting the pipeline, which also supplies Slovakia, another EU ŌĆŗneighbour of Ukraine.
Slovakia, whose Prime Minister Robert Fico is also sympathetic to the Kremlin, said it would refuse requests from Kyiv for emergency ŌĆŗelectricity supplies until oil flows resume via the Druzhba pipeline.
($1 = 0.8625 euros)

item.title: Exclusive: Trump tells Reuters US should have a role in choosing Iran's next leader, encourages Kurdish opposition
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Reuters on Thursday that he wants the United States to play a role in ŌĆŗchoosing Iran's next leader and encouraged Kurdish opposition groups to go on the ŌĆīoffensive.
Trump said in a telephone interview it was very early in the process of picking a new leader but that the late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba was an unlikely choice, without elaborating.
"We want ŌĆŗto be involved in the process of choosing the person who is going ŌĆŗto lead Iran into the future," Trump said. "We don't have to go ŌüĀback every five years and do this again and again."
Trump also encouraged Iranian Kurdish forces to go ŌĆŗon the offensive.
"I think it's wonderful that they want to do that, I'd be all ŌĆŗfor it," the president said.
When asked if the U.S. would provide or had offered air cover, he responded, "I can't tell you that," but added that the objective for the Kurds would be "to win."
"If they're ŌĆŗgoing to do that, that's good," Trump added.
Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the United ŌĆŗStates in recent days about whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in the western part ŌĆīof ŌüĀthe country, according to three sources with knowledge of the matter.
The Iranian Kurdish coalition of groups based on the Iran-Iraq border in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan has been training to mount such an attack in hopes of weakening the country's military, as the ŌĆŗUnited States and Israel ŌĆŗpound Iranian targets ŌüĀwith bombs and missiles.
Trump also signaled confidence that the major shipping route near Iran, the Strait of Hormuz, will remain open.
Closing the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz , a narrow chokepoint between Iran and Oman through which ŌĆŗa fifth of ŌüĀthe worldŌĆÖs crude oil and liquefied natural gas passes, has been one of Iran's main objectives, and shipping through the crucial energy artery has ground to a near halt after Iranian ŌüĀhits ŌĆŗon six vessels.
"They have no navy, you know the ŌĆŗnavy is now at the bottom of the sea," Trump said. "I'm watching Hormuz very closely."

item.title: Isolated and under fire: Iran strikes out as Russia and China stand aside
item.description:
DUBAI, March 5 (Reuters) - With its supreme leader killed and its war machine under relentless U.S. pressure , Iran now stands largely alone - its longtime partners Russia and China offering nothing more than diplomatic condemnations and expressions of concern.
Tehran has responded to the U.S. ŌĆŗand Israeli attacks by widening the conflict beyond the Middle East, firing missiles and drones with an impact that is reverberating through global energy markets , rattling capitals from Washington to Beijing, and ŌĆīparalyzing the shipping that carries 20% of the world's oil supply through the Strait of Hormuz.
Iranian missiles reached as far as Cyprus, Azerbaijan , Turkey and Gulf states, taking the war to their doorstep by targeting critical businesses, energy infrastructure and U.S. bases. Oil facilities, refineries and key supply routes were hit, causing severe disruption to crude and natural gas supplies.
With the Strait of Hormuz already shut, the attacks have sent energy prices soaring, destabilising global markets and forcing major economies to scramble , underscoring the worldŌĆÖs exposure to the ŌĆŗfallout from TehranŌĆÖs response to the war.
Russia and ChinaŌĆÖs restraint reflects a cold calculation, analysts say: intervening as Iran faces Israel and the United States would bring high costs, limited gains and unpredictable ŌĆŗrisks - burdens neither power appears willing to shoulder.
ŌĆ£Putin has other priorities, and chief among them is Ukraine,ŌĆØ said Anna Borshchevskaya, a Russia expert at the Washington Institute. ŌĆ£It ŌüĀwould be foolish for Russia to go into a direct military confrontation with the United States.ŌĆØ
A senior Russian source said ŌĆ£the escalation in and around Iran and the Gulf is already diverting attention from the war in Ukraine. ThatŌĆÖs ŌĆŗjust a fact. Everything else is just emotion about a ŌĆśfallen allyŌĆÖ,ŌĆØ the source said.
Beijing and Moscow have both helped Iran build military capacity to counter U.S. and Israeli pressure, supplying missiles, airŌĆædefence systems and technology intended to ŌĆŗbolster deterrence, complicate U.S. operations and raise the costs of attack. That support, however, now appears capped.
China has spent years embedding itself in Middle Eastern diplomacy, while Russia has cast Iran as a pillar of its anti-Western alignment.
Yet as the conflict flared, both powers were constrained -China by its dependence on Gulf energy and trade and by security priorities in Asia, and Russia by a grinding war in Ukraine that has sapped its capacity to shield partners and sharpened its need to preserve ties with ŌĆŗoil-rich Gulf states.
The result is a stark paradox: Iran remains strategically useful to both, but not useful enough to fight for.
With RussiaŌĆÖs military, diplomatic bandwidth and economic resources still absorbed by the war in Ukraine, President Vladimir ŌĆŗPutinŌĆÖs priority is to avoid escalation with Washington and safeguard RussiaŌĆÖs interests in the Middle East, rather than gamble on IranŌĆÖs battlefield fortunes.
ŌĆ£If Russia had supported Iran directly, it would have alienated the Gulf states and Israel,ŌĆØ Borshchevskaya said. ŌĆ£ThatŌĆÖs not ŌĆīwhat Putin wants."
BeijingŌĆÖs ŌüĀrestrained response reflects a longstanding strategy: avoiding binding security commitments far from its core interests.
Unlike the United States, whose alliances rest on mutual defence obligations, China prefers partnerships built on trade, investment and arms sales, ties that stop short of dragging it into costly conflicts beyond East Asia, said Evan A. Feigenbaum from the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace.
Beijing, one of the worldŌĆÖs largest trading powers and energy buyers, maintains ties with Iran and Gulf Sunni rivals, and in Latin America, never placed all its bets on Venezuela alone.
"If Beijing wanted to do more, it wonŌĆÖt redirect strategic attention or military assets from core security," argues Henry Tugendhat ŌĆŗat the Washington Institute. "It only cares about its name ŌĆŗabroad. It cares about Taiwan, the South China ŌüĀSea, and perceived threats from the U.S. and Japan.ŌĆØ
The conflict may even carry advantages for Beijing. From the sidelines, China can watch as U.S. forces are tied down far from East Asia and military stockpiles are depleted, while gaining a real-time view of American capabilities and operations, insights that could inform its thinking on a future ŌĆŗTaiwan scenario.
ChinaŌĆÖs key vulnerability remains energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz, which carries some 45% of its oil imports. But Beijing has built strategic reserves ŌĆŗand substantial volumes of Iranian oil ŌüĀalready sitting in tankers or storage, experts say.
They say the crisis has allowed Moscow and Beijing to recast themselves as mediators. China said Foreign Minister Wang Yi has spoken with European and Arab ministers to press for dialogue, while Putin has held similar calls with Gulf leaders and Iranian officials.
Russia also sees concrete benefits: rising oil prices strengthen its war economy, and a U.S. administration tied up in the Middle East has less bandwidth for ŌüĀUkraine.
Russia does not ŌĆŗbenefit from the collapse of the Iranian regime, but it is also not tying its fate to TehranŌĆÖs survival, said Borshchevskaya. Moscow ŌĆŗis hedging, preserving flexibility, regardless of the conflictŌĆÖs outcome, and would build ties with any new government, even one aligned with Washington.
The Russian source pointed to Syria as a precedent. Despite backing ousted president Bashar alŌĆæAssad for years, Moscow retained its Mediterranean bases and quickly ŌĆŗbuilt ties with SyriaŌĆÖs new leader, Ahmed alŌĆæSharaa, underscoring its willingness to trade loyalty for long-term leverage.

item.title: Trump says he needs to be involved in selecting Iran's next leader, Axios reports
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Axios ŌĆŗon Thursday that he needs to ŌĆībe personally involved in selecting Iran's next leader.
"Khamenei's son is unacceptable to me. ŌĆŗWe want someone that will ŌĆŗbring harmony and peace to Iran," ŌüĀAxios quoted Trump as saying ŌĆŗin an interview.
"I have to be ŌĆŗinvolved in the appointment, like with Delcy (Rodriguez) in Venezuela," Trump said.
Mojtaba Khamenei, son of ŌĆŗIran's late Supreme Leader, has survived ŌĆŗthe U.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran ŌĆīin ŌüĀwhich his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed, Iranian sources told Reuters on Wednesday.
A mid-ranking cleric with close ties ŌĆŗto Iran's ŌĆŗelite ŌüĀRevolutionary Guards, hardliner Mojtaba is one of the most ŌĆŗinfluential figures in the Iranian ŌĆŗclerical ŌüĀestablishment and is seen as a possible successor to his father.
Iran has ŌüĀnot ŌĆŗyet announced a new ŌĆŗleader.

item.title: Trump renews Netanyahu pardon call, saying only Iran should be troubling him
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday called again on Israel's president to grant Prime Minister Benjamin ŌĆŗNetanyahu a pardon for corruption charges, saying the premier should ŌĆīhave nothing on his mind but the war with Iran.

item.title: Swiss and EU vow to deepen cooperation on security and defence
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - Switzerland and the European Union on Thursday said they planned to deepen ŌĆŗcooperation on security and defence, including exchange ŌĆīof information in areas ranging from Ukraine to the Middle East.
The two sides issued a joint ŌĆŗdeclaration stating that aim following talks in ŌĆŗZurich between Swiss Foreign Minister Ignazio Cassis ŌüĀand EU foreign policy chief Kaja ŌĆŗKallas.
A draft of the declaration said the two ŌĆŗsides' top diplomats aimed to hold annual dialogue and step up exchanges on issues and places of ŌĆŗjoint interest including Ukraine, the Middle East, ŌĆŗAfrica, Asia, Latin America and the United States.
The two sides ŌĆīpledged ŌüĀto strengthen cooperation on foreign policy, security and defence, and Swiss representatives would be invited, as appropriate, to meetings of the ŌĆŗEU Foreign Affairs ŌĆŗCouncil and ŌüĀrelevant preparatory bodies, it said.
Issues of common interest also encompassed human ŌĆŗrights, peace mediation, multilateral affairs, restrictive ŌĆŗmeasures, ŌüĀconsular affairs, and crisis management abroad.
The declaration said the two planned to boost cooperation between ŌüĀEU ŌĆŗdelegations and offices and ŌĆŗSwiss embassies and missions in third countries and to international ŌĆŗorganisations.

item.title: France ramps up military aid to Lebanon as Macron calls on Hezbollah, Israel to halt attacks
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - France will strengthen its cooperation with the Lebanese Armed ŌĆŗForces and provide armored transport vehicles as well ŌĆīas operational and logistical support, French President Emmanuel Macron said on Thursday, as Lebanon was pulled deeper into the war in ŌĆŗthe Middle East earlier this week.
"Everything must be ŌĆŗdone to prevent this country, so close to ŌüĀFrance, from once again being drawn into war," Macron ŌĆŗsaid in a post on X.
"At this moment of ŌĆŗgreat danger, I call on the Israeli prime minister not to expand the war to Lebanon. I call on Iranian leaders not ŌĆŗto further draw Lebanon into a war that ŌĆŗis not its own," the French leader added.
After meeting with the ŌĆīchief ŌüĀof staff of the French military in Beirut, Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun asked Macron to intervene to "prevent the targeting of the southern suburbs following threats by the Israeli ŌĆŗarmy against ŌĆŗits residents," the ŌüĀLebanese presidency said in a separate statement.
France has said that it aims to ŌĆŗprevent escalation across the region and has taken steps ŌĆŗto ŌüĀprotect its own positions amid the wider conflict involving the United States, Israel and Iran .

item.title: Flights for US citizens stuck in Middle East ramping up, State Dept says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. State Department said on Thursday it was ramping up the provision of charter flights from the Middle East ŌĆŗafter criticism over the Trump administration's planning and initial assistance to ŌĆīU.S. citizens trying to leave the region since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran began.
The strikes that began on Saturday and Iran's retaliatory attacks on its neighbors have led to widespread ŌĆŗairspace closures, clogging busy aviation routes throughout the region and leaving ŌĆŗthousands stranded.
The department, headed by Secretary of State Marco Rubio, announced ŌüĀon Wednesday the first U.S. government charter flight departed the region bound ŌĆŗfor the U.S., giving few details.
"At the direction of @SecRubio, Department of State charter ŌĆŗflight and ground transportation operations are underway and will continue to ramp up with additional flights and ground transports taking place today," Assistant Secretary of State for Global Public ŌĆŗAffairs Dylan Johnson said in a post on X on Thursday.
It was ŌĆŗunclear from which countries charter flights would be departing.
American citizens in Kuwait, Bahrain, the United ŌĆīArab ŌüĀEmirates, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and Israel should fill out a crisis intake form to receive information about upcoming flights and ground transportation options, Johnson said, adding that a new task force has helped more than 10,000 Americans with ŌĆŗguidance since the crisis ŌĆŗbegan.
The State Department ŌüĀhad only issued warnings to U.S. citizens in Israel and Lebanon as tensions rose, but Iran's response has targeted ŌĆŗU.S. missions and civilian infrastructure in many more places, ŌĆŗincluding in ŌüĀGulf Arab nations that are major transport hubs.
U.S. security alerts for Americans in Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates, Iraq, Qatar and Bahrain went out after the conflict ŌüĀbegan. ŌĆŗThe U.S. on Monday urged Americans across 14 ŌĆŗcountries in the Middle East to immediately depart the region using "available commercial transportation" without offering any U.S.-government-vouched ŌĆŗmeans, drawing the ire of U.S. lawmakers .

item.title: Ukraine, Russia exchange 200 POWs each in latest swap
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - Ukraine and Russia exchanged 200 prisoners of war each on Thursday in their latest swap, ŌĆŗofficials on both sides said, despite a ŌĆīdeadlock in U.S.-backed negotiations to end the four-year conflict .
Kyiv and Moscow have carried out regular POW exchanges throughout the ŌĆŗwar, committing during recent talks in Geneva to ŌĆŗa new round of such swaps even ŌüĀas negotiations remain stuck over the question of ŌĆŗterritory.
"Every time our people come home, it proves ŌĆŗthat Ukraine is working to bring everyone back. No one is forgotten," President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on X.
"We involve mediators. ŌĆŗI am grateful to everyone who helps Ukraine. ŌĆŗI thank the United States for its support in making ŌĆīthis ŌüĀexchange possible."
Some of the POWs freed on Thursday were captured as far back as Moscow's siege of Mariupol in early 2022, Zelenskiy added.
Tearful ŌĆŗrelatives greeted loved ŌĆŗones - many ŌüĀof them draped in Ukrainian flags - as they emerged from a ŌĆŗbus that had arrived at an undisclosed ŌĆŗlocation ŌüĀto chants of "Welcome!"
The two sides are expected to trade a further 300 POWs each in a ŌüĀsecond ŌĆŗday of exchanges on Friday, ŌĆŗofficials said.

item.title: Finland to lift full ban on hosting nuclear arms, government says
item.description:
HELSINKI, March 5 (Reuters) - Finland plans to lift a long-standing ban on having nuclear arms on its territory, the government said on Thursday, aligning with Nordic neighbours ŌĆŗin a move that could open the door to deploying atomic ŌĆībombs on Finnish soil during times of war.
Finland's Nuclear Energy Act, passed in 1987, prohibits the import, manufacture, possession and detonation of nuclear explosives on its soil, seen by some ŌĆŗFinns as a clause that would benefit only Russia if there ŌĆŗever was a war.
While Finland maintained neutrality during the Cold War ŌüĀera, the country in 2023 joined the NATO military alliance in response ŌĆŗto nuclear-armed Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine the preceding year.
"The amendment is necessary ŌĆŗto enable Finland's military defense as part of the alliance and to take full advantage of NATO's deterrence and collective defence," Finnish Defence Minister Antti Hakkanen told a press conference.
The proposed ŌĆŗchange will next go to parliament where the right-wing coalition government holds ŌĆŗa majority.
Neighbours Sweden, Denmark and Norway have long-standing policies against nuclear weapons on their territories in ŌĆīpeacetime ŌüĀbut do not have legislative bans during war.
NATO allies France and Germany announced plans on Monday to deepen cooperation with European partners on nuclear deterrence, marking a policy shift as the continent faces rising threats from Russia and instability linked ŌĆŗto the Iran conflict .
Sweden's ŌĆŗdoctrine is to ŌüĀstation no permanent foreign troops or nuclear weapons on its soil in peacetime, Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson told reporters last ŌĆŗweek, when asked about the possibility of his country hosting French ŌĆŗnuclear arms .
"If ŌüĀwe were to find ourselves in a completely different situation, that particular formulation would not apply," Kristersson said.
Finland shares a 1,340-kilometre border with Russia and in 2024 ŌüĀsigned ŌĆŗa defence pact with the United States, allowing ŌĆŗit to use 15 of Finland's military facilities and zones.

item.title: Middle East chaos stems from erosion in international law, EU's Kallas says
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - The current turmoil in the Middle East is a direct ŌĆŗresult of the erosion in international law, EU foreign ŌĆīpolicy chief Kaja Kallas said on Thursday.
In a speech at the University of Zurich, Kallas said the international order has been undermined by major powers ŌĆŗacting unilaterally, pointing the finger particularly at Russia, but also ŌĆŗdirecting criticism at China and the United States.
"Today, the ŌüĀchaos we see around us in the Middle East is ŌĆŗa direct consequence of the erosion of international law," she said, ŌĆŗarguing that Russia's invasion of Ukraine had encouraged others to act with impunity.
China, she said, was also taking advantage of the erosion of international rules to ŌĆŗextend its influence in the Asia-Pacific region and put pressure ŌĆŗon Europe's economies.
"Without restoring international law, together with accountability, we are doomed to ŌĆīsee ŌüĀrepeated violations of the law, disruption and chaos," Kallas argued.
Turning to the United States, she said Washington's foreign policy shift had "rocked the transatlantic relationship to its foundation, with aftershocks in other parts of ŌĆŗthe world," and ŌĆŗcalling its impact ŌüĀon the international order "seismic."
"The current direction is a new world order characterised by competition and coercive ŌĆŗpower politics, a world order dominated by a handful ŌĆŗof ŌüĀmilitary powers who aim to establish and secure spheres of influence," Kallas said.
Kallas was delivering the Churchill Special Lecture some 80 years after ŌüĀBritain's ŌĆŗwartime leader Winston Churchill spoke at the ŌĆŗUniversity and called for the building of a "United States of Europe" following the devastation ŌĆŗof World War Two.

item.title: Seafarers can refuse to sail through Mideast Gulf region, main union says
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - Seafarers have the right to refuse to sail on ships passing ŌĆŗthrough the Middle East Gulf, including the Strait ŌĆīof Hormuz, after the threat level for the region was raised to its highest level, the leading labour union and shipping industry groups ŌĆŗsaid on Thursday.
Around 300 ships are anchored on ŌĆŗboth sides of the Strait while the U.S.-led war against ŌüĀIran escalates . Since February 28, nine ships have been ŌĆŗdamaged and at least one seafarer has been killed.
As ŌĆŗpart of negotiated arrangements reached on Thursday between mariners and commercial shipping companies, known as the International Bargaining Forum (IBF), seafarers can refuse to ŌĆŗsail into the area, with repatriation at the companyŌĆÖs cost ŌĆŗand compensation equal to two monthsŌĆÖ basic wage.
In addition, they will ŌĆīreceive ŌüĀhigher pay and compensation will be doubled in the event of death or disability, the International Transport Workers' Federation (ITF) said in a statement.
"TodayŌĆÖs designation ensures that seafarers on vessels ŌĆŗcovered by IBF ŌĆŗagreements have ŌüĀcritical protections if they operate in this dangerous region," ITF General Secretary Stephen Cotton said.
"The ŌĆŗfact we have to take these measures at ŌĆŗall ŌüĀis a damning indictment of the situation facing seafarers today. No worker should have to risk being killed or maimed simply ŌüĀfor ŌĆŗdoing their job ŌĆō particularly when that ŌĆŗjob is transporting the oil and goods that keep the worldŌĆÖs economies running."

item.title: Israel decided to kill Khamenei in November, defence minister says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 5 (Reuters) - Israel took the decision to kill Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in November and was planning to carry ŌĆŗout the operation around six months later, Defence ŌĆīMinister Israel Katz said on Thursday.
Khamenei was killed in the first hours of the U.S.-Israeli air campaign that began on Saturday ŌĆŗin the first assassination of a country's top ŌĆŗruler by an airstrike.
The joint air assault is nearing ŌüĀthe end of its first week after opening ŌĆŗsalvos killed the country's leaders and set off a ŌĆŗregional war, with Iranian attacks in Israel, the Gulf and Iraq, and Israeli attacks against Iran's ally Hezbollah in Lebanon .
"Already in November ŌĆŗwe were convened with the prime minister in ŌĆŗa very tight forum and the prime minister (Benjamin Netanyahu) set the ŌĆīgoal ŌüĀof eliminating Khamenei," Katz told Israel's N12 TV news. The timing was set for mid-2026, he said.
The plan was eventually shared with the Washington and brought forward around ŌĆŗJanuary after ŌĆŗprotests broke ŌüĀout Iran, when Israel was concerned its pressured clerical rulers might launch an attack ŌĆŗagainst Israel and U.S. assets in the ŌĆŗMiddle ŌüĀEast, Katz said.
Israel has said its aim is to eliminate the existential threat it sees in Iran's nuclear ŌüĀprogramme and ballistic ŌĆŗmissile project, and to bring ŌĆŗabout regime change. Iran's rulers have so far shown no sign of ŌĆŗrelinquishing power.

item.title: Europol warns Iran crisis raises threat of terror, extremism and cyberattacks
item.description:
MADRID, March 5 (Reuters) - The Middle East conflict will have "immediate repercussions" for European Union security with an increased threat of terrorism, serious and ŌĆŗorganised crime as well as violent extremism and cyberattacks, ŌĆīEuropean police body Europol told Spanish news agency EFE on Thursday.
Europol spokesman Jan Op Gen Oorth said he expected to see more cyberattacks against ŌĆŗEuropean infrastructure and an increase in online fraud using increasingly ŌĆŗsophisticated Artificial Intelligence and exploiting the flurry of information ŌüĀswirling about the conflict online, EFE reported.
Groups linked to Iran ŌĆŗcould seek to carry out "destabilising activities" within the EU, he added, ŌĆŗreferring to groups linked to the so-called Axis of Resistance, the network of anti-American and Israeli Shi'ite militias in countries including Iraq, Lebanon and Yemen. ŌĆŗThese could include terrorist attacks, intimidation campaigns, terrorist financing and ŌĆŗcybercrime.
"The level of terrorist threat and violent extremism in EU territory is considered ŌĆīhigh," ŌüĀhe told the news agency.
The terror threat could be heightened by individuals acting alone or small cells acting on their own initiative, he said.
"The rapid spread of polarising content on the internet ŌĆŗcan accelerate short-term ŌĆŗradicalisation processes among ŌüĀdiaspora communities within the EU and other individuals," he said.
Europol did not immediately return a Reuters ŌĆŗrequest for comment on the reported statements.
Iran and ŌĆŗIsrael on ŌüĀThursday were exchanging fire on a sixth day of war after Israel and the United States launched joint air strikes on Iran ŌüĀon ŌĆŗthe weekend. So far the attacks have ŌĆŗkilled more than 1,000 people including Iran's Supreme Leader, prompted Iran to attack neighbours ŌĆŗincluding Qatar and UAE along with energy shipments.

item.title: US, Qatar discuss acquiring Ukrainian drones to down Iran's Shaheds, source says
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Qatar are in discussions with Kyiv about acquiring Ukrainian interceptor drones as a cheap alternative to down Iranian Shahed UAVs amid the war in the Gulf, a source familiar with the ŌĆŗmatter told Reuters.
The early-stage talks are taking place between government officials, not companies, and the technology being discussed includes systems to listen out for ŌĆīincoming enemy drones and disrupt their communication signals, the source said.
Qatar's International Media Office did not respond to a request for comment. The Pentagon declined to comment.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday evening the United States had asked Kyiv for help in downing Shaheds.
"I have instructed that the necessary resources be provided and that Ukrainian specialists be present to ensure ŌĆŗthe necessary security," he said, without giving further details or mentioning Ukrainian interceptor drones.
Zelenskiy said earlier that Ukraine had also received similar requests from Middle ŌĆŗEastern countries and that he would only go ahead with deals if they did not weaken Ukraine's own defences against Russia's invasion.
He has ŌüĀalso said he would be open to exchanging drones for air defence missiles.
A second source - a Western diplomat in the Gulf - said a Ukrainian delegation had travelled to Doha ŌĆŗthis week to meet with Qatari officials to share Ukraine's experience in drone defence. A delegation also travelled to Abu Dhabi, the diplomat said.
Tehran has fired hundreds of missiles ŌĆŗand drones at Gulf countries after the U.S. and Israel launched a massive campaign of air strikes at Iran on Saturday and killed much of the Islamic Republic's senior leadership.
Gulf countries have managed to intercept most of those strikes, making use of the U.S.-made PAC-3 Patriot systems that Ukraine relies on to defend its own energy and military infrastructure from Russian missiles.
Ukraine, however, has developed ŌĆŗfar cheaper methods of downing the Shahed kamikaze drone during its four-year conflict with Russia, which has been using the Iranian-designed UAVs for much of the war.
Russia has ŌĆŗlaunched 19,000 long-range drones at Ukraine this winter, most of which it has downed, Kyiv said.
After the Iran war erupted, Ukraine's SBU security service warned Ukrainian companies not to sell weapons to ŌĆīMiddle Eastern ŌüĀcountries without permission from Kyiv, the first source said.
The SBU and Ukraine's defence ministry did not respond to requests for comment.
A third source said Britain was supporting Ukraine in early-stage talks with Gulf states about the use of Ukrainian drones to intercept Shaheds.
That source said some drones could potentially be supplied by Project Octopus, an existing interceptor drone joint venture between London and Kyiv.
Britain's Ministry of Defence did not respond to questions. Ukrspecsystems, the Ukrainian company behind the Octopus drone factory in the UK , declined to comment.
Zelenskiy said on ŌĆŗWednesday he had spoken with leaders of ŌüĀthe UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Jordan and Kuwait, without providing details.
Ukraine's discussions with Washington were first reported by the Financial Times, but Doha's interest and Britain's involvement have not previously been reported.
The U.S. and its Gulf allies have expended hundreds of air defence missiles , costing ŌĆŗmillions of dollars apiece, since the Iran conflict began.
Lockheed Martin produces around 600 PAC-3 missiles a year, although it plans ŌĆŗto increase that to ŌüĀ2,000 under a seven-year deal with the Pentagon.
Kyiv has been running low on those missiles for months, raising concerns that it would be unable to down Russian ballistic missiles, against which the Patriot is the only effective weapon in its arsenal.
Zelenskiy has proposed swapping Ukrainian interceptor drones for missiles for Patriot systems.
Taras Tymochko from Come Back Alive, a Ukrainian foundation that ŌüĀhas bought ŌĆŗtens of thousands of interceptor drones with donations, said it was unclear who, other than Ukrainian crews, ŌĆŗcould operate them.
"It's rather difficult to remove our pilots from their operations and send them to the Middle East," Tymochko said. "There's a significant need to scale up existing training capacities in Ukraine to share experience with ŌĆŗour partners."

item.title: Brazil rocked by probe of central bankers aiding failed Banco Master
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 5 (Reuters) - Evidence that two senior regulators at Brazil's central bank secretly advised embattled banker Daniel Vorcaro has sent shockwaves through the capital Brasilia, threatening to drag the institution deeper into a snowballing scandal.
The revelations add to a widening blast radius surrounding Vorcaro, owner of the liquidated Banco Master, whose downfall has exposed ŌĆŗa network of influence and conflicts of interest shaking trust in some of Brazil's most powerful institutions.
Vorcaro was arrested on Wednesday in the latest phase of an ongoing criminal investigation, accused of bribing ŌĆīthe two central bank officials - in addition to plotting to attack and intimidate several people he perceived as acting against his interests with an associate he called "Sicario," named after the hit men employed by Mexican cartels.
Among his targets were former employees, domestic workers and journalists.
For months, Brazilians have watched as the probe - first focused on fraud in Master's loan portfolio - grew to implicate public pension funds, a state-owned bank and high-ranking officials with ties to Vorcaro.
Unusual interventions by a federal audit court and the Supreme Court questioning the lender's shutdown - despite neither body having banking supervisory authority - failed ŌĆŗto derail the investigation but added to questions about the banker's outsized influence.
Amid all that, the central bank's decision in November to close Banco Master had reinforced views of the regulator as a bastion of hard-nosed public servants ŌĆŗsteeled against Brazil's politics.
Federal police shattered those impressions on Wednesday, alleging that Vorcaro likely bribed former central bank director Paulo Sergio Neves de Souza and Belline Santana, ex-head of ŌüĀthe banking supervision department, for tips and advice, according to cellphone messages obtained through court-authorized access to communications records.
Reuters could not immediately reach Souza or Santana or identify their lawyers.
The central bank declined to comment on the implications for its ŌĆŗreputation or regulatory decisions involving the officials.
In a public statement, the central bank said the federal police probe was key to clarifying the facts and any violations would receive the appropriate sanctions under the law.
A person with direct knowledge of the recent ŌĆŗinvestigations said the conduct uncovered was "absolutely unacceptable, absurd and horrifying," even if the central bank's decision-making structure ultimately led the regulator to the right outcome.
Despite any dissent from Souza and Santana, the source said, the central bank blocked regional lender BRB's bid to acquire Banco Master before liquidating the troubled lender.
Still, the revelations have amplified criticism that the central bank may have taken too long to act, allowing Master's problems to deepen while technical assessments that passed through Souza and Santana may have downplayed the severity of the situation, undermining decisions that could have ŌĆŗcome sooner.
"The central bank was slow to rein in Master and it was slow to liquidate it," said another source with knowledge of the regulator's decision-making.
Souza, who was directly responsible for overseeing banks as director of supervision, sat for nearly ŌĆŗsix years on the rate-setting Copom committee, between 2017 and 2023 - a period when Banco Master was expanding aggressively.
A court decision authorizing Wednesday's police operation cited messages showing he and Santana helped Vorcaro with regulatory matters, including prior review of filings to the central ŌĆībank.
The ruling cited ŌüĀevidence they may have received bribes while in their senior roles, including efforts by Vorcaro to formalize sham service contracts through consulting firms, which were then used to transfer funds to the two officials.
Both officials remained at the central bank in different banking supervisory roles until January, when they stepped down from their senior posts amid an internal probe. They remained career staff, now suspended by Wednesday's court order. A formal dismissal would require separate administrative proceedings.
"I was surprised and, above all, saddened. There is deep dismay," said one former director who worked with Souza. Three other sources who served alongside him voiced similar shock.
"It is very sad. But any wrongdoing by two officials must be separated from the institution and its staff," one source ŌĆŗsaid, adding that the central bank's own internal reviews contributed ŌĆŗto the investigation by federal police and prosecutors.
A federal ŌüĀpolice source, also speaking on condition of anonymity, agreed that findings so far pointed to corruption of individual civil servants rather than institutional failure.
A central bank public employee since 1998, Souza joined the bank's board under former President Michel Temer and remained through the administration of former President Jair Bolsonaro into the early months of President Luiz Inacio Lula ŌĆŗda Silva's current term, leaving in July 2023.
His tenure overlapped with the rapid rise of Banco Master, built largely on the sale of high-yield debt marketed to retail investors ŌĆŗas covered by the Credit ŌüĀGuarantee Fund (FGC).
When Banco Master was previously known as Banco Maxima before Vorcaro bought it in 2017, it was already on the central bank's confidential watchlist of troubled institutions, flagged for lending that ignored principles of "selectivity, liquidity and guarantees."
Approval for the young banker to take over the rebranded bank came in 2019, under then-governor Roberto Campos Neto. Two sources familiar with Vorcaro's influence said he long benefited from strong political ties in Brasilia that helped him cast a risky growth model as a boon for competition.
Although the lender held ŌüĀless than 1% ŌĆŗof Brazil's banking assets, its collapse last November - amid a cash crunch and mismanagement cited by the central bank - has cost the FGC fund ŌĆŗsome 40 billion reais ($7.7 billion), roughly a third of its available resources, a bill that later rose with the liquidation of other institutions under its umbrella.
The FGC is financed with mandatory contributions from banks, especially larger lenders, which will bear the brunt of new funding.
That process gained breathing room this week after ŌĆŗthe central bank allowed 30 billion reais in reserve requirements that would otherwise have been deposited at the regulator to be redirected to the FGC this year.
($1 = 5.2312 reais)

item.title: Trump ousts Kristi Noem, names Oklahoma senator as homeland security nominee
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - Kristi Noem, who oversaw U.S. President Donald Trump's aggressive immigration crackdown and faced bipartisan criticism in hearings this week, will leave her role as homeland security chief at the end ŌĆŗof the month.
Trump will tap Oklahoma Senator Markwayne Mullin to replace her, he said on his Truth Social platform, a move that would require U.S. Senate ŌĆīconfirmation.
Noem, a former governor of South Dakota, became one of Trump's most high-profile Cabinet secretaries with social media posts that portrayed immigrants in harsh terms, highlighting cases of alleged criminal offenders and using vitriolic language.
She faced criticism in January when she quickly labeled two U.S. citizens fatally shot by federal immigration agents in Minneapolis as committing "domestic terrorism." Videos that emerged after the deaths undercut the assertion by Noem and other Trump officials that ŌĆŗthe two deceased - Renee Good and Alex Pretti - were violent aggressors.
The public backlash for deaths led the Trump administration to move to a more targeted approach to immigration ŌĆŗenforcement in Minnesota after months of sweeps through U.S. cities that led to violent clashes with residents opposing the crackdown.
Democrats in the U.S. House ŌüĀof Representatives moved to impeach Noem and at least two Republicans in Congress called for her to lose her job after the incidents. During congressional hearings in March, Democrats and some Republicans ŌĆŗcriticized Noem for her approach to the immigration crackdown and management of DHS, including concern over a $220 million ad campaign that heavily featured Noem.
Trump told Reuters on Thursday that he did not ŌĆŗsign off on the ad campaign.
"I never knew anything about it," he said in a phone interview.
The ads prominently featured Noem, including a scene of her on horseback at Mount Rushmore in her home state of South Dakota.
In one of the congressional hearings this week, U.S. Senator John Kennedy, a Republican from Louisiana, asked Noem if Trump had approved the commercials.
"The president approved ahead of time you spending $220 million running TV ŌĆŗads across the country in which you are featured prominently?" Kennedy asked Noem.
"Yes, sir. We went through the legal processes, did it correctly," she replied.
The staffing change raises questions about whether the Trump administration could seek to intensify its mass deportation push or retreat to a more targeted approach. Under Noem's leadership, masked immigration agents surged into Los Angeles, ŌĆīChicago and Washington, ŌüĀD.C. , scouring neighborhoods and Home Depot parking lots in search of possible immigration offenders.
Mullin, who spent a decade in the House of Representatives before becoming a senator in 2023, also supports Trump's hardline immigration agenda. To become DHS secretary, Mullin would require Senate confirmation.
The popularity of Trump's immigration approach fell as agents detained U.S. citizens and tear-gassed streets in an attempt to drive up deportations, which last year fell short of the administration's goal of 1 million per year.
While Noem, 54, served as a prominent proponent of Trump's agenda, White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen ŌĆŗMiller, a long-time Trump aide, controls Trump's immigration policy.
Noem ŌĆŗwas quickly confirmed to lead the 260,000-employee ŌüĀDepartment of Homeland Security in January 2025 after Trump took office. On social media, she referred to immigrants convicted of crimes as "scumbags" even as the number of non-criminals arrested by immigration authorities rose under Trump.
She joined immigration enforcement operations on the ground in New York City and visited a ŌĆŗmaximum-security prison in El Salvador where Venezuelan immigrants deported by the Trump administration were being held without charges or access to lawyers.
The ŌĆŗnumber of migrants caught trying ŌüĀto illegally cross the U.S.-Mexico border plummeted under Trump's restrictive policies, a steep drop after high levels of illegal immigration under former President Joe Biden, a Democrat.
Noem, reflecting Trump's agenda, also took steps to cut legal immigration programs and increase vetting. She ended several Temporary Protected Status programs that provided work permits to hundreds of thousands of immigrants from Venezuela, Haiti and other nations, drawing ŌüĀlegal challenges.
After an ŌĆŗAfghan immigrant was accused of attacking National Guard members in Washington, D.C., Noem said she recommended that Trump ŌĆŗplace "a full travel ban on every damn country thatŌĆÖs been flooding our nation with killers, leeches, and entitlement junkies."
Critics said Noem demonized immigrants and promoted an immigration enforcement strategy that targeted non-criminal, working immigrants and families.
During Noem's tenure, ŌĆŗthe number of deaths in immigration detention rose to a two-decade high while staff in DHS oversight offices were slashed sharply.

ŌĆ£The corridors are ŌüĀnot dedicated exclusively to Syrian Air,ŌĆØ said Hosari. ŌĆ£Other airlines can use them to transit ŌĆŗor operate through Syrian airspace according to the usual regulatory procedures.ŌĆØ
In a Thursday ŌĆŗstatement, the authority said it had also reopened air routes in the northern part of Syrian airspace toward Turkey after conducting operational and technical assessments and reviewing regional developments affecting ŌĆŗaviation.

A week ŌüĀinto ŌĆŗthe regional conflict, Syria is emerging relatively sidelined, ŌĆŗwith Iranian presence ceased after the fall of President Bashar al-Assad in late 2024, and U.S. military bases ŌĆŗin the country evacuated last February.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine will help US to counter Iranian drones

item.title: Kosovo's parliament fails to elect president, risking new election
