Scores of other wealthy Asians are making enquiries or taking similar steps to move their Dubai-parked assets to the regional financial hubs of Singapore and Hong Kong, industry advisers and lawyers said, as the U.S.-Israel war on Iran clouds ŌĆŗthe Gulf's safe-haven aura and rattles investors.
While the rich typically diversify their investments across regions and asset classes, they choose where to be based depending on tax, regulatory, ŌĆŗprivacy and operational considerations.
Towards that end, Dubai has emerged in recent years as a preferred wealth hub for entrepreneurs and rich families in ŌüĀAsia, mainly from China, as they look to take advantage of its favourable policies. Moreover, with a property and infrastructure boom, the Gulf region has also become an investment destination.
The ŌĆŗtrend is now under sharp scrutiny, as the attacks on Dubai and Abu Dhabi have thrown into doubt the United Arab Emirates' (UAE) reputation for stability .
Singapore-based private wealth lawyer Ryan Lin said ŌĆŗsix or seven of his 20 Dubai-based clients, each holding an average of $50 million in assets, contacted him this week, with three planning immediate asset transfers to the city-state.
One client is "checking how quickly they can transfer everything to Singapore", Lin said.
Iris Xu, principal at global corporate and fund services provider Anderson Global, said 10 to 20 family offices have enquired this week with her firm about moving assets back ŌĆŗto Singapore from the Middle East on worries the conflict might drag on.
Family offices are one-stop firms that manage the portfolios of the wealthy.
"Dubai was always about tax benefits but ŌĆŗnow I think the tax benefits may not be the top priority for them," she said.
A wealth management adviser in Singapore, who did not wish to be identified because they were not authorised ŌĆīto talk ŌüĀto the media, said they had spoken to 13 UAE-based clients so far with more than half serious about moving assets to Singapore.
"Flying back and forth will be a challenge even if the conflict ends tomorrow. It is a confidence thing," said the adviser.
Grace Tang, CEO of Phillip Private Equity, said her predominantly Asian clients are skittish, with 10 to 20 asking about moving their wealth to Singapore and looking to preserve their capital.
Not all wealth managers, however, view the ongoing Middle East conflict as prompting immediate capital flight.
Dhruba Jyoti ŌĆŗSengupta, CEO of Dubai-based WRISE Private Middle ŌĆŗEast, a wealth management group, said the ŌüĀfirm has not seen "serious capital flight discussions", as clients were confident about the UAE's long-term resilience.
"They are sophisticated global investors, already diversified internationally, but deeply invested ... in the UAE's growth story," he said. "Despite the broader geopolitical turmoil in the region, clients are feeling safe and ŌĆŗsecure."
The UAE's banking and financial sector was resilient , strong, stable, and well-positioned to navigate regional developments, its central bank governor Khaled Mohamed Balama ŌĆŗsaid on Thursday, adding ŌüĀthat banks, financial firms, and insurers were operating normally and without disruption.
Leading Singapore-based wealth managers, Bank of Singapore and DBS Group (DBSM.SI) , opens new tab said their clients were closely observing the developments in the region and were taking a wait-and-watch approach, for now.
As the UAE scrambled to maintain its safe-haven status, some were going ahead with their expansion plans in the Emirates.
Jeremy Lim, co-founder of GrandWay Family Office, ŌüĀis in ŌĆŗthe process of opening a family office in Abu Dhabi and said his plans have not changed - as ŌĆŗlong as the UAE does not become directly involved in the conflict and barring any further escalation from Iran.
"The real deal-breaker for businesses would be if the UAE were to...become directly involved alongside one side in a ŌĆŗconflict," said Lim.

item.title: Justice Department releases missing FBI interviews in Epstein files with woman who made claims against Trump
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. Justice Department released FBI records on Thursday that summarize interviews of an unidentified woman in ŌĆīwhich she made accusations against President Donald Trump related to an alleged sexual encounter.
FBI agents interviewed the woman four times in 2019 as part of their investigation into accused sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein. The Justice Department had previously released a log confirming that the interviews took ŌĆŗplace but released a summary of only one of those four meetings, in which she accused Epstein of ŌĆŗmolesting her when she was a teenager.
The newly disclosed records , opens new tab , which were posted on the department's website on ŌüĀThursday, show she also claimed Trump attempted to force her to perform oral sex after Epstein introduced her to ŌĆŗthe future president in New York or New Jersey in the 1980s when she was between 13 and 15 years old.
The ŌĆŗWhite House did not immediately respond to questions about the disclosures. Politico, which first reported the disclosures, said White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt called the woman's claims "completely baseless accusations, backed by zero credible evidence."
The Justice Department has cautioned that some of the documents include "untrue and ŌĆŗsensationalist claims made against President Trump." Reuters could not independently confirm the accuracy of the woman's allegations, and FBI records ŌĆŗsuggest agents stopped speaking with her in 2019.
The Justice Department said in a post on the social media platform X that the records ŌĆīit ŌüĀreleased Thursday were among 15 documents that it had ŌĆ£incorrectly coded as duplicativeŌĆØ and not published as a result.
The disclosure comes as the Justice Department faces scrutiny in Congress over its handling of documents from the Epstein investigation, which it is required to make public. Democrats have accused Trump's administration of concealing records related to Trump, and a committee in the House of Representatives ŌĆŗvoted to subpoena Attorney General ŌĆŗPam Bondi so lawmakers ŌüĀcan question her about how the government is handling the disclosures.
Trump has said his association with Epstein ended in the mid-2000s and that he was never aware of the financier's sexual ŌĆŗabuse. Records previously released by the department show Trump flew several times on Epstein's plane in ŌĆŗthe 1990s, ŌüĀwhich Trump has denied. After the financier was first accused of sexual misconduct, Trump called the police chief in Palm Beach to say that "everyone has known he's been doing this," according to an FBI interview record.
In the report of the woman's final , opens new tab interview, conducted in October 2019, ŌüĀduring Trump's ŌĆŗfirst presidency, agents asked whether she would be willing to provide more ŌĆŗinformation about Trump. In response, the agent wrote, she "asked what the point would be of providing the information at this point in her life when ŌĆŗthere was a strong possibility nothing could be done about it."

item.title: India's tech state Karnataka bans social media for children under 16
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 6 (Reuters) - The southern Indian state ŌĆŗof Karnataka, home to ŌĆīthe tech hub of Bengaluru, has banned ŌĆŗthe use of ŌĆŗsocial media by those under ŌüĀthe age of ŌĆŗ16, state Chief Minister ŌĆŗSiddaramaiah said on Friday, becoming the first in India ŌĆŗto do so.
"With ŌĆŗthe objective of preventing adverse ŌĆīeffects ŌüĀof increasing mobile usage on children, usage of social media ŌĆŗwill be ŌĆŗbanned ŌüĀfor children under the age ŌĆŗof 16," Siddaramaiah, ŌĆŗwho ŌüĀuses only one name, said in his ŌüĀbudget ŌĆŗspeech.

item.title: Oil falls but set for steepest weekly gain since 2022
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 6 (Reuters) - Oil prices fell ŌĆŗon Friday for the first time in six days as the U.S. government is weighing potentially ŌĆīintervening in the futures market to blunt rising prices and issued waivers for Russian oil purchases to ease supply constraints from the Middle East war.
Brent crude futures were down 95 cents, or 1.1%, to $84.46 per barrel and West Texas Intermediate was down $1.08, or 1.3%, ŌĆŗto $79.93 as of 0440 GMT.
Still, Brent has surged 16.4% this week while the WTI jumped 19.2% on ŌĆŗtrack for the steepest weekly gain since Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in ŌüĀFebruary 2022.
The gains followed the start on February 28 of the war between the U.S. and Israel, on one side, and Iran that has halted tankers ŌĆŗmoving through the Strait of Hormuz , which typically carries roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil supply.
The conflict has since spread ŌĆŗacross the key Middle East energy-producing region, causing disruptions to oil output and the shutdowns of refineries and liquefied natural gas plants.
"With every passing day, halted activities in Hormuz will have two major impacts on oil: the inability to store 20 million barrels per day and the ŌĆŗlack of flow to the world, which could drive global energy prices higher," said Priyanka Sachdeva, senior market analyst ŌĆŗat Phillip Nova.
In the face of the potentially higher gains, the U.S. Treasury Department is expected to announce measures to combat rising energy ŌĆīprices from ŌüĀthe Iran conflict, including potential action involving the oil futures market, a senior White House official said on Thursday, without providing any details.
Such a move would mark an unusual attempt by Washington to influence energy prices through financial markets rather than physical oil supplies.
The U.S. Treasury also on Thursday granted waivers for companies to start buying sanctioned Russian oil ŌĆŗstored on tankers to ease ŌĆŗsupply constraints which have ŌüĀpushed refineries in Asia to reduce their fuel processing.
The first waivers were given to Indian refiners , who have responded by buying millions of barrels of Russian crude oil cargoes, reversing months of ŌĆŗpressure on them to halt the purchases.
Data from ship-tracking firm Kpler shows about 30 ŌĆŗmillion barrels of ŌüĀRussian oil are available and loaded on vessels in the Indian Ocean, Arabian Sea region and Singapore Strait, including volumes in floating storage.
Still, the recent gain in prices is relatively subdued compared to other price shocks such as in 2022 when ŌüĀRussia attacked ŌĆŗUkraine and prices rose above $100 a barrel.
"It's important to put this ŌĆŗmove into perspective: despite crude's almost 20% surge this month, the price is currently just $3.40 above its average over the last four years,ŌĆØ IG ŌĆŗanalyst Tony Sycomore said on Friday.

item.title: Exclusive: Indian refiners buying prompt Russian oil as Iran war hits supplies
item.description:
NEW DELHI/WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - Indian refiners are buying millions of barrels ŌĆīof prompt Russian crude oil cargoes as the South Asian nation seeks to navigate an oil supply crunch triggered by the Middle East conflict, six sources familiar with the matter said.
After months of Washington pressuring New Delhi to avoid buying Russian barrels in an effort to reduce money flowing to Moscow's war effort in Ukraine, the U.S. Treasury Department issued a 30-day ŌĆŗwaiver on Thursday allowing India to buy Russian oil currently stuck at sea.
"To enable oil to keep flowing into the global market, the ŌĆŗTreasury Department is issuing a temporary 30-day waiver to allow Indian refiners to purchase Russian oil," Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent ŌüĀsaid.
"This deliberately short-term measure will not provide significant financial benefit to the Russian government as it only authorizes transactions involving oil already stranded at sea," ŌĆŗhe said in a statement.
He called it a stopgap measure, as Washington expects India to eventually buy more U.S. oil.
India is vulnerable to energy supply shocks , with crude ŌĆŗstocks covering only about 25 days of demand. India gets about 40% of its oil imports from the Middle East through the Strait of Hormuz .
India was the top buyer of Russian seaborne crude after Moscow's 2022 Ukraine invasion, but in January, its refiners started to reduce purchases under pressure from Washington.
Cutting Russian oil purchases helped New Delhi avoid 25% tariffs and clinch ŌĆŗan interim trade deal with the U.S.
It is unclear whether the United States has allowed India to increase Russian purchases to offset potential Middle Eastern supply ŌĆŗlosses.
A source directly involved with the matter said India had approached U.S. President Donald Trump's administration seeking approval to buy Russian crude imports due to the Iran conflict.
India's oil ŌĆīand foreign ŌüĀministries did not respond to Reuters emails seeking comments. The White House and the U.S. Treasury Department did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
State refiners Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , Bharat Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab , Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab and Mangalore Refinery and Petrochemicals Ltd (MRPL.NS) , opens new tab are talking to traders for prompt delivery of Russian cargoes, according to the Reuters sources.
One of the sources said Indian state refiners have bought about 20 million barrels of Russian oil from traders so far.
HPCL and MRPL last received ŌĆŗRussian oil in November, according to ŌĆŗdata obtained from industry sources.
The traders ŌüĀare selling Russian Urals to India at a premium of $4-$5 per barrel to Brent on a delivered basis for arrival at Indian ports in March and early April, three of the sources said.
This is in contrast to a discount of ŌĆŗabout $13 per barrel for cargoes traded in February, traders said.
HPCL had bought two cargoes of Russian oil at a $13 ŌĆŗdiscount before the war ŌüĀstarted on February 28.
"India refiners are back in the market ... nowadays more than prices, availability of molecules is the issue," said one of the traders involved in Russian oil sales to India.
This source said Reliance Industries (RELI.NS) , opens new tab also approached his company for the purchase of prompt Russian oil cargoes.
Refiners in India had already started tapping Russian oil aboard ŌüĀvessels floating ŌĆŗoff the country's coast to make up for the loss of Middle Eastern crude, two sources ŌĆŗwith direct knowledge of the matter said earlier in the day.
Indian refiners did not immediately respond to Reuters emails sent out after business hours.

item.title: China in talks with Iran to allow safe oil and gas passage through Hormuz, sources say
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - China is in talks with Iran to allow crude oil and Qatari liquefied natural gas vessels safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz ŌĆŗas the U.S.-Israeli war on Tehran intensifies, three diplomatic sources told Reuters.
The war, ŌĆīwhich entered its sixth day on Thursday, has left the critical shipping passageway all-but shut, with countries around the world cut off from a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies.
China, which has friendly ŌĆŗrelations with Iran and relies heavily on Middle Eastern supplies, is unhappy about the ŌĆŗIslamic Republic's move to paralyse shipping through the Strait and is pressing Tehran ŌüĀto allow safe passage for the vessels, according to the sources.
The world's second-largest economy gets ŌĆŗabout 45% of its oil from the Strait.
Ship tracking data showed a vessel called the Iron ŌĆŗMaiden passed through the Strait overnight after changing its signalling to 'China-owner,' but far more sailings will be needed to calm global markets.
Crude oil prices are up more than 15% since the conflict began amid production stoppages ŌĆŗas Tehran attacks energy facilities in the Gulf as well as ships crossing the Strait.
Its ŌĆŗmissiles have also reached as far afield as Cyprus, Azerbaijan and Turkey, destabilising global markets and prompting major ŌĆīeconomies to ŌüĀwarn about inflation risks.
Crude tanker transits through the strait fell to four vessels on March 1, the day after hostilities broke out, versus an average of 24 a day since January, Vortexa vessel-tracking data showed.
Around 300 oil tankers remain inside the Strait, according to Vortexa and ship tracker ŌĆŗKpler.
Sugar industry veteran Mike ŌĆŗMcDougall told Reuters that ŌüĀMiddle East sugar executives say there are some ships transiting the Strait at the moment, all of which are either Chinese or Iranian-owned.
Jamal ŌĆŗAl-Ghurair, the managing director of Dubai-based Al Khaleej Sugar, told Reuters some ŌĆŗships carrying ŌüĀsugar are currently allowed to pass through the Strait while others are not, without giving further details.
Iran's government said earlier in the week that no vessels belonging to the United States, Israel, European ŌüĀcountries or ŌĆŗtheir allies would be allowed to pass through the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz, but the statement made no mention of China.

item.title: Seven countries warn EU not to upend energy market design
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - Governments of seven EU countries, including the Netherlands ŌĆŗand Sweden, warned the bloc's executive on Thursday against interfering with the system that ŌĆīsets Europe's energy prices, as officials in Brussels scramble to find ways to bring down bills.
Energy prices have lurched onto Europe's political agenda this year, as industries warn they cannot compete with companies in the United States and China, where bills ŌĆŗare lower. Soaring global oil and gas prices due to the Iran conflict have increased pressure on the ŌĆŗEuropean Commission to step in.
President Ursula von der Leyen has pledged to assess whether ŌüĀthe EU's current price-setting system should be revised, and the Commission is drawing up options to attempt ŌĆŗto curb energy prices. But analysts and officials say there is no quick fix.
In a letter to Energy ŌĆŗCommissioner Dan Jorgensen, seen by Reuters, seven EU governments warned that changing the system which underpins Europe's energy market would lead to a less efficient mechanism and ultimately increase bills.
"Intervening in the electricity market design and changing price formation mechanisms ŌĆŗwould also increase market and regulatory uncertainty, which is harmful for investments and European competitiveness," the letter ŌĆŗsaid.
Dated March 5, the letter was also signed by the energy ministers of Denmark, Finland, Latvia, Luxembourg and Portugal.
The EU's electricity system is designed so that the last power plant needed to meet total demand sets the power price. Often, that is a gas plant - so gas price spikes can send electricity prices soaring, even when much of the power is being produced from cheaper renewable sources.
The seven countries said "the EU ŌĆŗdependency on expensive, imported ŌĆŗgas" is the root ŌüĀcause of high prices, and not the power market design.
The signatories urged Brussels to instead expand cheaper renewable energy sources faster to limit the price-setting role of ŌĆŗgas in the power mix, and increase measures that encourage consumers to ŌĆŗuse energy when ŌüĀprices are low.
The letter sets up a clash with governments including Italy, which has announced national plans to remove carbon costs from gas power plants' bills - an intervention that would upend the price-setting system.
The signatory countries are ŌüĀlargely ŌĆŗwealthier EU members, and include Sweden and Finland, who have the ŌĆŗlowest power prices in the EU. Both produce more than 90% of their electricity from low-carbon sources - a stark difference from others ŌĆŗincluding Poland, which gets most of its electricity from CO2-emitting coal.

item.title: LloydŌĆÖs market engaging with US government over Gulf maritime plan, officials say
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - The LloydŌĆÖs of London market is engaging with the U.S. governmentŌĆÖs International Development ŌĆŗFinance Corporation over a plan to provide political risk insurance ŌĆīand guarantees for maritime trade in the Gulf , LloydŌĆÖs market officials said on Thursday.
"LloydŌĆÖs is engaging constructively with the U.S. Development Finance Corporation and relevant stakeholders, with ŌĆŗa clear focus on ensuring that the LloydŌĆÖs market ŌĆŗcontinues to lead as the global centre of excellence for ŌüĀwar risk insurance," a LloydŌĆÖs spokesperson said.
The LloydŌĆÖs Market Association (LMA), ŌĆŗwhich represents the interests of all underwriting businesses in the LloydŌĆÖs market, ŌĆŗwelcomed the engagement of U.S. President Donald Trump, its CEO Sheila Cameron said separately in a statement on Thursday.
"Since Sunday 1 March, there have been at ŌĆŗleast 40 transits of vessels through the Strait of Hormuz.
"There remain ŌĆŗapproximately 1,000 vessels, approximately half of which are oil and gas tankers, with ŌĆīan ŌüĀaggregate hull value exceeding $25 billion in the Persian/Arabian Gulf and surrounding waters,ŌĆØ Cameron said, citing data.
Cameron added that the vast majority of these vessels were insured in the London market and insurance "currently remains in ŌĆŗplace".
Insurance broker Marsh ŌĆŗsaid on Wednesday it ŌüĀhad met with U.S. officials to explore solutions for restoring maritime trade.
The U.S. Navy could begin ŌĆŗescorting oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz if ŌĆŗnecessary, Trump said ŌüĀon Tuesday, adding he had ordered the International Development Finance Corporation to provide political risk insurance guarantees for maritime trade in the Gulf.
Earlier ŌüĀthis ŌĆŗweek, London's marine insurance market widened the area ŌĆŗin the Gulf it deems as high risk as the conflict in the Middle ŌĆŗEast escalates.

item.title: EU countries give final approval to 2040 climate target for 90% emissions cut
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - European ŌĆīUnion countries gave the final approval on Thursday to a new climate target to slash greenhouse gas emissions 90% by 2040, pressing ahead with the bloc's ambitious climate agenda despite political resistance.
The new climate target ŌĆŗis a hard-fought political compromise, struck by governments and EU lawmakers last year. ŌĆŗIt is more ambitious than most major economies' emissions-cutting commitments, including China's.
In ŌüĀpractice, the target will require an 85% emissions reduction from European industries against 1990 ŌĆŗlevels. The EU will pay developing countries via carbon credits, so they cut emissions on ŌĆŗEurope's behalf to make up the rest, to reach 90%.
The EU agreed the target last year following months of wrangling between countries, such as Spain, which say worsening droughts and wildfires justified more ambitious ŌĆŗgoals, and those like Poland and Italy, which sought to soften the emissions cuts, ŌĆŗarguing that struggling industries cannot afford the upfront investments.
A reinforced majority of EU countries' ministers gave the ŌĆīfinal ŌüĀformal sign-off to the legally binding goal at a meeting in Brussels. The Czech Republic, Slovakia, Poland and Hungary opposed it. The climate target will now pass into EU law.
As part of the agreement, the EU will also consider the option in future to ŌĆŗuse international carbon credits ŌĆŗto meet a ŌüĀfurther 5% of its 2040 emissions reductions - potentially further softening the domestic efforts required.
The deal also delays the launch of a politically ŌĆŗsensitive new EU carbon market by one year, to 2028 - a ŌĆŗmove designed ŌüĀto win over countries sceptical of the climate goal.
The target, which is designed to keep Europe on track to meet its pledge to reach net-zero emissions by 2050, falls short of the ŌüĀ90% domestic ŌĆŗemissions cut recommended by the EU's climate science ŌĆŗadvisers.
It is also weaker than Brussels' original plan for the goal, reflecting disagreement between EU governments over the speed and ŌĆŗcost of their green agenda.

item.title: Israeli airstrikes pound Beirut suburb, Hezbollah warns Israelis
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, March 6 (Reuters) - Israel carried out heavy airstrikes on the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of Beirut overnight after ordering its residents to leave, while the Iran-backed group warned Israelis to ŌĆŗleave towns and villages at the frontier.
Explosions and flashes lit up the ŌĆīnight sky over the Beirut southern suburbs, Reuters footage showed. The Israeli military said it had carried out 26 waves of strikes overnight in the southern suburbs, saying targets included Hezbollah's command ŌĆŗcentres and weapons storage facilities.
On Thursday, an Israeli military spokesperson told residents ŌĆŗof the southern suburbs to move east and north, posting a ŌüĀmap showing four large districts of the capital he said they must leave, including ŌĆŗareas adjacent to Beirut airport.
Hezbollah, in a message published in Hebrew on its Telegram ŌĆŗchannel early on Friday, warned Israelis to leave towns within 5 km (3 miles) of the border.
"Your military's aggression against Lebanese sovereignty and safe citizens, the destruction of civilian infrastructure and the expulsion campaign ŌĆŗit is carrying out will not go unchallenged," Hezbollah said.
During fighting between Hezbollah ŌĆŗand Israel in 2024, tens of thousands of Israelis were evacuated from towns in the border area ŌĆībut ŌüĀmany have since returned. Israeli officials have previously said there are no plans to remove them for now.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in the Middle East on Monday, when Hezbollah opened fire, igniting a new Israeli offensive, with airstrikes focused on ŌĆŗBeirut's southern suburbs and ŌĆŗon southern and ŌüĀeastern Lebanon.
Israel has also ordered Lebanese to leave large areas of southern and eastern Lebanon.
The Lebanese health ministry has reported 123 ŌĆŗpeople have been killed and another 683 wounded as a result ŌĆŗof Israeli ŌüĀattacks this week. Its figures do not distinguish between civilians and combatants.
There have been no reported fatalities in Israel as a result of Hezbollah attacks.
Hezbollah, a Shi'ite Muslim group ŌüĀestablished ŌĆŗby Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, was badly weakened ŌĆŗby Israel during the 2024 war.

item.title: New ZealandŌĆÖs ruling party plunges in polls, PM Luxon rules out resigning
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Support for New ZealandŌĆÖs ruling National party has fallen to its lowest level in more than four years and Prime Minister Christopher Luxon is no ŌĆŗlonger votersŌĆÖ preferred leader, polling showed on Friday.
The monthly TaxpayersŌĆÖ Union-Curia ŌĆīpoll showed support for the conservative Nationals dropped 2.9 points to 28.4%, the worst showing for the party since late 2021.
Luxon, 55, and his coalition government, which has been in ŌĆŗpower since the 2023 election, have lost popularity over the past year ŌĆŗdue to a weakening economy and higher unemployment. The decline exposes ŌüĀthe centre-right bloc to strong competition from the Labour party which has seen ŌĆŗits polling numbers improve in recent months.
While the poll results fuelled speculation of ŌĆŗLuxonŌĆÖs resignation, he told local media he would "absolutely not" stand aside ahead of a general election on November 7.
"WeŌĆÖve got a lot of work to do and IŌĆÖve got skills that are ŌĆŗactually very useful at this time when weŌĆÖre trying to navigate some challenging ŌĆŗglobal environments," he told Newstalk ZB.
"Our biggest challenge in this country is our economy, and I ŌĆīunderstand ŌüĀour economy well."
The Nationals' coalition partners New Zealand First and ACT polled at 9.7% and 7.5% respectively.
Support for Labour rose 0.3 points to 34.4%, while the Greens recorded 10.5% and Te Pati Maori at 3.2%.
That would translate to ŌĆŗ61 seats at the ŌĆŗelection, enough for ŌüĀthe three parties to form government as a centre-left bloc, the poll said.
New Zealand has used a mixed-member proportional electoral ŌĆŗsystem since 1996, making coalitions the norm.
The poll, which surveyed ŌĆŗ1,000 people, ŌüĀalso found approval for Luxon fell 1 point to 21%.
Labour leader Chris Hipkins gained 4.7 points to 22.7%, overtaking Luxon as preferred prime minister.
Luxon, the former CEO of ŌüĀAir ŌĆŗNew Zealand, has faced criticism for some of ŌĆŗhis policies, including the closure of a separate health authority for indigenous people and a lack of ŌĆŗfunding increases for frontline employees.

item.title: More LNG tankers divert as Asian buyers seek cargoes
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 6 (Reuters) - More tankers carrying liquefied natural gas are diverting towards Asia as buyers ŌĆŗscramble for replacement cargoes after the Middle East war halted tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and ŌĆīdisrupted supplies from Qatar, the world's second-largest seller of the fuel.
Shiptracking data by analytics firms Kpler and LSEG show three LNG tankers diverting towards Asia so far.
Carrying U.S. cargoes from the Plaquemines and Corpus Christi LNG terminals, respectively, the Simsimah and Clean Mistral tankers pivoted toward the South Atlantic ŌĆŗon March 4 after heading northeast towards Europe.
Earlier this week, the BW Brussels, carrying a Nigerian cargo from Bonny ŌĆŗLNG, pivoted away from its initial Atlantic-bound course on March 3, and is now Asia-bound via the ŌüĀCape of Good Hope.
The price of the U.S. Henry Hub gas benchmark was $2.97 per million British thermal units on Thursday versus $17.01 ŌĆŗper mmBtu for the European Title Transfer Facility (TTF) benchmark and $15.495 per mmBtu for the Japan-Korea Marker, the Asian benchmark.
The higher European and ŌĆŗAsian prices are more than enough to offset the cost of longer distances to move the cargoes to Asia.
"Cargoes have started to be diverted to Asia, away from Europe in recent days, and the prevailing JKM-TTF spreads, averaging much above the U.S. shipping differentials, indicate flexible U.S. cargoes will ŌĆŗlikely start to come to Asia on stronger netbacks," said Energy Aspects analyst Kesher Sumeet.
While some Asian buyers are delaying spot ŌĆŗpurchases or not awarding tenders due to elevated prices, others are paying up to secure cargoes.
"Buyers from South Korea, India, Taiwan, Bangladesh, and Thailand ŌĆīhave ŌüĀbeen seeking replacement spot cargoes, though many remain hesitant to award tenders due to high prices or a lack of offers," said Sumeet.
"India and Bangladesh are reportedly securing spot cargoes above $20 per mmBtu, but the volumes awarded remain well below the levels impacted due to Qatari disruptions," he said, adding that Asian buyers will unlikely be able to replace all the lost Qatari cargoes for ŌĆŗMarch and April.
Bangladesh has secured two ŌĆŗspot cargoes from Gunvor and ŌüĀVitol at $28.28 per mmBtu and $23.08 per mmBtu, respectively.
Additionally, a power utility in western Japan is seeking replacement cargoes after previously expecting deliveries from Qatar beginning in June 2026, while another major Japanese ŌĆŗutility has been seeking prompt cargoes amid growing concerns that supply availability for late March and ŌĆŗearly April delivery ŌüĀmay tighten, said Rystad Energy analyst Masanori Odaka.
Cargo diversions could intensify competition between the Atlantic and Pacific basins. Asia takes more than 80% of Qatar's LNG exports, and Europe is increasingly relying on LNG to fill gas storage since the region halted most Russian pipeline ŌüĀgas imports ŌĆŗafter Moscow's full-scale invasion of Ukraine.
While global front-month arbitrage was open to Asia ŌĆŗearlier this week, the U.S. front-month arbitrage firmly pointed to Europe on Thursday, as high freight prices and a falling JKM-TTF spread make Europe more competitive, ŌĆŗsaid Spark Commodities analyst Qasim Afghan.

item.title: India invokes emergency powers, orders refiners to boost LPG output
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 6 (Reuters) - India has invoked emergency powers and directed refiners to maximise production of ŌĆŗliquefied petroleum gas (LPG) to prevent a shortage of the ŌĆīcooking fuel after supply disruptions caused by the Middle East crisis, a government order showed.
The world's second-biggest importer of LPG last year ŌĆŗconsumed 33.15 million metric tons of cooking gas, which ŌĆŗis a mixture of propane and butane.
Imports account for ŌüĀabout two-thirds of LPG consumption, with the Middle East ŌĆŗmaking up about 85ŌĆō90% of that supply.
All oil refiners are ŌĆŗasked to "maximise and ensure that propane and butane available with them are utilized for production of LPG", the order issued late on Thursday ŌĆŗshowed.
The government has asked producers to make LPG, propane and ŌĆŗbutane available to state refiners - Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab and ŌĆīBharat ŌüĀPetroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab for distribution to households.
The country has about 332 million active LPG consumers, government data showed.
A mandatory diversion of propane and butane for LPG production would curtail the output ŌĆŗof alkylates, ŌĆŗa gasoline blending ŌüĀcomponent, by Reliance Industries Ltd (RELI.NS) , opens new tab .
Reliance exported about four cargoes per month of alkylates last year, ŌĆŗaccording to LSEG data.
The government also ordered ŌĆŗrefiners not ŌüĀto divert propane and butane for petrochemical production.
Diverting propane and butane for LPG production will hurt margins for petrochemical companies ŌüĀthat make ŌĆŗproducts such as polpropyline and alkylates, ŌĆŗas they fetch better prices than LPG, a trade source said.

item.title: Pakistani, Afghan border forces clash as UN says war displaces 100,000
item.description:
KABUL, March 6 (Reuters) - Pakistani and Afghan troops exchanged fire at dozens of points along their border on Friday as ŌĆŗthe U.N. said their week-old conflict has forced the displacement of more than 100,000 people.
The South Asian nations show ŌĆīno signs of rapprochement in their worst fighting in years, adding to the volatility in a region also contending with U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran - a nation that borders both Afghanistan and Pakistan.
Combat has included Pakistani air strikes on Taliban government installations, such as the Bagram air base north of the Afghan ŌĆŗcapital Kabul.
Afghanistan's Ministry of Defense said Taliban forces struck Pakistani military installations along the 2,600-km (1,600-mile) border, ŌĆŗdestroying numerous posts and shooting down a drone.
Pakistani security sources said they carried out ground and air operations ŌüĀagainst military targets including Kandahar, the heartland of the Taliban and where its core leadership resides, and destroyed several Afghan border posts.
Dozens ŌĆŗgathered in Kabul on Friday to protest Pakistan's attacks on Afghan territory, chanting anti-Pakistan slogans, a witness said, while the Bakhter news ŌĆŗagency said a large gathering in Laghman Province demonstrated against Pakistan's recent attacks.
People living in border towns have told Reuters that troops begin exchanging heavy shelling after sunset, placing homes in the firing line just as families sit down to break their fast in the holy month of Ramadan.
Several said relatives and neighbours ŌĆŗhave fled.
"The situation in Afghanistan and Pakistan remains tense amid active conflict along the border," the United Nations refugee agency said, adding that ŌĆŗsome 115,000 people in Afghanistan and 3,000 in Pakistan were thought to have fled their homes.
Several countries have offered to negotiate a truce, most recently ŌĆīTurkey, although ŌüĀthe Iran war has diverted the attention of most Gulf states that had stepped forward.
Pakistani government spokesperson Mosharraf Zaidi said no negotiations were taking place to end the conflict.
"There is nothing to talk about. There will be no dialogue and no negotiations," he told state-owned Pakistan TV. "Terrorism from Afghanistan has to end - that is AfghanistanŌĆÖs problem. Pakistan's responsibility is to protect its citizens."
The conflict began ŌĆŗlast week with Pakistani air strikes ŌĆŗinside Afghanistan that Islamabad said ŌüĀtargeted militant strongholds. Afghanistan called the strikes a violation of sovereignty and announced retaliatory operations.
Islamabad has said Kabul provides safe haven to militants executing attacks on Pakistan from its soil. The Taliban has denied ŌĆŗaiding such groups and said militancy in Pakistan is an internal problem.
On Friday, the Taliban's defence ŌĆŗministry said it had ŌüĀalso struck a military base in Pakistan's southwestern Balochistan province. Reuters could not verify the strike, and Pakistan's military has not reported any damage in the region.
Both sides have regularly said they inflicted heavy damage on the other and killed hundreds of opposition troops, without providing evidence. ŌüĀReuters has ŌĆŗnot been able to verify the reports.
The U.N. mission in Afghanistan has said 56 ŌĆŗcivilians have been killed in the country and 128 wounded since fighting began. The Taliban government has said 110 civilians have been killed.
Pakistan has rejected both sets of ŌĆŗfigures, saying it targets only militants and support infrastructure.

item.title: CK Hutchison steps up legal push as Panama moves to seize ports
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Hong Kong conglomerate CK Hutchison (0001.HK) , opens new tab said on Friday it has escalated its legal fight in Panama after ŌĆŗthe government seized its port operations, petitioning for a review ŌĆīof the decree behind the takeover.
The company said Panama unlawfully occupied facilities, seized property and ignored consultations, prompting further national and international ŌĆŗlegal action.
CK Hutchison's Panama Ports Company (PPC), which manages ŌĆŗtwo terminals near the globally strategic Panama Canal, filed ŌüĀan administrative petition seeking reconsideration of Panama's executive action ŌĆŗthat led to the occupation of its facilities and ŌĆŗconfiscation of its property, CK Hutchison said.
Panamanian authorities searched the local ports unit of CK Hutchison a week ago, esclating the rift over ŌĆŗthe control of the two ports.
Panama's government in January annulled ŌĆŗthe deals that gave the company control of the two canal ŌĆīports ŌüĀfollowing a court ruling deeming the contracts unconstitutional.
The government said last week The Balboa and Cristobal ports at the centre of the dispute would be operated temporarily by Maersk (MAERSKb.CO) , opens new tab ŌĆŗand MSC.
CK Hutchison ŌĆŗhad agreed last ŌüĀyear to a $23 billion sale of dozens of ports worldwide, including the Panamanian terminals, to ŌĆŗa consortium led by BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab and Mediterranean Shipping ŌĆŗCompany.
The ŌüĀdeal has been criticised by Beijing but welcomed by U.S. President Donald Trump, who had declared he wanted to "reclaim" the ŌüĀPanama ŌĆŗCanal to reduce Chinese influence over ŌĆŗthe canal's key assets.

item.title: Iran war chokes aid corridors, obstructing global relief efforts
item.description:
GENEVA, March 6 (Reuters) - Key humanitarian air, sea and land ŌĆŗroutes are being constricted by disruption from the war in the Middle East, delaying life-saving shipments to ŌĆīsome of the world's worst crises, 10 aid officials have told Reuters.
The U.S.ŌĆōIsraeli war on Iran entered its seventh day on Friday, convulsing global markets and disrupting supply chains with airspace closures and the halt of shipping through the critical Strait of Hormuz .
Aid to Gaza and Sudan is grinding to ŌĆŗa halt and costs are soaring for help to the hundreds of millions suffering hunger crises around the ŌĆŗworld.
"People in dire need of assistance will have to wait longer for food," said Jean-Martin Bauer, ŌüĀDirector of Food Security at the World Food Programme.
Already, tents, tarpaulins and lamps destined for the Israeli-occupied Palestinian territories ŌĆŗof Gaza and the West Bank have become stuck in the supply chain, the International Organization for Migration said.
Aid groups say higher operational costs are straining budgets already facing massive donor cuts . The IOM said shipping firms were demanding emergency surcharges of approximately $3,000 per container.
Humanitarian groups stocking goods for rapid regional deployment at warehouses in Dubai's Humanitarian Hub face challenges moving ŌĆŗsupplies onto transit routes.
The International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies cannot move trauma kits to help the ŌĆŗIranian Red Crescent with search and rescue from its Dubai hub, where they sit in a 10 million Swiss franc ($13 million) emergency stockpile, ŌĆīsaid ŌüĀCecile Terraz, a director at the IFRC.
The group cannot move stock through Jebel Ali port - the region's largest container terminal, which was set on fire by the debris of an intercepted missile - from where cargo normally moves onto planes or into the Strait of Hormuz.
The World Health Organization's Dubai hub operations are also frozen, regional director Hanan Balkhy said, obstructing 50 emergency ŌĆŗrequests from 25 countries and ŌĆŗhampering operations such as ŌüĀpolio vaccination.
Ripple effects farther afield are also likely.
Famine-struck Sudan is particularly exposed due to additional restrictions since February 28 on the Suez Canal and the Bab el-Mandeb Strait at the southern entrance ŌĆŗto the Red Sea, the UNHCR said.
"We are particularly concerned about Africa," said a spokeswoman, ŌĆŗadding that ŌüĀsome cargoes were being sent around the Cape of Good Hope. The route takes up to three weeks longer.
Costs for fuel, transportation and insurance are also rising, and Terraz said the IFRC may have to cut deliveries to the Iranian Red Crescent.
Emma Maspero, ŌüĀsenior manager ŌĆŗin Copenhagen of the supply division of the U.N. children's body UNICEF, ŌĆŗsaid she hoped flights carrying perishable humanitarian goods such as vaccines could be prioritised amid the airspace restrictions. ($1 = 0.7799 Swiss francs)

item.title: Kosovo president dissolves parliament, calls snap election after failed presidential vote
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Kosovo's President Vjosa Osmani on Friday dissolved parliament and called snap elections after lawmakers failed to elect ŌĆŗa new head of state within a constitutional deadline.
The parliament ŌĆīhad until midnight on Thursday to choose a president before Osmani's term ends in April, but opposition parties refused to participate in the vote. Under the Balkan country's ŌĆŗlaw, failure to elect a new president triggers snap parliamentary ŌĆŗelections.
The move extends a political deadlock in Kosovo, which is ŌüĀEurope's youngest nation and has aspirations to join the European Union. It ŌĆŗhad no functioning government for most of last year as the fractured ŌĆŗparliament failed to elect a speaker for months.
The next elections will be the third in just over a year. Kosovo already held a snap election on December ŌĆŗ28 after failing to form a government following the February 2025 poll.
"A ŌĆŗparliament that cannot elect a president cannot continue indefinitely to drag out the process ŌĆīas ŌüĀis being attempted," Osmani said in a statement. ŌĆ£No one should wish for another political cycle, especially at this moment when the country needs stability."
Osmani is due to meet political parties on Friday before setting an election ŌĆŗdate.
A resounding election ŌĆŗwin by Prime ŌüĀMinister Albin Kurti's Vetevendosje party in December looked likely to end the stalemate. At the time, many political analysts ŌĆŗbelieved that the opposition would participate in votes to ŌĆŗelect ŌüĀthe president.
However, although Vetevendosje won enough seats in the parliament to form the government it failed to secure the opposition's participation required to elect ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗpresident.
The opposition parties have asked for a ŌĆŗconsensual candidate, but Kurti has nominated his foreign minister, Glauk Konjufca.

item.title: Indonesia to restrict social media access for children under 16, minister says
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 6 (Reuters) - Indonesia will restrict access to social media platforms for children under 16, its communications and digital ministry said on Friday, making it the latest country to install online guardrails ŌĆŗto reduce the risks of addiction and cyberbullying.
A number of governments have imposed curbs on ŌĆīsocial media for children amid mounting concerns over the impact that social media is having on the safety and mental health of minors.
Australia introduced a ban on social media for under-16s in December , and Spain also said last month ŌĆŗthat it would ban access to social media for minors under 16.
Indonesia's neighbour Malaysia announced in ŌĆŗNovember that it would also ban social media for users under ŌüĀthe age of 16 starting from 2026.
Meutya Hafid, Indonesia's Communications and Digital Minister, ŌĆŗsaid in a video statement that the government will "delay access" to social media accounts for children ŌĆŗunder 16 through a ministerial regulation issued on Friday.
Starting March 28, accounts owned by children under 16 on "high risk platforms" will be gradually deactivated, Meutya said, adding the platforms include TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, ŌĆŗand Roblox.
"The process will be done gradually until all platforms perform their obligations," ŌĆŗshe said, without elaborating on what they need to do to meet the new requirements.
"We realise this ŌĆīmay ŌüĀcause discomfort in the beginning. Children may complain and parents may be confused dealing with their complaints."
"Our children are facing risks, from porn, cyberbullying, online fraud to most importantly, addiction," she said, adding Indonesia will be the first non-Western country to impose ŌĆŗsuch restrictions.
TikTok, Meta, which ŌĆŗowns Facebook and ŌüĀInstagram, and Roblox did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The details of the new regulation have yet to be revealed. Ministry officials did ŌĆŗnot immediately respond to a request for comment.
Internet penetration in ŌĆŗIndonesia, a ŌüĀcountry of about 280 million people, reached 79.5% in 2024, according to a survey of 8,700 people by the Indonesia internet service providers' association.
The survey showed 48% of children ŌüĀunder ŌĆŗ12 had access to the internet, with some ŌĆŗrespondents of that age group using Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok. The survey showed internet penetration stood at 87% ŌĆŗamong "Gen Z" users aged 12 to 27.

item.title: Romania will not host nuclear components on its territory for deterrence, says president
item.description:
BUCHAREST, March 6 (Reuters) - Romania will not host nuclear components on its territory in the medium term, centrist President Nicusor ŌĆŗDan said late on Thursday, in response to ŌĆīFrench plans to expand nuclear deterrence and collaborate with European partners.
Earlier this week, President Emmanuel Macron said France would expand its nuclear arsenal ŌĆŗand potentially allow European partners to host French ŌĆŗwarplanes on nuclear deterrence missions.
France and Germany have ŌüĀestablished a nuclear steering group to discuss deterrence issues and ŌĆŗwill start concrete cooperation this year. Greece, Poland, the ŌĆŗNetherlands, Belgium, Denmark and Sweden could take part in French nuclear wargames, Macron said, while on Tuesday Romania said it too had ŌĆŗbeen invited to talks.
"As a NATO state, Romania is ŌĆŗunder the NATO nuclear umbrella provided by the United States," Dan ŌĆītold ŌüĀreporters after an official visit to Warsaw.
"Being protected by the NATO umbrella does not imply the presence of nuclear elements on Romanian territory, and in the medium term, ŌĆŗhosting nuclear ŌĆŗcomponents is out ŌüĀof the question."
Though France and Britain are both nuclear powers, most European countries ŌĆŗhave relied primarily on the United States for ŌĆŗdeterring ŌüĀany potential adversaries ŌĆö a decades-old pillar of transatlantic security.
Macron's initiative comes amid criticism from the Trump administration of Europe's ŌüĀwillingness ŌĆŗand ability to defend itself ŌĆŗand also amid concerns about growing global geopolitical instability as the United ŌĆŗStates and Israel strike Iran .

item.title: Australia complains to China after encounter between military helicopters
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 6 (Reuters) - Australia has raised concerns with China following an "unsafe and unprofessional" encounter between two military ŌĆŗhelicopters, the defence department said on Friday.
An ŌĆīAustralian military helicopter was flying over international waters in the Yellow Sea when it was intercepted by a Chinese helicopter ŌĆŗon Wednesday, a statement said.
The Chinese helicopter ŌĆŗmatched the Australian aircraft's altitude before "closing in to ŌüĀan unsafe distance", increasing speed and then ŌĆŗrolling towards it, requiring the Australian crew to take "evasive ŌĆŗaction".
"This was an unsafe and unprofessional manoeuvre that posed a risk to our aircraft and its personnel," the statement said.
Australia ŌĆŗwas undertaking a routine patrol in the ŌĆŗYellow Sea as part of the international effort to enforce ŌĆīUnited ŌüĀNations Security Council sanctions against North Korea, it said.
No injuries were reported in the encounter between the People's Liberation Army-Navy helicopter and the aircraft of ŌĆŗthe Australian ŌĆŗDefence Force.
The ŌüĀincident is the latest in a series of military encounters involving China that Australia ŌĆŗhas called out publicly in similar terms.
In October , ŌĆŗit ŌüĀalso criticised as "unsafe and unprofessional" the actions of a Chinese fighter jet that dropped flares near one ŌüĀof ŌĆŗits maritime patrol planes.
The Chinese ŌĆŗembassy did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Maersk suspends two shipping services due to Middle East crisis
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 6 (Reuters) - Maersk (MAERSKb.CO) , opens new tab , one of the world's biggest container shipping groups, said on Friday it ŌĆŗhas temporarily suspended two services linking ŌĆīthe Middle East to Asia and Europe as the Iran conflict continues to disrupt global supply ŌĆŗchains.
The Danish group said in a ŌĆŗstatement it would halt its FM1 service ŌüĀconnecting the Far East to the ŌĆŗMiddle East and its ME11 service linking the ŌĆŗMiddle East to Europe
"This decision has been taken as a precautionary measure to ensure the safety of ŌĆŗour personnel and vessels while minimizing ŌĆŗoperational disruption across our wider network," Maersk said in ŌĆīan ŌüĀadvisory to customers.
Tensions in the Middle East have escalated sharply after the United States and Israel launched their most ambitious attacks on ŌĆŗIran in decades ŌĆŗon ŌüĀSaturday in an operation that killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
The ŌĆŗcrisis has left 147 container ships ŌĆŗsheltering ŌüĀin the Gulf, triggering port congestion and freight rate increases that are rippling across ŌüĀglobal supply ŌĆŗchains from Asia to ŌĆŗEurope, according to freight analytics firm Xeneta.

item.title: South Korea, US militaries discuss moving Patriot missiles to Iran war, Seoul says
item.description:
SEOUL, March 6 (Reuters) - South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun said on Friday the ŌĆŗU.S. and South Korean militaries are discussing the possible redeployment of some ŌĆīU.S. Patriot missile defence systems based in South Korea to be used in the war against Iran .
Cho was responding to questions at a parliamentary hearing following ŌĆŗmedia reports on Friday that units of the U.S. ŌĆŗmobile missile interceptor system had been moved to the ŌüĀOsan Air Base in South Korea from other locations in ŌĆŗthe country.

item.title: 52 French ships stuck in the Gulf amid Iran conflict, minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 6 (Reuters) - Around 50 French ships are ŌĆŗcurrently blocked in the ŌĆīPersian Gulf and another eight in the Red ŌĆŗSea, France's Transport Minister ŌĆŗPhilippe Tabarot said on ŌüĀFriday, as Paris ŌĆŗseeks support to build a ŌĆŗcoalition of countries to secure shipping traffic in the ŌĆŗregion.
"There are around ŌĆŗfifty ships - 52 to be precise - ŌĆīin ŌüĀthe Persian Gulf and eight in the Red Sea, and we ŌĆŗare ŌĆŗalso ŌüĀin permanent contact with the crews, ŌĆŗas there are ŌĆŗFrench ŌüĀsailors aboard a number of these vessels," Tabarot ŌüĀtold ŌĆŗFrench broadcaster ŌĆŗCNews/Europe 1.

item.title: China's decarbonisation plan takes cautious steps as world backtracks on climate
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China on Thursday released a new five-year plan to reduce the carbon emissions of its economy, mainly by relying on its booming renewable sector to limit coal use and greenhouse gases, but some analysts viewed it as underwhelming.
Under the plan, China aims to reduce its carbon intensity, or carbon emissions per unit of gross domestic product, by 17% ŌĆŗfrom 2026 to 2030, not enough to meet its pledge under the Paris climate agreement of a 65% cut from 2005 to 2030, analysts said.
The new plan did not, as international ŌĆŗobservers hoped it would, set a goal for total emissions to head lower before 2030.
China's carbon intensity goal is "alarmingly lax," said Lauri Myllyvirta, co-founder ŌüĀof the Helsinki-based Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA), who said it would allow emissions to increase by 3% to 6% over the next five years given China's economic growth ŌĆŗtarget .
CREA research previously found that China would need a 23% cut over the next five years to meet its Paris commitment.
Since Washington exited the Paris agreement and retreated from its climate policies last ŌĆŗyear, China has looked to fill a void in global leadership on climate and clean energy left by the world's biggest historic emitter.
During the five-year plan that ended last year, China reduced its carbon intensity by just 12%, missing its previous target of 18%. That failure and its modest new decarbonisation goal are unlikely to inspire other countries to set their own ambitious emissions-reductions targets, analysts said.
"China is quickly becoming the largest emitter historically speaking, ŌĆŗand so the legitimacy to claim that developed economies have to do a much larger share is kind of running out for China, so they know that they have to do ŌĆŗmore," said Nis Grunberg, lead analyst for the Mercator Institute for China Studies in Germany.
Under its new program, Beijing outlined annual targets for reaching 30 million metric tons of coal replaced by renewable energy in ŌĆīfive years but ŌüĀdid not impose limits on coal consumption. The country is rapidly expanding its wind and solar power capacity.
With the new plan, Beijing has shifted focus from controlling the energy intensity of its economy to carbon intensity.
The so-called "dual control" system will introduce industry, company and project-level carbon emissions controls, according to the five-year plan.
In 2026, China plans to cut its carbon intensity by around 3.8%, according to a report from China's top state planner, the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC). China has said it expects that its carbon emissions will peak before 2030.
Still, the new carbon intensity ŌĆŗtarget will not be easy to achieve, said ŌĆŗYao Zhe, a Beijing-based policy adviser for ŌüĀGreenpeace East Asia, pointing to continued energy demand growth from Chinese manufacturing .
"The focus increasingly shifts to more challenging areas - decarbonising hard-to-abate sectors, integrating a much larger share of renewables into the power system and building a more flexible and resilient electricity system," said Muyi Yang, a senior energy analyst ŌĆŗfor Ember, an energy think tank.
China's carbon emissions fell 0.3% last year thanks to reductions in the transport, power, cement and metals sectors, but ŌĆŗit is not yet clear ŌüĀwhether emissions will go up again before peaking.
While China has fallen short of some short-term climate targets, Li Shuo, director of the China Climate Hub at the Asia Society Policy Institute in the U.S., said renewable energy technology will help China bend its emissions curve downward.
President Xi Jinping told the United Nations in September that his country would expand wind and solar power capacity, ŌüĀalready the ŌĆŗworld leader, six fold from 2020 levels by 2035. Based on current trends, China is on pace to exceed ŌĆŗthat target.
In the next five years, China will introduce minimum quotas for renewable energy consumption, according to the NDRC, as well as measures to phase out outdated coal-fired equipment and facilities and reach a peak in coal consumption. Previously, ŌĆŗChina had set out to phase down its coal use.
(This story has been corrected to fix the base year for China's Paris agreement carbon intensity target to 2005, not 2026, in paragraph 2)

item.title: Trump administration considering looser protections for North Atlantic right whale
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - The Trump administration on Tuesday took the first ŌĆŗstep toward loosening regulations ŌĆīthat protect the endangered North Atlantic right whale from ŌĆŗvessel strikes.
Here are some ŌĆŗdetails:

item.title: Coastal El Nino threatens Peru's booming agro-exports with heavy rains
item.description:
LIMA, March 3 (Reuters) - Peru's booming agricultural export sector could suffer this year from heavy rains and floods caused by the coastal El Nino weather phenomenon, business leaders and climate agencies warn, ŌĆŗas effects begin to be felt on local fruit plantations.
The coastal El Nino is ŌĆīa periodic climate event tied to several months of abnormally warm waters primarily off the northern Pacific coast of Peru and Ecuador, generating heavy rainfall.
Gabriel Amaro, leader of the agricultural producers' association, said that although the phenomenon ŌĆŗis currently weak, it has destroyed some 6,000 hectares (14,826 acres) of northern Peruvian fruit ŌĆŗcrops and could become moderate before July.
"It wasn't in our plans," Amaro told ŌüĀReuters, noting companies were already taking precautions for the rains, which could damage infrastructure as ŌĆŗwell as harvests.
Traditionally a key mining nation, Peru's agricultural sector broke new records with $15 billion in exports last ŌĆŗyear - up 17% from the prior year as it shipped blueberries, grapes, avocados, cocoa beans, asparagus, mangoes and citrus fruits primarily to the U.S., China and Europe.
Peruvian farmers are currently harvesting avocados and will begin picking blueberries, ŌĆŗcitrus fruits and pomegranates in May.
"This year, export growth will most likely be lower," Amaro said.
Peru's ENFEN climate agency predicts above-normal rainfall on Peru's northern coast starting in March, without ruling ŌĆīout "extreme events."
Mario ŌüĀSalazar, chairman of the agro-industry committee of the private exporters' association, told a recent press conference that mango production was already down 10% and blueberries were at risk of developing fungi after the rains and later becoming dried out from post-rain heat.
Peru is the world's top exporter of blueberries , ŌĆŗwhich brought in $2.5 billion last ŌĆŗyear.
The Lima Chamber ŌüĀof Commerce estimates that a weak-to-moderate coastal El Nino could bring daily potential losses of over 291 million soles ($85 million) across the agriculture, trade and ŌĆŗmanufacturing sectors of seven Peruvian regions.
Heavy rains that damaged transport infrastructure and ŌĆŗbasic service ŌüĀpushed the government to declare a state of emergency across 14 regions last week.
Floods and landslides during the rainy season, which began late last year, have affected 85,000, according to Peru's disaster agency CENEPRED.
In 2017 , ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗcoastal El Nino left 162 people dead and caused economic ŌĆŗlosses equivalent to 2% of gross domestic product.
Forecasters have predicted that the global El Nino phenomenon , which impacts temperatures and rainfall ŌĆŗworldwide, could begin this year.
($1 = 3.4138 soles)

item.title: EU axes steel emissions label from 'made in Europe' law, draft shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 3 (Reuters) - The European ŌĆŗUnion has scrapped plans for an emissions label for steel in its upcoming "made in Europe" ŌĆīlaw, which had initially been intended to help make green steel central to efforts to revive industries, a draft of the proposal showed.
The last-minute change ahead of expected publication on Wednesday of a proposal for a law officially named ŌĆŗthe EU's Industrial Accelerator Act would be a blow to low-carbon steelmakers. They have called ŌĆŗfor the label to help make their products more visible and attractive to ŌüĀconsumers.
Some departments inside the European Commission, however, expressed concern that the label would create more complex ŌĆŗbureaucracy for companies when another EU product labelling law is also being drawn up to address emissions from ŌĆŗsteel, EU officials told Reuters.
Earlier drafts of the law , reviewed by Reuters, included a voluntary label for the emissions intensity of steel, and a methodology companies would use to calculate this. A more recent draft, reviewed by Reuters on Tuesday, ŌĆŗaxed the label.
The latest draft said Brussels would instead include measures ŌĆŗto support demand for low-carbon steel through an EU law on sustainability standards for products. It did not give a ŌĆīdate ŌüĀfor when this proposal would be made public.
The Industrial Accelerator Act has been repeatedly delayed and the draft could change before publication. A European Commission spokesperson declined to comment.
The law will require that when public money is spent to support technologies, a minimum share of those products is manufactured locally.
The latest ŌĆŗdraft, seen by Reuters, still ŌĆŗincluded a requirement ŌüĀthat steel bought through public procurement would need to be at least 25% low-carbon, to try to kick-start demand for greener steel.
Low-carbon steel producer Hydnum Steel ŌĆŗsaid in a statement a label was needed now and the EU ŌĆŗshould not "let the 'perfect' ŌüĀbe the enemy of the 'timely'".
"Without this classification, the impact of measures designed to create lead markets will be delayed, failing to direct vital investment toward European producers at this critical juncture," it said.
A spokesperson for ŌüĀsteel industry ŌĆŗassociation Eurofer said delaying the label "risks kicking decisions further down ŌĆŗthe road at a time when investors need certainty now".
Across Europe, a number of green steel projects have been delayed or run into ŌĆŗdifficulties as the technology remains new and investment costs high.

item.title: India braces for hotter-than-normal summer, more heatwave days expected
item.description:
MUMBAI, Feb 28 (Reuters) - India is set to experience a hotter-than-normal summer this year, with heatwave days during March and May expected to exceed the seasonal average, the weather office said on Saturday.
In March, the first month of the summer season, minimum temperatures are likely to remain above average across most areas, said Mrutyunjay Mohapatra, director-general of the India Meteorological Department (IMD).
In February, both maximum and minimum temperatures were above average, making it the fifth-warmest February since records began in 1901, according to IMD data.
Winter crops such as wheat, rapeseed, and chickpeas are planted from October to December and require cold weather conditions throughout their growth cycle for optimal yields.
Above-average temperatures in March could affect the grain size of maturing winter-sown crops, potentially reducing yields, said a Mumbai-based dealer with a global trade house.
India, the world's second-largest wheat producer and biggest importer of edible oils, is counting on bumper 2026 crops to export surplus wheat and cut costly imports of palm, soy and sunflower oils.
"Above-normal heatwave days are expected over most parts of the country during March to May 2026," said Mohapatra.

item.title: Anti-data centre protests mount
item.description:
LONDON, Feb 27 (Reuters) - Activists are set to take to British streets on Friday for two days of protests against the expansion of data centres to serve booming demand for artificial intelligence, and the impact of the facilities on communities and the environment.
The protests, coordinated by environmental charity Global Action Plan, are part of a growing international backlash against the power- and water-hungry sites needed to meet surging demand for AI computing power.
"Big Tech's unchecked construction of hyperscale AI data centres is putting the UK's climate targets at risk," said Oliver Hayes, Head of Campaigns at Global Action Plan, in a statement.
Among the biggest is set to be the 'March Against The Machines' event starting outside the offices of OpenAI on Saturday at midday.
While there is no formal definition of what a data centre is in Britain or how many there are, a techUK report , opens new tab from November 2024 put the number at around 450.
The British energy regulator said 1 40 , opens new tab data centres had signalled they wanted to plug into the grid and could require 50 gigawatts of power. By comparison, it said peak British electricity demand on February 11 was 45 GW.
OpenAI said in January it would create a community plan for each of the sites in its Stargate operation, a $500 billion initiative to build AI data centres for training and inference.
Tech companies are investing directly in power infrastructure as energy access becomes a critical constraint on AI expansion, with the push for larger and more numerous data centres driving electricity demand higher.
In Havering, east London, Ian Pirie, Coordinator Friends of the Earth Havering, said plans to build locally were "completely inappropriate in a semi-rural Green Belt area", citing what he called its power and water needs and the destruction of farmland.
Leigh Tugwood, Co-chair of Iver Heath Residents Association, protesting against a build in Buckinghamshire, said he was concerned that datacentre development was being fast-tracked at the expense of local communities.
"We are, therefore, in support of a moratorium on all future hyperscale data centre development unless and until there is informed debate, a public inquiry and a meaningful community-designed engagement framework that ensures ownership of the process by those most likely to be impacted," he said.

item.title: CEO of World Economic Forum quits
item.description:
ZURICH, Feb 26 (Reuters) - The president and CEO of the World Economic Forum, Borge Brende, said he was stepping down on Thursday, a few weeks after the forum launched an independent investigation into his relationship with late U.S. sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
Brende, who became president of the WEF in 2017, announced his decision in a statement following disclosures from the U.S. Justice Department that showed the Norwegian had three business dinners with Epstein and had also communicated with the disgraced financier via email and text message.
"After careful consideration, I have decided to step down as President and CEO of the World Economic Forum. My time here, spanning 8-1/2 years, has been profoundly rewarding," said Brende, a former Norwegian foreign minister.
Issued by the WEF, the statement made no mention of Epstein. However, Brende told Norwegian media he was sorry about how he had handled his dealings with the American and that he did not want the issue to be a distraction for the forum, which organises the annual Davos summit.
Brende has previously said he was unaware of Epstein's past and criminal activities before first meeting him in 2018, and that he regretted not having investigated him more thoroughly.
Brende's decision to quit follows a series of revelations relating to Epstein, who in 2008 was convicted of soliciting prostitution from a minor. The revelations have roiled business and political elites, and even the British royal family.
In their own statement, Andre Hoffmann and Larry Fink, co-chairs of the Geneva-based WEF, said an independent review conducted by outside counsel into Brende's ties with Epstein had concluded and had found no additional concerns beyond what has already been disclosed.
Brende told Norwegian business daily Dagens Naeringsliv that while the review had not uncovered any previously unknown issues relating to Epstein, the case risked drawing attention away from the forum's work.
He said he regretted he had not been more open about the dinners and subsequent communications he had with Epstein.
"Like many others, I have felt a great deal of discomfort at being linked to Jeffrey Epstein, and I was afraid that the contact would be portrayed as something other than what it was. That is the honest answer," Brende told the newspaper.
Text messages from 2018-19 released among the Epstein files appear to show a friendly tone between the two men, with Brende calling Epstein a "brilliant host" and saying "missing you Sir" after a dinner with Epstein, former Trump aide Steve Bannon and a former Norwegian government minister. Their last communication was a week before Epstein was arrested in 2019.
Hoffmann, vice-chair of Swiss pharma giant Roche (ROG.S) , opens new tab , and Fink, the CEO of BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab , thanked Brende for his contributions to the forum.
"His dedication and leadership have been instrumental during a pivotal period of reforms for the organization, leading to a successful annual meeting in Davos," they said.
WEF Managing Director Alois Zwinggi will serve as interim president and CEO, and the forum's Board of Trustees would oversee the leadership transition, including a plan to identify a permanent successor, they added.
The U.S. Justice Department has released more than 3 million pages of documents relating to Epstein, who died by suicide in jail in 2019 while awaiting trial on sex-trafficking charges.
His ties to a long list of business and political leaders, including U.S. President Donald Trump, former President Bill Clinton and Tesla CEO Elon Musk are under close scrutiny.
Abroad, the revelations have prompted criminal investigations of Britain's Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, the former Duke of York, and other prominent figures.
Brende's resignation follows the departure last year of the WEF's founder and longstanding chair Klaus Schwab after he came under pressure from a whistleblower alleging misconduct.
The WEF subsequently said an internal investigation had found no evidence of material wrongdoing by Schwab.

item.title: Warmer winters leave Pakistan festival on thin ice
item.description:
By Akhtar Soomro
Filed Feb. 24, 2026, 10:38 p.m. GMT
Photography by┬ĀAkhtar Soomro
Reporting by Ariba Shahid and Salah Uddin
Filed Feb 27, 2026, 23:00 p.m.┬ĀGMT
Every winter for decades, the pool in front of Aleena GulŌĆÖs house in PakistanŌĆÖs Hunza Valley has transformed into an ice rink, framed by jagged Himalayan peaks and the stone walls of Altit Fort.
This year, it did not.
Gul can see the swimming pool that doubles as a hockey arena from her bedroom.
For years, she would wake up at dawn, lace her skates and step straight from her front door onto solid ice.
After four years away at university, she returned eager to play again, but has found herself waiting for winter to arrive.
ŌĆ£ThereŌĆÖs a big difference between 2018 and now,ŌĆØ said Gul, 21, captain of her team and among the first women in Hunza to take up the sport. ŌĆ£Winter used to begin in November and everything would freeze. ItŌĆÖs January now and the ice still hasnŌĆÖt frozen properly.ŌĆØ
Across PakistanŌĆÖs northern mountains, winters are arriving later and behaving unpredictably. Cold spells are shorter, freezeŌĆōthaw cycles unstable. In the wider Hindu KushŌĆōHimalayan region, scientists report fewer extreme cold events and shorter snow seasons; what locals call a ŌĆ£snow drought,ŌĆØ when snowfall fails to settle.
The change is visible in Hunza. Data compiled by WeatherWalay, a climate analytics platform, shows average winter precipitation has fallen about 30% since the late 2010s, recording four consecutive years below normal. Some recent winters have also been 2ŌĆō3┬░C milder, so there is less snow to sustain the ice.
Unlike European resorts with artificial snow, HunzaŌĆÖs tournament depends entirely on natural ice.
In a valley heavily dependent on tourism, winter sport now hinges on weather that no longer follows old rhythms.
Ice under pressure
For eight seasons, AltitŌĆÖs pool has hosted the Karakoram Interlude, a community-run tournament that draws teams from across northern Pakistan and extends the tourist season beyond summer.
On good years, the rink glows under floodlights, spectators leaning over stone parapets tea cups in hand, their breath rising in white clouds.
This year, organisers prepared the rink as always, pouring water at night and smoothing the surface by hand to allow temperatures of below ŌĆō20┬░C to set the layers.
ŌĆ£We stayed up until 3 a.m. trying to help it freeze,ŌĆØ Gul said. ŌĆ£WeŌĆÖre doing everything we can.ŌĆØ
In 2024, ŌĆ£we started seeing a sudden change in weather patterns like snowfall, freezing levels and overall temperatures,ŌĆØ said Sadiq Saleem, 31, president of the Altit Town Management Society and a founding member of the youth organisation SCARF, which pioneered ice hockey in the valley.
Thin puddles formed where blades scratched the ice. Hairline cracks spread beneath the surface. Organisers pressed their palms to the ice, checking for flex and listening for cracks.
ŌĆ£We worked on this arena for a week,ŌĆØ said Naseer Uddin, 34, co-founder of SCARF. ŌĆ£But when the sun came out strongly, it ruined everything.ŌĆØ
The opening ceremony went ahead under floodlights, but organisers warned the rink was too fragile to support entire teams.
Only captains stepped forward to unveil jerseys beside sponsors, wary of the thinning ice.
The traditional opening night friendly match was cancelled.
Chasing cold
There was little time to argue with the weather.
Within hours, organisers moved through AltitŌĆÖs lanes, calling players and knocking on doors. The tournament was moved nearly two hours north to Sost, one of the last towns in Pakistan before the Chinese border, where colder air offered better odds of sufficient ice.
They had done this before.
Two winters ago, when the pool in Altit also failed to freeze, the ice in Sost, about 2,800 metres (9,186 feet) above sea level ŌĆö roughly 300 to 400 metres higher than Altit ŌĆö held firm.
This year, that solution faltered too.
For Gul, it felt like chasing a season that kept retreating.
In Sost, the rink lay on an exposed stretch of valley floor near the Khunjerab Pass, beneath steep, wind-cut ridges funnelling cold air down from even higher elevations.
And while the surface was firmer than AltitŌĆÖs pool, some parts were thin. Players tested it cautiously before committing their weight.
Three matches were scheduled that first day. Only one went ahead.
ŌĆ£When we reached the rink the ice wasnŌĆÖt in good condition,ŌĆØ Gul said. ŌĆ£Teams still played, but it was very difficult. WeŌĆÖve never experienced this before.ŌĆØ
Skaters stumbled where the ice had cracked and softened, blades catching unexpectedly.
Each evening, organisers poured water across the rink, hoping the temperatures would help it freeze.
ŌĆ£Our event depends entirely on natural ice,ŌĆØ said Saliha Ibrahim, 21, part of the organising team. ŌĆ£If we canŌĆÖt improve the surface, we may have to consider changing the venue again.ŌĆØ
Winter canŌĆÖt pay the bills
It is not only the players who feel the strain.
Unpredictable winters ripple through caf├®s, guesthouses and transport operators. Smaller guesthouses without heating struggle as pipes freeze, cutting off water, then thaw and refreeze unpredictably, raising the risk of bursts and costly repairs.
Globally, fewer regions can reliably host winter sports as temperatures rise.
In Hunza, a district of fewer than 100,000 people, residents are confronting that reality without artificial snow or refrigeration systems ŌĆö and without certainty.
Winter has long been quieter than summer in Hunza, but residents say erratic snowfall, floods and impassable roads deter visitors who come for snow-covered peaks and frozen lakes, just as the Karakoram Interlude had begun drawing travellers from across Pakistan and beyond.
Naseera Khatoon runs Murku Caf├® overlooking the pool in Altit. Her daughter, Arifa, plays ice hockey. In previous years, tournament week meant steady customers, with families lingering long after matches ended over meals of traditional soup and dumplings.
This year, despite the ceremony, her caf├® remained quiet.
ŌĆ£Usually we earn income during the tournament,ŌĆØ she said. ŌĆ£This time, there was very little.ŌĆØ
She remembers winters when snowfall was heavy enough to close schools for months and families stored food in advance of long, cold spells.
ŌĆ£We used to dry vegetables and store groceries because roads would close [in winter],ŌĆØ she said. ŌĆ£Now food is available year-round, but the snow and ice are disappearing.ŌĆØ
Kareem ul Hayat, who oversees the restored 900-year-old Altit Fort, said winter tourism had grown as events like ice hockey drew visitors. In recent winters, he said, numbers had waned.
ŌĆ£In the past, the mountains stayed white,ŌĆØ he said. ŌĆ£Now the snow disappears quickly.ŌĆØ
Title on thin ice
Back in Sost, the tournament continued.
Players adjusted their game to the more challenging conditions. Spectators tightened their scarves against the wind.
Yahya Karim, 20, a player from Altit, said the surface was unlike anything the team had trained on.
ŌĆ£I expected better ice conditions, but when I saw the rink I felt a bit sad,ŌĆØ he said. ŌĆ£Many of our players fell. The surface had too many bumps and wasnŌĆÖt strong.ŌĆØ
They had chased winter north. But even there, the cold remained elusive.
On a scarred, uneven rink, GulŌĆÖs team emerged victorious, a title claimed on fragile ice far from home.
The Wider Image
Photography: Akhtar Soomro
Reporting: Ariba Shahid, Salah Uddin
Photo editing: Maye-E and Nat Casta├▒eda
Design: Nat Casta├▒eda
Editing: Saad Sayeed, Lucy Craymer and Kate Mayberry

Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the worldŌĆÖs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: Spain's wild horses in peril, despite key role in wildfire prevention
item.description:
By Nacho Doce
Filed March 10, 2025, 10:41 a.m. GMT
Photography and reporting by Nacho Doce Reporting by Charlie Devereux
Filed: March 25, 2025, 8 a.m.┬ĀGMT
The wildfires that used to burn each summer in the woods above the Spanish village of Barro have diminished to almost none since Lucia Perez started grazing wild horses there.
ŌĆ£There used to be fires every year but since 2019 when we started coming here weŌĆÖve had one small fire in the first year and nothing since,ŌĆØ said Perez, 37, explaining how by clearing the undergrowth between the trees the horses help stop fires igniting and spreading.
Fire prevention is one of several roles wild horses play in preserving the northwestern region of GaliciaŌĆÖs delicate ecosystems, scientists say. But EuropeŌĆÖs largest herd of wild horses has dwindled to less than half the 22,000 that roamed its mountains, forests and heathlands in the 1970s.
On the Serra da Groba heath 80 km (50 miles) southwest of Barro, a herd of wild horses feasts on the yellow flowers of gorse, ŌĆ£selectively clearingŌĆØ a plant that is especially combustible, said Laura Lagos, a researcher at the University of A Coruna.
This allows other plants such as purple-flowering heather and white asphodels to thrive, she said. It also helps preserve the heather around peat bogs, abundant in sphagnum mosses, one of the most effective ecosystems for capturing carbon.
