A 2021 study by the University of A Coruna, in which Lagos took part, found that grazing heathlands by wild horses was best for preventing wildfires while promoting plant biodiversity and capturing carbon, when compared to other potential uses of the land such as planting long-term pine forests, short-term Eucalyptus forests or grazing by domestic animals.
ŌĆ£Studies show that where wild horses graze the heathŌĆÖs biodiversity increases,ŌĆØ Lagos said. ŌĆ£They are a fundamental part of the ecosystem. They benefit both the flora and the fauna associated with these open spaces.ŌĆØ
Grazing by sheep and cows can also prevent fires, but the horses have particular physiological characteristics that allow them to thrive in GaliciaŌĆÖs terrain, including moustaches that appear designed to protect their lips from the prickly gorse.
ŌĆ£They seem specially adapted to eat gorse and are hardy - a cow canŌĆÖt live year round on the heath,ŌĆØ Lagos said.
Neolithic rock carvings
Drier, hotter weather due to climate change is increasing the frequency of wildfires in Galicia. The region lost 96,900 hectares through fires between 2001 and 2023, according to Global Forest Watch.
This has coincided with the proliferation in Galicia of the Eucalyptus tree, a pyrophyte species that depends on fires to help it release and spread seeds. Eucalyptus trees also reduce pasture for the horses as only ferns grow under them.
Brought from Australia to Galicia by a missionary in the 19th century, they have spread thanks to demand for their wood from local pulp mills and now account for 28% of all trees in Galicia, according to a local government study.
In September, the village of San Colmede was spared from a fire that ripped through 270 hectares of Eucalyptus and scrub in the surrounding hills thanks to grazing by animals and by the native oaks, sweet chestnuts and birches that grow near the village and burn more slowly, acting as a shield, said firefighter Cesar Leiros.
ŌĆ£We saw the village surrounded by fire but thanks to native trees, the clearing we do, and the clearing our animals do, the village was saved,ŌĆØ Leiros said.
Rock carvings depicting wild horses being hunted by men are evidence of their existence in Galicia dating back to the Neolithic period.
In time, humans and horses developed a relationship. Known as ŌĆ£besteirosŌĆØ in the Galician dialect, people would look out for the health of the free-roaming horses in exchange for occasionally domesticating one or selling it for meat.
They would round up the horses once a year in events known as the ŌĆ£rapa das bestasŌĆØ or ŌĆ£shearing of the beastsŌĆØ to be deloused, vaccinated, and have their manes cut to make it harder for wolves to catch them.
These evolved into festivals, with the most famous in Sabucedo drawing thousands of tourists who come to see the horses wrestled to the ground to be treated.
ŌĆśEcological jewelŌĆÖ
Perez, who works in a school, runs a smaller rapa with her stonemason husband Niko Souto, 41, in the village of Amil. They donŌĆÖt make a profit, she said. The proceeds help pay for fencing to stop the horses wandering onto roads or farmland.
Costs, including vet fees, insurance, fencing, microchipping and GPS equipment for tracking, are multiplying, Souto said.
The horses are classified as livestock and Javier Alvarez-Blazquez, a retired lawyer who owns ten horses in the Serra da Groba, wants new legislation that recognises their unique circumstances.
The Galician regional government should help besteiros cover some of the costs of looking after them, he said. The sale of a foal can generate 60 euros ($65.08) but it doesnŌĆÖt compensate for expenses - excluding insurance and erecting fencing - of about 100 euros a year per horse, he said.
Increasing the number of horses could even save the government money, said Melina Barrio, an economist at the University of Alcala. She estimates that each horse carries out clearing to prevent fires that would cost between 8,000 and 10,000 euros per year if humans did it.
GaliciaŌĆÖs regional government acknowledged the role wild horses play in preventing forest fires and preserving ecosystems, but said that the current legislation was adequate for their conservation because the horses can generate an income by being sold or through tourism from the rapa festivals.
ŌĆ£Although these animals are bred in the wild, in reality they have an owner and there is an economic benefit,ŌĆØ the government said in an emailed statement.
ŌĆ£In this sense, it is logical to consider horses as livestock, in accordance with European and Spanish legislation on the matter, and therefore they are not exempt from complying with obligations relating to health, animal welfare or herd control,ŌĆØ it said.
Besteiros receive some help on costs, such as with the implanting of microchips for identification, the government added.
Carlos Souto, NikoŌĆÖs father, said the horses were descending from the hills more frequently in search of pasture as Eucalyptus plantations spread, meaning they needed more minding.
From visiting his horses once a week he is now going every morning, while Niko and Lucia go in the evening after work.
Niko said the horses were becoming used to their presence and their behaviour was changing - they were becoming less wild.
Alvarez-Blazquez said without the besteiros, the horses would die out, killed by cars or local farmers angry at the horses transgressing onto their crops and tree plantations.
ŌĆ£We are guardians doing it as a hobby. Our only aim is to preserve this animal that we believe is an ecological jewel,ŌĆØ he said.
The Wider Image
Photography and reporting: Nacho Doce
Reporting and writing: Charlie Devereux
Photo editing and design: Marta Montana and Maye-E Wong
Text editing: Janet Lawrence and Sharon Singleton
LICENSE THIS STORY
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the worldŌĆÖs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: China scientists rush to climate-proof potatoes
item.description:
By Florence Lo
Filed Nov. 18, 2024, 5:06 p.m. GMT
Photography and reporting by┬ĀFlorence Lo
Reporting by Mei Mei Chu and ┬ĀXiaoyu Yin
Filed November 27, 2024, 11:00 p.m.┬ĀGMT
In a research facility in the northwest of Beijing, molecular biologist Li Jieping and his team harvest a cluster of seven unusually small potatoes, one as tiny as a quailŌĆÖs egg, from a potted plant.
Grown under conditions that simulate predictions of higher temperatures at the end of the century, the potatoes provide an ominous sign of future food security.
At just 136 grams (4.8 oz), the tubers weigh less than half that of a typical potato in China, where the most popular varieties are often twice the size of a baseball.
China is the worldŌĆÖs biggest producer of potatoes, which are crucial to global food security because of their high yield relative to other staple crops.
But they are particularly vulnerable to heat, and climate change, driven by fossil fuel emissions, is pushing temperatures to dangerous new heights while also worsening drought and flooding.
With an urgent need to protect food supplies, Li, a researcher at the International Potato Center (CIP) in Beijing, is leading a three-year study into the effects of higher temperatures on the vegetable. His team is focusing on ChinaŌĆÖs two most common varieties.
ŌĆ£I worry about what will happen in the future,ŌĆØ Li said. ŌĆ£Farmers will harvest fewer potato tubers, it will influence food security.ŌĆØ
LiŌĆÖs team grew their crop over three months in a walk-in chamber set at 3 degrees Celsius above the current average temperature in northern Hebei and Inner Mongolia, the higher altitude provinces where potatoes are usually grown in China.
Their research, published in the journal Climate Smart Agriculture this month, found the higher temperatures accelerated tuber growth by 10 days, but cut potato yields by more than half.
Under current climate policies, the world is facing as much as 3.1 C of warming above pre-industrial levels by 2100, according to a United Nations report released in October.
In Inner Mongolia, dozens of workers clutching white sacks rush to gather potatoes dug up from the soil before the next downpour.
ŌĆ£The biggest challenge for potatoes this year is the heavy rain,ŌĆØ said manager Wang Shiyi. ŌĆ£It has caused various diseases... and greatly slowed down the harvest progress.ŌĆØ
Meanwhile, seed potato producer Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company has invested in aeroponic systems where plants are grown in the air under controlled conditions.
Farmers are increasingly demanding potato varieties that are higher-yielding and less susceptible to disease, particularly late blight, which caused the Irish Potato Famine of the mid-19th century and thrives in warm and humid conditions.
ŌĆ£Some new and more aggressive (late blight) strains have begun to appear, and they are more resistant to traditional prevention and control methods,ŌĆØ said general manager Li Xuemin, explaining the Inner Mongolia-based companyŌĆÖs strategy.
The research by CIP, which is headquartered in Lima, is part of a collaborative effort with the Chinese government to help farmers adapt to the warmer, wetter conditions.
In the greenhouse outside Li's lab, workers swab pollen on white potato flowers to develop heat-tolerant varieties.
Li says Chinese farmers will need to make changes within the next decade, planting during spring instead of the start of summer, or moving to even higher altitudes to escape the heat.
ŌĆ£Farmers have to start preparing for climate change,ŌĆØ Li said. ŌĆ£If we donŌĆÖt find a solution, they will make less money from lower yields and the price of potatoes may rise.ŌĆØ
A Chinese flag flutters next to sacks of newly harvested potatoes, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers load a lorry with sacks of newly harvested potatoes at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Sprinklers on a centre pivot irrigation system water growing potato plants, at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Old Barag Banner, Inner Mongolia, China, June 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato tubers dug up by harvesters lie on the ground, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researcher Li Yafei rinses a potato plant, before separating the tubers and weighing them, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 14, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Lab workers sort vessels with potato plantlets as they prepare them for propagation, in a tissue culture laboratory at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato plantlets propagated in a laboratory lie on the potting mix as workers transplant them in a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A lab worker holds potato plantlets propagated in a laboratory to transplant them in a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researcher Li Yafei sets up a device to measure the photosynthesis rates of potato plants grown inside a heat chamber, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 2, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato plants grow in a pot at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 14, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A worker adjusts the hose as she waters potato plantlets transplanted from a laboratory, in a greenhouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researchers transport a cart of potato plantlets to a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A worker places clasps on potato plants to help them grow vertically, at a greenhouse under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 15, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato flowers grow in a greenhouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers pack potato plants into containers to transplant them to the field, in a greenhouse at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers sort sacks of seed potatoes to be distributed to other facilities, in a warehouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 2, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers rest on mats placed outside a greenhouse during their lunch break, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers sit in a bus for a lunch break Workers sit in a bus for a lunch break, after their work to transplant greenhouse-grown potato plants to the field was disrupted by the rain, at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 16, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
The Wider Image
Photography: Florence Lo
Reporting: Mei Mei Chu, Florence Lo, Xiaoyu Yin
Writing: Mei Mei Chu
Photo editing: Maye-E Wong and Marta Montana Gomez
Text editing: Kate Mayberry
Design: Marta Montana Gomez
LICENSE THIS STORY
Amid IsraelŌĆÖs military assault, the worldŌĆÖs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ŌĆō but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
ItŌĆÖs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the worldŌĆÖs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the worldŌĆÖs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: BuffettŌĆÖs Berkshire Hathaway operates the dirtiest set of coal-fired power plants in the US
item.description:
In his letter to investors last year, Berkshire Hathaway Chairman Warren Buffett urged readers to come ŌĆ£inhale the air, drink the waterŌĆØ and attend the companyŌĆÖs annual shareholder meeting in Omaha, his hometown.
The letter, a document pored over by shareholders, analysts and press every year for insights into the legendary investorŌĆÖs thinking, playfully sought to link the success of his conglomerate with the setting of the heartland city, in eastern Nebraska along the Iowa border.
One detail omitted: Federal emissions data show that OmahaŌĆÖs air quality ranks in the bottom third of U.S. cities, fouled in part by coal-fired power plants owned by Berkshire in neighboring Iowa. A modeling tool of the Environmental Protection Agency, or EPA, shows that pollution from the plants each year causes up to seven premature deaths, $104 million in healthcare expenses and 1,800 lost school days in the metropolitan area of Omaha, with nearly 1 million people.
The calculations, performed by Reuters using the EPAŌĆÖs modeling data for 2023, were reviewed and confirmed by three outside emissions scientists consulted for this report. The scientists are all independent research experts with no affiliation with the news agency or the electricity industry.
The air over Omaha, and other areas near BerkshireŌĆÖs dozen coal-fired power plants nationwide, casts a pall over the environmental record of a company that touts itself at the vanguard of clean energy. Despite investments to date of $41 billion in renewable energies, mostly wind and solar power, Berkshire operates the dirtiest corporate coal fleet in the U.S. by at least one important measure, a Reuters investigation shows.
BerkshireŌĆÖs coal plant fleet, operated by three utility companies it has acquired in recent decades, emits more nitrogen oxides than any other coal fleet in the country, federal emissions data show. Reuters analyzed the most recent full calendar year of EPA emissions data available for coal plants, from 2023.
Known as NOx, nitrogen oxides are poisonous gases and key components of smog and haze. They help form lung-damaging ozone and tiny soot particles that can cause respiratory illness and premature death in communities up to hundreds of miles away.
Berkshire has lobbied regulators and filed lawsuits to avoid installing expensive pollution controls, known as SCR scrubbers, that can reduce NOx and that are more commonly employed at coal plants owned by rivals. And the company has said it plans to operate some of its coal-fired plants ŌĆō aging, decades-old facilities in Iowa, Nevada, Utah and Wyoming ŌĆō for years after many of its corporate competitors plan to shut theirs, company disclosures show.
In an email response to detailed questions from Reuters, Berkshire said its electric utilities operate their coal plants ŌĆ£in full compliance with state and federal environmental laws, regulations and requirements.ŌĆØ The company said it hasnŌĆÖt employed more SCR scrubbers because it is ŌĆ£an expensive technology for our customers,ŌĆØ but that it has reduced emissions through other methods, retired some coal plants early and converted others to gas. Berkshire didnŌĆÖt address the toll its coal plants have exacted on the health of residents affected by their emissions.
Its efforts to reduce pollution, the company added, have sharply cut NOx emissions from its electricity portfolio over the last two decades. Coal power now accounts for only 22% of BerkshireŌĆÖs power generation, it said, down from 71% in 2005. Berkshire said it plans to exit coal power entirely by 2049.
The Reuters analysis used federal emissions data ŌĆō submitted to the EPA by plant operators themselves ŌĆō to determine the NOx pollution produced by BerkshireŌĆÖs coal fleet and compare it against industry peers. Reporters also used an EPA tool known as Co-Benefits Risk Assessment, or COBRA, to see how BerkshireŌĆÖs coal plant pollution affects the health of communities.
Widely used by scientists and regulators, COBRA assigns an economic value to pollution variables, calculating the economic and public health costs of contaminated air. Reuters also reviewed thousands of pages of regulatory and corporate documents and interviewed more than three dozen energy and medical experts as well as people living near BerkshireŌĆÖs plants.
The examination found:
Abel declined to comment for this story.
The EPA didnŌĆÖt respond to requests from Reuters for comment on the health tolls of emissions from BerkshireŌĆÖs plants.
Despite advances in renewable energy across the U.S., electricity generation still creates significant costs for the environment and public health. At a time of surging electricity demand because of intense heat waves, power-hungry data centers for artificial intelligence and the ever-growing needs of the U.S. economy, BerkshireŌĆÖs resistance to clean up its coal-fired plants worries doctors and environmental scientists.
ŌĆ£Warren Buffett is making a multi-layered environmental mess heŌĆÖs not being required to clean up,ŌĆØ said Brian Moench, a doctor whose group, Utah Physicians for a Healthy Environment, has studied the impact of BerkshireŌĆÖs coal plants.
Known for his folksy demeanor, frugal lifestyle and generous philanthropy, Buffet over more than half a century built Berkshire into a champion of American industry. Its trillion-dollar portfolio, forged through acquisitions such as the power plants, ranges from railways to insurance, fast food to real estate. Buffett, 94, is known for his hands-off approach to Berkshire companies, allowing managers to navigate the disparate industry and regulatory environments each confronts.
BerkshireŌĆÖs late vice chairman, Charlie Munger, told shareholders at the 2019 annual meeting that ŌĆ£the environmental stuff is done one level down from us. I think Greg Abel is just terrific at it.ŌĆØ Pushing back at the time against shareholder proposals for Berkshire to share more information with the public about its emissions, Munger added: ŌĆ£We are not going to do reports like everyone else.ŌĆØ
The energy policies are likely to outlast BuffetŌĆÖs tenure. Abel, who has led energy operations since the early 2000s, is widely expected to succeed the billionaire at BerkshireŌĆÖs helm.
Electricity has been fundamental to modern life and economic growth for more than a century. The risks and costs associated with its production are an afterthought for some users. As with cars and industrialized foods, convenience often outweighs concerns about downsides, particularly because much of the toll on health happens slowly and not always in plain sight.
ŌĆ£We all know coal plants have this continuous impact that is going to elevate the risk of asthma, heart attacks and death,ŌĆØ said Elena Krieger, who oversees scientific research at PSE Healthy Energy, a California-based policy institute. ŌĆ£But it is very, very difficult to pinpoint a single death. And that makes it very hard to point the finger at any given facility.ŌĆØ
The harm is considerable.
According to ReutersŌĆÖ COBRA analysis, the roughly 200 coal plants still operating in the U.S. each year cause up to 8,400 premature deaths and $130 billion in excess healthcare costs. BerkshireŌĆÖs coal plants are estimated to cause up to 260 deaths and $3.9 billion in health expenditures, the model shows. ReutersŌĆÖ analysis, Krieger said, offers ŌĆ£a reasonable portrait of the magnitude of the health impacts from these coal plants.ŌĆØ
The news agencyŌĆÖs COBRA calculations were also reviewed by Nicholas Mailloux, an expert in the health effects of energy systems at the University of Wisconsin. Tim Canty, a University of Maryland professor who is an expert on pollution at coal plants, reviewed ReutersŌĆÖ calculations of NOx emissions.
NOx, among the worst air pollutants, is a precursor for hazardous ground-level ozone and fine particulate matter. Wildfires and vehicle tailpipes are also large sources of NOx, which can travel over 1,000 miles to cause faraway smog and soot. The particulate pollution NOx helps form can penetrate the bloodstream and brain, contributing to respiratory issues, cancers and other illnesses. Children are particularly vulnerable.
Joel Schwartz, a Harvard University epidemiologist who studies pollution and human health, said there is no known safe threshold for exposure to soot particles formed from NOx. ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs affecting systems that do bad things all over the human body,ŌĆØ he told Reuters.
Coal plants are also big emitters of climate-warming carbon dioxide, or CO2, a gas not removed by SCR scrubbers. A research report from the University of Massachusetts-Amherst last year found that Berkshire ranked fourth among the top CO2 emitting companies ŌĆō from any industry ŌĆō in the U.S. The report relied on data from 2022.
Unlike many big industrial firms, Berkshire doesnŌĆÖt issue annual reports breaking out its company-wide CO2 emissions. The company says it is aiming for net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
Coal-fired electricity is largely in its twilight in the U.S.
The countryŌĆÖs remaining coal plants generate about 16% of its electricity. ThatŌĆÖs almost twice the level of the European Union, which has more aggressively pursued renewable energy. But itŌĆÖs a fraction of the 52% reliance on coal in 1990, when the U.S. had nearly 500 coal plants, according to data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration and from Eurelectric, a Brussels-based industry group.
Wind and natural gas, now the leading fuel for U.S. power generation, account for much of BerkshireŌĆÖs electrical output. But the company still relies on coal for over a fifth of its generation, according to company data.
Berkshire recently slowed some initiatives to wean itself further.
In Utah, its PacifiCorp unit, after announcing plans to invest heavily in renewable energy, last year changed course and said it would prolong the life of its two coal plants there. In an April statement, PacifiCorp said slower-than-expected changes to regulation made it attractive to do so.
ŌĆ£The twilight of coal seems to be turning into an odd sunrise,ŌĆØ said Ulla Reeves, director of the clean air program at the National Parks Conservation Association, one of several environmental groups that in federal court have sought stricter regulations against the Utah plants.
Berkshire began adding electric utilities to its portfolio in 2000, when it purchased Iowa-based MidAmerican Energy. Among that companyŌĆÖs five coal-fired plants is Walter Scott Jr Energy Center, a 50-year-old facility in Council Bluffs, Iowa, 10 miles down the Missouri River from BerkshireŌĆÖs Omaha headquarters. MidAmericanŌĆÖs four other Iowa coal plants all add to BerkshireŌĆÖs NOx tally, but Walter Scott, EPA data show, is the largest industrial source of NOx pollution in the Omaha area, emitting 4,000 tons in 2023 alone.
The facility is named in honor of one of BuffettŌĆÖs childhood friends, who was also a big investor in MidAmerican. It operates with two boilers, one of which doesnŌĆÖt have an SCR scrubber. Pollution from the plant settles over Omaha on days when winds blow from the south and southeast, a typical weather pattern, according to Dan Goldberg, a George Washington University professor who uses satellite data to examine real-time changes in air quality.
In 2023, metro Omaha residents had ŌĆ£goodŌĆØ air to breathe during only half the days of the year, according to standards used by the EPAŌĆÖs Air Quality Index. It ranked 299 in air quality out of 416 small and large U.S. metro areas, according to a Reuters analysis of EPA data. Metro Omaha had 25 days in 2023 when the air quality was deemed unhealthy for sensitive groups, ranking in the bottom 10% among U.S. metro areas by this metric. That was largely caused by lung-damaging ozone topping EPA limits on 23 days, the most so far for Omaha this century.
ŌĆ£If I had an asthmatic child, I would definitely not want to live in Omaha,ŌĆØ said Wilma Subra, a Louisiana-based scientist and environmental consultant who formerly served on the EPAŌĆÖs National Environmental Justice Advisory Council.
Omaha Mayor Jean Stothert didnŌĆÖt respond to messages seeking comment on the cityŌĆÖs air quality.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said MidAmerican is investing billions in renewable energy. Its Iowa coal plants operate under the ŌĆ£detailed oversight of local regulatory agenciesŌĆØ and as part of MidAmericanŌĆÖs ŌĆ£promise to customers to deliver safe, reliable and affordable energy.ŌĆØ
One day last year, a massive coal heap sat outside the Walter Scott plant. When wind kicks up over the Missouri River, black dust flies from the stockpile into nearby neighborhoods. ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs like a black cloud rolling through,ŌĆØ said Treg Hecker, who lives two miles away and says he sometimes has to hose the powder off the sides of his house. Medical research shows that communities near coal stockpiles can have increased risk of lung, kidney and other ailments because of such dust.
ŌĆ£Our facilities employ methods to eliminate coal dust from leaving the facility,ŌĆØ Berkshire said in its email, ŌĆ£and our current leadership team is not aware of any formal complaints that have been filed with regulators.ŌĆØ
About 11 miles northwest of Walter Scott lies North Omaha, a predominantly Black area with the highest prevalence of asthma in Nebraska. The asthma rate in North Omaha is nearly 13% of the population, according to 2021 data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, outpacing the state average of 8.8% and the national average of 7.7%.
For years, members of OmahaŌĆÖs Black community have complained about pollution from another nearby coal plant, North Omaha Station. The plant, which operates without an SCR scrubber, is just two miles from the neighborhood. Operated by Omaha Public Power District, a state-run utility, it has been targeted at public hearings by neighborhood activists pressing for stricter pollution curbs there.
OPPD declined to comment.
Over the past 20 years, EPA data show, Walter Scott has produced 403,000 tons of NOx and soot-producing sulfur dioxide pollution, 22% more than North Omaha Station. Preston Love Jr, a longtime advocate for North Omaha residents, said the risks from BerkshireŌĆÖs plant have largely escaped community notice.
ŌĆ£People are not aware of what is happening to them,ŌĆØ Love told Reuters. ŌĆ£They live and die without ever knowing.ŌĆØ
In September 2023, Memphis Zabawa, a 12-year-old boy from West Omaha, another of the cityŌĆÖs neighborhoods, competed in a local cross-country race. Despite an asthma diagnosis, Memphis was running at a time of high pollution in Omaha. The cityŌĆÖs Air Quality Index, a metric public officials use to warn the public about unhealthy air, that week topped 154 for tiny soot pollution, a measure of fine particle emissions, the highest score of the year. Soot pollution on race day registered a score of 75, a ŌĆ£moderateŌĆØ level that can still pose risks for some asthmatics.
During the race, Memphis carried his asthma inhaler and used it at least once, an Omaha Police Department report shows. But while running down a hill, he collapsed. His heart stopped beating. A nurse raced to MemphisŌĆÖ side and started CPR, police said. Paramedics rushed him to a local hospital, where he recovered.
Medical research has shown that coal plant pollution can exacerbate asthma and that tiny soot particles can trigger heart attacks. ThereŌĆÖs no way to know, though, whether that weekŌĆÖs air quality, let alone emissions from local power plants, caused MemphisŌĆÖ cardiac arrest. Linking a precise cause to any individualŌĆÖs illness is often difficult, even impossible. MemphisŌĆÖ parents, Erin and Justin Zabawa, told Reuters they canŌĆÖt assign blame for their sonŌĆÖs collapse.
About 1,000 miles to the west, Utah is known for Rocky Mountain slopes, red rock arches and national parks with panoramic vistas. Less advertised are the stateŌĆÖs air quality problems, which experts say in part stem from heavy reliance on coal power ŌĆō source of nearly half of UtahŌĆÖs electricity, federal energy data show, around three times the national average.
Ringed by mountains, Salt Lake City, the stateŌĆÖs fast-growing capital, often ranks among the worst cities on the American Lung AssociationŌĆÖs list for ozone pollution and dangerous particulate matter. The valleyŌĆÖs topography traps smog from industrial emissions, vehicles and other sources, sometimes creating a bowl of hazy pollution.
Hunter and Huntington, the two coal plants Berkshire owns more than 100 miles to the south of Salt Lake City, generate enough electricity to power about 800,000 homes. Built in the 1970s, during a coal mining boom in central Utah, the plants became part of BerkshireŌĆÖs portfolio in 2006, when it acquired PacifiCorp, owner of utility companies in several western states. Neither of the plants is equipped with SCR scrubbers.
In 2023, EPA data show, Hunter and Huntington had NOx pollution rates around 60% higher than the national average for coal plants, and more than twice the average rate of plants with SCR scrubbers. The two plants are responsible for some of Salt LakeŌĆÖs smog, causing up to $8.4 million a year in healthcare costs there, according to EPA data.
Much of their emissions also fuel regional haze pollution elsewhere, notably at Arches and Canyonlands national parks. The natural range of visibility at Canyonlands, which attracts hundreds of thousands of visitors to its gorges and buttes each year, on a clear day can span a majestic 200 miles. But in 2023, pollution some days dimmed the range by nearly 40%, according to federal visibility data for national parks.
The pollution is caused by a variety of industrial sources and vehicles. Within five years, however, the Hunter and Huntington coal plants are projected to be the top two emitters of the NOx particle pollution at Canyonlands, according to the EPA. The plantsŌĆÖ NOx output also helps form health-harming ozone pollution. Canyonlands and nearby wilderness areas have exceeded the federal health limit for ozone on 19 different days since 2018, including three in 2024, according to EPA air monitor data.
Hunter and Huntington also embody BerkshireŌĆÖs success opposing stricter NOx curbs, including a well-documented legal battle that enabled it to avoid the installation of SCR scrubbers there. Led by Abel, the Buffett protege and Berkshire energy boss, pushback against regulators has been driven by his focus on the bottom line, according to people familiar with the companyŌĆÖs tactics.
Abel is ŌĆ£a consummate numbers guy,ŌĆØ said Steven Guyer, an attorney and former environmental director at MidAmerican, BerkshireŌĆÖs first utility. ŌĆ£Everything is about the earnings.ŌĆØ Guyer left the company in 2008 and is now an outspoken critic of BerkshireŌĆÖs environmental policies at the Iowa Environmental Council, an organization that advocates for clean energy.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said it has made some of PacifiCorpŌĆÖs plants cleaner even without SCR scrubbers. Last year, for instance, PacifiCorp converted two of the four coal boilers at its Jim Bridger plant, in Wyoming, to run on natural gas.
In 2015, during the administration of former President Barack Obama, the EPA was evaluating tougher ozone pollution standards. That year, according to agency memos, Abel met twice with Gina McCarthy, EPA administrator at the time. The memos didnŌĆÖt disclose details about the meetings.
The EPA declined to comment on the meetings.
McCarthy didnŌĆÖt respond to requests for comment.
The next year, the EPA ordered PacifiCorp to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Within five years, the agency said, the scrubbers would cut NOx pollution from the two plants by around 80%. ŌĆ£EPA acts to reduce regional haze in Utah and improve visibility in National Parks,ŌĆØ read a press release about the decision at the time.
Months later, PacifiCorp filed suit to block the plan in a Denver federal court. During two years of litigation, Berkshire resisted the EPAŌĆÖs orders, arguing that the steep price tag for two SCR scrubbers at each of the plants would outweigh the benefits. The estimated total cost of $711 million, it said, would force PacifiCorp to hike rates for customers.
The EPA hired Andover Technology Partners, a Boston-area pollution control consultant, to audit BerkshireŌĆÖs estimate. Andover said SCR projects at the two plants could be done for $517 million, or about 30% less.
PacifiCorp, the consultancy found, overestimated costs by including items like parking lot signs, crushed rock for roadways and employee eye-wash stations. Those expenses, Andover said, have nothing to do with SCR controls, adding that PacifiCorp also inflated estimates for property taxes and the fuel needed to generate steam. ŌĆ£They make the projects look as expensive as possible,ŌĆØ said James Staudt, Andover TechnologyŌĆÖs owner.
In 2016 comments published by the EPA, PacifiCorp described AndoverŌĆÖs review as ŌĆ£grossly flawed.ŌĆØ The EPA, in the same comments, defended the report.
In 2020, under the administration of former President Donald Trump, the EPA reversed the Obama-era order to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Because PacifiCorp in 2015 had shut another Utah plant, also coal-fired, it had helped the government meet long-term goals, the agency said.
As of 2023, Berkshire had avoided installing SCR scrubbers on all but four of 24 coal-fired boilers at a dozen power plants, EPA data show. In Utah and Wyoming alone, according to a letter PacifiCorp sent the EPA in 2022, Berkshire has saved up to $2 billion by not having to install more advanced pollution controls.
In recent years, air quality experts and clean power advocates had some new reasons to be hopeful about Utah. In a May 2023 regulatory filing, PacifiCorp said it planned to shutter Hunter and Huntington by 2032. In their place, the company wrote, it was considering construction of two small nuclear plants on the sites.
The company was planning other clean energy investments over the next decade, too. In regulatory filings, PacifiCorp said the investments would create 13 gigawatts of renewable wind and solar power infrastructure in various Western states, enough electricity to power roughly 10 million average U.S. homes.
But optimism was short-lived.
In June 2023, PacifiCorp lost a high-profile lawsuit filed by fire survivors in Oregon, where a jury found its power lines responsible for sparking a wildfire. The judgment raised the specter that Berkshire utilities could face billions more in similar liabilities. Forced to set aside cash, regulatory filings show, PacifiCorp pared back many of its planned investments in renewable power.
In April of last year, PacifiCorp said in a regulatory filing that it would keep its Utah coal plants running for years longer, still with no SCR scrubbers. HuntingtonŌĆÖs operations are now expected to last an additional four years, until 2036, and HunterŌĆÖs a full decade longer than previously foreseen, until 2042, the filing said.
Pollution from the plants doesnŌĆÖt just stay in Utah. COBRA modeling shows it also increases health harms hundreds of miles away, in big cities like Denver, Los Angeles and Phoenix.
Separately, the EPA recently suffered a setback to its ŌĆ£Good Neighbor Plan,ŌĆØ part of a longstanding initiative to limit the impact that coal plant emissions have on surrounding states. Under the plan, UtahŌĆÖs coal plants would be subject to new rules that crack down on interstate ozone pollution. In June, however, the U.S. Supreme Court blocked the plan , pending further litigation from opponents including Berkshire. Among utility rivals, the industrial giant has been the most active in its opposition, submitting 125 different written objections to the initiative, according to an EPA summary document.
The decision leaves ŌĆ£upwind States free to keep contributing significantly to their downwind neighborsŌĆÖ ozone problems,ŌĆØ wrote Justice Amy Coney Barrett in a dissenting opinion. ItŌĆÖs unclear what changes may occur to the federal regulatory landscape in the next Trump administration, but the incoming president campaigned on promises to curb environmental rules and encourage energy companies to further tap fossil fuels.
State officials in Utah have been friendly to the industry. As with other supporters of continued coal generation, Utah legislators have cited the fuelŌĆÖs reliability compared with wind and solar power, whose output can vary depending on weather and daylight. ŌĆ£Many people sleep better knowing these coal plants can still supply the grid,ŌĆØ said James Scott Holladay, an energy economist at the University of Tennessee.
In March, the Utah State Legislature passed a law that could preserve coal as a preferential power source there. Utah lawmakers also offered PacifiCorp protection against wildfire liabilities, allocating taxpayer money to a fund that could cover costs from blazes sparked by power lines.
At last yearŌĆÖs shareholder meeting, Buffett and Abel lauded the fund and praised Utah for providing electric utilities with a favorable regulatory environment. Utah, Abel said, is ŌĆ£the best example we have. ItŌĆÖs the gold standard across the country.ŌĆØ
By Tim McLaughlin and Joshua Schneyer
To determine BerkshireŌĆÖs NOx emissions from its fleet of 12 U.S. coal plants, and to compare their NOx emissions rate against those of other large corporate coal power plant operators, Reuters analyzed public EPA emissions data for 2023, the most recent full calendar year available.
The EPA breaks out the data for the 200 or so coal-powered plants that still supply electricity to the U.S. power grid.
The data show coal boilers at plants Berkshire controls released 35,891 tons of NOx in 2023, more than any other single coal plant fleet operator in the country. Reuters also compared Berkshire plantsŌĆÖ average NOx emissions rate to that of other large U.S. corporate coal plant fleets ŌĆō those owned by the 20 utility operators with coal plants in the Standard and PoorŌĆÖs Utilities Index.
The NOx rate takes into account a coal fleetŌĆÖs NOx emissions by weight relative to the heat energy it produces from burning coal, in British thermal units, or BTUs, a standard methodology used by the EPA.
BerkshireŌĆÖs coal fleet had a higher average NOx emissions rate ŌĆō 0.163 pounds per million BTUs ŌĆō than any of its big corporate utility peers. It did not have advanced pollution controls ŌĆō selective catalytic reduction, or SCR, scrubbers ŌĆō on most of its coal-fired boilers.
To estimate the healthcare costs and negative health outcomes attributable to BerkshireŌĆÖs coal plant emissions in the Omaha metropolitan area and nationwide, Reuters used the EPAŌĆÖs Co-Benefits Risk Assessment Health Impacts Screening and Mapping Tool, or COBRA.
COBRA provides the range of estimated health costs associated with coal plant pollution in a given area. Reuters used the EPAŌĆÖs baseline emissions data estimates for 2023. COBRA results for metro Omaha include the upper range of estimated impacts within the "Omaha, NEŌĆōIA, Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA)," an eight county, bi-state area including the City of Omaha and surrounding areas.
To explore the air quality metrics in metro Omaha and elsewhere, Reuters analyzed the EPAŌĆÖs Air Quality Index data, which relies on air monitoring equipment nationwide. Reuters analyzed air quality data from 416 U.S. urban areas. The analysis excluded areas where air monitoring readings were incomplete.

item.title: Is climate change lighting a fuse under Iceland's volcanoes?
item.description:
Scientists are racing to find out whether the rapid retreat of glaciers could drive a surge in eruptions as magma builds under the island nation ŌĆö and if so, whether the same might occur at ice-covered volcanoes around the world, putting many lives at risk.
By GLORIA DICKIE Photography by STOYAN NENOV Graphics by SAM HART and ANURAG RAO Video by KUBA ST─ś┼╗YCKI
Filed Oct. 26, 2024, 10 a.m. GMT
Toxic sulphurous┬Āgas, carrying┬Āthe┬Ātelltale reek of rotten eggs, wafted through vents in the steep walls of IcelandŌĆÖs Viti crater, while carbon dioxide bubbled to the surface of the milky blue crater lake. Veils of steam wreathed the landscape of loose rock in eerie half-light.
Through this forbidding terrain ŌĆō ŌĆ£VitiŌĆØ is derived from the Icelandic for ŌĆ£hellŌĆØ ŌĆō Michelle Parks, a volcanologist with the Icelandic Meteorological Office, picked her way toward the waterŌĆÖs edge one day last August. With a monitor strapped to her hip to warn her if the gases┬Āreached dangerous levels, she stooped to submerge a temperature probe in the lake ŌĆō 26.4 degrees Celsius (79.5 degrees Fahrenheit), consistent with recent readings.
That was reassuring, at least for the time being. The crater was formed when Askja, a volcano in Vatnajokull National Park in IcelandŌĆÖs central highlands, uncorked in an explosive eruption in 1875. AskjaŌĆÖs last eruption, in 1961, was milder, and for decades after, the volcano was quiet. But in 2021, Parks and other scientists keeping tabs on it were shocked to find that in just a few months, the volcano had rapidly expanded, uplifting by 11 centimetres┬Ā(4.3 inches). This phenomenon, called inflation, occurs when magma or pressurised┬Āgases┬Āaccumulate under a volcano, pushing the ground upwards and outwards.
In the three years since, AskjaŌĆÖs bloat has reached about 80 cm (32 inches). That uplift, scientists estimate, is the result of 44 million cubic metres┬Ā(1.6 billion cubic feet) of magma flowing into the existing reservoir around three km (two miles) beneath the surface.
Volcanologists have established a correlation between magma buildup under a volcano and subsequent eruption. But they donŌĆÖt know exactly how much magma is needed to help trip an eruption.┬ĀThat is why Parks and her colleagues are closely monitoring the temperature and acidity of VitiŌĆÖs crater lake. A jump in either, indicating that more gases┬Āare pushing in from below, would suggest the volcano is moving closer to an eruption. So far, those metrics have remained stable, but the scientists watching Askja donŌĆÖt take that for granted.
ŌĆ£ThereŌĆÖs so much uncertainty on when this will erupt ŌĆö if it will erupt,ŌĆØ Parks said. It could be years, or decades, she said, or ŌĆ£it could be sooner.ŌĆØ
An eruption could be catastrophic, though smaller ones are much more likely. On the scale volcanologists use to measure the size of explosive eruptions, Askja is capable of one equivalent to that of Mount St. Helens in the U.S. state of Washington in 1980, although its eruptive style would differ. That eruption, which sparked the Australia-born ParksŌĆÖ lifelong passion for volcanoes, unleashed a force equivalent to 25,000 Hiroshima-type atomic bombs.
The aim of the volcano observatory at the Icelandic Meteorological Office is to monitor the countryŌĆÖs volcanoes for changes in activity, like those now observed at Askja, helping to protect the nationŌĆÖs nearly 400,000 citizens and the hordes of foreign tourists who visit every year to enjoy IcelandŌĆÖs world-renowned geothermal attractions.
ŌĆ£If itŌĆÖs a big explosion, then itŌĆÖs really dangerous for the people around here, the tourists.ŌĆØ
However, the teamŌĆÖs work has taken on broader significance. Last year, Parks and colleagues with the University of Iceland received government funding for a pioneering research project across 12 institutions to test a theory that could have dire implications not just for Iceland, but for every person on the planet: Whether the rapid retreat of glaciers as a result of human-caused climate change will trigger increased volcanic activity.
The basic process underpinning the idea is simple. The tremendous weight of glaciers and ice sheets can tamp down volcanoes. When the ice retreats, the downward pressure on the planetŌĆÖs thin outer crust and much thicker underlying mantle┬Āeases, allowing the ground to rebound. This change in pressure spurs dynamic forces beneath volcanoes to produce more magma and alter its movement, influencing eruptions.
ŌĆ£Iceland is essentially one of the best places in the world to study this ŌĆ” because we have both volcanism┬Āand glaciers,ŌĆØ Parks said. ŌĆ£At the end of the day, what weŌĆÖre aiming for with this project is a much bigger picture. ItŌĆÖs the future of volcanic eruptions. How large can they be? ŌĆ” And whatŌĆÖs in store for us in the future, not only in Iceland but for the rest of Europe and potentially farther afield.ŌĆØ
Ground surface inflation at Olafsgigar monitoring station
Jan 2021
Source: Icelandic Meteorological Office with data processing by Sigr├║n Hreinsd├│ttir
In July 2021, one of the observation stations at the vast Askja volcanic system showed that the ground there was uplifting ŌĆö a process called inflation ŌĆö indicating that magma was accumulating under the volcano after decades of deflation. The sudden reversal coincided with a burst of seismic activity in the area.
The rapid uplift continued through September 2021 and then gradually slowed as earthquakes persisted.
As of September 2024, Askja was still expanding, having inflated by about 80 cm in just three years. Scientists fear an eruption could be increasingly likely, but the timing and intensity remain uncertain.
Uncorking cataclysm
What scientists already know of IcelandŌĆÖs eruptive history supports the theory.
When the thick glaciers and ice caps that had covered the North Atlantic island during the last major Ice Age receded between about 15,000 and 10,000 years ago, underlying volcanoes responded with fury. In 2002, scientists calculated changes in IcelandŌĆÖs volcanic activity over time by analysing┬Āthe chemical composition of lava rock samples. They found that eruption rates surged an estimated 30 to 50 times during and shortly after the ice loss compared to the preceding Ice Age and recent times.
It was likely a cataclysmic scenario, with a ŌĆ£ridiculous amount of eruptions,ŌĆØ Parks said, as rivers of lava reshaped the island and ash rained into surrounding seas. Askja, too, registered a major explosive eruption during this time.
Once again, scientists say, the elements needed to set off another surge in eruptions are converging. Glaciers now cover just 10% of Iceland, but that ice still weighs on more than half the nationŌĆÖs 34 active volcanic systems, and it is rapidly melting as global temperatures climb. In the past 130 years, IcelandŌĆÖs glaciers have lost about 16% of their volume, with half of that in just the past three decades. Scientists predict roughly half of the remaining volume will be gone by this centuryŌĆÖs end.
Already, the magma chambers beneath Iceland may be responding to the loss of ice, and not just those directly under glaciers. Askja, which has been free of ice for 10,000 years, and much of Iceland are rebounding because pressure changes from glacial retreat affects large parts of the EarthŌĆÖs crust and mantle.
Over the last three decades, magma has been produced beneath Iceland at a rate 2 to 3 times what it would have been without the ice loss, according to preliminary modelling┬Āresults from ParksŌĆÖ project shared exclusively with Reuters. ŌĆ£More magma is coming in underneath Iceland, and we just donŌĆÖt need it,ŌĆØ Parks said. ŌĆ£WeŌĆÖve got enough.ŌĆØ
Scientists first theorised┬Āin the 1970s that melting ice might impact volcanic eruptions. But only recently have they begun to understand the scale of the potential threat. Four years ago, volcanologists compiled the first comprehensive global database of volcanoes under ice or within five km (three miles)┬Āof it, publishing their findings in the journal Global and Planetary Change. They found that some 245 active or potentially active volcanoes around the world met the criteria, from the Andes to North AmericaŌĆÖs Cascade Mountains and Alaska, to RussiaŌĆÖs Kamchatka Peninsula and Antarctica.
The same study also found that about 160 million people live within 100 km (62 miles) of at least one of those volcanoes and that 20,000 people are within the immediate vicinity. Numbers like that underscore the threat if glacial melt were to push more volcanoes to pop off, unleashing deadly floods and mud flows and spewing vast clouds of ash and deadly gases┬Āinto the atmosphere.
A global concern
A 2020 study identified 245 volcanoes within a 5 km radius of a glacier.
Volcanoes near or under ice
Iceland
Fault lines
ICELAND
Atlantic Ocean
0
300 km
CHILE
Pacific Ocean
0
300
Arctic Ocean
Alaska
RUSSIA
Atlantic Ocean
0
1,000
A 2017 study identified 138 suspected volcanoes beneath Antarctica
ANTARCTICA
Sources: ŌĆśGlobal Mapping of Future GlaciovolcanismŌĆÖ Global and Planetary Change, December 2020; 'A new volcanic province: an inventory of subglacial volcanoes in West Antarctica' , Geological Society, London,┬ĀMay 2017
The effects wouldnŌĆÖt end there. Eruptions rich in carbon dioxide, a greenhouse gas, could exacerbate global warming, outlasting the sulphur┬Āaerosols in the atmosphere that can cause initial periods of cooling. And the volcanoes now known to sit under the West Antarctic Ice Sheet could help speed ice melt into the ocean, raising┬Āsea levels.
Evidence suggests that what happened in Iceland after the last Ice Age was duplicated to a lesser extent around the world. Eruptions were between 2 and 6 times higher globally between 12,000 and 7,000 years ago owing to more frequent eruptions in areas that were losing their ice cover, according to a 2009 study in the journal Earth and Planetary Science Letters.
A violent history
Iceland is uniquely primed for frequent volcanic eruptions because of its location at the juncture of two tectonic plates that are pulling apart over an upwelling of anomalously hot material in the EarthŌĆÖs mantle. Many smaller eruptions have little impact beyond their immediate vicinity, though they are still able to put on spectacular shows. Others can have lethal effects far beyond Iceland.
When Laki, southwest of Askja, erupted in 1783-84, the fluorine it expelled contaminated the islandŌĆÖs plants and water sources, killing more than half of IcelandŌĆÖs livestock. This agricultural collapse led to a famine that killed around a fifth of the islandŌĆÖs human population, while the resulting haze of sulphurous┬Āfog that later reached Europe may have contributed to the deaths of thousands more people. And as far away as Africa and Asia, the eruptionŌĆÖs effects weakened monsoons, initiating more famines.
Near the southern coast under a small ice cap lies Katla, considered one of the worldŌĆÖs most dangerous volcanoes under ice. Katla has racked up more than 20 eruptions since the Middle Ages, averaging one every 60 years, and it is past due for another, having last erupted in 1918. During that eruption, heat pouring from KatlaŌĆÖs caldera rapidly melted the ice atop the volcano, releasing a torrent of water greater than the combined discharges of the Amazon, Mississippi, and Yangtze rivers at its peak. And, like Laki, Katla has explosive potential.
ŌĆ£The time for Katla to erupt is coming close ... It is high time for European governments and airline authorities all over Europe and the world to start planning for the eventual Katla eruption," then-Icelandic President Olafur Ragnar Grimsson told BBC News in 2010, following the eruption of Eyjafjallaj├Čkull. The ash cloud from that eruption caused Ōé¼1.3 billion (US$1.4 billion) in losses from cancelled┬Āflights during a six-day European airspace ban, making it the worldŌĆÖs costliest eruption in the last century.
The Icelandic government has emergency plans in place for a Katla-style eruption and works with local police districts to create near-term risk assessments for other volcanoes.
But the government has also tapped its fiery foundation to its benefit, using it to heat homes and businesses and draw big-spending tourists to rejuvenating geothermal baths. After the Eyjafjallaj├Čkull eruption, the Icelandic government made volcanoes a pillar of a now multibillion-dollar tourism industry. Souvenir shops in the capital, Reykjavik, sell lava rocks from a recent eruption on the Reykjanes Peninsula for 2,000 Icelandic krona (US$14) apiece.
ŌĆ£Watching your friends disappearŌĆØ
Like many Icelanders, 29-year-old Iris Ragnarsdottir Pedersen and her father, 62-year-old Ragnar Frank Kristjansson, have an intimate knowledge of the extremes of their land of ice and fire. Along the south coast in Svinafell, Ragnarsdottir Pedersen, a mountain guide, lives with her husband and their Icelandic sheepdog, Blika. Her father, a retired national park manager, has a small turf-roofed summer house next door. Towering over them is a large cliff, beyond which lies Vatnajokull ice cap and, under it, the volcano Oraefajokull.
After Oraefajokull erupted in 1362, sailors reported seaborne pumice floating ŌĆ£in such masses that ships could hardly make their way through it.ŌĆØ
Father and daughter both recall joining fellow Icelanders over the years to marvel at the glowing fountains and rivers of lava produced by eruptions. They also are familiar with the impact of climate change.
For 25 years, Kristjansson has volunteered as a glacier monitor with the Icelandic Glaciological Society, tracking the retreat of two glaciers that flow out of the Vatnajokull ice cap, the nationŌĆÖs largest. Every autumn, he treks for hours to one of the glaciers, Skeidararjokull, to take measurements that he sends to the Icelandic Meteorological Office. ŌĆ£It's a lonely walk toward the glacier ŌĆö 15 km in the black sand,ŌĆØ he said.
Kristjansson used to be able to stride right up to the edge of Skeidararjokull. But as it has rapidly shrunk over the past decade, a lake has formed at the foot of the glacier, blocking his way. He now has to use special binoculars to measure the distance. This year, he said, one point along the glacierŌĆÖs edge showed a retreat of 300 metres┬Ā(984 feet), which is the greatest he has recorded.
Ragnarsdottir Pedersen has witnessed the retreat from year to year on treks to the glaciers with her father and by simply looking out her windows. ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs just devastating to see,ŌĆØ she said. ŌĆ£IŌĆÖve sometimes said to people, ŌĆśItŌĆÖs like watching your friends disappear.ŌĆÖ ŌĆØ
As a child, she knew Oraefajokull lurked under the Vatnajokull ice cap. But it only became a concern when the volcano started rumbling in 2017, just as she and her husband began planning to build their home near it. When she felt the earthquakes and caught the smell of sulphur┬Āfrom the rivers, she said, ŌĆ£YouŌĆÖre like, ŌĆśOh yeah, we actually have this powerful, quite dangerous volcano right above our home.ŌĆÖ ŌĆØ
Ultimately, she and her husband werenŌĆÖt concerned enough to cancel the move, having faith that scientists would closely monitor the volcano for any danger. ŌĆ£People have lived in Svinafell since the settlement of Iceland,ŌĆØ she said.
A scientific mecca
Oraefajokull is one of six active volcanoes covered by the Vatnajokull ice cap. As Vatnajokull has thinned and retreated, some of the volcanoes beneath it, as well as nearby Askja, have become agitated. Oraefajokull has calmed since its 2017 awakening. Grimsvotn and Bardarbunga, two volcanoes under the ice cap that are being assessed in ParksŌĆÖ┬Āproject, have together notched five eruptions in the past 30 years, compared with just one under the ice cap in the preceding 40 years.
Parks and her scientific colleagues said itŌĆÖs too early to conclude that the increased activity under Vatnajokull is linked to rapid loss of ice cover. Other scientists have established that clusters of eruptions can occur in natural cycles under the ice cap. But to determine whether climate change may also be playing a part, they will have to collect more data on ice retreat and magma generation and feed it into complex computer models.
Because of its continuing unrest, Askja is the main draw for scientists, who flock to its enormous calderas when IcelandŌĆÖs brief summer allows for field research. Like most of IcelandŌĆÖs volcanoes, Askja does not conform to the stereotypical cone shape of volcanoes such as JapanŌĆÖs iconic Mount Fuji. Instead, fissures snake across a sprawling Mars-like landscape carpeted with lava rocks and pockmarked with craters from past eruptions.
To reach the centrepiece┬Āof the Askja system, an 11-square-km (four sq miles) caldera lake called Oskjuvatn, scientists journey hours across the highlands in Super Jeeps and Land Rovers balanced on 35-inch tires. Oskjuvatn was formed in an explosive 1875 eruption that shot out huge volumes of airy pumice, much of it still covering the area around Askja, with fist-sized chunks tossed about by strong winds.
Above the magma reservoir near the western side of the lake is where Askja has been inflating fastest, a lure for researchers. On a visit in August, Reuters encountered a team of three scientists from the University of Geneva at the lakeŌĆÖs edge, loading equipment into an inflatable dinghy. Their aim: to measure carbon dioxide concentrations in the lake and collect water samples from its deepest points.
The data is not easy to get. The numbingly cold and often tempestuous waters are dangerous. Landslides on the calderaŌĆÖs steep scarp can launch tsunamis big enough to swamp neighbouring┬ĀViti crater. In 1907, two German scientists set off onto the lake and were never seen again. The Geneva teamŌĆÖs boat would be the first on the lake in nearly a decade. The University of Geneva researchers suited up in thick thermal gear, just in case.
Weight of glacier
Increased volatility from below
Magma migration pathways change
More magma is generated, and existing magma bodies may be destabilised
The great weight of glaciers places downward pressure on the EarthŌĆÖs crust and mantle, in effect dampening upward force from magma chambers beneath volcanoes.
As the ice cover melts, this downward pressure eases, enabling more rock in the mantle to melt and form magma.
The rebound of the crust can also affect stress fields in the Earth, altering the movement of magma and creating new fractures underneath a volcano. Magma reservoirs can become unstable and move closer to eruption.
As magma pushes upward, it can cause the ground to expand and trigger swarms of small earthquakes. When the pressure is high enough, an eruption can burst through the surface.
One of them, geologist Nicolas Oestreicher, said the work was important given both AskjaŌĆÖs explosive capabilities and the increased activity around the lake. ŌĆ£If itŌĆÖs a big explosion, then itŌĆÖs really dangerous for the people around here, the tourists,ŌĆØ Oestreicher said.
His team, which later returned safely from their boat trip, was one of several from Iceland and abroad that day racing to figure out what was going on at Askja. University of Iceland geophysicist Freysteinn Sigmundsson, co-head with Parks of the government-funded project, was there, too. ŌĆ£Today may be the richest day in all of AskjaŌĆÖs┬Āhistory in terms of the number of scientists here,ŌĆØ he said.
Sigmundsson has been coming to the volcano almost every year since 1990 and he knows the terrain well. Shouldering a surveying tripod, he clambered with sure-footed expertise across beds of jagged, crunchy lava, searching for round metal markers that scientists anchored in the area in the 1960s and 1970s. These would tell him where to set up his equipment to check how much Askja had grown or shifted over the past year.
The measurements Sigmundsson and his colleagues took in August showed 12 cm (five inches) of uplift at Askja since the previous year, confirming that the volcano was still in a state of unrest and could erupt at any time.
From the Andes to Antarctica
Regardless of what they ultimately find, the interplay between volcanoes and ice will remain a chief worry among volcanologists. The most deadly eruption in the last 100 years was that of Nevado del Ruiz in the Colombian Andes in 1985. Some 23,000 people were killed after a superheated amalgam of ash, lava fragments and gases┬Āknown as a pyroclastic flow melted snow and ice near the volcanoŌĆÖs summit, sending massive rivers of mud and debris rushing down the volcanoŌĆÖs flanks.
ŌĆ£Any eruption where there is a possibility of (snow or ice) interacting with the hot stuff adds to the elements of danger,ŌĆØ said Ben Edwards, a volcanologist at Dickinson College in Pennsylvania and lead author of the 2020 study that produced the database of ice-clad volcanoes.
Edwards and his fellow researchers drew up a list of the worldŌĆÖs most dangerous volcanoes that lie under glaciers,┬Ābased on the volume of ice on or near each one, the frequency of past eruptions, and the population living within 30 km (19 miles). Seven of the top 10, they found, are in the Andes. The most dangerous is Villarrica┬Āin Chile, with more than 35,000 people living in its shadow. Edwards is in the middle of a five-year study seeking to understand how Villarrica responded to glacial retreat at the end of the last Ice Age.
The small number of studies to date of the interaction between retreating ice and volcanoes in other parts of the world, scientists said, mean the research underway in Iceland will help create a template for what could happen elsewhere. ItŌĆÖs not a perfect comparison: The underlying plate tectonics in Iceland differ from those in the Andes. Magma reservoirs also lie much deeper in the crust in the Andes than in Iceland, Edwards said.
ŌĆ£We donŌĆÖt have a very good physical understanding of all the processes that control melting (of rock into magma) deeper in the mantle,ŌĆØ said David Pyle, a volcanologist at the University of Oxford who studies Chilean volcanoes. ŌĆ£Whereas in Iceland, the conceptual model is much better developed because in a way itŌĆÖs geologically simpler.ŌĆØ
Antarctica, where both ice and volcanoes are abundant, has become an area of concern in recent years. While a handful of volcanoes were known to reside under the vast West Antarctic Ice Sheet, in 2017 scientists reported detecting another 91 possible volcanoes hidden there.
How many of them are active or have the potential to awaken is difficult to ascertain. ŌĆ£There are probably two or three which are certainly active. But there may be as many as 100 or 150,ŌĆØ said John Smellie, a former senior volcanologist with the British Antarctic Survey who has completed 27 field seasons on the continent and won two Polar Medals from the British royals.
If climate change kicks off a new era of eruptions in the coming decades, Smellie said, it would hasten the already rapid loss of the continentŌĆÖs ice cover due to climate change, adding to rising sea levels and swamping the worldŌĆÖs big coastal cities. Antarctica is shedding more than 150 billion metric tons┬Āof ice a year, according to satellite measurements, and scientists believe that as oceans continue to warm, the West Antarctic Ice Sheet is fast approaching a tipping point beyond which the melt cannot be stopped.
ŌĆ£If an eruption adds even a small amount to global sea level, it will exacerbate effects already happening due to climate change,ŌĆØ Smellie said.
Under the worst-case scenario, rapid melting of the West Antarctic Ice Sheet would ease the pressure on buried volcanoes, triggering eruptions. This in turn would speed up ice loss, awakening even more volcanoes that melt more ice, and so on, leading to global catastrophe. However, Smellie said, that is unlikely to happen because thousands of nearly simultaneous eruptions would be needed to melt even a small proportion of the ice sheet.
In the danger zone
Few people make their home near Askja. The closest village lies about 60 km (37 miles) away. But some 13,000 people visit the volcano during the summer months, when Askja is more accessible, according to data from the national park authority. Often, tourists climb down into Viti crater to swim in the Instagram-worthy lake, despite signs that warn them about acidic water, which can irritate skin, and falling rock.
After a week of field work at and around Askja last August, the volcanologists gathered park rangers and local police and lodging staff in a nearby ranger hut one evening for a safety briefing on the state of the volcano.
Related content
In the Andes, mountain guides bear witness to shrinking glaciers
Climate change thaws worldŌĆÖs northernmost research station
Iceland builds defences for a future of increased volcanic activity
Iceland volcano erupts near town after weeks of quake activity
Parks hovered over her laptop displaying a series of charts and graphs on her most recent findings that showed Askja was still inflating. Sigmundsson, standing in the doorway, explained to the rangers and police that predicting eruptions is an imprecise science; they may not necessarily have weeks of seismic activity as a warning of an imminent eruption. ŌĆ£The time scale can be quite short,ŌĆØ he said. ŌĆ£Hours, maybe ŌĆ” You would want to have a plan if something happens to quickly evacuate the caldera.ŌĆØ
One of the greatest risks to tourists is a phreatic explosion ŌĆö a blast of hot steam, ash and rocks that comes with little warning. ThatŌĆÖs what formed Viti crater nearly 150 years ago. In 2019, a phreatic explosion at New ZealandŌĆÖs White Island volcano killed 22 people who were visiting the island at the time.
At SigmundssonŌĆÖs words, the room fell quiet. One of the police officers then piped up with a plan: ŌĆ£We just have to hope that it blows up in the middle of the winter.ŌĆØ
By Gloria Dickie
Graphics: Sam Hart and Anurag Rao
Photography: Stoyan Nenov
Video: Kuba St─Ö┼╝ycki
Video production: Lauren Cook, Holly Murtha and M├Ła Womersley
Photo editing: Maye-E Wong and Eve Watling
Art direction: Eve Watling
Visual editing: Feilding Cage
Edited by Katy Daigle and John Blanton
Amid IsraelŌĆÖs military assault, the worldŌĆÖs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ŌĆō but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
ItŌĆÖs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the worldŌĆÖs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the worldŌĆÖs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: 'Sustainable' logging operations are clear-cutting CanadaŌĆÖs climate-fighting forests
item.description:
Nonprofit environment watchdogs put their stamps of approval on countless wood products that get touted as responsibly produced. But Reuters found that the timber firms these groups certify are harvesting large swaths of CanadaŌĆÖs older forests, which are critical to containing global warming.
By CHRIS KIRKHAM , GRANT SMITH and JESSICA DINAPOLI Photographs by CHRIS HELGREN and DAVID JACKSON
Filed Sept. 7, 2024, 10 a.m. GMT
With its vast expanses of forest, Canada has the most ŌĆ£certifiedŌĆØ sustainable timber operations of any nation, according to the nonprofit organizations that attest to the environmental soundness of logging practices.
Such forestry-standards groups were born in the 1990s out of rage┬Āover tropical rainforest destruction. Today, they put their leafy seals of approval on toilet paper, two-by-fours and other wood and paper goods to assure eco-conscious consumers and investors they were responsibly produced.
Yet research shows Canadian forests have seen some of the worldŌĆÖs largest declines in ecologically critical primary and old-growth woodlands over the last two decades, even as sustainability-certification programs grew to include nearly all of CanadaŌĆÖs logging.
To track destruction of older woodlands in these certified zones, Reuters analyzed forestry data in Ontario, a major logging province. The analysis found that about 30% of the certified boreal forests harvested from 2016 to 2020 were at least 100 years old. That resulted in the loss of 377 square miles of these older forests, an area the size┬Āof New York City and Washington D.C. combined, the analysis found.
CanadaŌĆÖs forests ŌĆō accounting for 9% of the worldŌĆÖs total ŌĆō are considered critical to containing global warming. Environmental advocates have long pushed to end logging in primary or old-growth forests, which soak up far more climate-damaging carbon than logged-and-replanted areas. Primary forests are those that show no sign of previous harvesting. They can include old-growth areas ŌĆō some with trees hundreds or thousands of years old ŌĆō but also relatively newer woodlands that, for instance, might have regrown after wildfires.
Forest-certification nonprofits have chosen to allow logging of older forests through a host of concessions to industry. The harvesting of such areas in Ontario came despite the fact that 94% of the provinceŌĆÖs managed forests are certified by one of the two dominant environmental-certification┬Āorganizations in Canada, the analysis found. Reuters analyzed satellite-derived logging data, government forest-age estimates and forest-certification maps to estimate the harvest of forests at least 100 years old in OntarioŌĆÖs certified zones.
ŌĆ£Why the heck are they allowing logging ŌĆō certified logging ŌĆō in primary forests that are over 100 years old?ŌĆØ asked Dominick DellaSala, a conservation biologist with environmental group Wild Heritage who studies Canadian logging impacts.┬ĀŌĆ£For Canada to claim that itŌĆÖs doing sustainable management, itŌĆÖs laughable. To put a certification seal of approval on it is more alarming.ŌĆØ
The rapid loss of older Canadian forests┬Āhighlights the flaws of certification programs that have come under heavy influence of the logging and forest-products┬Āindustries, a Reuters investigation has found. The damage has come under the watch of the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC), the worldŌĆÖs first such certification organization, founded in 1993 with environmentalist support; and the Sustainable Forestry Initiative (SFI), a rival founded by a timber┬Āand forest-products┬Ātrade group the following year.
This account is based on the Reuters analysis of Ontario forests, a review of hundreds of pages of FSC and SFI audits, along with policy and strategy documents, and interviews with 20 current or former FSC employees or members and more than a half-dozen┬Āresearchers who study the environmental effects of Canadian logging.
In a statement, FSC said it has not wavered from its original commitment to responsible forest┬Āmanagement and┬Āthat its certification standards are ŌĆ£robust and credible.ŌĆØ SFI said its standards are ŌĆ£strong and continuously improvingŌĆØ and that its certification has become a ŌĆ£highly trusted solutionŌĆØ to the growing demand for products from sustainably managed forests.
Neither organization commented on the Reuters analysis or on whether they considered harvesting large sections of century-old┬Āforests to be sustainable.
The FSC and SFI certify logging companiesŌĆÖ practices in specific forests and examine consumer-product supply chains. Their seals of approval ŌĆō a leaf insignia┬Āfor SFI, and a tree with a checkmark for the┬ĀFSC ŌĆō have become essential to timber and forest-products┬Āfirms amid rising pressure for environmental stewardship.
But these companies hold immense leverage over the big forest-certification nonprofits, which depend heavily on the industry for funding through certification fees, Reuters found. And since its inception, the FSC has watered down its forestry standards in response to the competitive threat posed by SFI and other industry-friendly certifiers, according to environmentalists and more than a dozen current and former FSC staffers and members, who advise the organization on policy and strategy.
Companies are free to choose which certifier to use, allowing them to avoid those with stricter standards and giving them clout to lobby all certifiers for permissive policies, said the FSC staffers and members.
ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs easy to pull the wool over peopleŌĆÖs eyes about what is good forestry.ŌĆØ
Widespread certification of British Columbia timber operations over the past two decades hasnŌĆÖt stopped the disappearance of more than half of the provinceŌĆÖs old-growth woodlands over that period. Logging caused the vast majority of the declines in the biggest old-growth trees storing the most carbon, according to one 2021 study in the Canadian Journal of Forest Research and another last year in the journal Frontiers in Forests and Global Change.┬ĀStudies in 2009 and 2017 examined areas of Quebec woodlands and found┬Āsections┬Āof forests dominated by trees more than a century old had dwindled to between 13% and 28% of the forest amid heavy logging. Without logging, these older sections would account for between 40% and 68%┬Āof these woodlands, the researchers estimated.
Herb Hammond, a veteran forest ecologist,┬Āran a British Columbia nonprofit┬Āorganization that conducted some of CanadaŌĆÖs first FSC audits in the late 1990s.┬ĀHe later left the organization, frustrated with what he described as┬Ātoo many compromises with industry.
ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs easy to pull the wool over peopleŌĆÖs eyes about what is good forestry,ŌĆØ he said. ŌĆ£Certification has turned out to be a bit of a dogŌĆÖs breakfast. It doesnŌĆÖt really mean anything.ŌĆØ
A ŌĆśchess moveŌĆÖ
Forestry certification has become ubiquitous in the global forest-products┬Ātrade, helping companies such as Procter & Gamble, Starbucks and Penguin Random House appeal to eco-conscious consumers and investors. Those three companies declined to comment.
The certifying trend started in the 1990s when environmental organizations including Greenpeace, Friends of the Earth and the World Wildlife Fund helped launch the FSC after failing to secure forest-conservation pledges from governments worldwide. They hoped to incentivize companies instead with a market-driven system that branded goods as sustainable, stimulating demand from discerning buyers.┬ĀThe FSC was founded in 1993 with a membership of business, environmental and community representatives.
Still, many companies were wary of aligning with environmentalists. The following year, the American Forest &┬ĀPaper Association, a trade-group, started the SFI as an industry-friendly alternative. The trade association said its discussions about sustainable forestry began earlier, in 1990, and included input from academics and conservation groups.
Competition from the industry-backed SFI forced the FSC to reckon with how to maintain rigorous forestry standards while recruiting companies to certify, 10┬Ācurrent and former FSC members said. A 2002 FSC management report highlighted the need to ŌĆ£rapidly increase the supply of certified timberŌĆØ or risk losing out to ŌĆ£an ever-increasing number of competing certification schemes.ŌĆØ
The FSC launched an internal push to boost its market share that led to compromises with industry and weaker harvesting restrictions, according to FSC documents and the FSC members.
Weakening FSC standards didnŌĆÖt stop the SFIŌĆÖs growth, however. The FSC certified about 46 million hectares of Canadian forests┬Āat the end of 2023, less than half the SFIŌĆÖs 119 million hectares, according to the Forest Products Association of Canada, an industry group. Globally, the FSC certifies 160 million hectares compared to 295 million hectares by the Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certification┬Ā(PEFC). The PEFC is a global organization that oversees the SFI, which covers North America, and affiliated certifiers in other regions.
Both the FSC and the SFI largely survive on industry-paid fees. FSC International reported in 2022 that such fees accounted for 86% of its $58 million in annual revenue. The SFI derived 77% of its $12 million in revenue from such fees, according to its 2022 tax return.
Some environmental groups and advocates, while acknowledging the FSCŌĆÖs shortcomings, continue to view the organization as the best option among imperfect alternatives. Jen Skene, a policy director at the Natural Resources Defense Council, said FSC certification represents a minimum standard.
