ŌĆ£FSC is the most credible certification system out there,ŌĆØ she said, while adding that it should be viewed as ŌĆ£a floor, not a ceilingŌĆØ for sustainability standards.
FSC told Reuters it had not weakened standards in response to SFI competition. Instead, FSC said, the rivalry has prompted it to ŌĆ£improve and refineŌĆØ its certification process to ensure it remains ŌĆ£the gold standard for responsible forestry.ŌĆØ
SFI said competition among certifiers doesnŌĆÖt ŌĆ£exert a downward pressure on standardsŌĆØ but rather promotes continuous improvement. The PEFC said it allows regional groups including the SFI to develop their own standards, which the PEFC said┬ĀŌĆ£contributes to long-term commitment to sustainable forest management┬Āpractices.ŌĆØ
Though some corporations favor FSC-certified wood, few customers know the difference among certifying groups and their labels.
Peter Wood, a forestry lecturer at the University of British Columbia who has served on FSC-rulemaking committees, called the SFIŌĆÖs creation a ŌĆ£chess move.ŌĆØ
The industry ŌĆ£wanted to take the power away from FSC, and it worked,ŌĆØ he said. ŌĆ£Now, everything is certified.ŌĆØ
Race to the bottom
FSCŌĆÖs early standards emphasized the need to protect primary and old-growth forests. One pivotal provision read: ŌĆ£Primary forests ŌĆ” shall be conserved. Such areas shall not be replaced by tree plantations or other land uses.ŌĆØ
But companies complained the policy was too restrictive and difficult to enforce, said Grant Rosoman, a Greenpeace forests advisor and former FSC International board member.
FSC members spent years debating policy changes and in 1999 removed requirements to conserve primary forests. Instead, the FSC adopted a more subjective requirement to protect forests with ŌĆ£high conservation value,ŌĆØ based on a complex matrix of environmental, economic┬Āand cultural characteristics.
That vague language, still in effect, gives companies broad influence over which forests qualify for protection. It has also spawned an industry of consultants ŌĆō hired and paid┬Āby forest-products firms┬ĀŌĆō to perform studies determining which woodlands┬Āhave high conservation value, according to FSC audits and six current and former FSC members.
Rosoman of Greenpeace was among the FSCŌĆÖs┬Āmembers┬Āwho approved the language at the time. He now regrets it, believing its subjectivity enabled destruction of critical forests. ŌĆ£The continued logging of primary forests and old-growth forests was never resolved,ŌĆØ he said.
FSC acknowledged that its rules allow certified logging in such areas but said the high conservation value designation aims to ensure such harvesting is ŌĆ£conducted with the highest level of scrutiny and responsibility.ŌĆØ
In another major concession, FSC in 2004 introduced the ŌĆ£FSC MixŌĆØ system, which created a new label for products containing up to 30% wood┬Āfrom non-certified sources.
The move came after pressure from pulp-and-paper companies including Klabin of Brazil, SCA of Sweden and Mondi of South Africa, as well as book publishers and furniture manufacturers, said Rosoman, who participated in the negotiations. Mondi did not comment. SCA said it could not address its role at the time because the company has since been split into two firms. Klabin did not answer questions on whether the company influenced the FSC Mix rules. But┬Āit said the label eased the logistical burden of separating wood from certified and non-certified sources, a view echoed by SCA.
FSC Mix has since become the certification groupŌĆÖs dominant label, accounting for more than three-fourths of the FSC-product trade, according to a 2017 FSC paper. The paper added that FSC Mix was┬ĀŌĆ£the main source of income for the operating costs of FSC.ŌĆØ
ŌĆ£I just wanted to turn away from the whole project, and warn people: ŌĆśDonŌĆÖt trust it.ŌĆÖŌĆØ
The FSC told Reuters it doesnŌĆÖt know what percentage of FSC-certified products use the Mix label today. The label, it said, helps companies ŌĆ£transition to more sustainable practices.ŌĆØ
FSC Mix rules give companies wide latitude to use the label.┬ĀSome consumer-products firms┬Āare allowed to put the Mix label on products that contain no FSC-certified material at all because the FSC gives them credit for certified content in other products they sell.
The SFI also offers a label ŌĆō ŌĆ£SFI Certified SourcingŌĆØ ŌĆō that makes no guarantees that products contain any wood from certified forests, so┬Ālong as companies meet certain other conditions.
Phil Guillery, a former FSC United States board member and supply chain integrity director, said allowing uncertified wood into the FSC system brought many more timber and forest-products┬Ācompanies into the organization and gave them more clout.
ŌĆ£They understood and learned about the politics of FSC, and they became very powerful,ŌĆØ he said.
Wood, the University of British Columbia lecturer, served on two FSC groups that starting in 2011 tried to overhaul what internal critics had called a weak system of company self-assessments to ensure their FSC Mix products did not contain wood from ŌĆ£unacceptable sources,ŌĆØ such as illegally harvested forests. The rules changes took eight years in a process that was heavily influenced by industry, he said.
The FSC told Reuters the process resulted in a ŌĆ£substantial strengtheningŌĆØ of rules governing non-certified wood.┬ĀWood had a different take, saying the endless deliberations did little to screen out problematic sources of timber. He called his involvement ŌĆ£an awful experience.ŌĆØ
ŌĆ£I just wanted to turn away from the whole project,ŌĆØ he said, ŌĆ£and warn people: ŌĆśDonŌĆÖt trust it.ŌĆÖŌĆØ
Certified forest devastation
Environmentalists criticize the FSC but generally take a harsher view of the SFI, citing its founding by an industry group and weaker forestry standards.
The SFI disputes that it serves only industry interests, telling Reuters its standards reflect input from a diverse group of collaborators including environmentalists on its board.
Environmental groups including the Sierra Club, Stand.earth and the Natural Resources Defense Council say the impact of the SFIŌĆÖs industry-friendly approach is clear in British Columbia, where the organization has dominated certification.
The province, a showcase of CanadaŌĆÖs raw beauty and diverse ecosystems, has seen old-growth forests decline┬Āby more than 50% over the last two decades, according to the 2021 and 2023 studies. A subset of ŌĆ£highly productiveŌĆØ old-growth woodlands ŌĆō forests with the largest┬Ātrees storing the most carbon, and also the most attractive to logging companies ŌĆō has declined by an estimated 85%.
The SFI became the certifier of choice in British Columbia largely because industry perceived the FSCŌĆÖs early rules as too onerous, said Karen Tam Wu, an FSC consultant during the 2000s.
The timber industry and CanadaŌĆÖs government share in the logging wealth. CanadaŌĆÖs forests are generally on public land, which means provincial governments get a cut of the revenue from every felled tree. In British Columbia, that totaled more than $7.3 billion┬Āover the decade ending in March of this year, according to the provinceŌĆÖs forest ministry.
British Columbia in 2020 announced a plan to protect its dwindling old-growth forests after years of public pressure. A year later, officials released maps showing at-risk areas where it called for a deferral of logging. But the government never barred logging in those zones, instead leaving it to industry discretion.
Some major firms chose instead to continue harvesting, including Vancouver-based Canfor Corp, a global timber-and-pulp producer.
Canfor in 2022 whacked about 3,700┬Āacres of old-growth forest the government had recommended┬Āfor deferral of logging, according to satellite imagery analysis from Stand.earth. The provincial government said earlier this year that more than 50,000 acres of old-growth forest had been harvested in areas it sought to protect.
BCŌĆÖs Ministry of Forests said it is not seeking to end all old-growth logging and that harvesting in some areas is ŌĆ£possible and necessaryŌĆØ to support ŌĆ£local, sustainable jobsŌĆØ while protecting forests.
SFI certified CanforŌĆÖs vast western Canada operations in 2019, 2021, 2022┬Āand again last year. None of the publicly released audit summaries ever mentioned the cutting of old-growth forests. Major auditing firm KPMG, which conducted the reviews, had no comment.
Nothing in SFIŌĆÖs standards would have prevented┬Ālogging of old-growth forests.
SFI said old-growth-forest harvesting in British Columbia is ŌĆ£contentious,ŌĆØ involving negotiations among governments, industry and indigenous communities. It said its standards require compliance with ŌĆ£all applicable laws.ŌĆØ
Canfor said it is working with indigenous groups, communities and government to ŌĆ£review old-growth management and seek input into our proposed harvesting.ŌĆØ
ŌĆśLike printing moneyŌĆÖ
Logging companiesŌĆÖ ability to choose their own watchdogs poses the biggest obstacle to promoting high sustainability standards, environmental advocates said.
The auditing structure all but guarantees logging companies can get certified, said Simon Counsell, who was an FSC founding member while with the nonprofit group Friends of the Earth. HeŌĆÖs now an FSC critic.
ŌĆ£ThereŌĆÖs a clear, vested financial interestŌĆØ for the auditor, because granting FSC certifications leads to more auditing opportunities, Counsell said. ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs like printing money.ŌĆØ
The FSC said it avoids conflicts of interest by outsourcing reviews and certification to independent auditors who examine companiesŌĆÖ forestry practices and are paid by the firms being certified. The companies, it said, pay a separate ŌĆ£annual administration┬ĀfeeŌĆØ based on their forest-products revenue that goes to the FSC after being collected by the auditor.
In one example of industry influence over sustainability audits, a major Canadian timber company, Resolute Forest Products, defeated an effort in 2014 to strip its FSC certification in a western Ontario forest by suing and ultimately firing its auditor.
Resolute for years faced charges from scientists and environmentalists that its clear-cuts in the FSC-certified Black Spruce Forest had decimated habitat for threatened woodland caribou. As early as 2012, auditors at the Rainforest Alliance, a nonprofit hired by Resolute, found the timber company failed to meet FSC habitat-protection requirements. Another 2013 Rainforest Alliance audit examined complaints from environmental groups that ResoluteŌĆÖs logging ŌĆ£will lead to the extirpation of caribou from the Black Spruce Forest.ŌĆØ
Auditors suspended ResoluteŌĆÖs certification in January 2014, citing a failure to meet FSC forest-protection requirements.┬ĀIn May 2014, Resolute sued the Rainforest Alliance and its auditors, personally, calling their reviews flawed and biased. The company sought $400,000 in damages.┬ĀIt also asked for an injunction blocking the auditŌĆÖs public release, which an Ontario court granted.┬ĀThe lawsuit noted that certification was ŌĆ£critical to ResoluteŌĆÖs business model.ŌĆØ
The lawsuit was settled in 2015, with the alliance agreeing to assign new auditors to redo ResoluteŌĆÖs negative review. The follow-up audit found Resolute met FSC standards and had resolved the issues from the earlier audit.
Chris Wedeles, one of the original auditors Resolute sued, said he was ŌĆ£disappointedŌĆØ that the new auditors ŌĆ£reviewed the same evidence and came to a different conclusion.ŌĆØ
The Rainforest Alliance reinstated ResoluteŌĆÖs certification.┬ĀResolute dumped the alliance anyway, moving its auditing business in 2016 to SAI Global, which has re-certified the company every year since.
After the settlement, ResoluteŌĆÖs then-CEO Richard Garneau told FSCŌĆÖs international director┬Āgeneral in a 2015 letter that the firm would pull out of FSC unless the certifier addressed the companyŌĆÖs complaints about burdensome FSC requirements. A top Resolute executive was elected to FSC CanadaŌĆÖs board in 2021 and continues to serve today.
Resolute did not answer questions about its forestry practices or its lawsuit but said it upholds ŌĆ£the highest standardsŌĆØ in forestry management.
SAI Global, Garneau and the Rainforest Alliance, which no longer conducts FSC forestry audits, declined to comment.
The FSC said it was not involved in the dispute between Resolute and its auditor and that it wasnŌĆÖt influenced to change its standards by GarneauŌĆÖs 2015 letter. FSC pointed to recent suspensions of certifications in Quebec as evidence of its commitment to protect caribou.
Meanwhile, problems with caribou in the Black Spruce Forest persist.
In 2020 and 2021, SAI Global auditors found that Resolute could not substantiate the effectiveness of its caribou-conservation plan. The auditors resolved the matter, however, after a Resolute consultant argued that logging would decline to a level that could sustain caribou populations ŌĆō though not until 2039.
Bulldozing forests for oil
One of┬Āthe worldŌĆÖs largest stretches of certified forests is in northern Alberta, where the FSC has signed off on the logging practices of Alberta-Pacific Forest Industries Inc.
Over the last two decades, about 878,000 acres of these woodlands, an area more than twice the size of┬ĀLos Angeles, have been set aside to make way for oil companies to operate open-pit mines, drilling sites and pipelines in CanadaŌĆÖs oil┬Āsands. The oil exploration involves clear-cutting and bulldozing the forest. Some environmentalists consider it one of the world's most destructive industrial projects.
Alberta-Pacific Forest Industries holds logging rights to the forest, which includes old-growth woodlands, according to company disclosures and environmental studies. A clause in the companyŌĆÖs agreement with Alberta allows regulators to allocate chunks of the woods for oil-and-gas development.
When that happens, the FSC allows Alberta-Pacific to do a carve-out: removing the FSC certification from the land marked for oil development, while retaining certification for the surrounding forest. The arrangement has allowed Alberta-Pacific to retain certification in the area since 2005 despite the oil-related destruction.
FSC said it encourages certified firms to ŌĆ£engage in dialogueŌĆØ and use their influence to affect land-use decisions such as oil-and-gas development. But the organization said such decisions are ŌĆ£outside of FSCŌĆÖs direct certification scopeŌĆØ and are governed by provincial and national laws.
Alberta-Pacific said it is proud to have been FSC-certified since 2005 and that it has a goal of ŌĆ£maintaining biodiversity and other forest values.ŌĆØ It said FSCŌĆÖs policies allow carve-outs for oil development because the resulting environmental impacts are ŌĆ£beyond the full controlŌĆØ of Alberta-Pacific.
Alberta-Pacific makes money from the oil┬Ādevelopment: Under an agreement with Alberta, it receives compensation from oil-and-gas firms for the destroyed forests. It can also sell wood from forests cleared for oil mining under the ŌĆ£FSC MixŌĆØ label, FSC audits show.
Some of the oil is extracted through surface mining, a process that requires the forest to be bulldozed and stripped of vegetation and soil to make way for pits that can be hundreds of feet deep.
ŌĆ£The mining is totally unsustainable,ŌĆØ said Barry Robinson, an Alberta environmental attorney who has specialized in oil-and-gas issues. ŌĆ£It will be generations before it ever grows trees again.ŌĆØ
To estimate the amount of Ontario primary and old-growth forest harvested in areas with sustainable certification,┬ĀReuters analyzed satellite-derived logging data from the National Terrestrial Ecosystem Monitoring System for Canada , forest-stand attribute data from OntarioŌĆÖs Forest Resources Inventory and forest-certification maps from the Forest Products Association of Canada .
The news organization focused on the provinceŌĆÖs productive boreal forest stands within OntarioŌĆÖs forest management units, as defined in the Forest Resources Inventory, and calculated ages in 2016 through 2020 based on the last-known age of the stand.
Reuters used forest certification maps from 2016 through 2020 to determine how much of the annual harvest was in these areas. Reuters then combined the certified forests harvest data with boreal forest stands to estimate the harvests by age in those areas.
Reuters consulted with nine experts in either forestry or geographic information systems in developing and reviewing its methodology.

By Chris Kirkham, Grant Smith and Jessica DiNapoli
Photography: Chris Helgren and David Jackson
Photo editing: Corinne Perkins
Graphics and art direction: John Emerson and Jillian Kumagai
Edited by Vanessa OŌĆÖConnell and Brian Thevenot
Amid IsraelŌĆÖs military assault, the worldŌĆÖs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ŌĆō but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
ItŌĆÖs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the worldŌĆÖs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the worldŌĆÖs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: Azerbaijan says it is evacuating its diplomats from Iran for their own safety
item.description:
BAKU, March 6 (Reuters) - Azerbaijan is evacuating its diplomats from Iran for their own safety, Foreign Minister ŌĆŗJeyhun Bayramov said on Friday, a day ŌĆīafter Baku said four Iranian drones had crossed its border and injured four people in the Nakhchivan exclave.
Speaking at ŌĆŗa news conference in Baku, Bayramov ŌĆŗsaid that Azerbaijan was evacuating employees from its ŌüĀembassy in Tehran and its consulate general in ŌĆŗTabriz, the largest city in Iran's northwest, where many ŌĆŗethnic Azerbaijanis live.
"On the orders of President Ilham Aliyev, instructions have been given to evacuate our embassy in ŌĆŗTehran and the consulate general in Tabriz ŌĆŗfrom Iran, and these instructions are already being implemented. ŌĆīWe ŌüĀcannot put the lives of our people at risk," he said.
Azerbaijan reacted angrily on Thursday to what it said was an incursion ŌĆŗby Iranian ŌĆŗdrones into ŌüĀits Nakhchivan exclave, with President Aliyev threatening retaliation. Iran said it had ŌĆŗnot launched the drones.
The two ŌĆŗcountries ŌüĀhave long had tense relations, with Azerbaijan's close relations with Israel drawing Tehran's ire.
Azerbaijan shares ŌüĀa ŌĆŗmajority Shi'ite Islamic faith with ŌĆŗIran, but its government is firmly secular.

item.title: UN human rights chief to travel to Washington amid concerns over Middle East crisis
item.description:
GENEVA, March 6 (Reuters) - The United Nations human rights chief will travel to Washington ŌĆŗthis month, he said on Friday, ŌĆībut expressed doubt he could influence the development of the Middle East crisis amid mounting ŌĆŗconcerns.
"I hope to go to ŌĆŗWashington D.C. later this month, and we ŌüĀwill see what this will ŌĆŗbring," UN human rights chief Volker Turk ŌĆŗtold reporters in Geneva.
"We have obviously been in touch, but to be honest, I don't ŌĆŗthink it's in any way meaningful ŌĆŗin terms of influencing the current trajectory," he ŌĆīadded.
Turk ŌüĀurged international actors to rapidly de-escalate the regional conflict, now into its seventh day after U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran.
Israel said ŌĆŗon Friday ŌĆŗit ŌüĀlaunched "broad-scale" air strikes on Iran's infrastructure. It also continued strikes on Beirut's ŌĆŗsouthern suburbs, which are under ŌĆŗan evacuation ŌüĀorder .
Large booms rang out across Tel Aviv and other parts of Israel as ŌüĀIsraeli ŌĆŗdefence systems attempted to ŌĆŗintercept an Iranian rocket volley on Friday morning.

item.title: Russia says it will respond if Finland hosts nuclear weapons, accuses Helsinki of stoking tension
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 6 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Friday that Finland's plan to lift a ban on hosting nuclear arms raised tensions in ŌĆŗEurope and posed a potential threat to Russia, to which Moscow ŌĆŗwould respond if such a deployment took place.
Finland, ŌüĀwhich shares a long border with Russia, maintained neutrality ŌĆŗduring the Cold War but joined NATO in 2023 ŌĆŗin response to Russia's war in Ukraine.
On Thursday it said it was planning to lift a longstanding ban on having nuclear weapons ŌĆŗon its territory in a move that could open ŌĆŗthe door to placing them there during times of war.
"This is a ŌĆīstatement ŌüĀthat leads to an escalation of tensions on the European continent," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"This statement adds to Finland's vulnerability, a vulnerability provoked by the actions ŌĆŗof the Finnish ŌĆŗauthorities. The ŌüĀfact is that by deploying nuclear weapons on its territory, Finland is beginning to ŌĆŗthreaten us. And if Finland threatens us, ŌĆŗwe take ŌüĀappropriate measures."
Russia's actions in Ukraine and the unpredictable actions of U.S. President Donald Trump, especially his stated aim ŌüĀof ŌĆŗtaking over Greenland, have caused European ŌĆŗgovernments to rethink their security, including the role of nuclear arms.

item.title: Blanket evacuation orders in Lebanon raise international law concerns, says UN human rights chief
item.description:
GENEVA, March 6 (Reuters) - Large-scale evacuation orders issued by the Israeli army for southern Lebanon and Beirut's southern suburbs raise serious concerns ŌĆŗunder international law, the U.N. human rights chief said on Friday.
"These blanket, ŌĆīmassive displacement orders we are talking here about hundreds and thousands of people," said United Nations human rights chief, Volker Turk.
"This raises serious concern under international humanitarian law, ŌĆŗand in particular when it comes to issues around forced transfer," ŌĆŗhe added.
Israel carried out heavy airstrikes on the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of ŌüĀBeirut overnight after ordering its residents to leave, while the Iran-backed ŌĆŗgroup warned Israelis to leave towns and villages at the frontier.
On Thursday, an ŌĆŗIsraeli military spokesperson told residents of the southern suburbs to move east and north, posting a map showing four large districts of the capital he said they must leave, ŌĆŗincluding areas adjacent to Beirut's airport.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in ŌĆŗthe Middle East on Monday when Hezbollah opened fire, triggering a new Israeli offensive, ŌĆīwith ŌüĀairstrikes focused on Beirut's southern suburbs and on southern and eastern Lebanon.
"Lebanon is becoming a key flashpoint. I'm extremely concerned and worried about the latest developments," Turk said in Geneva following the firing of rockets into Israel by ŌĆŗHezbollah and the ŌĆŗIsraeli army's strong ŌüĀresponse in recent days.
Hezbollah, in a message published in Hebrew on its Telegram channel early on Friday, warned Israelis ŌĆŗto leave towns within 5 km (3 miles) of the ŌĆŗborder.
During fighting ŌüĀbetween Hezbollah and Israel in 2024, tens of thousands of Israelis were evacuated from towns in the border area, but many have since returned. Israeli officials ŌüĀhave ŌĆŗpreviously said there are no plans to ŌĆŗremove them for now.
"The world urgently needs to see steps to contain the Middle East crisis," ŌĆŗTurk added.

item.title: Iran war exposes fragility of Western aluminium market
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - The Iran war is exposing a growing vulnerability in the West's supply of aluminium , a metal classified as a critical manufacturing input by both the United States and the European Union.
The London Metal Exchange (LME) aluminium price hit a four-year high of $3,418 per metric ton on Wednesday after one Gulf producer, the Qatalum joint venture between Norsk ŌĆŗHydro (NHY.OL) , opens new tab and Qatar Aluminum Manufacturing (QAMC.QA) , opens new tab , started powering down its smelter and another, Aluminium Bahrain (ALBH.BH) , opens new tab , declared force majeure .
The continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz risks further disruption to a regional production hub that ŌĆīaccounts for 23% of non-Chinese supply.
Aluminium has historically been cushioned against such unforeseen supply hits by high inventory and excess smelter capacity in China, where producers would lift run-rates at the first sign of rising prices.
Inventory cover is now much reduced and China no longer has much spare capacity to flex, rendering the market much more sensitive to the sort of disruption currently unfolding in the Middle East.
The LME's daily stocks reports show that aluminium has been leaving LME warehouses ŌĆŗin Malaysia's Port Klang at a daily rate of 2,000 tons since the start of January.
No one has paid much attention.
LME aluminium stocks lost much of their signalling power over the ŌĆŗlast 10 years as traders and banks tussled for metal to lock into lucrative warehouse deals.
The resulting churn, shifting metal in and out of the LME's warrant ŌüĀsystem, served to obscure any read-through to what was happening in the physical supply chain.
But the daily stocks noise has been masking a steady depletion of what was a 3-million-ton aluminium mountain at the start ŌĆŗof the decade.
Combined registered and off-warrant stocks ended February at 583,000 tons, the lowest level since the LME started publishing off-warrant inventory figures in 2020.
Moreover, a significant amount of what remains is Russian aluminium, which ŌĆŗaccounted for 58% of warranted stocks at the end of January.
That's not a lot of use to many Western buyers. The U.S. and Britain banned the import of Russian metal in 2024 to prevent Moscow from funding its war in Ukraine, and the EU will follow suit this year.
The pool of usable metal in the LME system is therefore far smaller than even the falling headline figure suggests.
The shift in stock dynamics is a reflection of profound structural changes in aluminium's supply ŌĆŗlandscape.
Chinese producers are now running up against the government's mandated annual capacity cap of just over 45 million tons.
Chinese production growth slowed from 4% in 2024 to 2% last year, with smelters running at ŌĆŗan annualised rate of 44.5 million tons in December, according to the International Aluminium Institute (IAI).
The production slowdown is causing China's trade with the rest of the world to change.
Chinese manufacturers have been importing more primary metal, particularly from ŌĆīRussia. The ŌüĀworld's largest producer imported a record 2.5 million tons last year together with just over 1 million tons of unwrought alloy, according to the World Bureau of Metal Statistics, which sources data from official customs figures.
And China has been exporting less semi-manufactured products such as tube, sheet and foil. Outbound shipments fell by almost 10% year-on-year in 2025, equivalent to a Western market loss of almost 600,000 tons.
In other words, China is importing more aluminium metal and exporting fewer finished products, tightening Western supply at both ends of the chain.
Western smelters have even less capacity flex than their Chinese peers.
Production outside of China did no more ŌĆŗthan flat-line last year, according to the IAI.
Here the ŌĆŗcore problem is the price of energy, a ŌüĀcrucial cost component in the electrolytic smelting process.
There is significant idle smelter capacity in both the U.S. and Europe but restarts need to compete with other sectors, particularly data centres, for scarce long-term power supplies.
Indeed, high power prices continue to take their toll on existing plants. South32 (S32.AX) , opens new tab is currently in the process of placing its ŌĆŗMozambique smelter on care and maintenance after failing to negotiate an economically viable power contract.
While Gulf producers are currently in the firing line, the ŌĆŗevolving energy shock from the ŌüĀIran war risks further undermining the West's ability to build longer-term supply resilience.
Aluminium is a ubiquitous feature of modern life, used in everything from homes to cars to food packaging.
It is also key to the energy transition.
Back in 2020 the World Bank identified aluminium as a "high-impact" and "cross-cutting" metal in all existing and potential green energy technologies.
However, it is a metal facing ever more price volatility as the global market emerges from a prolonged period ŌüĀof surplus to ŌĆŗone where supply looks a lot more problematic and stocks are much lower.
The Iran war is a wake-up call for a ŌĆŗvery important metal.
( Andy Home is a Reuters columnist. The opinions expressed are his own)

And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe ŌĆŗto hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Asia has limited options to diversify from Mideast energy reliance
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 6 (Reuters) - Asian energy buyers are scrambling to find alternatives as the Iran war creates unprecedented supply disruption, but the region has limited longer-term options to reduce its heavy reliance on Middle Eastern oil.
The world's top crude importing ŌĆŗregion buys 60% of its oil and petrochemical feedstock from the Middle East, where the war that started with Israeli and U.S. attacks on Iran nearly a week ŌĆīago has pushed up global energy prices and threatens to drive inflation and hurt economic growth.
Unable to receive Middle Eastern crude, refiners from China to Southeast Asia are looking for expensive alternatives that will take weeks or months to arrive, while some are cutting output. This week, China and Thailand , opens new tab suspended exports of oil products while Vietnam halted crude exports, which typically go to Australia.
However, alternative sources have drawbacks including distance, refinery configurations, long-term contracts and cost.
For example, oil shipped from ŌĆŗWest Africa and the Americas takes 1-1/2 to 2 months to reach China, meaning orders need to be placed three months in advance.
By comparison, it takes roughly 25 ŌĆŗdays for oil to reach China via the Strait of Hormuz.
Also, switching crude grades changes product yields at refineries, which must adjust their operations.
"If ŌüĀyou put a new crude into the refinery, you have to change the cutoff points (boundaries separating crude into different products). You have to change gasoline blending. There's a lot of things ŌĆŗyou need to change. It's hard work," said Adi Imsirovic, director of consultancy Surrey Clean Energy.
"This is why diversification has been so poor in a lot of countries," he said.
Energy Aspects analyst Richard ŌĆŗJones said some governments may seek diversification at the margins, but many Asian refiners are tied to Middle East term contracts.
"Simply put, even replacing a modest share of the roughly 16 million barrels a day of Middle Eastern crude that arrives to Asia with Atlantic basin supply is not feasible," he said.
In Japan, which has sourced 95% of its oil from the Middle East since halting nearly all Russian oil imports after Moscow's invasion ŌĆŗof Ukraine, refiners run ageing plants optimised for Middle Eastern crude.
With gasoline demand declining, refiners have been wary of investing in upgrades needed to take on new sources such as Canada's ŌĆŗheavy TMX.
Muyu Xu, senior analyst at Kpler, said Japanese refiners could seek to blend lighter WTI or West African crude with heavier grades from the Americas to approximate the characteristics of Middle Eastern medium-sour.
"The caveat, ŌĆīhowever, is the ŌüĀlogistical complexity and refinery operational risks," she said.
For the nearer term, Japan can tap a stockpile of roughly 250 days.
Top importer China has smaller reserves - roughly 78 days' worth - but a more diverse supplier profile, sourcing roughly half of its oil from the Middle East including Iran, where it has been the top buyer. China also buys from Russia despite western sanctions, as well as from mainstream producers.
India, with just 25 days of reserves and reliance on the nearby Middle East for 55% of its oil, is scrambling to find alternatives, with Washington this week giving it a ŌĆŗone-month reprieve to buy Russian oil after U.S. President Donald ŌĆŗTrump pressured it with punitive tariffs to curb ŌüĀits purchases from Moscow.
The market for liquefied natural gas is much smaller and tighter. No.2 producer Qatar's move to halt production due to the war has had a swift impact, with India rationing gas to industrial customers.
Michal Meidan, head of China energy research at the Oxford Institute for ŌĆŗEnergy Studies, said the situation could lead to fuel switching and demand destruction.
"Long term, South Asian countries could look to limit the share of gas ŌĆŗin their energy mix and ŌüĀfollow China's model of reliance on coal and renewables," she said.
Tim Zhang, founder of Singapore-based Edge Research, said Asia could increase the share of non-fossil fuel such as renewables and nuclear in its energy mix or diversify its conventional fuel supply.
Surrey's Imsirovic said a prolonged disruption could prompt governments to reconsider their reliance on Middle East energy entirely.
"It's going to be like the Asian Currency Crisis or something. Definitely, ŌüĀpeople will ŌĆŗseriously have to rethink," he said.
"In sunny Asia, get some solar panels and buy an EV. End of story."

item.title: Exclusive: Slovakia seeks to raise Russian gas imports before EU ban kicks in
item.description:
PRAGUE/WARSAW, March 6 (Reuters) - Slovakia's national gas company SPP is in talks with ŌĆīGazprom to increase Russian gas imports this year and next, compared with 2025 levels, ahead of a European Union ban on Russian gas, two sources familiar with the matter said.
EU gas prices have jumped 50% this week after ŌĆŗQatar halted liquefied natural gas exports following U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran and ŌĆŗTehran's retaliation. SPP began the talks before the conflict erupted.
SPP said in November it ŌüĀhad received only one third of its gas from Russia in 2025 to date, after transit ŌĆŗthrough Ukraine ended in December 2024. Russian supplies had previously made up most of its roughly 3 ŌĆŗbillion cubic metres of annual imports.
The company may lift Russian purchases to as much as 100% of its needs until 2027 if discussions succeed, the sources said.
Slovakia last year switched to importing Russian gas via Turkey, but capacity ŌĆŗconstraints limited the flows.
SPP, which covers about two-thirds of Slovak gas demand, has a long-term supply ŌĆŗcontract with Gazprom running until 2034.
SPP and Gazprom declined to comment.
EU rules bar countries from increasing contract volumes ŌĆīfor Russian ŌüĀgas as part of the bloc's drive to punish Moscow for its invasion of Ukraine , but they allow some necessary amendments to existing deals.
One source said the talks included unspecified changes to the contract, which covers "large volumes". Any amendment would require an EU exemption to be assessed by the Slovak ŌĆŗEconomy Ministry, the source added.
The ŌĆŗministry did not ŌüĀrespond to a request for comment.
EU states must end Russian LNG imports by the end of 2026, though pipeline flows can continue until November 1, ŌĆŗ2027, if a country is struggling to fill storage with non-Russian gas.
After ŌĆŗthen, SPP has ŌüĀbeen exploring options to potentially import LNG mainly via Poland and Germany, but also Italy, the sources said. One source added it had received around 20 offers for non-Russian gas after 2027.
Slovakia, along with ŌüĀHungary, is ŌĆŗalso exempt from the EU's ban on buying Russian ŌĆŗoil due to difficulties in accessing alternative supplies, though those imports have been disrupted recently by damage to the Druzhba pipeline.

item.title: Kremlin says the war in Iran has fuelled significant demand for Russian energy
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 6 (Reuters) - The Kremlin ŌĆīsaid on Friday that the war in Iran had fuelled demand for Russian energy products, a day after the U.S. Treasury issued ŌĆŗa 30-day waiver allowing India to buy ŌĆŗRussian oil currently stuck at sea.
The conflict, ŌüĀwhich entered its seventh day on Friday, has left ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz, a critical shipping passage all-but ŌĆŗshut, with countries around the world cut off from a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies.
Kremlin spokesman Dmitry ŌĆŗPeskov told reporters that Russia has been and remained ŌĆŗa reliable supplier of oil and gas, both via pipelines and ŌĆīin ŌüĀliquefied form.
"We are seeing a significant increase in demand for Russian energy resources in connection with the war in Iran. Russia has been and remains a reliable ŌĆŗsupplier of ŌĆŗboth oil and ŌüĀgas ŌĆö including pipeline gas and liquefied natural gas," Peskov told reporters.
"It also remains ŌĆŗcapable of guaranteeing the continuity of all ŌĆŗdeliveries for ŌüĀwhich contracts have been concluded."
Peskov declined to disclose possible volumes of Russian oil supplies to India following Washington's ŌüĀwaiver, ŌĆŗwhich followed months of U.S. pressure on ŌĆŗNew Delhi not to buy Russian oil.

item.title: What's in China's new five-year plan for commodity markets
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China unveiled its 15th five-year plan on Thursday at its annual parliamentary meeting, outlining Beijing's priorities for the economy and sectors slated for policy support and funding.
Here's a summary of what matters for commodity markets:
METALS ŌĆŗAND CRITICAL MINERALS
OVERCAPACITY
CLIMATE, POWER AND COAL
OIL AND GAS

item.title: Snam CEO sees no short-term gas supply issues for Italy
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - Italy's gas supplies should not face short-term disruption from the crisis in the Middle East, the CEO of state-controlled gas grid operator Snam (SRG.MI) , opens new tab said on Thursday.
"All the vessels (due ŌĆŗin March) that take gas from Qatar left before the explosion of ŌĆŗthe crisis, they passed the Strait of Hormuz and are already moving ŌüĀtowards the final destination so we do not expect any disruptions during March," ŌĆŗAgostino Scornajenchi said while presenting Snam's strategy to 2030.
"We will see what happens in April," ŌĆŗhe added, noting there were limited volumes due to come to Italy from Qatar.
Qatar accounts for around 7 billion cubic metres (bcm) of Italy's annual gas imports, covering roughly 11% of the Mediterranean ŌĆŗcountry's consumption.
Italian gas storage facilities are currently 45% full, compared with an average of ŌĆŗaround 30% in Europe.
Under its new strategy, Snam plans to invest 14 billion euros ($16 billion) over ŌĆīthe ŌüĀnext five years, focusing on gas infrastructure including storage assets and liquefied natural gas terminals.
The gas grid operator expects to divest non-core assets worth 1.6 billion euros and sees potential selective acquisitions worth 1.2 billion euros.
Scornajenchi said Snam was happy with its 11.4% ŌĆŗstake in gas ŌĆŗdistributor Italgas (IG.MI) , opens new tab , but its ŌüĀholding in De Nora (DNR.MI) , opens new tab was not a core investment, though there was no urgency to sell it.
Snam also said it was ŌĆŗdoubling down on developing a carbon capture and storage project ŌĆŗlaunched with ŌüĀEni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab and would inject 400 million euros into the storage business.
The group reported a 10.3% rise in adjusted full-year net profit to 1.42 billion euros, in line with its guidance. It ŌüĀforecast ŌĆŗa profit of around 1.45 billion euros for 2026. ŌĆŗNet income is expected to grow an average 4.5% per year until 2030 under its new plan.
($1 = ŌĆŗ0.8621 euros)

item.title: Ukraine returns 300 soldiers in second day of POW swaps with Russia, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Ukraine brought home ŌĆŗ300 servicemen and ŌĆītwo civilians from Russia on the ŌĆŗsecond day ŌĆŗof a prisoner swap, ŌüĀPresident Volodymyr ŌĆŗZelenskiy said on ŌĆŗFriday.
Zelenskiy said that privates, sergeants, and officers ŌĆŗwere defending ŌĆŗUkraine at different front ŌĆīareas ŌüĀfrom Donetsk and Luhansk in the east to ŌĆŗKherson ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe south. Zelenskiy posted ŌĆŗpictures of ŌĆŗmen ŌüĀexiting buses, hugging and sharing ŌüĀcigarettes.

item.title: Sri Lanka moving 208 rescued Iranian ship crew to naval camp, sources say
item.description:
GALLE, Sri Lanka, March 6 (Reuters) - Sri Lankan authorities said on Friday they were escorting a second Iranian naval vessel to ŌĆīharbour and moving 208 of its crew to a camp, two days after a U.S. submarine sank an Iranian warship in the same area.
President Anura Kumara Dissanayake said his island nation had a "humanitarian responsibility" to take in the crew, as the ŌĆŗU.S. and Israeli war with Iran raged , wreaking havoc on global markets and disrupting trade and travel.
The ŌĆŗSri Lankan Navy identified the second Iranian ship as naval auxiliary vessel IRIS ŌüĀBooshehr.
Iranian sailors were seen dragging suitcases and carrying bags as they disembarked ŌĆŗin Sri Lanka, in pictures shared by the Presidential Media Division.
Other images showed Sri Lankan navy tug boats ŌĆŗand naval vessels approaching the Booshehr, which Dissanayake earlier said would be moved to the Trincomalee harbour on the eastern coast.
"About 15 crewmen are still aboard the Iranian ship to help with navigation," a Sri Lankan government source said.
The ship ŌĆŗwas experiencing engine problems, that source and another government official said. Both asked not to be named ŌĆŗgiven the sensitivity of the issue.
The crew was brought to the port in Sri Lanka's commercial capital Colombo where they ŌĆīhad ŌüĀmedical check-ups and were then moved in groups to a navy camp in Welisara, about 18 km (10 miles) away, the officials added.
The defence ministry declined to comment and the navy spokesman's office could not be reached.
The ship Booshehr, which had found itself stranded in Sri Lanka's exclusive economic zone outside ŌĆŗits maritime boundary, reached the ŌĆŗarea a day after ŌüĀthe Iranian warship IRIS Dena was sunk while returning from India after a naval exercise.
Dena was hit by a torpedo from a U.S. submarine in the ŌĆŗIndian Ocean, about 19 nautical miles off Sri Lanka's coast, killing 87 people ŌĆŗon board ŌüĀand dramatically widening the scope of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
Iran's foreign minister thanked Sri Lanka for helping rescue survivors from the warship.
"That vessel ... was ceremonial, unloaded, unarmed," Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Saeed Khatibzadeh told reporters in ŌüĀNew Delhi, ŌĆŗwhere he is attending a conference.
Both Washington and Tehran are ŌĆŗkey trade partners for Sri Lanka. The United States accounts for about 40% of its apparel exports and Iran is one of ŌĆŗits main tea buyers.

item.title: Middle East situation is 'major humanitarian emergency', UN refugee agency says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.N. refugee agency said on Friday that nearly 100,000 people ŌĆŗhave been displaced within Lebanon and tens of ŌĆīthousands of Syrian refugees there have fled back over the border, calling the situation in the region a "major humanitarian ŌĆŗemergency".
Israel has issued large-scale evacuation orders for southern Lebanon ŌĆŗand parts of Beirut amid hostilities with ŌüĀthe Iran-backed Lebanese group Hezbollah since a U.S.-Israeli ŌĆŗair campaign against Iran began on February 28.
"UNHCR has ŌĆŗdeclared the escalating crisis in the Middle East as a major humanitarian emergency requiring an immediate response across the region ŌĆŗand into Southeast Asia," Ayaki Ito, the U.N. ŌĆŗrefugee agency's Director of Emergency and Programme Support, told a Geneva ŌĆīpress ŌüĀbriefing.
Ito added that the figures given for the scale of displacement so far are likely an underestimate.
He said that some 100,000 people have been displaced within Iran in ŌĆŗthe first days ŌĆŗof the ŌüĀconflict and that UNHCR staff there are receiving hundreds of calls daily from ŌĆŗIranians seeking assistance.
The World Health Organization is ŌĆŗstepping ŌüĀup disease surveillance in Lebanon due to the mass displacement, said regional director Hanan Balkhy.
"It worries us very ŌüĀmuch, the ŌĆŗnumbers of the displaced populations ŌĆŗand the lack of adequate water and sanitation," she said.

item.title: Lithuania prosecutes five people over 2024 parcel blasts
item.description:
VILNIUS, March 6 (Reuters) - Lithuania's deputy ŌĆīgeneral prosecutor said on Friday he has initiated court proceedings against five ŌĆŗpersons over detonations of ŌĆŗparcels carried by DHL and DPD in ŌüĀEurope in 2024 .
The suspects were ŌĆŗcitizens of Russia, Ukraine and ŌĆŗLithuania and were being accused of terrorism, Deputy General Prosecutor Arturas Urbelis told ŌĆŗa press conference.
Lithuania's prosecution service ŌĆŗand criminal police last year said the ŌĆīdetonations ŌüĀof the parcels in Germany, Britain and Poland were organised and supervised by Russian citizens with ŌĆŗties to ŌĆŗRussia's military ŌüĀintelligence.
Security officials in Lithuania said in 2024 the ŌĆŗfour parcels were part ŌĆŗof ŌüĀa test run for a Russian plot to trigger explosions on ŌüĀcargo ŌĆŗflights to the ŌĆŗUnited States.

item.title: Hungary to expel seven Ukrainian nationals, government spokesperson says
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 6 (Reuters) - Hungary is going to ŌĆŗexpel all seven ŌĆīUkrainian nationals who were detained while ŌĆŗthey were ŌĆŗtransporting cash and gold ŌüĀfrom Austria ŌĆŗto Ukraine, government ŌĆŗspokesman Zoltan Kovacs wrote on X on Friday.
Kovacs ŌĆŗalso said ŌĆŗthat the team transporting ŌĆīcash ŌüĀand gold was "supervised by a former general of ŌĆŗthe Ukrainian ŌĆŗSecurity ŌüĀService, with a former ŌĆŗmajor of ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀUkrainian Air Force acting as deputy ŌüĀand ŌĆŗassisted ŌĆŗby individuals with military experience."

item.title: Israel's Hezbollah attacks are likely to continue beyond Iran war, source says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 6 (Reuters) - Israeli attacks against Lebanon's Iranian-backed ŌĆŗHezbollah group will likely continue after its joint air war with the U.S. against Iran ends, ŌĆīa source briefed on Israel's military strategy told Reuters, describing the two fronts as unconnected.
Israel warned Lebanon before the war that it would strike the country hard if Hezbollah, the most powerful of Iran's regional proxies, gets involved. On Monday, Hezbollah ŌĆŗfired rockets at Israel, sparking Israeli strikes on Beirut's southern suburbs and Lebanon's east and south.
The military ŌĆŗon Thursday warned Lebanese to leave Beirut's southern suburbs after ordering them to clear ŌüĀa broad swathe of the south as it carries out air strikes that a military source said ŌĆŗare aimed at "removing the threat" posed by Hezbollah.
Early on Friday, Hezbollah warned Israelis to leave towns near the border.
The ŌĆŗsource, who spoke on the condition of anonymity to describe Israel's assessment of fighting with Hezbollah, said Israel would not tolerate residents of northern Israel, who have not evacuated their towns and villages, to be under fire. The source did not ŌĆŗrefer to the Hezbollah warning.
That meant that Israeli operations in Lebanon would likely continue even when the ŌĆŗIran strikes draw to a close, the source said. Israel's military did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Two ŌĆīsenior Lebanese ŌüĀsecurity officials and a foreign security official based in Lebanon said they also expected Israel to pursue military operations in Lebanon even once the broader conflict with Iran came to a close.
"This is about ending Hezbollah once and for all," one of the Lebanese security officials said, of the group, which held major sway over ŌĆŗthe Lebanese state before Israeli ŌĆŗattacks in 2024 killed ŌüĀits leader and many of its fighters.
All three officials said a long-term Israeli military occupation of the entire border strip of southern Lebanon was likely.
Israel has said ŌĆŗit will not evacuate its border towns and has sent more soldiers into ŌĆŗLebanon, saying ŌüĀthis was a defensive measure meant to protect its citizens who live nearby.
The Lebanese health ministry has reported that 123 people have been killed and another 683 wounded as a result of Israeli attacks this week. Its figures ŌüĀdo not ŌĆŗdistinguish between civilians and combatants.
Trump said on Tuesday the U.S. ŌĆŗand Israeli air attacks that began on Saturday had been projected to last four to five weeks but could go on longer.

item.title: EU halts visa-free travel for Georgian officials
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 6 (Reuters) - The European Commission said on Friday it ŌĆŗwas suspending visa-free travel for ŌĆīholders of Georgian diplomatic, service and official passports in response to ŌĆŗwhat it called a ŌĆŗdeliberate and persisting violation of ŌüĀthe commitments taken under ŌĆŗits visa-free regime.
"Representatives of Georgian authorities ŌĆŗhave to use their diplomatic or service passport when travelling to ŌĆŗthe EU for official and ŌĆŗdiplomatic purposes. Not doing so may ŌĆīresult ŌüĀin the issuance of an entry ban," the Commission said in a statement.
Once among the ŌĆŗmost ŌĆŗdemocratic and ŌüĀpro-Western of the successor states to emerge ŌĆŗfrom the Soviet Union, ŌĆŗGeorgia ŌüĀhas turned increasingly authoritarian since the outbreak of the war in ŌüĀUkraine ŌĆŗand has deepened economic ŌĆŗties with neighbouring Russia.

item.title: UN official: 100,000 Lebanese in shelters after 'unprecedented' Israeli warnings
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 6 (Reuters) - About 100,000 people have fled to shelters in Lebanon and the number of displaced is expected to rapidly increase ŌĆŗfollowing "unprecedented" Israeli warnings ordering people out of large parts ŌĆīof the country, a senior U.N. official said on Friday.
With war raging between Israel and Lebanon's Hezbollah, the Israeli military on Thursday ordered residents ŌĆŗout of Beirut's southern suburbs, including areas controlled by ŌĆŗthe Iran-backed group, as well as parts of the eastern ŌüĀBekaa Valley, after ordering people out of a swathe of ŌĆŗsouth Lebanon on Wednesday.
"What we saw in the last couple of ŌĆŗdays is, I would say ... unprecedented in terms of the scale here in Lebanon of the warnings, the displacement orders, and the reaction, the panic ŌĆŗalso, that this has all created," Imran Riza, U.N. Humanitarian ŌĆŗCoordinator in Lebanon, told Reuters.
"At the moment, there are about 100,000 people that ŌĆīare, ŌüĀas of this morning, in some 477 collective shelters. There are some 57 shelters that still have some space, but basically the capacity is being reached very, very quickly," Riza said.
Noting the ŌĆŗpanic and gridlock ŌĆŗcaused by ŌüĀthe Israeli displacement orders, Riza said: "We had people moving all over the place and not knowing ŌĆŗwhere to go to. So yes, I think we're ŌĆŗgoing ŌüĀto have an increased number quite quickly," he said.
He noted that more than a million people were uprooted in Lebanon during a ŌüĀwar between ŌĆŗHezbollah and Israel in 2024, 75-80% ŌĆŗof whom were not in shelters. "This time again, the majority will not be in ŌĆŗshelters probably," he said.

item.title: Indonesian court acquits four activists accused of inciting deadly 2025 student protests
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 6 (Reuters) - An Indonesian court on Friday acquitted an activist accused of inciting the country's worst riots in over two decades in August last year, a ŌĆŗruling described by rights groups as a small victory for civil liberties in the Southeast ŌĆīAsian country.
Thousands of students, human rights activists and civilians joined nationwide protests against the government's spending priorities, including a decision to increase financial incentives for lawmakers.
The protests turned violent after the killing by police of a motorcycle taxi driver in ŌĆŗthe capital Jakarta, with the riots lasting into September and ending with 10 ŌĆŗpeople killed.
Nearly 7,000 people have been arrested as of February 2026, with 500 ŌüĀsentenced and 125 were still in detention, according to a report released by an ŌĆŗindependent fact-checking organisation set up by human rights groups.
A court in Central Jakarta on Friday acquitted ŌĆŗDelpedro Marhaen, who works for the Lokataru Foundation, a non-government organisation providing legal assistance for protesters. Delpedro was widely considered to be the face of the protests after his arrest in September.
Three other people, including Delpedro's colleague ŌĆŗand two other activists, were also acquitted.
All the defendants were declared not guilty of inciting people ŌĆŗto join the August protests, and were also acquitted on the charges of triggering riots by spreading fake ŌĆīnews ŌüĀand hate speech through social media, the judge said.
The judge also ruled that they had not incited minors to join the protests, thus absolving them of the charge of child exploitation.
"The ruling not only belongs to us but also belongs to all political prisoners.. Imagine we ŌĆŗhave been arrested for ŌĆŗsix months but ŌüĀnow we are proven not guilty," said Delpedro after the trial, as he wrapped himself in an Iranian flag.
A rights group known ŌĆŗas the Commission for the Disappeared and Victims of Violence called ŌĆŗthe ruling ŌüĀan "oasis in the rollback of Indonesia's democracy".
Another group, Indonesian Legal Aid Foundation, described the verdicts as a small victory for civil liberties but said the government must act to protect freedom of expression.
The protests ŌüĀwere President ŌĆŗPrabowo Subianto's first test since his landslide election victory in ŌĆŗ2024 , analysts have said.
The unrest has been described as the worst since 1998, when student protests led to the toppling ŌĆŗof former President Suharto and three decades of authoritarian rule.

item.title: Saudi Aramco boosts Red Sea oil shipments as Hormuz disruption curbs exports
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 6 (Reuters) - Top oil exporter Saudi Arabia is increasing shipments from the Red ŌĆīSea, but the volumes are far from enough to offset the drop from the crisis-hit Strait of Hormuz, shipping data showed.
Saudi Arabia and other Gulf producers - the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Iraq - have halted Hormuz shipments since Saturday after the United States ŌĆŗand Israel attacked Iran and Tehran retaliated with drone and missile strikes.
With hundreds of vessels now stuck ŌĆŗnear the strait, Aramco has told some buyers to load cargoes at the Red Sea ŌüĀport of Yanbu, sources said.
The terminal loaded 9.4 million barrels, or 1.9 million barrels per day (bpd), in the ŌĆŗfirst five days of March, up about 60% from 1.1 million bpd in February and 1.3 million bpd ŌĆŗin January, according to LSEG data.
Saudi Arabia exports over 7 million bpd, of which around 6 million bpd pass through the Strait of Hormuz. The kingdom can, in theory, reroute up to 5 million bpd to the Red Sea via the East-West pipeline.
The ŌĆŗRed Sea port could handle in excess of 4.5 million bpd, but rarely loaded more than 2.5 million ŌĆŗbpd, traders said, and the pipeline is mainly designed to transport Arab Light, one of several crude grades the kingdom ŌĆīproduces.
The Red ŌüĀSea route also carries risks from Houthi forces, whose attacks disrupted shipping during the Israel-Gaza war.
Some tanker fixtures for Yanbu failed due to high freight rates and security risks, traders and ship brokers said. One such was tanker Pantanassa, which had been due to load at Yanbu on March 28 and 29 to deliver oil to South Korea.
Around 10 ŌĆŗtankers are expected to call ŌĆŗat Yanbu, according to ŌüĀKpler data. Deals for at least four more tankers were being fixed on Wednesday and Thursday, shipping sources said.
India's Reliance provisionally agreed to lift one supertanker with up ŌĆŗto 2 million barrels of crude and a smaller Suezmax ship that can carry ŌĆŗup to 1 ŌüĀmillion barrels, the sources, speaking on condition of anonymity because the deals are confidential, said.
Reliance was not immediately available for comment.
Shippers are struggling to find tankers in proximity to Yanbu and it will take several days for new ships to ŌüĀarrive, ŌĆŗJaniv Shah from Rystad Energy said on a conference call on Thursday.
Nearly ŌĆŗ10% of mainstream oil tankers are trapped near the Strait of Hormuz, according to Rystad.

item.title: BP trims board as part of chairman Manifold's reset plan
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - BP (BP.L) , opens new tab Chairman Albert Manifold on Friday announced plans for a slimmer board as ŌĆīpart of the British oil company's reset strategy, with former Shell chief financial officer Simon Henry among those departing.
The leaner board is one of Manifold's changes since arriving as chairman in October and urging a ŌĆŗfaster shift at BP back to oil and gas investments.
In December, Murray Auchincloss abruptly departed ŌĆŗas CEO, with Woodside Energy's Meg O'Neill set to take over the ŌüĀjob in April.
The other directors stepping down from the board are Melody Meyer and Karen ŌĆŗRichardson, Manifold said. Henry was appointed to the board in September, while Richardson and Meyer ŌĆŗjoined in 2021 and 2017, respectively.
After the changes, the board will consist of 10 directors, down from 13. Manifold said the majority will have been appointed in the last five years.
"We believe that a leaner ŌĆŗboard is a more agile board, and that this is essential at this point in ŌĆŗthe execution of BPŌĆÖs reset strategy," Manifold said. "It will allow for faster decision-making and sharper oversight, both ŌĆīof ŌüĀwhich are critical to driving long-term shareholder value."
BP's profits for last year were 16% lower than the previous year's at $7.5 billion. The British oil company announced in February it had suspended its share buybacks to focus on shrinking its debt.
The company embarked on a major strategy shift last ŌĆŗyear, cutting billions in ŌĆŗplanned renewable energy initiatives ŌüĀand shifting its focus back to traditional oil and gas.
This followed a foray into renewables by Auchincloss' predecessor, Bernard Looney.
Woodside's O'Neill is ŌĆŗBP's first external hire for the post in more than a century. ŌĆŗShe will ŌüĀreceive a base salary of 1.6 million pounds, according to the company's annual report on Friday.
Outgoing CEO Auchincloss earned 5.3 million pounds ($7.07 million) last year, down from 5.4 million pounds in 2024, ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗannual report showed.
BP's Annual General Meeting will take place ŌĆŗApril 23.
Shares in BP rose 10% last year, while shares in rivals Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab and Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab increased by 12% ŌĆŗand 11%, respectively.
($1 = 0.7499 pounds)

item.title: BREAKINGVIEWS
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters Breakingviews) - What endgame for Iran are investors pricing in? Judging by moves in financial markets since U.S. President Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu launched the bombing campaign that killed ŌĆŗIranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on Saturday, they seem to expect a swift end to the conflict. Yet that ŌĆībegs the question of what happens afterwards. There's a risk that investors are confusing base-case and best-case scenarios.
There are many possible outcomes in Iran. One scenario is that the current government falls apart following the death of scores of senior leaders. The country could then split along factional or ethnic lines, as Libya did after Muammar Gaddafi ŌĆŗwas overthrown. A second option is that the Islamic Republic limps on in roughly its current form. Or Iran could embrace a ŌĆŗmore open and democratic form of government, reintegrating its economy with the world and encouraging Washington to phase out ŌüĀsanctions.
The first two scenarios could see oil prices spiking above $100 a barrel, from around $87 today, Capital Economics reckons. ThatŌĆÖs because internal turmoil would disrupt ŌĆŗIranŌĆÖs daily output exceeding 3 million barrels, while an outcome where the hardline Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) keeps its grip on power would probably involve ŌĆŗTehran continuing to threaten tankers that sail through the Strait of Hormuz. Only the last scenario would lead to increased Iranian oil production, an economic recovery, and prices of the black stuff falling to $50 a barrel over time.
The relative calm in financial and commodities markets suggests investors expect a swift end to the conflict and to ŌĆŗdisruption, plus a high probability of a benign scenario thereafter. Despite the recent jump, oil prices remain far below the $130 a barrel level they ŌĆŗapproached following RussiaŌĆÖs invasion of Ukraine four years ago. European gas prices have jumped 60% to 52 euros per megawatt-hour, way below the 100 euros per MWh at ŌĆīwhich they ŌüĀconsistently traded in 2022. Meanwhile, gas prices for delivery a few years hence have hardly budged, implying a short interruption.
Washington claimed on Thursday it had destroyed over 60% of TehranŌĆÖs missiles and launchers, plus 30 of its ships. Trump also asserted that Iran wanted talks, that the U.S. would have to be involved in choosing the next leader of Iran, and IRGC ally Mojtaba Khamenei, the late leader's hardline son, was an unlikely choice as a ŌĆŗsuccessor.
Still, a fifth of the worldŌĆÖs ŌĆŗoil supply is currently trapped ŌüĀbehind the Strait of Hormuz. On Goldman Sachs figures, oil prices will rise even if the status quo persists for only a few months. The IRGC has been taking a stronger governing role since the latest war ŌĆŗcommenced, Reuters reported on Wednesday citing six Iranian and regional sources. As such, the market may be attaching too ŌĆŗmuch weight to ŌüĀa swift end to the conflict ŌĆö and choosing the wrong base case.
Follow George Hay on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Nepal's rapper-mayor Balendra Shah poised to become prime minister
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - After Nepal's historic youth-led uprising last September killed 77 people and forced then-Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli to resign, a 35-year-old rapper-turned-politician posted a typically terse ŌĆŗmessage to millions of followers on social media.
"Dear Gen Z, the resignation of your killer has come," Balendra Shah - popularly known only as Balen - wrote. "Now your generation ŌĆīwill have to lead the country. Be prepared."
Five months on, the musician who cut his political teeth in 2022 when he became the mayor of the capital Kathmandu, is poised to become Nepal's next prime minister following the country's first election since the September uprising.
Shah's Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) was leading in around 100 seats, far ahead of its main rivals, early counting trends from the election commission showed on Friday.
Final results, covering 165 seats decided by direct vote and 110 through proportional ŌĆŗrepresentation, are expected within days.
The Nepali Congress, currently in second place, has already conceded defeat, and analysts said the RSP's dominant showing means it will likely form the next government.
"Balen ŌĆŗShah is so popular that now buses coming to Kathmandu have stickers on them saying, 'Headed to Balen's city'", said Bipin Adhikari, a constitutional law ŌüĀexpert who teaches at Kathmandu University.
If Shah is able to take power, it would cap a dramatic rise for a man who entered the public spotlight with rap music critical of the establishment ŌĆŗand parleyed his popularity to ascend to high political office.
It would also potentially reshape the politics of Nepal , a small Himalayan nation wedged between China and India, that has long been dominated by a handful ŌĆŗof established parties .
Some of Shah's nationwide appeal is driven by the work he has done as the mayor of Kathmandu, where he focused on improving the urban infrastructure, such as waste management, and ensuring the delivery of services like healthcare.
He has also faced criticism, including from Human Rights Watch, for allegedly using police to seize the properties of street vendors and landless people.
Shah - who resigned as mayor in January to contest the general election - ŌĆŗdid not respond to requests for an interview and questions from Reuters sent via email.
Unlike much of Nepal's political elite comprising veterans from older generations, Shah has made it a habit to ŌĆŗlargely shun the mainstream press.
Instead, it is his prolific social media presence, with over 3.5 million followers on platforms like Facebook, that enables him to connect directly with young Nepalis.
"What makes Balen special is that he stays ŌĆīconnected with ŌüĀthe youth through his short messages on social media, but it would not be a cakewalk for him after becoming prime minister," said independent political analyst Puranjan Acharya.
Born to a father who practiced traditional Ayurvedic medicine and a homemaker mother, Shah showed an early inclination towards poetry that evolved into a love of rap music, influenced by American artists including Tupac Shakur and Curtis "50 Cent" Jackson, according to an aide.
After securing an undergraduate degree in civil engineering in Nepal, Shah went on to study for a master's degree in structural engineering in southern India - by which time he had ŌĆŗalready emerged as a rap star in his ŌĆŗhome country.
His songs, often taking on Nepal's ŌüĀruling class, struck a chord with many in a country where about 20% of the 30 million population live in grinding poverty.
Released in 2019, one of Shah's best-known songs, "Balidan" - or sacrifice in the Nepali language - has over 12 million views on YouTube.
Its lyrics read:
"Let me speak, sir, it is not a ŌĆŗcrime,
Let me open the mind, I am not a curse to the palace,
My mind is not bad, it is not afraid to speak ŌĆŗthe truth."
Last ŌüĀDecember, Shah joined the RSP, led by former TV host-turned-politician Rabi Lamichhane, as its prime ministerial candidate.
In its manifesto, Shah's RSP has vowed to create 1.2 million jobs and reduce forced migration, in an effort to tap into frustration over unemployment and low wages that have pushed millions of Nepalis to search for work overseas.
The party has also pledged to raise Nepal's per capita income from $1,447 to $3,000, more than double the ŌüĀnation's economy to $100 ŌĆŗbillion GDP and provide safety nets such as healthcare insurance for the entire population - all within five years.
At the national ŌĆŗlevel, analysts foresee that if he is elected, much of Shah's success will depend on the talent he surrounds himself with to overhaul a moribund administrative system, riven by corruption.
"It needs a team, experts and support," Acharya said, "Under the existing ŌĆŗstate apparatus, he can't perform and he will be finished like wood attacked by termites."

item.title: Exclusive: Iran spent years fostering proxies in Iraq. Now, many arenŌĆÖt eager to join the war
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 6 (Reuters) - Iran has spent decades and billions of dollars preparing foreign proxy fighters like A.J., a commander in a pro-Iranian paramilitary group in Iraq, for a moment just like this. Since the U.S. and Israel went to war on the Islamic Republic a week ago, A.J. has been awaiting marching orders from Tehran.
But they have yet to come. And so as the leadership in Tehran faces a potentially existential threat, many of the fighters and militia groups the Iranians cultivated in Iraq have so far not entered the fight for them. There has been no mass mobilization of Iran's proxies inside Iraq, one of the last redoubts of the Islamic Republic's once-formidable system of alliances stretching from Gaza, Lebanon and Syria to Yemen and Iraq.
Some pro-Iranian groups in Iraq have claimed attacks in recent days, to be sure. One group said it had fired drones at "enemy bases in Iraq and the region," and several explosions rocked the northern city of Erbil, a Kurdish stronghold that hosts a U.S. base. But most missile and drone attacks have come directly from Iran, Kurdish officials say. The more than two-dozen ŌĆŗattacks claimed online in the name of the Islamic Resistance of Iraq ŌĆō a label used by various militants ŌĆō have caused no significant damage, and in some cases there is no evidence of the attacks.
Even if direct orders do come from Tehran, A.J. believes that they'll only be issued to two or three of the dozens of Iraqi Shi'ite Muslim paramilitaries nurtured by Iran. "I just don't think most of them are reliable anymore," he told Reuters. "Some will act. Others would have front groups that could launch attacks with deniability. But many are just ŌĆīlooking out for their own interests these days."
The trajectory of A.J.'s personal journey as a member of an Iranian-backed force in Iraq tracks the rise and fall of Iran's strategy of spreading proxy militias through the region, under the leadership of the elite Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps and its expeditionary Quds Force, to fight America and Israel. His is the story of how the Israelis and Americans wore down and diminished most of these proxies, leaving the Islamic Republic facing its most perilous moment largely alone.
A.J., who is from Shi'ite-majority southern Iraq, spoke on condition he not be identified, for fear of being targeted by Israeli or U.S. strikes. Reuters is using the initials of one of his nicknames for clarity.
A.J. blamed several factors for the reduced military potency of Iran's Iraqi proxies: Israel and America's war of attrition against other regional allies, the loss of Syria as a supply line, and the transition of key commanders into Iraqi political and economic life.
His assessment is shared by more than two dozen people interviewed by Reuters, including militia members, Iraqi and Western officials, Shi'ite clerics, and close watchers of Iran's once-vaunted "Axis of Resistance." They painted a picture of a proxy network hollowed out by years of targeted assassinations of hard-to-replace leaders; the loss of secure bases for training and weapons transit; and the transformation of Iraqi commanders into wealthy politicians and businessmen with more to lose than gain from confronting the West.
The Iraqi militia leaders "don't want sanctions on them as individuals, they want to have access to Western healthcare, to have their children educated abroad," said Gareth Stansfield, a professor of ŌĆŗMiddle East politics at Exeter University and senior fellow at the Atlantic Council, who has advised the British and regional governments. "That's accelerated since the 12-day war" between Israel and Iran last June, he said.
Iraqi security officials and paramilitary insiders say Iran's proxies could yet enter the fray in force if the war drags on, if there's a U.S.-Israeli attack they perceive as being against Shi'ites as a whole, or if U.S.-backed Kurdish groups attack Iran.
Even if they wanted to fight, though, these Iran-backed groups lack the means they once had. They have used outmoded weaponry in their handful of attacks since the war began, ŌĆŗaccording to Iraqi security officials. Tehran has sent no new weapons to his group since the battle with Israel last year, A.J. said. Reuters couldn't determine if this was the case for other pro-Iran militias in Iraq.
During last year's confrontation with Israel, Iran's Revolutionary Guards instructed A.J.'s group to retaliate, which they did, firing drones toward Israel. But moving weapons now would be "too risky, they could be spotted by reconnaissance," A.J. said.
Israel's military told Reuters that "terrorist factions in Iraq operate ŌüĀas a proxy of Iran."
"Operations against the Iranian-led resistance axis, combined with a clear understanding that Israel would not stand idly by as its civilians were attacked, have led to a decrease in attacks from Iraqi territory toward Israel," it said in a statement.
The Iraqi and Iranian governments didn't respond to Reuters questions for this story. The White House and the Pentagon also didn't respond to requests for comment.
On day two of the war, A.J. and his comrades mourned Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, killed by an airstrike during the Israeli-U.S. assault on Tehran.
Still, no orders came to attack.
In Baghdad, thousands of Iraqi supporters of the ayatollah, including off-duty fighters ŌĆŗfrom Iran-backed paramilitary groups, nevertheless rushed the gates of the fortified Green Zone, chanting "death to America" and hoping to attack the U.S. embassy.
They never managed to reach the bridge leading into the Green Zone, and were beaten back and tear-gassed by Iraqi riot police. None of the well-known commanders of Iranian proxy militias were in sight.
Qais al-Khazali, a U.S.-sanctioned commander whose militia's banners were raised by the protesters, issued an anodyne statement on X condemning the U.S. and asking supporters to show their anger by "wearing black." Khazali in years past had threatened American interests, and men he commanded had killed U.S. troops in Iraq in 2007. This time, he made no call to arms.
Khazali's office ŌĆŗdidn't respond to a request for comment.
One protester in Baghdad bemoaned the lack of support from top pro-Iran paramilitary leaders. "Where are you?" the protester chided in a video posted online. "If you don't come stand with us and burn the (American) embassy, you are cowards."
The protester was referring to a similar incident in 2019, when Iran-backed protesters and militants attacked the U.S. embassy with firebombs in response to American air raids in Iraq and Syria that killed dozens of their paramilitary comrades.
On that occasion, the leaders had stood among them, including Khazali. The moment marked a high point of Iranian Shi'ite proxy power in the region.
Sixteen years earlier, Iraqi Shi'ite militants fought the Americans with Iranian support after the 2003 U.S. invasion toppled Sunni ruler Saddam Hussein. The militants went on to embed themselves in Iraqi government institutions. The number of Shi'ite paramilitaries swelled after the rise of Islamic State in 2014, as men rushed to defend their country against the extremist Sunni group.
The Shi'ite commanders, many close to Iran for decades, capitalized on the victory over Islamic State in 2017 to win seats in parliamentary elections the next year. They also came to dominate the Popular Mobilization Forces, a 150,000-strong state paramilitary umbrella organization formed to fight Islamic State.
The growing power of Iran-backed paramilitaries in Iraq coincided with the political rise of Hezbollah in Lebanon. In Syria, President Bashar al-Assad, an Iran ally, meanwhile weathered a civil war with Iranian proxy help.
The U.S. embassy assault in 2019 would be a turning point. It triggered the U.S. assassination in early 2020 of fabled Iranian commander ŌĆŗQassem Soleimani, head of the Islamic Revolutionary Guards' Quds Force, who directed overseas operations and coordinated Iran's proxies.
The killing, ordered by President Donald Trump , sent the militias scrambling for a coordinator. Soleimani's replacement, Esmail Ghaani, lacks the same stature and authority, many militia figures say.
A.J. proudly keeps a picture on his phone of him meeting Ghaani. But he said there's "no comparison" between the two leaders. "Soleimani was not just a once in a generation leader, he was a once in history leader," he said.
Reuters was unable to reach Ghaani for comment.
After Soleimani's death, Iran's most trusted proxy, Lebanese Hezbollah, stepped in to coordinate the various Tehran-backed groups across the region. A.J. said a Lebanese political figure close ŌĆŗto Hezbollah would bring the factions together in Beirut to talk strategy. A.J.'s group still kept operatives in Beirut and Tehran at that time.
That would soon change.
The outbreak of war in October 2023 between Israel and Iran's Palestinian ally Hamas drew in Hezbollah. That led to the Israeli assassination in September 2024 of Hezbollah's charismatic leader Hassan Nasrallah.
"Nasrallah was also irreplaceable. Leaders like this come along only once," A.J. said. The killing of Nasrallah and most of Hezbollah's senior leadership meant Beirut was no longer safe, he said.
His group soon confined its operatives to Iraq and Tehran. "We used to train in Lebanon on drone systems. Now it's Tehran," he told Reuters a few days before the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran.
All the sources Reuters ŌĆīinterviewed agreed that Nasrallah's killing dealt a ŌüĀsevere blow to the whole axis, impacting the Iraqis' ability to visit Beirut.
"Everything changed after Nasrallah was killed," said Mustafa Fahs, a Lebanese political activist in close contact with Iraqi Shi'ite leaders.
Fahs said the decapitation of Hezbollah's leadership loosened the group's grip on state institutions in Lebanon, including Beirut airport, depriving Iraqi proxies of a means to visit without scrutiny by Lebanese government intelligence.
In recent days, Hezbollah has managed to conduct limited attacks, firing rockets and drones into Israel from Lebanon. Hezbollah and the Lebanese government didn't respond to requests for comment.
A.J.'s group and other Iranian proxies were deployed to Syria from across the region in 2011 to prevent the collapse of Assad's regime in an uprising that morphed into a civil war dominated by Sunni Islamist rebels. For A.J. and his comrades, the mission was to protect Shi'ite shrines in Syria. For the wider Iran-backed axis, Syria provided a crucial land route from Iran to Hezbollah in Lebanon and enabled the movement of weapons and fighters across the region. With their help, plus Russian support, Assad held on.
The proxies reduced their presence in Syria around 2020 when it looked like Assad's regime had survived, but still kept offices and weapons in Syria for use against Israel, A.J. said.
But things were changing. During a tense meeting of Iran-backed factions in Damascus in 2023, A.J. said he and fellow Iraqi commanders warned Syrian military officials that they were dangerously infiltrated by Israeli agents. "There were enemy agents everywhere in Syria, just waiting to give us away," he said.
In the ensuing months ŌĆō just before Nasrallah's killing ŌĆō Israel started assassinating Iranian commanders in Syria. Syrians bought off by Israel were giving coordinates for the attacks, A.J. said. Michael Knights, an expert on Iraqi factions at New York-based risk consultancy Horizon Engage, who has worked closely with the U.S. government in sanctions enforcement, said Israel had local agents helping provide the targeting.
The Israeli military didn't address specific questions about the targeting of Iranian commanders in Syria.
Assad's ouster in December 2024 was a hammer blow to Tehran and its proxies. With Iran's axis weakened and Nasrallah dead, Syria ŌĆŗwas taken over by former Al Qaeda fighters led by Ahmed al-Sharaa, who would become the country's president in 2025.
The sudden defeat sent the remaining pro-Iran factions ŌĆŗscattering, with Iraqi groups withdrawing across the border.
"Damascus was the key for coordinating the axis of resistance," A.J. said. "That was a big turning point ŌüĀfor us."
Syria's government didn't respond to questions for this story.
