The group launched 60 drones and rockets on March 2, the first day it attacked Israel, and a similar number the following day, said the second foreign official, who tracks Hezbollah's activities closely.
But on March 4, Hezbollah launched more than double that number of projectiles, a sign it had been ŌĆŗable to draw from its larger caches, the official said.
ALMA, ŌĆŗan Israeli think tank that monitors security on Israel's ŌüĀnorthern border, said it assessed that Hezbollah's arsenal on the eve of its attack included approximately 25,000 rockets and missiles, most of them short- and medium-range.
A video published by Hezbollah on March 4 showed a fighter setting up a drone in a wooded area. Riad Kahwaji, a Dubai-based defence analyst and founder of the Institute for Near East and Gulf Military ŌĆŗAnalysis, identified the drone in the video as a Shahed-101, which he told Reuters could be produced locally.
Hezbollah has also dispatched fighters from its ŌĆŗelite Radwan force back to ŌüĀsouthern Lebanon, Reuters reported this week . They had been withdrawn from the area after the 2024 conflict.
Israeli strikes after the 2024 ceasefire included the targeting of what Israel said were Hezbollah training camps. In late February, the Israeli military said it struck eight military compounds used by the Radwan force to store weapons and prepare for a confrontation.
The Israeli official and the first foreign official said Hezbollah had been struggling to recruit new operatives as a result.
The group lost 5,000 fighters in the 2024 war, an unprecedented blow ŌüĀto its fighting ŌĆŗforce, though the second Lebanese source said it still had some 95,000 fighters left.
In the lead-up to its entry into the current regional war, ŌĆŗHezbollah had become convinced Israel would carry out a major strike on the group that would seek to "disable its ability to retaliate," the first Lebanese source said.
A third foreign official familiar with Hezbollah's thinking said that assessment had driven the group's decision to launch the first salvo, fearing Israel would eventually ŌĆŗturn its attention from Iran to Hezbollah.
"They knew they were next on the list," the official said.

item.title: US files complaints for $15.3 million in proceeds allegedly linked to Iranian oil network
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The United States on Friday filed two civil ŌĆŗforfeiture complaints for more ŌĆīthan $15.3 million used to allegedly fund an illicit Iranian oil ŌĆŗdistribution network, the Justice ŌĆŗDepartment said in a statement.
The ŌüĀcomplaints by the Justice ŌĆŗDepartment allege that the funds ŌĆŗwere being used by Mohammad Hossein Shamkhani to "operate multiple distribution companies" ŌĆŗengaged in selling and ŌĆŗshipping Iranian oil and other commodities in ŌĆīviolation ŌüĀof U.S. sanctions.
Hossein Shamkhani is the son of Ali Shamkhani, it said, a ŌĆŗtop adviser ŌĆŗto ŌüĀthe former Supreme Leader of Iran Ayatollah ŌĆŗAli Khamenei. Both Ali ŌĆŗShamkhani ŌüĀand Ali Khamenei were killed in the U.S. and ŌüĀIsraeli strikes ŌĆŗagainst Iran ŌĆŗlast week.

item.title: Swedish coast guard boards suspected stateless ship in the Baltic Sea
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The Swedish coast guard said it boarded and took control of a suspected stateless vessel in Swedish ŌĆŗwaters of the Baltic Sea on Friday, and it ŌĆīwas carrying out investigations on the ship.
"At 3:50 p.m. today, the Coast Guard took control of a vessel with unclear flag ŌĆŗstatus and thus suspected of being a stateless vessel," ŌĆŗit said in a statement, adding that the ŌüĀboarding had taken place off the coast of the ŌĆŗsouthern Swedish town Trelleborg.
The ship Caffa was sailing under a ŌĆŗGuinean flag, but the coast guard said it regarded the vessel to be stateless "based on national and international legislation". Swedish police said the ŌĆŗship was suspected of sailing under a false flag.
According ŌĆŗto ship-tracking service Marine Traffic, Caffa is a 96-meter general cargo ŌĆīship.
Sweden's ŌüĀminister for civil defence, Carl-Oskar Bohlin, said in a post on X that the ship was a freighter on Ukraine's sanctions list.
"The ownership structure is unclear and there are ŌĆŗsuspicions that the ŌĆŗvessel is ŌüĀnot insured. As recently as this summer, the ship is said to have changed from ŌĆŗa Russian to a Guinean flag," Bohlin ŌĆŗwrote.
The coast ŌüĀguard said it had launched a preliminary investigation into suspected violation of maritime law regarding lack of seaworthiness.
"The Coast Guard ŌüĀhas personnel ŌĆŗon board to gather information ŌĆŗabout the condition of the vessel and crew, conduct searches, and conduct interviews," ŌĆŗit added.

item.title: Ghana peacekeeping battalion hit by missile attacks in Lebanon, Ghana army says
item.description:
ACCRA, March 6 (Reuters) - The headquarters of Ghana's United ŌĆŗNations peacekeeping battalion in Lebanon ŌĆīwas hit by missile attacks on Friday, leaving two soldiers critically ŌĆŗinjured, Ghana's armed forces ŌĆŗsaid in a statement.
Lebanon was pulled ŌüĀinto the war in the ŌĆŗMiddle East on Monday, when Hezbollah ŌĆŗfired rockets at Israel that ignited a new Israeli offensive against the group.
Ghanaian ŌĆŗsoldiers are deployed as part ŌĆŗof the UNIFIL mission in Lebanon.
The statement ŌĆīfrom ŌüĀGhana's armed forces did not say who fired the missiles, but it said Ghana's foreign ŌĆŗministry ŌĆŗhad protested ŌüĀthe incident at U.N. headquarters in New York.
In ŌĆŗaddition to the two ŌĆŗcritically ŌüĀinjured soldiers, the statement said that another soldier "has been traumatised" ŌüĀwhile ŌĆŗthe officers' mess ŌĆŗfacility was hit and burned down.

item.title: Germany pulls further troops out of Middle East
item.description:
BERLIN, March 6 (Reuters) - Germany has pulled additional Bundeswehr troops out of the Middle East, ŌĆŗa military spokesman told Reuters on Friday, ŌĆīthe seventh day of a U.S.ŌĆōIsraeli war on Iran that has convulsed the region.
Soldiers deployed ŌĆŗwith the UNIFIL mission in Lebanon ŌĆŗare being withdrawn due to the security ŌüĀsituation, the spokesperson said after the ŌĆŗBundeswehr already sharply reduced its presence in Erbil ŌĆŗin northern Iraq.
The RND newspaper network, which first reported the news, said that German troops stationed ŌĆŗin Bahrain had already returned home, and ŌĆŗpreparations for withdrawals from Kuwait were underway. The spokesperson ŌĆīdeclined ŌüĀto comment on this.
The RND report also said that soldiers and staff from the German embassy in Baghdad were being ŌĆŗrelocated to Jordan. ŌĆŗThe ŌüĀforeign ministry did not respond to a request for comment from ŌĆŗReuters on the move.
Roughly 500 German ŌĆŗsoldiers ŌüĀare deployed across the region, mainly in Iraq and Jordan. Officials have recently moved ŌüĀpersonnel ŌĆŗout of camps and ŌĆŗreduced some contingents amid heightened security risks.

item.title: Barclays says Brent could test $120/bbl if Middle East tensions persist
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Barclays said on Friday that Brent crude could potentially test $120 a barrel if the Middle ŌĆŗEast conflict persists for another couple of weeks.
"These numbers ŌĆīmight seem too high, especially given widespread pessimism about the oil market outlook heading into this year, but we reiterate that fundamentals are stronger ŌĆŗand risks are bigger than the Russia-Ukraine conflict , when we ŌĆŗsaw these levels materialize," Barclays added.
Oil prices have jumped ŌüĀsharply as the widening U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran has effectively closed ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz, constraining Middle East supplies.
Shipping through the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz, which carries about a fifth of global oil and oil and liquefied natural gas, has been disrupted after Iran threatened to fire on ŌĆŗpassing vessels.
Brent crude futures were trading around $93.60 per barrel and ŌĆŗWest Texas Intermediate were at $91.62 as of 1857 GMT.
Barclays said oil volumes stranded ŌĆīon ŌüĀtankers in the Middle East Gulf have risen by about 85 million barrels since the conflict began, adding that risks to oil prices remain skewed to the upside.
U.S. President Donald Trump demanded ŌĆŗIran's "unconditional surrender" on ŌĆŗFriday, a dramatic ŌüĀescalation of his demands a week into the war he launched alongside Israel, which could make ŌĆŗit more difficult to negotiate a swift end ŌĆŗto hostilities.
"Production shut-ins ŌüĀin Iraq and Kuwait are already happening and might spread to UAE and even Saudi Arabia over time," Barclays said.
Barclays said ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗfarŌĆæend 10% scenario now implies Brent could ŌĆŗhit $150 a barrel before the end of the month.

item.title: Trump to forge ahead with immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to forge ahead with his aggressive immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller and a new homeland secretary nominee who shares Trump's ŌĆŗhardline view, current and former U.S. officials and lawmakers said.
The Republican president fired Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on Thursday after months of controversy over heavy-handed immigration enforcement tactics ŌĆīand lawmaker questions about government contracts and turmoil within her department.
But Miller - the White House deputy chief of staff and the architect of TrumpŌĆÖs immigration agenda - retains control of the issue, three U.S. officials said. And TrumpŌĆÖs pick to become homeland secretary, U.S. Senator Markwayne Mullin of Oklahoma, is viewed as closely aligned with TrumpŌĆÖs approach.
ŌĆ£Stephen is a survivor,ŌĆØ one of the officials said, requesting anonymity to discuss internal dynamics. The official said NoemŌĆÖs firing was not a referendum on Trump's restrictive ŌĆŗagenda, but rather on the execution of it.
In response to a request for comment, a White House official said Miller helps coordinate a wide range of issues - from immigration to counter-cartel ŌĆŗoperations - and is "working to ensure the President's policy agenda is implemented."
The Department of Homeland Security and Mullin's office did not respond to requests for comment.
Trump won ŌüĀback the White House in 2024 with a campaign that heavily focused on stopping illegal immigration and ramping up deportations. Immigration was one of Trump's best-polling issues after returning to office - and a key ŌĆŗfocus for the Republican Party broadly. But amid backlash over his aggressive enforcement in U.S. cities, support for his immigration approach declined in recent months , Reuters/Ipsos polls show.
Republicans currently hold narrow majorities in both chambers of ŌĆŗthe U.S. Congress but are heading into midterm elections in November that threaten their control on Washington.
White House spokesperson Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday in an X post related to NoemŌĆÖs dismissal that TrumpŌĆÖs immigration goals remained unchanged.
ŌĆ£President TrumpŌĆÖs immigration agenda is keeping our border secure and deporting illegal alien criminals from our country, and it will continue without interruption,ŌĆØ she said.
Under NoemŌĆÖs leadership, DHS sent thousands of federal immigration agents to Los Angeles, Chicago, Minneapolis and other Democratic-led cities to seek ŌĆŗout immigration offenders, sweeping through residential neighborhoods and chasing day laborers in Home Depot parking lots.
But after federal agents shot and killed two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis - Renee Good and Alex Pretti - the administration said it ŌĆŗwould move to a less public-facing ŌĆ£targetedŌĆØ approach to immigration arrests.
Current and former federal immigration officials said there had been no immediate policy shift since NoemŌĆÖs departure was announced, but several said they expected the administration to continue to avoid ŌĆīhigh-profile surges into ŌüĀU.S. cities following the fallout in Minneapolis.
Mullin, 48, became a senator in 2023 after a decade in the U.S. House of Representatives. Like Noem, he is a rancher and owns a cattle ranch in Oklahoma that also serves as a wedding venue, among other businesses.
Even as a freshman, Mullin has stood out from the crowd of 100 senators, at times presiding over the Senate wearing a large, light-colored cowboy hat.
He had a short-lived career as a mixed martial arts fighter and garnered widespread media attention in 2023 when he rose from his chair during a Senate hearing to challenge Teamsters President ŌĆŗSean O'Brien to "stand your butt up" and fight.
Mullin voted ŌĆŗfor a Republican-backed funding package last year ŌüĀthat devoted a historic $170 billion to immigration enforcement through September 2029 and backs core elements of TrumpŌĆÖs immigration platform.
Republican lawmakers praised Mullin after the news that Trump would nominate him for DHS secretary, a position that requires majority confirmation in the Senate.
ŌĆ£He's strong on the border and that's what we need,ŌĆØ Republican Senator Eric ŌĆŗSchmitt of Missouri told reporters on Thursday. ŌĆ£The deportations will continue."
Noem was sharply criticized by both Democrats and Republicans for swiftly saying Good and Pretti were ŌĆŗengaged in ŌĆ£domestic terrorismŌĆØ after they ŌüĀwere killed and before a full investigation.
Mullin similarly portrayed Pretti as a threat despite video evidence that undercut that claim. He said Pretti was "a deranged individual" who had a loaded pistol and intended "to cause max damage," during an interview with Fox News on January 24, the day Pretti was killed.
Democrats on Capitol Hill welcomed NoemŌĆÖs removal but said the Trump administrationŌĆÖs approach to immigration enforcement still needed to change. Democrats have blocked funding for DHS ŌüĀsince mid-February in ŌĆŗa push to force the White House to moderate its tactics.
Senator Chris Murphy, a Democrat from Connecticut, said funding talks are ŌĆŗbeing run out of the White House and that he does not expect NoemŌĆÖs firing to break the stalemate.
ŌĆ£I think weŌĆÖre better off without her, but she wasnŌĆÖt running the department,ŌĆØ Murphy said on Thursday. ŌĆ£Stephen Miller runs that department and will continue to ŌĆŗrun the department so I donŌĆÖt really think much will change.ŌĆØ

item.title: Israeli military says unintentionally struck UN agency truck in Gaza
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Israel's military said on Friday that a "firing component" launched by its navy unintentionally struck a fuel ŌĆŗtruck belonging to a United Nations agency in Gaza ŌĆīthe previous day, an incident that prompted the agency to publicly call for a full investigation.
The United Nations Office for Project Services, ŌĆŗwhich oversees fuel distribution in Gaza, said that the ŌĆŗempty fuel truck was struck on Thursday around ŌüĀ5 a.m. from the direction of the sea, causing damage ŌĆŗto the vehicle. There were no injuries.
"Our teams are taking ŌĆŗextraordinary risks every day to keep humanitarian operations and life-sustaining services running," UNOPS Executive Director Jorge Moreira da Silva said in a statement, ŌĆŗcalling for an investigation into the incident.
"They should not ŌĆŗhave to do that under fire," he said.
In response to Reuters questions, ŌĆīthe ŌüĀIsraeli military said that the incident occurred during defensive naval activity, and that a firing component deviated from its intended trajectory.
The fuel truck sustained "minor damage", the military said in a ŌĆŗstatement. The military ŌĆŗdid not ŌüĀsay what type of munitions had been fired, or what had been the navy's intended ŌĆŗtarget.
"The incident was reviewed, and lessons were learned ŌĆŗaccordingly," ŌüĀit said, without providing further details.
The fuel truck had been on its way to the Kerem Shalom crossing when it was ŌüĀstruck, ŌĆŗand the truck's movements had been ŌĆŗcoordinated with Israeli authorities in advance, UNOPS said.

item.title: Private credit woes come for Larry FinkŌĆÖs legacy
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Rising disquiet in private credit is uncomfortable for a lot of Wall Street titans, but especially for Larry Fink. The BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab boss moved aggressively to transform his firm, the kingpin of public-markets investing for ŌĆŗthe masses, into a private-markets rival to the likes of Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab or Apollo Global Management (APO.N) , opens new tab . The timing now looks awkward, as worries ŌĆīabout overheated lending mount and a key retail fund caps investor withdrawals. FinkŌĆÖs legacy project , and his companyŌĆÖs strengths, will be tested.
As part of a $28 billion string of deals to move into private markets, BlackRock agreed to acquire non-bank lender HPS Investment Partners in 2024. Included in that $12 billion acquisition was HPS Corporate Lending Partners, or HLEND, a nontraded business development ŌĆŗcompany (BDC). Such funds target affluent individual investors, allowing them to withdraw their cash up to a set cap, typically of 5% of shares per ŌĆŗquarter. According to a shareholder letter , opens new tab , HLEND received requests totaling 9.3%, but will only offer redemptions up to its limit, ŌüĀas it was designed to do.
This is far from the first hiccup in private credit. A Blue Owl (OWL.N) , opens new tab fund barred regular withdrawals, instead selling assets to raise ŌĆŗcash and return a huge chunk at once. To satisfy elevated redemptions at BCRED, the largest nontraded BDC around, Blackstone employees and the firm itself put in ŌĆŗa combined $400 million to ensure all requests were covered.
HLEND, though, is the first BDC of its kind to actually prorate redemptions, though others are expected to end up following suit, according to Kevin Gannon of investment bank RA Stanger. Turning theoretical fine-print restrictions into reality may well annoy, and possibly frighten, customers.
When BlackRock bought HPS, private credit was running red-hot. ŌĆŗAssets under management in direct lending funds alone had climbed to $889 billion, according to Preqin data. Apollo and its ilk saw an addressable market in ŌĆŗthe tens of trillions of dollars by expanding into IOUs secured by specific assets. These strategies are increasingly pitched as a fit for the kind of mom-and-pop savers that made ŌĆīBlackRock a ŌüĀcolossus, with $14 trillion of assets under management.
The company got there in part by inking perhaps the most successful deal in modern asset management history, snapping up BarclaysŌĆÖ BGI in 2009 for $13.5 billion and riding then-explosive growth in exchange traded funds. If anyone has a track record of cracking new markets and reaching the retail masses through M&A, itŌĆÖs Larry Fink.
His public-markets business is still immensely successful, and HLEND is only one small piece of a larger plan. Still, rivals ŌĆŗlike Blackstone are increasingly trying to eat ŌĆŗBlackRock's lunch by selling retail products. ŌüĀMeanwhile, from Apollo boss Marc Rowan to JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon, warnings of a coming private credit , opens new tab shakeout are rising. As HLENDŌĆÖs investor letter put it, disruptive periods breed new opportunities. BlackRockŌĆÖs challenge is to prove it can capitalize on ŌĆŗthem.
Follow Jonathan Guilford on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran's UN envoy says 1,332 Iranian civilians killed in war
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Iran's U.N. ambassador ŌĆŗAmir Saeid Iravani ŌĆīon Friday said at ŌĆŗleast 1,332 ŌĆŗIranian civilians had ŌüĀlost their ŌĆŗlives so ŌĆŗfar in the conflict with Israel ŌĆŗand the ŌĆŗUnited States and ŌĆīthousands ŌüĀmore had been injured.
He made his comments ŌĆŗwhile ŌĆŗspeaking ŌüĀto reporters at ŌĆŗthe United ŌĆŗNations ŌüĀin New York.

item.title: EU countries joining US, Israel attacks would become Iran's "legitimate target", Deputy Minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 6 (Reuters) - European Union member states that join the U.S. and ŌĆŗIsrael attacks on Iran would ŌĆībecome "legitimate targets" for Iran, Deputy Foreign Minister Majid Takht-Ravanchi said in an interview ŌĆŗwith TV station France 24.
"Any ŌĆŗcountry that joins in the ŌüĀaggression against Iran, joins America and ŌĆŗIsrael in the aggression against ŌĆŗIran, definitely, they will be also legitimate targets for Iran retaliation," Takht-Ravanchi said.

item.title: UK's Starmer discusses military, intelligence support with Saudi crown prince
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer spoke ŌĆŗto Saudi Arabia Crown ŌĆīPrince Mohammed bin Salman after Iran's strikes ŌĆŗon the kingdom ŌĆŗamid a sprawling conflict in the ŌüĀregion, Downing Street ŌĆŗsaid in a statement , opens new tab on ŌĆŗFriday.
"Additional British fighter jets, helicopters and a destroyer were ŌĆŗbeing sent to ŌĆŗthe region, and the UK ŌĆīstood ŌüĀready to support the defence of the Kingdom of Saudi ŌĆŗArabia should ŌĆŗit ŌüĀbe needed," Starmer told bin ŌĆŗSalman.
The two leaders ŌĆŗalso ŌüĀdiscussed greater intelligence cooperation in support of ŌüĀdefensive ŌĆŗoperations and civilians.

item.title: US, Ecuador bomb drug trafficker camp near Colombia border, militaries say
item.description:
WASHINGTON/QUITO, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Ecuador carried out a ŌĆŗjoint operation targeting drug trafficking operations in the South American ŌĆīcountry, authorities in both countries said on Friday, with the U.S. calling the move "lethal kinetic operations."
Neither the U.S. Southern Command, a branch of its military that oversees forces ŌĆŗin Latin America, nor Ecuador's defense ministry, said if anyone ŌĆŗwas killed or captured in the strike, which Ecuador dubbed ŌüĀoperation "Total Extermination."
The operations used helicopters, aircraft, river boats and drones to ŌĆŗlocate and bomb a drug traffickers' training camp in north-east Ecuador near ŌĆŗthe Colombian border, Ecuador's defense ministry said in a statement.
The camp belonged to the Comandos de la Frontera (CDF), a Colombian crime group made up of FARC dissidents, and ŌĆŗhad a capacity for 50 people, it added.
Ecuadorean President Daniel Noboa ŌĆŗhas made a military crackdown on organised crime a cornerstone of his administration, and ŌĆīhis ŌüĀgovernment imposed tariffs on its larger neighbor Colombia, accusing it of not doing enough to fight drug trafficking.
He is set to travel to Miami this weekend to take part in the Trump administration's "Shield of the Americas" ŌĆŗsummit, which brings ŌĆŗtogether many right-wing ŌüĀleaders across the region with a focus on regional security and organized crime .
"The United States is a key ally ŌĆŗin this fight," the defense ministry said.
"At the ŌĆŗrequest of ŌüĀEcuador, the Department of War executed targeted action to advance our shared objective of dismantling narco-terrorist networks," Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell wrote on X.
The ŌüĀoperation followed ŌĆŗa similar U.S-Ecuadorean operation announced by the ŌĆŗU.S. Southern Command earlier this week .

item.title: US skips congressional review to approve munitions sale to Israel

WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.S. State Department ŌĆŗsaid on Friday ŌĆīit has made a determination approving a ŌĆŗpossible foreign military ŌĆŗsale to Israel of ŌüĀmunitions and munitions ŌĆŗsupport for $151.8 million, without congressional ŌĆŗreview.

item.title: US judge dismisses case by deported student who declined flight back
item.description:
BOSTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A U.S. judge who had ordered President Donald Trump 's administration ŌĆŗto facilitate the return of a college student it deported to Honduras said on Friday the "sad truth" is ŌĆīthat her decision not to board a plane it arranged back to the United States meant her case must be dismissed.
Any Lucia Lopez Belloza, a 20-year-old freshman at Babson College in Massachusetts, declined on February 27 to board a flight arranged by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement to bring her back ŌĆŗto the United States after the administration said it would try to deport her again if she returned.
Lopez Belloza, ŌĆŗwho came to the United States from Honduras when she was 8, has said she was unaware ŌüĀshe was subject to a final order of removal that was entered when she was 11.
Her lawyers had urged Boston-based U.S. ŌĆŗDistrict Judge Richard Stearns to let Lopez Belloza continue the lawsuit she filed after being detained by immigration authorities at Boston's ŌĆŗLogan International Airport in November while traveling to spend the Thanksgiving holiday with her family in Texas.
But Stearns reaffirmed his earlier conclusion that he did not have jurisdiction to hear the case concerning her detention because by the time it was filed on November 21, she had been flown by ŌĆŗimmigration authorities to Texas.
His sole remaining basis for jurisdiction would have been to enforce an order issued by another judge just ŌĆŗminutes after Lopez Belloza's case was filed barring her from being deported or transferred out of Massachusetts for 72 hours. Lopez Belloza was flown ŌĆīfrom Texas ŌüĀto Honduras the next day despite that judicial order.
A U.S. government lawyer later apologized to Stearns for a "mistake" made by a ICE officer who failed to properly alert others in the agency about the existence of the judicial order.
Stearns on February 13 ordered the administration to rectify the error by facilitating her return to the United States. The administration last week said it would do so ŌĆŗby having Lopez Belloza board the ŌĆŗICE flight from Honduras to ŌüĀTexas.
But the administration also said ICE planned , opens new tab to move to deport her again upon arrival and had the authority to detain her. Lopez Belloza said the "nightmare" situation caused her to decline to board ŌĆŗthe flight and remain in Honduras.
"The sad truth is that when Any declined the flight she ŌĆŗalso waived this ŌüĀcourt's only remaining basis for jurisdiction," Stearns wrote.
Stearns said had she boarded the plane, the judicial order barring her swift deportation would have remained in effect, giving her "ample opportunity" to file a new case in Texas to challenge her detention.
Todd Pomerleau, Lopez Belloza's lawyer, said ŌüĀhe will ŌĆŗappeal.

item.title: Iran war enters second week as Trump demands 'unconditional surrender'
item.description:
BEIRUT/WASHINGTON/JERUSALEM, March 7 (Reuters) - The expanding war in Iran entered its second week on Saturday amid renewed uncertainty about how or when hostilities will end, as U.S. President Donald Trump declared he would only accept Tehran's "unconditional surrender" and Israel traded fresh attacks with Iran and Lebanon.
Trump's comments ŌĆŗon social media on Friday came hours after Iran's president announced that unnamed countries had begun mediation efforts, briefly raising the possibility, however faint, of a diplomatic resolution a week after ŌĆīthe U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran.
"There will be no deal with Iran except UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER!" Trump wrote. "After that, and the selection of a GREAT & ACCEPTABLE Leader(s), we, and many of our wonderful and very brave allies and partners, will work tirelessly to bring Iran back from the brink of destruction, making it economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before."
Trump has offered shifting explanations of his war aims, raising the possibility of an extended regional conflict that has already spilled well beyond Iran's borders, shaken global financial markets ŌĆŗand sent oil prices soaring.
In response to the attack, Iran has targeted Israel as well as multiple Gulf states that host U.S. military installations.
Inside Israel on Friday, explosions could be heard as Israeli defenses ŌĆŗactivated to shoot down incoming Iranian fire. The UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia all reported fresh drone and missile attacks.
Meanwhile, Israel pursued a major expansion of the ŌüĀwar in Lebanon, pounding the capital Beirut on Friday after ordering an unprecedented evacuation of the city's entire southern suburbs.
Israel also launched a new wave of attacks on Iran, saying 50 of its warplanes had hit a bunker ŌĆŗstill being used by Iran's leadership beneath slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's destroyed Tehran compound.
Early on Saturday, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that Mehrabad Airport in Tehran had been struck.
There was no immediate comment from Iran's Revolutionary ŌĆŗGuard or Hezbollah.
Israel has extended its bombing to Lebanon to root out Hezbollah , the Shi'ite militia allied to Iran that has been a dominant faction in Lebanese politics since the 1980s. Hezbollah fired on Israel this week to avenge the death of Khamenei.
"We're sleeping here in the streets - some in cars, some on the street, some on the beach," said Jamal Seifeddin, 43, who fled Beirut's southern suburbs and spent the night on the streets in the downtown district.
About 300,000 people have been displaced in Lebanon in the past four days, ŌĆŗaccording to the Norwegian Refugee Council.
The Israeli military says it has destroyed 80% of Iran's air-defense systems in the first week of the campaign and disabled more than 60% of its missile launchers.
Trump's demand for Iran's surrender, ŌĆŗand the likelihood that it would complicate any quick path to ending the conflict, sent European and U.S. stock indexes tumbling on Friday. Oil prices hit their highest prices in years with the critical shipping lane of the Strait of Hormuz effectively ŌĆīclosed down.
Trump ŌüĀtold Reuters in a telephone interview on Thursday that he must have a say in selecting Iran's new supreme leader to replace Khamenei, killed on the war's first day , a demand he repeated on Friday in a remarkable assertion of power over the country of more than 90 million people.
Iran's U.N. ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani, told reporters that new leadership would be chosen "in accordance with our constitutional procedures and solely by the will of the Iranian people - without any foreign interference."
Israel has said openly that it aims to overthrow Iran's ruling system. It has been bombing parts of western Iran to support Iranian Kurdish militias who hope to exploit the war to seize towns near the frontier, according to three sources ŌĆŗfamiliar with Israel's talks with the factions.
Iran has cast the ŌĆŗwar as an unprovoked attack and describes the ŌüĀkilling of Khamenei as an assassination.
Earlier on Friday, Iran President Masoud Pezeshkian posted on social media: "Some countries have begun mediation efforts." He did not identify the countries or provide further details.
Russia is providing Iran with locations of U.S. warships and aircraft in the Middle East after Iran's ability to locate U.S. forces was degraded, the Washington ŌĆŗPost reported, citing three officials familiar with the intelligence.
Russian missions in the U.S. did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the report.
Trump met with executives from ŌĆŗseven defense contractors on Friday, who ŌüĀhe later said had agreed to accelerate weapons production. The administration has been pressuring contractors as Iran and other recent operations have drawn down supplies.
Karoline Leavitt, a White House spokeswoman, said the U.S. has enough weapons stockpiles to meet the needs of its Iran operations, which she said would take about four to six weeks to complete.
At least 1,332 people have been killed in Iran since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on February 28, Iravani said, citing the Iranian Red Crescent ŌüĀSociety.
The Lebanese health ŌĆŗministry has reported 123 people killed and 683 wounded as a result of Israeli attacks. Iranian attacks have killed 11 people in ŌĆŗIsrael since the war started, and at least six U.S. service members have been killed.
Two U.S. officials told Reuters that military investigators believed it was likely that U.S. forces were responsible for an apparent strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children on the first day ŌĆŗof the war. The investigators have not yet reached a final conclusion.

item.title: Pakistani convicted of plotting to kill Trump over death of Iran commander
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A Pakistani man was convicted on Friday of planning to kill President Donald Trump and other prominent U.S. politicians two years ago at the behest ŌĆŗof Iran , the Department of Justice said.
Asif Merchant was accused of trying to recruit ŌĆīpeople in the U.S. in a plan targeting Trump and others in retaliation for Washington's killing of Iranian military commander Qassem Soleimani in 2020, when Trump was in his first term.
Targets in the ŌĆŗ2024 plot also included then-President Joe Biden and Nikki Haley, who ran ŌĆŗagainst Trump that year for the Republican presidential nomination, federal prosecutors said.
Merchant ŌüĀwas convicted of "murder for hire and attempting to commit an act of terrorism transcending ŌĆŗnational boundaries," directed by the Iranian authorities, the DOJ said , opens new tab in a statement.
The trial in ŌĆŗthe New York City borough of Brooklyn started last week, days before Trump ordered an assault on Iran, carried out with Israel, that has expanded into the region's biggest war in years.
Merchant admitted to ŌĆŗjoining the plot with Iran's elite Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps but testified he did ŌĆŗso unwillingly, to protect his family in Tehran.
Merchant said he was never ordered to kill a ŌĆīspecific person ŌüĀbut that his Iranian handler named three people in the course of conversations in the Iranian capital.
Law enforcement thwarted the plan before any attack occurred. A person Merchant contacted in April 2024 to help with the plot reported his activities and became a ŌĆŗconfidential informant, the DOJ ŌĆŗsaid. Merchant was arrested ŌüĀand pleaded not guilty that year.
The Revolutionary Guards have a central role in Iran, with a combination of military and economic power and ŌĆŗan intelligence network. Tehran has denied accusations that it targeted Trump ŌĆŗor other ŌüĀU.S. officials.
U.S. and Israeli attacks since Saturday have killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador. Many top Iranian leaders, including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali ŌüĀKhamenei, ŌĆŗhave been killed.
The U.S. military has said six of its service ŌĆŗmembers were killed in a strike on a facility in Kuwait, while Israeli tallies show at least 10 ŌĆŗcivilians have been killed across Israel.

item.title: Tornadoes kill four, injure more than a dozen in rural┬ĀMichigan
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - Tornadoes and strong storms killed at least four people and injured more than a dozen in southern Michigan on ŌĆŗFriday, destroying multiple homes and leaving hundreds of residents without power, ŌĆīlocal authorities said.
Soon after the National Weather Service issued tornado warnings for the area on Friday, videos on social media claiming to be from the towns of Three ŌĆŗRivers and Union City showed massive whirlwinds tearing roofs off buildings ŌĆŗand lifting debris into the air.
Local media footage showed smashed buildings ŌüĀand cars, and poles, trees and road signs that had been blown ŌĆŗover.
Three fatalities and a dozen injuries were reported in Branch County, where Union ŌĆŗCity is located, the county's sheriff's office said.
One person was killed and several injured in Cass County, according to local authorities there.
"Multiple large structures - including homes and pole barns - ŌĆŗsustained damage ranging from major structural impacts to complete destruction," according to ŌĆŗa statement on a website for Cass County.
Power outages had affected hundreds of people, local ŌĆīofficials ŌüĀsaid.
"We have multiple utility poles down, transformers damaged, and long stretches of power lines on the ground. The north side of Union Lake sustained some of the most severe damage, with roughly two miles of line brought ŌĆŗdown in that area ŌĆŗalone," according to ŌüĀa posting on Union City's official Facebook page that said restoration may take several days.
Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer said ŌĆŗshe activated a State Emergency Operations Center for monitoring ŌĆŗthe situation.
"The ŌüĀstate is ready to fulfill any resource requests that may arise. We've yet to receive any just yet. It's sounding like the local response is and ŌüĀhas ŌĆŗbeen able to address what's been a very ŌĆŗdevastating afternoon and evening in southwest Michigan," Clayton Cummins, a spokesperson for Michigan emergency services and ŌĆŗpolice, told Reuters.

item.title: Iran conflict boosts U.S. Gulf oil prices to highest since 2020
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. Gulf Coast heavy grades continued to surge on Friday as the Iran conflict spurred several Middle Eastern producers of heavy crude to curtail ŌĆŗproduction and drove buyers to scoop up U.S. barrels.
Prices of Mars ŌĆīsour crude, the flagship crude produced in the U.S. Gulf of Mexico and favored by refiners globally, traded at a $11 premium to U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude on Friday, ŌĆŗbrokers said. That was the highest since April 2020, and up $4 from ŌĆŗThursday.
Just a week ago it traded at a premium of $1.50.
Other heavy grades ŌüĀsuch as the Heavy Louisiana Sweet and the West Texas Sour also ŌĆŗrose.
Benchmark crude oil prices have surged since the initial attacks last week, with Brent ŌĆŗcrude settling at $92.69 a barrel, its highest level since October 2023 on Friday.
The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has forced several countries, including Iraq, to curb output. The strait is a ŌĆŗkey route for medium and heavy sour crude from the Persian Gulf, and ŌĆŗthose flows are now largely cut off. Additional production cuts announced in Kuwait on Friday ŌĆīalso ŌüĀhelped lift Mars prices, a trader said.
ŌĆ£Refiners that rely on these grades will need to find similar, or roughly similar, alternatives to replace the lost barrels, so Mars and other U.S. Gulf sour heavies and mediums are natural substitutes and are ŌĆŗgetting bid up ŌĆŗaggressively,ŌĆØ Kpler lead ŌüĀAmericas oil analyst Matt Smith said, adding that buyers, especially in Asia, are scrambling for more of these medium and ŌĆŗheavy crude barrels.
"This time of year also marks the shift ŌĆŗfrom winter ŌüĀinto driving season, when demand typically rises across all crude grades," said Tim Snyder, chief economist at Matador Economics, adding that ultimately the supply disruption caused by war ŌüĀwas ŌĆŗdriving prices.
"In the short term we will continue ŌĆŗto see these grades rise until we see the Strait of Hormuz open up," Snyder said.

item.title: US pump prices surge as Iran war upends global energy supply
item.description:
MARIETTA/NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. retail gasoline and diesel prices are soaring as the U.S.-Israel war with Iran constrains oil and fuel exports, which could be a political test for President Donald Trump's Republican Party ahead ŌĆŗof midterm elections in November.
Fuel prices jumped more than 10% this week as oil rose above $90 a barrel, its highest in years, adding pain at the ŌĆīpump for consumers already strained by inflation. Trump on Thursday shrugged off higher gasoline prices in an interview with Reuters , opens new tab , saying "if they rise, they rise."
The president had vowed to lower energy prices and unleash U.S. oil and gas drilling during his second term, but much of his tenure has been marked by volatility and uncertainty amid shifts in policies like tariffs and geopolitical turmoil. The U.S. is the world's largest oil producer. It is a major exporter ŌĆŗbut also imports millions of barrels a day since it is the world's largest oil consumer.
As of Friday, the national average prices for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a ŌĆŗgallon, up 11% from a week ago and the highest since September 2024, according to data from the motorists association AAA. Diesel was at $4.33, ŌüĀup 15% from a week ago, surging to the highest since November 2023.
U.S. motorists in parts of the Midwest and the South, including states that supported Trump, have ŌĆŗseen some of the steepest increases in fuel costs since the conflict in Iran started.
In Georgia, a swing state, average retail gasoline prices rose 40.1 cents a gallon over the past week, ŌĆŗaccording to fuel tracking site GasBuddy.
Andrenna McDaniel, a healthcare insurance worker in South Fulton, Georgia, said she was surprised to see prices skyrocket overnight.
"They jumped up so quickly," she said on Friday, adding that she does not agree with the war at all.
McDaniel, a Democrat, said that for now she is only driving for the most important things, and feels lucky that she works from home so she does not have to drive as ŌĆŗmuch as other people do.
Georgia voted for Donald Trump in the 2024 election.
Trump voter Richard Soule, 69, a U.S. Air Force veteran and a retired firefighter, said a little pain at the pump ŌĆŗis worth Trump's efforts to protect America.
"When President Trump went in there and bombed out their nuclear, and they just thumbed their nose at it, I believe he did the right thing at the right ŌĆītime," Soule said ŌüĀon Friday as he filled up his Ford F-150 truck in Marietta, Georgia.
Other states, including Indiana and West Virginia have seen prices rise by 44.3 cents and 43.9 cents, respectively.
More pain may be on the way, analysts said, as oil prices continue to trend upward. On Friday, U.S. oil futures settled at $90.90 a barrel, up nearly $10 and the biggest single-day rise since April 2020.
"Given current market conditions, the national average price of gasoline could climb toward $3.50 to $3.70 per gallon in the coming days if oil continues rising and supply disruptions persist," GasBuddy analyst Patrick De ŌĆŗHaan said.
The disruptions in the Middle East and ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz, a key trade ŌüĀconduit, have boosted demand for U.S. oil abroad, which in turn has driven up prices for domestic refiners too.
"The U.S. has weaned itself off of its dependence on Middle Eastern crude, but obviously Asian refineries, and to a lesser extent, European refineries have not," Denton Cinquegrana, chief oil ŌĆŗanalyst with OPIS. "That's what you're seeing happen in the spot market, because the demand for U.S. exports rise, and so the price rise."
Seasonal ŌĆŗfactors could add further ŌüĀpressure. Gasoline prices typically go up in the spring and peak in the summer due to higher gasoline demand and production of summer-blend gasoline, which is more costly to produce.
Diesel fuel saw an even more aggressive jump since Iran began retaliating against U.S. and Israeli strikes, significantly disrupting shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Global diesel inventories have remained in tight supply due to heavy demand for heating and power generation ŌüĀduring a ŌĆŗprolonged winter in the U.S. and other parts of the world and a structural tightness of refining capacity.
Sticker prices ŌĆŗof everything from food to furniture go up when the cost of diesel goes up, as the fuel is mainly used in freight transportation, manufacturing, agriculture, and global shipping, analysts said.
"In a world where buzzword seems to be 'affordability', that is ŌĆŗcertainly not going to help," Cinquegrana said.

item.title: World's largest urban coffee plantation welcomes new trees in Brazil
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 6 (Reuters) - The world's largest urban coffee ŌĆŗplantation, located in Brazil's sprawling city of Sao Paulo, this week welcomed some ŌĆī1,500 new coffee plants as researchers prepare to study their capacity to resist climate change and pests.
Sao Paulo's Biological Institute was established in 1927 with the mission of tackling a crisis caused by pests ŌĆŗlike the coffee berry-borer beetle which devour the beans hidden inside coffee cherries.
Brazil ŌĆŗis the world's top producer of arabica coffee, as well as the ŌüĀsecond-biggest producer of canephora coffees, which include varieties like robusta and conilon.
The plantation in ŌĆŗSao Paulo's Vila Mariana neighborhood, which already boasted more than 2,000 coffee plants, welcomed arabica ŌĆŗvarieties this week billed as resistant to pests and coffee rust, a type of fungus, as well as other plants that are more tolerant of drought-like conditions.
"The Biological Institute was created to control the ŌĆŗcoffee berry-borer (which) was controlled using parasitoids, a biological control method," institute researcher and agricultural engineer ŌĆŗHarumi Hojo told Reuters on Thursday.
In her hand, Hojo held two coffee cherries, showing the difference between ŌĆīa ŌüĀhealthy fruit with smooth white coffee beans and another rotten on the inside after being devoured by a beetle.
Over the years, the institute began to investigate other factors affecting coffee plants, like soil and climate, and now its range of varieties grown side by side ŌĆŗunder the same conditions ŌĆŗshows how different ŌüĀplants handle pests, disease and climate pressures, Hojo said.
Arabica coffee plants are also sensitive to hotter and drier weather caused by climate ŌĆŗchange, and 300 of the new plants at the institute's plantation ŌĆŗare tolerant to ŌüĀwater deficits.
Now, research has produced coffee varieties that are resistant to droughts, Hojo said, adding that it would be valuable in the future to have coffee plants that can hold out ŌüĀfor ŌĆŗirrigation with captured rain, rather than groundwater sources that ŌĆŗmay be scarce.
"We know that climate change and water availability are going to be problems for our future," Hojo ŌĆŗsaid.

item.title: One week into Iran war, the dangers for the US and Trump multiply
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 7 (Reuters) - One week into the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran that has plunged the Middle East into turmoil, President Donald Trump faces a growing list of risks and challenges that raise questions about whether he will be able to translate military successes into a clear geopolitical win.
Even after the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and devastating blows against Iranian forces on land, at sea and in the air, the crisis has ŌĆŗquickly widened into a regional conflict that threatens a more prolonged U.S. military engagement with fallout beyond TrumpŌĆÖs control.
That is a scenario that Trump had avoided in his two terms in the White House, preferring swift, limited operations like the January 3 lightning raid in Venezuela ŌĆīand JuneŌĆÖs one-off strike on IranŌĆÖs nuclear sites.
ŌĆ£Iran is a messy and potentially protracted military campaign,ŌĆØ said Laura Blumenfeld of the Johns Hopkins School for Advanced International Studies in Washington. ŌĆ£Trump is risking the global economy, regional stability and his own Republican Party's performance in the U.S. midterm elections.ŌĆØ
Trump, who came to office promising to keep the U.S. out of "stupidŌĆØ military interventions, is now pursuing what many experts see as an open-ended war of choice unprompted by any imminent threat to the U.S. from Iran, despite claims to the contrary by the president and his aides.
In doing so, analysts say he has struggled to articulate a detailed set of objectives or a clear endgame for Operation Epic Fury, the biggest U.S. military operation since the 2003 Iraq invasion, offering shifting rationales ŌĆŗfor the war and definitions of what would constitute victory.
White House spokesperson Anna Kelly rejected that assessment, saying Trump has clearly outlined his goals to "destroy IranŌĆÖs ballistic missiles and production capacity, demolish their navy, end their ability to arm proxies, and prevent them from ever obtaining a nuclear weapon.ŌĆØ
However, if the ŌĆŗwar drags on, American casualties mount and the economic costs of interrupted Gulf oil flows multiply, TrumpŌĆÖs biggest foreign policy gamble could also hurt his Republican Party politically.
Despite criticism from some Trump supporters opposed to military ŌüĀinterventions, members of his Make America Great Again movement have largely backed him on Iran so far.
But any softening of their support could imperil RepublicansŌĆÖ control of Congress in the November midterm elections, given opinion polls showing opposition to the war among the broader electorate, including a crucial bloc of independent voters.
ŌĆ£The American people are ŌĆŗnot interested in repeating the mistakes of Iraq and Afghanistan,ŌĆØ said Brian Darling, a Republican strategist. ŌĆ£The MAGA base is split between those who relied on no-new-war promises and ones who are loyal to TrumpŌĆÖs judgment.ŌĆØ
High on the list of analystsŌĆÖ concerns is the mixed messaging from Trump and his aides on whether he is seeking ŌĆ£regime changeŌĆØ in ŌĆŗTehran.
At the outset of the conflict, he suggested that overthrowing IranŌĆÖs rulers was a goal, at least by fomenting internal rebellion. Two days later, he stopped short of mentioning that as a priority.
But then on Thursday, Trump told Reuters he would play a role in picking IranŌĆÖs next leader and encouraged Iranian Kurdish rebels to launch attacks. That was followed by his demand in a social media post on Friday for IranŌĆÖs "unconditional surrender."
Across the region, the dangers have escalated with Iran's retaliatory strikes on Israel and other neighbors as it seeks to sow chaos and raise the costs for Israel, the U.S. and its allies.
Showing that Iran may still be able to activate proxy groups, LebanonŌĆÖs Hezbollah militia has renewed hostilities with Israel, expanding the war to another ŌĆŗcountry.
American casualties have been low so far, with six service members killed, and Trump has largely shrugged off the prospects for more to come while declining to completely rule out deployment of U.S. ground troops.
Asked whether Americans should worry about Iran-inspired attacks at home, Trump said in a Time magazine interview published on Friday: ŌĆ£I guess ŌĆ” ŌĆŗLike I said, some people will die.ŌĆØ
But Jonathan Panikoff, a former deputy U.S. national intelligence officer for the Middle East, said: ŌĆ£Nothing is likely to hasten an early end to the war more than American casualties ŌĆ” ThatŌĆÖs what Iran is counting on.ŌĆØ
VENEZUELA MISCALCULATION?
Many analysts believe Trump, who has shown an increasing appetite for military action in his second term, miscalculated that the ŌĆīIran campaign would unfold ŌüĀlike the Venezuela operation earlier this year.
U.S. special forces captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, opening the way for Trump to coerce more compliant former loyalists into giving him considerable sway over the countryŌĆÖs vast oil reserves ŌĆō without any extended U.S. military action needed.
By contrast, Iran has proved a much tougher, better-armed foe with an entrenched clerical and security establishment.
Even the joint U.S.-Israeli "decapitation" strike that killed Khamenei and some other senior leaders has failed so far to prevent Iran from mounting a military response and has raised questions whether they could be replaced by even more hardline figures.
Looming over the conflict, however, is whether Iran could slide into chaos and break apart if its current rulers fall, further destabilizing the Middle East.
Mark Dubowitz, CEO of the Foundation for the Defense of Democracies, a nonprofit research institute considered hawkish on Iran, praised TrumpŌĆÖs overall war strategy but said the president needs to make clear publicly that he does not want to see the country disintegrate.
For now, however, one of the most pressing ŌĆŗconcerns is IranŌĆÖs threat to the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow chokepoint through ŌĆŗwhich a fifth of the world's oil passes. Tanker traffic has halted, ŌüĀwhich could have grave economic consequences if it lasts.
Though Trump has publicly dismissed any concern about already-rising U.S. gas prices, he and his aides have scrambled for ways to mitigate the warŌĆÖs impact on energy supplies as voters tell pollsters that the cost of living is their top concern.
ŌĆ£It's an economic pain point on the U.S. economy that it seems was not fully anticipated," said Josh Lipsky at the Atlantic Council think tank in Washington.
One former U.S. military official close to the U.S. administration said ŌĆŗthe widening of the war's economic impact had caught TrumpŌĆÖs team by surprise in part because those with knowledge of oil markets were not consulted ahead of the attack on Iran.
The White House's Kelly said, ŌĆ£The Iranian regime is being ŌĆŗabsolutely crushed" but did not specifically address concerns ŌüĀabout preparations for a war.
Trump made his decision to press ahead with the strikes
despite warnings from some senior aides that the escalation could be difficult to contain, according to two White House officials and a Republican close to the administration.
Some traditional U.S. allies were caught off guard. "It's a decision-making circle of one," said one Western diplomat.
The warŌĆÖs duration is a major unknown likely to determine the extent of its repercussions. With the price tag of the Iran campaign mounting by the day, Trump has said that the operation could last four or five weeks or ŌĆ£whatever it takesŌĆØ but has offered little explanation of what he envisions will follow.
Retired U.S. Army Lieutenant General Ben Hodges, who served in Iraq and Afghanistan ŌüĀand formerly commanded the ŌĆŗU.S. Army in Europe, commended the U.S. military for its tactics in Iran. But he told Reuters: "From a political, strategic and diplomatic standpoint, it seems not to have been thought all the way ŌĆŗthrough.ŌĆØ
Trump also has a lot riding on helping oil-producing Gulf Arab states weather the Iran crisis given they have long hosted U.S. bases and have made pledges of massive new U.S. investments to him.
While Gulf allies appear to have fallen in line to support the campaign, especially after Tehran targeted them with missile and drone strikes, not everyone in the region is onboard with TrumpŌĆÖs war.
In an open ŌĆŗletter to Trump published on Thursday, UAE billionaire Khalaf Al Habtor, a frequent visitor to Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida, asked: "Who gave you the right to turn our region into a battlefield?"

item.title: As war spreads, airline pilots contend with drones, missiles - and stress
item.description:
LONDON/COPENHAGEN/BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - Airline pilots have faced escalating risks in recent years, from drone incursions to flight paths squeezed by conflict. Now U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran are making the skies even more perilous and ratcheting up the pressure on those flying through them.
The outbreak of war in the Middle East has put hundreds of ballistic ŌĆŗmissiles and attack drones into the skies above some of the world's busiest airports. Tehran's retaliation against the U.S. and its allies has included hitting airports, grounding scores of flights from Dubai to Abu Dhabi. A trickle ŌĆīof rescue flights has made it through for thousands of stranded passengers.
Reuters spoke to eight pilots and more than a half-dozen aviation and security insiders who said the accumulation of conflicts - from Ukraine to Afghanistan and Israel - has increased the burden on pilots, forcing them to manage shrinking airspace and the wider use of military drones far from active war zones. That's increased the strain on the mental health of pilots desperate to keep themselves and their passengers safe.
"We are not military pilots. We are not trained to deal with these kinds of threats in the air," Tanja Harter, a pilot with Middle ŌĆŗEast experience and president of the European Cockpit Association, told Reuters.
The current crisis is the latest in a string of security threats the sector has faced over the years, she said, which could cause "fear and anxiety" for pilots. Airlines ŌĆŗnow often have peer programs to help, she said, adding as a pilot she would not want to "share airspace with missiles."
Airspace safety has worsened in the last two-and-a-half years as conflicts have grown, ŌüĀindustry experts said, through a combination of GPS spoofing - maliciously tricking planes about their position - and increased numbers of missiles and drones .
An Air France (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab flight to bring stranded French nationals home from the United Arab Emirates turned back due to missile fire on Thursday. A ŌĆŗLufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab pilot on Friday diverted from Riyadh to Cairo over regional security fears.
Middle East-trained pilots have become wearily used to emergencies, the head of Lebanon's civil aviation body said. The escalating conflict put those skills to the test quickly. Video footage ŌĆŗon March 5 showed planes taking off from Beirut airport as smoke billowed over buildings in the Lebanese capital.
"Middle East pilots have always faced crises, so from the start we trained how to deal with contingencies, emergencies and everything else," said Captain Mohammed Aziz, director general of Lebanon's civil aviation authority.
"No one can give you a guarantee that they won't bomb the airport or will bomb the airport."
One Middle East Airlines' pilot with a decade of experience said routes to Beirut have become more complex. In the past, shoulder-fired anti-aircraft missiles in Lebanon usually had a range of 15,000 feet, so ŌĆŗpilots would increase altitude to stay out of range, he said, while planes often carried extra fuel in case they were forced to divert.
Still, most missile strikes are far enough away not to be a risk and pilots are often too busy to ŌĆŗworry about them.
"You're actually busy enough on the plane trying to make sure you have clearance to land, that everything is in order, so you don't have time to process your emotions over what's happening outside the plane," he said.
The risks are not ŌĆīconfined to the ŌüĀMiddle East. Since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, drones have become a key weapon on both sides. Airports in European cities from Stockholm to Munich have faced drone disruptions - suspected though not confirmed to be linked to the conflict.
Airline Captain Christian von D'Ahe, a commercial aircraft pilot for 15 years and head of the Danish Air Line Pilots Association, is alarmed by the emerging threat.
"Drones are not easily detected," Von D'Ahe said. "We can see them in the air, and they're very small. So sooner or later, something will happen."
Drones striking an aircraft's engines could cause total power loss, while damage to wings could compromise a jet's ability to manoeuvre.
Most registered planes emit a signal via a transponder, a device that identifies aircraft to radar, but drones do not, leaving pilots in the dark. Regular radars used ŌĆŗby airports struggle to catch drones. Specialised radar exists, but is ŌĆŗtypically operated by law enforcement or the military.
Dedrone, a ŌüĀcompany that produces counterdrone technology, said there were over 1.2 million drone violations in the U.S. in 2025, with more expected in coming years.
Airports can use radar, frequency sensors and jamming tools to counter drones, while some systems can "spoof" them off course. But safety concerns mean airports cannot shoot drones down.
Tim Friebe, an air traffic controller in Germany and a ŌĆŗvice president at the Air Traffic Controllers European Unions Coordination (ATCEUC), said drones were a "threat that is growing," while airports often had limited options.
"For now we have reports, pilot reports, or ŌĆŗsometimes controllers spot drones. The problem ŌüĀis there's not much you can do except shutting down the airport," he said.
Drones shut down , opens new tab some of the world's biggest airports from Munich to London's Gatwick last year, driving operators to beef up their foreign object and drone detection systems, according to half a dozen industry officials.
Moritz Burger, a commercial pilot based in Germany, recalled spotting an object that looked like a balloon with a structure underneath as he was about to land at a European airport.
"I was looking out of the window and suddenly there appeared an object that passed by just ŌüĀbelow our aircraft. ŌĆŗWe could see it for maybe one, maximum two seconds," he said, adding it startled him and left him no time to take any evasive ŌĆŗaction.
"When you encounter such a near-miss or some passing object, there is not enough time to react. So it is unrealistic to expect that pilots could fly around such an object. There's pretty much nothing we can do."

item.title: Russia hits Ukraine with drones, missiles, kills at least seven in Kharkiv
item.description:
KYIV, March 7 (Reuters) - Russia launched a barrage of drones and missiles ŌĆŗat Ukraine overnight on Saturday, killing at least ŌĆīseven people, including two children, in the northeast city of Kharkiv, Ukrainian officials said.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said that ŌĆŗRussia launched 480 drones and 29 missiles ŌĆŗtargeting the energy sector and railway infrastructure across ŌüĀthe country.
"There should be a response from partners ŌĆŗto these savage strikes against life," Zelenskiy said on ŌĆŗthe Telegram app.
"Russia has not abandoned its attempts to destroy Ukraine's residential and critical infrastructure, and therefore support should continue," ŌĆŗZelenskiy said, urging partners to continue air defence ŌĆŗand weapons supplies.
Oleh Syniehubov, Kharkiv regional governor, said that seven ŌĆīpeople, ŌüĀincluding two children, were killed after a Russian ballistic missile slammed into a five-storey residential building.
Rescue workers continue to work on the site, clearing the rubble, ŌĆŗhe said.
In ŌĆŗKharkiv, seven ŌüĀresidential apartment houses, commercial and administrative buildings, electricity distribution lines, and cars were ŌĆŗdamaged by the Russian attacks, Syniehubov said.
Ukrainian ŌĆŗofficials ŌüĀsaid that Russia attacked four railway stations and other railway infrastructure in central Ukraine and port infrastructure in ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗsouthern Odesa region, setting on ŌĆŗfire containers with vegetable oil and damaging a grain warehouse.

item.title: Refugees, migrants in Lebanon find rare sanctuary from Israeli strikes in Beirut church
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - When Israeli strikes began pummelling BeirutŌĆÖs southern suburbs early on Monday, Sudanese refugee ŌĆŗRidina Muhammad and her family had no choice but to flee home on foot, eventually reaching the only shelter ŌĆīthat would accept them: a church.
Eight months pregnant, Muhammad, 32, walked with her husband and three children for hours in the dark streets until they found a car to take them to the St. Joseph Tabaris Parish, which has opened its doors to refugees and migrants.
They are among 300,000 people displaced across Lebanon this week by heavy ŌĆŗIsraeli strikes, launched in response to a rocket and drone attack into Israel by the Lebanese armed group Hezbollah.
Just 100,000 of the ŌĆŗdisplaced are in government shelters. Others are staying with relatives or sleeping in the streets. But migrants and ŌüĀrefugees say government shelters were never an option for them, saying they were turned away during the last war between Hezbollah and Israel.
MuhammadŌĆÖs oldest ŌĆŗdaughter, now seven, stopped speaking after the 2024 war.
This time, they are even more vulnerable: their home was destroyed in this weekŌĆÖs strikes and ŌĆŗMuhammad is due to give birth at the end of the month.
ŌĆ£I don't know if there's a doctor or not, but I'm really scared about it because I haven't prepared any clothes for the baby, nor arranged a hospital, and I don't know where to go,ŌĆØ she told Reuters as her younger daughter leaned ŌĆŗagainst her pregnant belly.
Muhammad said she was registered with the United NationsŌĆÖ refugee agency (UNHCR) but had not received support.
ŌĆ£Us, as refugees, ŌĆŗwhy did we register with the U.N., if they are not helping us in the most difficult times?ŌĆØ she said.
Dalal Harb, a spokesperson for UNHCR Lebanon, ŌĆīsaid the ŌüĀagency had mobilized but reaching everyone immediately was extremely challenging given the scale and speed of displacement. The UNHCR operation in Lebanon is currently only around 14% funded, she said.
The Jesuit Refugee Service (JRS), which helped the church host displaced in 2024, is doing so again.
Michael Petro, JRSŌĆÖ Emergency Shelter Director, said the church was full within the first day of strikes, with 140 people from South Sudan, Ethiopia, Bangladesh, and other countries sheltering ŌĆŗthere.
ŌĆ£There are many, many more people ŌĆŗcoming than there were in 2024, ŌüĀand we have fewer and fewer places to put them,ŌĆØ he said.
Petro said he was told weeks ago that government shelters would be open to migrants if war erupted.
But when the strikes began and even ŌĆŗLebanese struggled to find shelter, the policy seemed to change, he said.
ŌĆ£We're hearing from hotlines up to ŌĆŗgovernment officials and ŌüĀministries that migrants are not welcome,ŌĆØ Petro said.
LebanonŌĆÖs Minister for Social Affairs Haneen Sayyed did not respond to a request for comment. On Thursday, Sayyed said Beirut shelters were full.
When Israeli strikes began, Othman Yahyeh Dawood, a 41-year-old Sudanese man, put his two young sons on his motorcycle.
They drove 75 ŌüĀkilometres (46 miles) ŌĆŗfrom the southern Lebanese town of Nabatieh to St. JosephŌĆÖs, where they had sheltered ŌĆŗin 2024.
ŌĆ£I know the area is safe and there are people who will welcome us,ŌĆØ he said.
ŌĆ£We don't know where to go; there's war there (in the south), war here (in ŌĆŗBeirut), war in Sudan, and nowhere else to go,ŌĆØ he said.

item.title: In Perpignan, French far right tests tough-on-crime blueprint
item.description:
PERPIGNAN, France, March 7 (Reuters) - In the streets of Perpignan, a faded Mediterranean city near France's border with Spain, the incumbent far-right mayor Louis Aliot is pitching a simple message as he asks voters for another term: more police, more cameras and more order.
The National Rally (RN) mayor ŌĆŗhas made security the centrepiece of his administration, and his party is holding up this city of 122,000, the biggest it controls, as a blueprint for governance that it hopes to ŌĆīreplicate elsewhere ahead of nationwide municipal elections this month.
Aliot, who leads in polls despite an embezzlement conviction that could bar him from office if his appeal fails, said Perpignan has been a laboratory for RN governance since he won city hall in 2020 and a showcase for its national ambitions when the French vote for a new president in 2027.
"When the National Rally is in office, well, we govern, we run cities and we run them well," he said in an interview.
Wins in other cities would give Marine ŌĆŗLe Pen's party a springboard going into next year's presidential election. Polls show the RN performing well in Toulon, Nice and Marseille, although a two-round system makes final outcomes hard to forecast.
The ŌĆŗRN's law-and-order message resonates with many voters in Perpignan, where just streets from the Catalan-influenced, historic centre lie run-down neighbourhoods that are among France's poorest.
Two weeks before the ŌüĀMarch 15 and 23 elections, RN leader Jordan Bardella and hard-left rival Jean-Luc M├®lenchon - both presidential hopefuls - held duelling weekend rallies, turning the city into a symbolic battleground.
"Faced with the violence engulfing our country year after year, we ŌĆŗintend to make public order an absolute priority," Bardella told supporters.
Aliot promised 50 more police officers and 200 surveillance cameras, particularly in outer suburbs to which the middle class has fled from poverty-stricken inner neighbourhoods that are home ŌĆŗto the city's sizeable North African and Roma populations.
The city has already expanded its municipal force to 199 officers from 161 in 2020, and now deploys 1.6 municipal police per 1,000 residents - proportionally the highest among French cities of more than 100,000 and nearly three times Paris's 0.6, according to Reuters' analysis of Interior Ministry data.
More aggressive policing has seen drug-trafficking cases climb sharply, with Perpignan now reporting the seventh most among over 50 large French cities, up from 18th in 2020. Overall trafficking cases ŌĆŗopened by police have more than doubled, while fixed-penalty fines for minor possession have quadrupled.
The security focus comes at a cost. Municipal debt stands at 1,600 euros per inhabitant, well above the 1,200 average in similar-sized ŌĆŗcities, according to Finance Ministry data.
Property tax rates are higher than in 71% of comparable cities, and 98% of peers have lower local business taxes.
Even so, business weekly Challenges ranked Perpignan the third best-run city in France last week, a ŌĆītalking point for ŌüĀAliot's allies as they try to convert security-minded voters beyond the RN's base.
Aliot leads with 44% in the first round according to an early December Ifop poll, benefiting from a fractured opposition split among five rivals.
A far-right political veteran, Aliot has been central to Le Pen's campaign to soften the image of the anti-immigration party and make it more palatable to the mainstream voter. Opinion polls project either Le Pen or Bardella, her prot├®g├®, winning the first round of next year's presidential vote and facing a tight run-off contest.
On the ground, reactions were mixed. At a city-centre market, retiree Marie Nivet said she welcomed cleaner streets and visible policing, though she wanted more action on ŌĆŗdrugs.
She was undecided on whether to support Aliot because ŌĆŗof the looming appeal court verdict over misuse ŌüĀof EU funds that also threatens Le Pen's political survival .
Aliot said he would appeal any unfavourable outcome to France's highest court, but that others in his team could take his place if needed.
The legal uncertainty didn't bother Laure Gu├®rin, a retired private-school administrator, who said she would back Aliot because of the RN's law-and-order stance.
Others were more ŌĆŗwary. Christian Pyguillem, a retired property manager, credited Aliot with cleaner streets and better security, but said entrenched poverty in some neighbourhoods requires deeper urban renewal ŌĆŗthan any mayor has attempted.
"We'll ŌüĀgive him another six years, but it's a vote motivated by the local situation, I don't agree with their other ideas," he said.
Rivals said the security push has failed to deliver and that city finances have deteriorated. Centre-right challenger Bruno Nougayr├©de accused Aliot of neglecting local duties for national politics and putting publicity before results.
"These five years have been marked by complete inaction for the people of Perpignan," Nougayr├©de told Reuters. "A lot of resources went into PR, but the ŌüĀcity itself ŌĆŗhas not changed."
Hard-left candidate Micka├½l Idrac called Aliot's record "catastrophic," blaming "frenzied" investment in arming police and surveillance cameras. He also criticised what he ŌĆŗdescribed as divisive rhetoric.
Aliot countered that voters would judge by how they feel in their streets. He casts the RN as the party willing to enforce order after decades of drift by rivals on left and right.
"If the left had defended the poor, we wouldn't be ŌĆŗhere today. If the right had defended order, we wouldn't be here today," Aliot said.

item.title: India let Iran warship dock the day US sank another off Sri Lanka, officials say
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 6 (Reuters) - India has allowed an Iranian warship to dock as a humanitarian gesture, Foreign Minister Subrahmanyam Jaishankar said on Saturday, after the U.S. ŌĆŗsank another Iranian navy vessel off neighbouring Sri Lanka.
The Lavan docked ŌĆīat India's southern port of Kochi on Wednesday, the same day the U.S. submarine struck Iranian navy frigate Dena, after an urgent request from Tehran, an Indian government source told ŌĆŗReuters.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said destroying the Iranian navy is one ŌĆŗaim of the war he and Israel launched against the Islamic ŌüĀRepublic a week ago.
The Lavan - an amphibious landing vessel, according to the U.S. ŌĆŗNaval Institute's online news site - and two other ships "were coming in for a ŌĆŗfleet review and then they got, in a way, caught on the wrong side of the events," Jaishankar told the annual Raisina Dialogue event.
"I think we really approached it from ŌĆŗthe point of view of humanity, of other than whatever the legal ŌĆŗissues were," he said. "I think we did the right thing."
At least 87 people were killed in ŌĆīthe U.S. ŌüĀattack on the Dena in Sri Lanka's exclusive economic zone 19 nautical miles off the coast, outside its maritime boundaries.
India received the docking request for the Lavan on February 28, the day the Iran war started, the source ŌĆŗsaid late on Friday, ŌĆŗadding that the ŌüĀrequest "was urgent as the vessel had developed technical issues".
Its 183 crew members have been accommodated at naval facilities in ŌĆŗKochi, said the source, who asked not to be identified ŌĆŗciting confidentiality.
The ŌüĀDena was on its way back from a naval exercise organised by India, according to the drill's website and Sri Lankan officials.
Sri Lankan authorities said on Friday ŌüĀthat ŌĆŗthey were escorting the Iranian naval ship Booshehr ŌĆŗto a harbour on the eastern coast and moving most of its crew to a navy camp ŌĆŗnear Colombo.

item.title: Iran to suspend strikes on neighbours unless attacks come from them
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian ŌĆŗsaid on ŌĆīSaturday that its temporary leadership ŌĆŗcouncil had ŌĆŗapproved the suspension ŌüĀof attacks ŌĆŗagainst neighboring countries ŌĆŗunless an attack on Iran came ŌĆŗfrom those ŌĆŗcountries.
The announcement came as ŌĆīIran ŌüĀcontinued to launch attacks in the ŌĆŗregion ŌĆŗin ŌüĀresponse to U.S.-Israeli strikes ŌĆŗon the ŌĆŗIslamic ŌüĀRepublic.

item.title: Azerbaijan says it foils Iranian plots including plan to attack major pipeline
item.description:
BAKU, March 7 (Reuters) - Azerbaijan said it had prevented several acts of "terrorist" sabotage planned by Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), including ŌĆŗa plot to attack a major oil pipeline running ŌĆīthrough the South Caucasus to Turkey.
The targets included the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan (BTC) oil pipeline, the Israeli embassy in Azerbaijan, an Ashkenazi synagogue, and a leader ŌĆŗan ancient Jewish community in Azerbaijan called the Mountain ŌĆŗJews, according to a State Security Service statement cited ŌüĀby the Azertag state news agency late on Friday.
Iran ŌĆŗhas not commented on the statement.
The BTC pipeline travels via Georgia ŌĆŗand Turkey and sends oil to Europe, and also accounts for roughly a third of Israeli oil imports. Any damage to its infrastructure ŌĆŗcould drive global energy prices even higher as the war in ŌĆŗthe Middle East enters its second week.
The Azerbaijani statement came just a ŌĆīday ŌüĀafter Baku vowed to retaliate for what it said was an incursion of four Iranian drones into its Nakhchivan exclave, which injured four people and damaged airport infrastructure. Iran flatly denied it ŌĆŗsent the drones ŌĆŗinto Azerbaijan.
Baku said ŌüĀan investigation found two Iranian citizens and an Azerbaijani national had colluded to bring over ŌĆŗseven kilograms of the C-4 explosive into Azerbaijan ŌĆŗon ŌüĀthe instructions of the IRGC. Authorities have issued international arrest warrants for four people.
On Friday Azerbaijan ordered the evacuation of its ŌüĀdiplomats ŌĆŗfrom Iran, citing safety concerns, amid ŌĆŗalready tense relations between the two countries over Baku's ties to Israel and ŌĆŗTurkey.

item.title: Putin calls for immediate halt to Iran conflict
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 7 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin expressed his condolences to Iran's president over the ŌĆŗnumerous civilian casualties resulting from "the armed Israeli-American ŌĆīaggression against Iran" and called for an immediate halt to hostilities, the Kremlin said.
In a phone ŌĆŗcall late on Friday with Iranian President ŌĆŗMasoud Pezeshkian, Putin expressed his deep condolences ŌüĀover the killing of Iranian Supreme ŌĆŗLeader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, members of Khamenei's family, ŌĆŗIranian political and military leaders and "numerous civilians".
"Vladimir Putin reaffirmed Russia's principled stance in favour of an immediate ŌĆŗcessation of hostilities, the rejection of force ŌĆŗas a method to solve any issues surrounding Iran or ŌĆīarising ŌüĀin the Middle East, and a swift return to the path of diplomatic resolution," the Kremlin said.
Putin said he was in ŌĆŗconstant contact with ŌĆŗthe leaders ŌüĀof Gulf Cooperation Council member states.
"Masoud Pezeshkian expressed gratitude for Russia's ŌĆŗsolidarity with the Iranian people as ŌĆŗthey ŌüĀdefend their sovereignty and the independence of their country. He also provided a detailed update ŌüĀon ŌĆŗthe developments during the ŌĆŗlatest active phase of the conflict," the Kremlin said.

item.title: Inside the Nepal campaign that could vault Balendra Shah to power
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 7 (Reuters) - The campaign that swept aside Nepal's established political heavyweights and brought 35-year-old rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah to the cusp of power emerged out of the top floors of a six-story building in west Kathmandu.
Shah is the candidate for prime minister from the ŌĆŗRastriya Swatantra Party, which is leading the vote count two days after Nepal's first election since youth-led protests in September forced the previous elected government out of power.
The final results ŌĆīfor 165 seats in direct, first-past-the-post tallies and 110 seats through proportional representation are expected by next week, according to the election commission.
And if current trends hold, Shah - or Balen, as he is better known - will have successfully harnessed a wave of support unleashed by the September uprising with one of the most sophisticated campaigns ever seen in the small Himalayan nation.
Reuters interviewed six RSP officials to gain insight into Shah's campaign, much of it coordinated out of the ŌĆŗtop three floors of the party's headquarters in the capital city's Balaju neighbourhood and heavily bankrolled by the Nepali diaspora.
"We are overwhelmed by the support and love we received from ŌĆŗpeople on the ground," said Bigyan Gautam, a member of RSP's national campaign team.
Shah's stunning performance stands in contrast with Bangladesh's election last month, where ŌüĀa youth-driven party fizzled at the polls after emerging out of the "Gen-Z" protests that unseated the country's long-serving premier in 2024.
The heart of RSP's political machine is the Research, Strategy and ŌĆŗDocumentation Department, run by an 11-member board overseeing 300 party workers divided into three national groups that supplement smaller teams led by individual candidates.
These national-level teams plan election tactics, organise rallies, create and manage online ŌĆŗcontent, and track ground-level campaigning and feedback from across Nepal, three officials said.
In the run-up to Thursday's vote, the party also followed a carefully calibrated media strategy, with Shah delivering a major speech every eight days, allowing enough time for each rally to be amplified by a 660-strong social media team.
The RSP also held road shows in five to seven districts each day, backed by daily brief appearances in one of Nepal's seven provinces where Shah would ŌĆŗmeet and connect with the voters.
"If you keep giving speeches, people get confused," a party official said, asking not to be named. "We let opposition parties raise some issues, and then respond once. This way, ŌĆŗour message stays clear."
The centralised campaign system and large events were funded directly by the party, which has received large donations from Nepalis living abroad, particularly from those in the United States, according to two officials.
