But individual ŌĆīcandidates were ŌüĀresponsible for organising and financing their own campaign events, said RSP's Treasurer Lima Adhikari.
Before he joined RSP in December ahead of the polls, Shah served as the mayor of Kathmandu, a position he had won in 2022 by leveraging his popularity as one of Nepal's biggest rap stars, with millions of followers on social media.
On January 19, Shah stood with RSP founder and TV host-turned-politician Rabi Lamichhane at a rally in the capital city of Nepal's Madhesh province and told the thousands gathered: "A Madhesi boy is going to become prime minister."
Although Madhesh and the surrounding Terai plains are the most populous ŌĆŗregions of Nepal, the country has long been ŌĆŗdominated by political elites from Kathmandu and its ŌüĀmountainous belt.
Shah's January speech went viral on social media, underlining the early success of the RSP's bet that he could become the first leader from the plains to secure the premiership, three party officials said.
"It was very clear to us that the nation was fed up with the old corrupt leaders ŌĆŗand they were seeing hope in young leaders like Balen Shah and Rabi Lamichhane," said Birendra Kumar Mehta, a member of RSP's central committee.
"The ŌĆŗparty saw this as an ŌüĀopportunity."
Shah himself is contesting the election from Jhapa-5, a constituency in the plains that has long been part of the home turf of K.P. Sharma Oli, who quit as prime minister in the wake of the September protests.
In this rural pocket of Nepal, Shah has stuck with his unorthodox campaign playbook, shunning sit-down media interviews, making impromptu stops to talk with voters, and forming a group of youth volunteers across the constituency.
Part of ŌüĀhis team, reinforced ŌĆŗby the RSP campaign in Kathmandu, has also been collecting voter feedback and complaints, including on development projects and general ŌĆŗgovernance.
"People from every part of Nepal will feel that the government is theirs and most importantly, that it is there for them," said D.P. Aryal, RSP's vice chairperson, adding that Shah's administration will look to bring in outside experts to bolster ŌĆŗits capacity.
"Youth engagement and the maximum integration of technology will also be among our top priorities."

item.title: Israel carries out rare airborne raid in Lebanon; 16 killed in strikes
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - Israel's military carried out an airborne operation that ŌĆŗdropped troops into a town in eastern Lebanon overnight, residents and Lebanese state media said on ŌĆīSaturday, as heavy Israeli strikes on the area left more than a dozen people dead.
Lebanon was plunged deeper into the conflict engulfing the Middle East on Monday when Iran-backed Hezbollah launched rockets and drones into Israel, which has responded with heavy bombardment across Lebanon's south, east and ŌĆŗnear the capital Beirut.
Overnight, Israeli helicopters dropped troops near the town of Nabi Chit in Lebanon's eastern ŌĆŗBekaa Valley, Lebanese state media and residents said. Residents and Hezbollah fighters fired on the ŌüĀtroops as they moved on foot, the reports said.
The Israeli troops withdrew and left by helicopter as Israeli strikes ŌĆŗpounded Nabi Chit and nearby towns, state media and residents said. Israel's military did not immediately respond to Reuters questions ŌĆŗon the incident.
Lebanon's health ministry said 16 people had been killed in Israeli attacks in the last 24 hours in Nabi Chit. A spokesperson told Reuters the toll included people killed in strikes throughout Friday as well as during intense overnight bombardment, and that it was ŌĆŗexpected to rise further.
Two security sources told Reuters that three army troops were among the dead.
Israeli strikes have killed ŌĆŗmore than 200 people across Lebanon, and orders to evacuate have displaced around 300,000 people, only a third of whom are now ŌĆīliving in ŌüĀgovernment shelters.
Shawki al-Masri, who lives in a town adjacent to Nabi Chit, described the overnight bombing as "a night of hell".
"We heard the helicopters over our house all night - they were so low we thought they would land on us," he told Reuters.
"People in the town woke up and started shooting at them, then the warplanes started bombing. ŌĆŗIt was a very violent ŌĆŗnight and only calmed ŌüĀdown when the sun came up," he said.
In a statement overnight, Hezbollah said it had fired on Israeli troops who had been dropped near Nabi Chit by four Israeli helicopters. ŌĆŗThe group said the Israeli troops withdrew.
While Israeli forces are carrying out ground operations ŌĆŗin southern Lebanon, ŌüĀpushing deeper into a strip of border villages, airborne operations are rare. In a 2024 war between Israel and Hezbollah, Israeli naval forces kidnapped a man from a Lebanese coastal town.
The United Nations on Saturday warned that the conflict was set to get "even ŌüĀworse," ŌĆŗand that talks between Israel and Lebanon "must be pursued with urgency" to ŌĆŗend hostilities.
Its Special Coordinator for Lebanon, Jeanine Hennis-Plasschaert, said in a statement that it was "clear that ongoing military actions will not deliver a lasting win ŌĆŗto anyone".
"Rather, they will only deepen instability and inflict further suffering," she said.

item.title: India raises cooking gas prices as Iran war hits supply
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 7 (Reuters) - Indian companies have raised the prices of liquefied petroleum ŌĆŗgas, mostly used as a cooking ŌĆīfuel, for the first time in about a year, as global prices surge with the U.S.-Israel war ŌĆŗon Iran disrupting supplies from the ŌĆŗMiddle East.
Indian Oil Corp, the country's top ŌüĀrefiner and LPG seller, has increased ŌĆŗthe prices of a 14.2-kg LPG cylinder in ŌĆŗDelhi by 7% to 913 rupees ($9.93), according to its website.
State refiners IOC, Bharat Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab and Hindustan ŌĆŗPetroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab raised prices in tandem.
India, the ŌĆŗworld's second-biggest importer of LPG, last year consumed 33.15 ŌĆīmillion ŌüĀmetric tons of cooking gas, a mixture of propane and butane, with imports accounting for about two-thirds of LPG consumption. Middle ŌĆŗEastern LPG accounts ŌĆŗfor ŌüĀ85% to 90% of those imports.
India on Friday asked refiners to boost ŌĆŗLPG production to avoid any shortage ŌĆŗof ŌüĀcooking gas in the country.
Indian companies have also raised the prices of 19-kg commercial LPG ŌüĀcylinders, ŌĆŗmainly used by hotels and ŌĆŗrestaurants, to 1,883 rupees from 1,768.50 rupees.
($1 = 91.9280 Indian ŌĆŗrupees)

item.title: White House seeks bolder action on energy prices amid Iran conflict
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The White House is asking federal agencies to step up efforts ŌĆŗto address soaring energy prices from the Iran conflict , signaling a concern that steps taken so far may not be enough, ŌĆīaccording to two people familiar with the matter.
Senior officials have asked the departments of Energy, Transportation and Treasury and the Environmental Protection Agency to provide more policy options, with an emphasis on measures that President Donald Trump could implement without congressional approval, one of the sources told Reuters.
The requests indicate that the White ŌĆŗHouse is preparing for the possibility that bolder action may be needed if oil and gas prices continue to climb. ŌĆŗPolitical analysts say higher gasoline prices could hurt Trump and his Republican Party in November's midterm elections, ŌüĀwhen control of Congress is at stake.
ŌĆ£Obviously the White House is coordinating with the interagency on this important issue, if we were ŌĆŗnot, it would be a problem. President Trump and his entire energy team have had a strong game plan to keep oil prices ŌĆŗstable well before Operation Epic Fury began, and they will continue to review all credible options and execute on them when appropriate," White House spokeswoman Taylor Rogers said in a statement.
U.S. and global crude oil futures climbed above $90 a barrel on Friday, with U.S. prices rising more than 12% as Middle Eastern ŌĆŗsupply remained constrained by the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz amid the expanding U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Gasoline prices in the U.S. have ŌĆŗsurged in recent weeks, reaching levels not seen since late 2024. The national average for regular unleaded has climbed above $3.30 per gallon, while diesel has ŌĆīrisen to $4.26 ŌüĀper gallon.
The White House has taken a cautious approach to intervening in energy markets, wary that an overly aggressive strategy could backfire. Officials say any broad measures must be carefully calibrated, noting that heavy-handed steps that fail to lower gasoline or crude prices could unsettle markets, erode confidence and trigger political backlash. Analysts, meanwhile, have expressed skepticism about how much the White House can do to rein in prices.
Officials ŌĆŗhave discussed a wide array ŌĆŗof options, including a federal ŌüĀgasoline tax holiday and looser environmental regulations around summer gasoline that will allow higher blends of ethanol, Reuters previously reported .
The Treasury Department is weighing a plan that involves using the oil futures market, Reuters reported , but ŌĆŗthere is no immediate plan to announce the move.
Trump ordered on Tuesday that the U.S. International ŌĆŗDevelopment Finance Corporation provide ŌüĀinsurance against losses stemming from political instability or conflict for maritime trade in the Gulf. The move came after oil and liquefied natural gas tanker transit ground to a halt in the Strait of Hormuz , a chokepoint for about 20% of the world's daily oil supply.
Markets met the ŌüĀmove with ŌĆŗsome skepticism. Analysts question whether financial guarantees alone can offset the operational and security ŌĆŗrisks posed by the growing tensions in the region.
On Friday, the administration announced it would provide reinsurance for losses up to $20 billion in the Gulf region to bolster confidence ŌĆŗfor oil and gas shippers during the war with Iran.

item.title: Shipper MSC to introduce emergency fuel surcharge
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Shipping company MSC said on Saturday it would implement an ŌĆŗemergency fuel surcharge to all ŌĆīcargo from the Mediterranean (including West Mediterranean, Adriatic, East Mediterranean, Greece and Turkey) ŌĆŗand Black Sea to the ŌĆŗIndian sub-continent, Red Sea and East ŌüĀAfrica, effective March 16.
It said ŌĆŗthe surcharge would be $30 per ŌĆŗtwenty-foot equivalent unit (TEU) from the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Red Sea for ŌĆŗdry containers, and $50 per TEU ŌĆŗfor refrigerated containers.
Dry containers from the Mediterranean ŌĆīand ŌüĀBlack Sea to East Africa will be charged $60 per TEU, while refrigerated containers will be charged $90 ŌĆŗper TEU, ŌĆŗthe world's ŌüĀlargest carrier of ocean container cargo said.
MSC will ŌĆŗalso impose a surcharge of $40 ŌĆŗper ŌüĀTEU from the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Indian sub-continent ŌüĀfor ŌĆŗdry containers, and $60 ŌĆŗper TEU for refrigerated containers.

item.title: South Africa central bank to redraft risk scenarios as Iran war boosts oil price
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - South Africa's central bank will redraft its risk scenarios for its next rate-setting ŌĆīpolicy meeting as the widening Middle East conflict pushes up oil prices, the country's central bank governor told Reuters.
The central bank is scheduled to decide on March 26 on interest rates after keeping its main lending rate unchanged at 6.75% in a split decision in late January, citing at ŌĆŗthe time that policy makers wanted to see inflation expectations fall further.
"We had our baseline (in the January meeting) and ŌĆŗwe had an optimistic scenario and an adverse scenario," said South African Reserve Bank Governor Lesetja ŌüĀKganyago. He said the adverse scenario had assumed the average oil price for the year would reach $75 per barrel and ŌĆŗthe rand would weaken to 18.50 to the dollar.
Now the previous adverse scenario, he said, "is gone - it was in the past ... ŌĆŗwe will come up with a completely new one."
The Middle East crisis set off by Israel and Washington's bombing of Iran lifted Brent crude futures to more than $94 per barrel this week, while the rand weakened to 16.82 to the dollar.
Kganyago said a 10% move in the exchange ŌĆŗrate would have a much stronger impact on inflation in South Africa than a similar jump in oil prices.
"Yes, it's the ŌĆŗadverse scenario, but it is not playing out as we had feared," Kganyago said, adding that policy makers would only get concerned once ŌĆīthey saw ŌüĀthe impact of the exchange rate filter through to prices.
"The call that as a policy maker you must make is - is this transitory, or is it persistent? And you only respond to the persistent, not to the transitory - and that is not an easy call to make."
The knock-on effects from global tensions that have kept markets on edge in recent months - and the ŌĆŗmost recent crisis - would be ŌĆŗdominating discussions at the next ŌüĀmeeting, Kganyago added, speaking on the sidelines of an investor conference in London.
"We will discuss what is the impact of this geopolitics on the markets, on oil, emerging markets, and on ŌĆŗexchange rates."
Asked whether South Africa would continue to buy dollars - as it has since the ŌĆŗstart of the ŌüĀyear - after the rand weakened nearly 4% this week against a strong greenback, the governor said the policy had not changed.
"If we think that there are cheap dollars available in the market, we will pick them up," he said, adding that overall reserve levels ŌüĀhad been ŌĆŗbolstered not only by dollar purchases, but by the rising value of gold in ŌĆŗthe central bank's war chest and the proceeds of borrowing by the Treasury.
Central bank data released earlier on Friday showed that South Africa's net foreign reserves rose ŌĆŗto $75.84 billion at the end of February from $74.88 billion in January.

item.title: Flash floods in Nairobi kill 10, disrupt flights at major airport
item.description:
NAIROBI, March 7 (Reuters) - Aid workers ŌĆīpulled bodies from floodwaters across Nairobi on Saturday after overnight flash floods killed at least 10 people, swept away dozens of cars and ŌĆŗdisrupted flights at East Africa's biggest airport, authorities said.
In ŌĆŗthe industrial neighbourhood of Grogan, security guard John Lomayan, ŌüĀ34, looked at the body of an elderly man ŌĆŗhe recognised - a roadside egg seller - trapped beneath a car that ŌĆŗhad been washed away when the Nairobi River burst its banks.
"I saw him being carried by the water from up there," he said, gesturing ŌĆŗup the road. "We didn't know where he had gone. ŌĆŗIt is only now that we see him under the car".
A Reuters reporter ŌĆīsaw ŌüĀthree bodies pulled from underneath cars. Police said 10 people had been confirmed dead so far.
Scientists say global warming is worsening floods and droughts across East Africa by concentrating rainfall ŌĆŗinto shorter, more ŌĆŗintense bursts. ŌüĀA 2024 World Weather Attribution study found climate change had made devastating rains in the region ŌĆŗtwice as likely as before.
Kenya Airways said ŌĆŗthe rains ŌüĀhad disrupted flights to Nairobi and forced some to divert to the coastal city of Mombasa.
"So many cars, so much stuff, ŌüĀI ŌĆŗdon't know. Everything was just (washed away). ŌĆŗAll of the water (came) ... from that river," shocked resident Cedric Mwanza said, referring ŌĆŗto the Nairobi River.

item.title: Days after Iran strikes, Trump hosts Latin American leaders with China in focus
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump will host Latin American leaders in Florida on Saturday as his administration seeks to counter China's ŌĆŗgrowing influence in the region, a diplomatic show just days after U.S. strikes on Iran opened a new front in the Middle ŌĆīEast.
The summit comes as Trump prepares for talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing at the end of March. The Trump administration hopes to pull Latin America closer to Washington after years of growing Chinese trade, lending and infrastructure investment in the region.
Saturday's gathering, dubbed the "Shield of the Americas," also gives Trump a chance to project strength closer to home even ŌĆŗas the conflict in the Middle East leads to consequences he may not fully control, such as rising prices for oil and gas.
Kristi Noem ŌĆŗwill be special envoy for the "Shield of the Americas", Trump posted Thursday. Noem was Homeland Security secretary until Trump removed her ŌüĀfrom that post this week after mounting criticism of her from Congress.
The summit brings together conservative leaders aligned with Trump on security, migration and economics, reflecting a ŌĆŗbroader rightward shift in parts of Latin America.
Among those expected to attend are Argentine President Javier Milei, Chile's president-elect Jose Antonio Kast and Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele, ŌĆŗwhose gang crackdown, criticized by human rights groups, has become a model for parts of Latin America's right.
Politicians from across the region have toured Bukele's sprawling "mega-prison," where the United States last year deported more than 200 Venezuelans without trial.
Also expected are Honduran President Nasry Asfura, who narrowly won a disputed election with Trump's backing, and Ecuador's President Daniel Noboa, who has ŌĆŗechoed parts of Trump's economic agenda and recently announced joint operations with the U.S. in a military crackdown on drug trafficking.
Many of the leaders share Trump's ŌĆŗhardline view of crime and migration, favoring crackdowns over deeper social fixes and private business over the state. Their rise reflects a broader rightward turn in parts of Latin America ŌĆīat a ŌüĀtime when the region is being pulled between Washington and Beijing.
Ryan Berg, who heads the Americas Program at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, wrote this week that the summit is the first time in Trump's second term that Washington has brought together Latin American leaders in this way.
He said the talks are likely to focus on security, drug trafficking, money laundering and China's role in ports, telecommunications and other infrastructure.
Berg said China's trade with the region hit ŌĆŗa record $518 billion in 2024, with ŌĆŗBeijing loaning more than $120 billion to ŌüĀgovernments across the Western Hemisphere.
China's increased involvement in Latin America - from satellite tracking stations in Argentina and a port in Peru to economic support for Venezuela - has been an irritant for successive U.S. administrations.
China has expanded its reach through trade, loans ŌĆŗand infrastructure, while the Trump administration has pushed governments in the region to curb Beijing's role in ports, energy ŌĆŗprojects and other strategic ŌüĀassets.
That pressure was on display recently when Panamanian authorities moved against a Hong Kong-based firm tied to operations in the Panama Canal, a key global freight channel.
Washington has also taken more direct steps in the region.
The United States captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3 and moved to seize control of the country's oil exports, and ŌüĀhas tightened enforcement ŌĆŗof the decades-long U.S. embargo on Cuba.
Several Trump administration officials have told Reuters that Trump's ŌĆŗmove against Maduro was intended in part to counter China's ambitions, and that Beijing's days of leveraging debt to get cheap oil from Venezuela were "over."

item.title: Nigerian army kills 45 in Katsina clash
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - At least 45 "bandits" were killed in a clash ŌĆŗwith Nigerian troops in ŌĆīthe Danmusa area of Katsina state in northern Nigeria, according ŌĆŗto a statement from ŌĆŗthe Katsina State Government on ŌüĀSaturday.
The violence followed an attack ŌĆŗby armed gangs of ŌĆŗmen from neighboring Zamfara state who rode into Alhazawa village in Musawa ŌĆŗon March 5 and ŌĆŗattempted to steal cattle, the statement ŌĆīsaid.
The ŌüĀattackers returned the following day and encountered troops stationed at the army's Forward Operating ŌĆŗBase ŌĆŗin ŌüĀDan Ali near Maidabino ward, leading to a ŌĆŗclash in which ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀarmy killed 45 of the attackers, the statement said.
A captain ŌüĀand ŌĆŗtwo soldiers ŌĆŗdied in the encounter, the statement added.

item.title: Iranian hardline clerics seek swift naming of new supreme leader
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Two influential and hardline Iranian clerics have called for the swift selection of a new supreme leader to help guide ŌĆŗthe nation amid a new wave of U.S. and Israeli ŌĆīstrikes, Iranian media reported on Saturday.
The calls by the clerics suggest that at least some in the clerical establishment are uncomfortable with leaving a three-man council in ŌĆŗcharge, even temporarily under constitutional rules, after the killing of ŌĆŗSupreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said ŌüĀthe U.S. should have a role in choosing the new leader, ŌĆŗa demand Iran has rejected.
Naser Makarem Shirazi, a grand ayatollah, which means ŌĆŗhe commands a broad following for his religious rulings, said an appointment was needed swiftly to "help better organise the countryŌĆÖs affairs", state media reported.
Last week, two senior ShiŌĆÖite ŌĆŗreligious authorities also issued fatwas, or religious decrees, calling on Muslims ŌĆŗaround the world to avenge the killing of Khamenei. Makarem Shirazi said it was ŌĆīa ŌüĀreligious duty for Muslims "until the evil of these criminals is eradicated from the world."
Grand Ayatollah Hossein Nouri Hamedani also urged members of the Assembly of Experts, a clerical body charged with choosing the new leader, ŌĆŗto accelerate the ŌĆŗprocess of picking ŌüĀKhamenei's successor, state media reported.
Following rules laid out in Iran's constitution, a three-man council comprising the president, a ŌĆŗsenior cleric and the head of the judiciary, ŌĆŗhas taken ŌüĀon the supreme leader's role until the Assembly of Experts decides.
The constitution states a supreme leader should be chosen within three months, although with ŌüĀwar raging, ŌĆŗit is not immediately clear how quickly ŌĆŗthe 88-member Assembly of Experts can convene. Sources have said some clerics have held some ŌĆŗconsultations online.

item.title: Ukraine's interceptor drone makers look at exports to the Gulf as Iran war flares
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Ukrainian manufacturers of cheap interceptor drones designed to knock out enemy unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) say they have the capacity to export in large volumes, amid enquiries from the United ŌĆŗStates and Middle East prompted by the Iran war .
Hundreds of drones based on Iran's Shahed model and now made in Russia fill Ukraine's skies during frequent attacks, ŌĆīand many are downed by air defences including Western missiles, fighter jets, truck-mounted guns and interceptor drones.
Now, as Iran launches drones at U.S. allies across the Gulf and beyond in response to intense U.S. and Israeli strikes, Ukraine is hoping the Middle East crisis will increase its leverage with allies by showing them the expertise it has built during four years of Russia's full-scale invasion could be invaluable to partners' own long-term security.
With missiles sometimes costing millions of dollars each, ŌĆŗand in short supply as Western armies scramble to bolster their own defences, interceptors are seen as an efficient and cost-effective way to counter attacks by relatively cheap ŌĆŗdrones.
The United States and its allies in the Middle East have swiftly sought Ukraine's help with supplying such interceptors.
President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday ŌüĀUkraine would provide assistance in response to a U.S. request for help and said earlier that Middle Eastern nations had approached Kyiv.
Zelenskiy did not specify what this would entail, but a source familiar with ŌĆŗthe matter told Reuters that the United States and Qatar were in talks to purchase Ukrainian interceptor drones.
SkyFall, a major manufacturer of drones including interceptors, said its manufacturing capacity ŌĆŗhad outgrown Ukraine's ability to purchase its systems and the company was ready to export.
"We have had interest and inquiries from our (allies) and countries in the Middle East," said Ares, a representative of SkyFall who wore a balaclava as he spoke to hide his identity.
Ares spoke on condition that only his call-sign be used.
SkyFall says its P1-SUN interceptor has downed more than 1,500 Shaheds and 1,000 other drones since it entered operations four months ago.
"The ŌĆŗcompany is ready to provide any necessary assistance if we get the green light from our government," he said, adding that it would only do so if it did not affect Ukraine's ŌĆŗability to defend itself.
Ihor Fedirko, CEO of UCDI, Ukraine's state-backed arms manufacturers' association, estimated that manufacturers of interceptor UAVs and other counter-drone systems were making about twice as many as the Ukrainian military needed to ŌĆīdeal with Russia's frequent ŌüĀbarrages .
SkyFall asked that the location of its factory not be disclosed. During a recent visit, Reuters reporters saw long rows of 3D printers churning out plastic drone components and halls of workers soldering and assembling drones.
The company estimated it could manufacture up to 50,000 interceptor drones a month and export 5,000 to 10,000 without impinging on Ukraine's needs.
Although Ukraine does not shoot down every drone fired by Russia, this is not always down to munition availability and can instead be a question of co-ordination.
Kyiv is striving to better layer its air defences in order to allow fewer targets ŌĆŗthrough, and sees boosting interceptor drone use as ŌĆŗa key piece of the puzzle.
In February, ŌüĀthey were responsible for 70% of the drones downed in and around Kyiv, according to Ukraine's military.
Most Ukrainian interceptor drones cost a few thousand dollars or less. In comparison, PAC-3 missiles used in the coveted U.S. Patriot air defence system, can cost $4 million each and ŌĆŗtend to be used to bring down enemy missiles.
Shahed-136 drones, designed by Iran and now mass-manufactured by Russia, are estimated to cost between $50,000 ŌĆŗand $100,000 each.
SkyFall markets the ŌüĀP1-SUN as a highly cost-effective option. Depending on the model's specifications, it charges Ukraine's military about $1,000 per drone.
"If we are talking about export and ... assistance to partners, then the price will most likely be higher," Ares said, although he believed it would still represent the cheapest option.
Industry representatives said one of the biggest obstacles to using Ukrainian interceptor drones in the Gulf was pilots rather than drones. Ukraine is ŌüĀthe only ŌĆŗcountry with drone crews who know how to use these systems in combat.
SkyFall has its own academy that provides ŌĆŗa three-week course to new pilots, and the company said it would be ready to send instructors abroad in the event that Ukraine's government allowed it to sell drones to other countries.
It also said it has developed capabilities to pilot ŌĆŗits drones remotely, meaning drones could potentially be flown in the Gulf from a screen in Ukraine.

item.title: China's Xi says the military must be politically loyal, root out corruption
item.description:
SHANGHAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping said on ŌĆŗSaturday the military must ŌĆībe loyal to the Communist Party and root out ŌĆŗcorruption, according to a ŌĆŗreport by the official Xinhua ŌüĀnews agency.
"There is ŌĆŗno place for corruption to ŌĆŗhide," said Xi.
"The fight against corruption must move forward."
The remarks ŌĆŗcome amid the annual ŌĆŗmeetings of the nation's top political ŌĆībodies, ŌüĀwhere officials unveiled this year's economic targets.
Two senior Chinese officials were absent ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗWednesday from ŌüĀthe ranks of China's highest tier ŌĆŗof power at ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀopening of the annual meetings, with top Communist Party ŌüĀcadres ŌĆŗensnared in ŌĆŗa wave of purges .

item.title: Lebanon's Hezbollah warns residents in Israel's north to evacuate
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 7 (Reuters) - Lebanese Shi'ite militant group Hezbollah on Saturday warned ŌĆŗresidents of a northern Israeli city near ŌĆīthe border with Lebanon to evacuate and head south.
Hezbollah did not specify what action, if any, it ŌĆŗplanned to take against the city, ŌĆŗwhich lies only a few kilometres from ŌüĀthe border.
"Warning. All residents of Kiryat Shmona ŌĆŗare asked to evacuate immediately. Head south," it ŌĆŗsaid in a statement.
Lebanon was pulled into the widening U.S.-Israel war with Iran on Monday after Hezbollah, an ŌĆŗIranian-aligned group, fired rockets and drones into ŌĆŗIsrael. Israel responded with heavy strikes across Lebanon's south, ŌĆīeast ŌüĀand near Beirut.
Tens of thousands of Israelis living in the north evacuated their homes after war broke out in Gaza between Israel and ŌĆŗPalestinian group ŌĆŗHamas in ŌüĀ2023, as Hezbollah began firing rockets and other projectiles at northern ŌĆŗcommunities.
Residents returned after a November 2024 ŌĆŗceasefire ŌüĀbetween Israel and Hezbollah. Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz has this week repeatedly urged Israelis ŌüĀin ŌĆŗthe north to remain in ŌĆŗtheir homes, saying the military will protect them.

item.title: Trump says Iran will be 'hit very hard' on Saturday
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. President ŌĆŗDonald Trump said Iran ŌĆīwould be "hit very hard" on Saturday and that he ŌĆŗwas considering widening the ŌĆŗareas and groups of people ŌüĀbeing, targeted, without providing ŌĆŗdetails.
"Today Iran will be ŌĆŗhit very hard! Under serious consideration for complete destruction and certain ŌĆŗdeath, because of Iran's ŌĆŗbad behavior, are areas and groups ŌĆīof ŌüĀpeople that were not considered for targeting up until this moment in time" ŌĆŗTrump ŌĆŗsaid ŌüĀin a post on Truth Social.
He also ŌĆŗnoted that Iran had ŌĆŗapologised ŌüĀto its neighbours for its strikes against them, ŌüĀwhich ŌĆŗhe cast as ŌĆŗa surrender.

item.title: Pope Leo names former envoy to Duterte's Philippines as ambassador to US
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 7 (Reuters) - Pope Leo on Saturday named a ŌĆŗsenior Church diplomat who represented the Vatican in the Philippines during former President Rodrigo ŌĆīDuterte 's war on drugs as the Vatican's U.S. ambassador and envoy to the Trump administration.
Italian Archbishop Gabriele Caccia, 68, served for two years in Manila before becoming the Vatican's U.N. ambassador in 2020.
His appointment follows Vatican ŌĆŗcriticism of U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran , with the Vatican's top diplomat, Cardinal Pietro Parolin, saying ŌĆŗon Wednesday that the military campaign had undermined international law .
Leo, the first U.S. ŌüĀpope, has also openly criticised some of Trump's right-wing policies. He has described Trump's immigration crackdown, ŌĆŗwhich has convulsed cities across the United States, as " inhuman ."
Caccia, widely seen as one of the more ŌĆŗexperienced diplomats in the Vatican's foreign service, will arrive in Washington as some U.S. bishops have ramped up criticism of Trump.
He will replace Cardinal Christophe Pierre, 80, who is retiring and had been the Vatican's U.S. ŌĆŗambassador since 2016.
Three top U.S. cardinals in January decried the direction of American foreign policy, saying ŌĆŗthe country's "moral role in confronting evil around the world" was in question.
In ŌĆīManila, ŌüĀCaccia was known for tamping down tensions between local Catholic bishops and Duterte, who frequently attacked Church officials in speeches.
Duterte, who left office in 2022, is being held in The Hague over murders committed during his war on drugs .
The Vatican, which maintains about 110 embassies worldwide, considers Washington one ŌĆŗof its most important ŌĆŗdiplomatic postings.
The ambassador ŌüĀmust represent the Church's priorities at the White House, and envoys over the decades have contested policies of various U.S. administrations.
The Vatican's U.S. ambassador ŌĆŗalso plays a sweeping role inside the national Church. He is ŌĆŗresponsible for recommending ŌüĀto the pope U.S. priests who could serve as bishops, shaping the lives of the country's 72 million Catholics.
Caccia, originally from Milan, has previously served as the Vatican's ambassador to Lebanon and ŌüĀspent seven ŌĆŗyears as the number-three official in the Vatican's powerful ŌĆŗSecretariat of State.
The Vatican and the U.S. formally established diplomatic relations in 1984 under President Ronald Reagan. None of the ŌĆŗprevious six Vatican ambassadors in Washington had served first at the United Nations.

item.title: Trump cannot end protections for 350,000 Haitians, US appeals court rules
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - A divided U.S. appeals court has refused to let the Trump administration revoke legal protections that allow more than 350,000 Haitians to live and ŌĆŗwork in the U.S. and avoid being returned to their gang-violence -stricken country.
A 2-1 ŌĆīpanel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit late on Friday rejected , opens new tab the administration's bid to pause a February 2 ruling that blocked the U.S. Department of Homeland ŌĆŗSecurity from ending Haiti's Temporary Protected Status.
TPS is a humanitarian program that shields ŌĆŗeligible migrants from deportation and allows them to work.
Under outgoing Homeland ŌüĀSecurity Secretary Kristi Noem, the department has moved to end TPS for a dozen ŌĆŗcountries as part of President Donald Trump's immigration crackdown, arguing the program was never ŌĆŗintended to serve as a "de facto amnesty."
The administration had asked the D.C. Circuit to stay U.S. District Judge Ana Reyes' February order while it appeals. Her decision came in a class-action lawsuit brought by ŌĆŗHaitians seeking to prevent DHS from exposing them to deportation.
Reyes found that Noem's November ŌĆŗmove to end the Haitians' legal protections likely violated TPS termination procedures and the U.S. Constitution's ŌĆīFifth ŌüĀAmendment guarantee of equal protection under the law.
The administration on appeal noted that the U.S. Supreme Court had twice allowed it to end TPS for Venezuelans.
But U.S. Circuit Judges Florence Pan and Brad Garcia, both appointed by Democratic President Joe Biden, distinguished the cases ŌĆŗand said Haitians sent ŌĆŗhome would "be vulnerable ŌüĀto violence amid a 'collapsing rule of law' and lack access to life-sustaining medical care."
U.S. Circuit Judge Justin Walker, a Trump appointee, ŌĆŗdissented, saying the case and the earlier Supreme Court litigation involving ŌĆŗVenezuelans were "the ŌüĀlegal equivalent of fraternal, if not identical, twins."
DHS did not respond to a request for comment.
Haitians were first granted TPS in 2010 after a devastating earthquake. The U.S. has ŌüĀrepeatedly renewed ŌĆŗthe designation, most recently under the Biden administration ŌĆŗin July 2024.
At that time, DHS cited Haiti's "simultaneous economic, security, political, and health crises", driven by gangs and ŌĆŗthe absence of a functioning government.

item.title: Turkey considering deployment of F-16s to Cyprus, ministry source says
item.description:
ANKARA, March 7 (Reuters) - Turkey is considering ŌĆŗthe deployment of ŌĆīF-16 aircraft to Cyprus, a Turkish ŌĆŗdefence ministry ŌĆŗsource said on ŌüĀSaturday.
The person said ŌĆŗsuch a ŌĆŗmove was one of the steps being ŌĆŗconsidered amid ŌĆŗthe phased planning underway ŌĆīto ŌüĀensure the security of the breakaway Turkish Cypriot ŌĆŗstate ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe north of the ŌĆŗisland, as ŌĆŗconflict ŌüĀspreads in the region.

item.title: EU looks to soften energy bill pressures for industry, document shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 7 (Reuters) - The European Union is examining energy ŌĆītaxes, network charges and carbon costs as possible areas for short-term measures to ease pressure on industries hit by high energy prices, a document seen by Reuters showed.
Brussels is looking ŌĆŗfor quick fixes after companies warned they cannot compete with rivals in ŌĆŗChina and the U.S. - even before this week's surge in oil ŌüĀand gas prices sparked by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
European Commission President ŌĆŗUrsula von der Leyen has pledged to present options for EU leaders to consider at ŌĆŗa summit on 19 March.
A Commission paper prepared for a meeting of EU Commissioners on Friday showed the bloc is exploring short-term measures to help the hardest-hit regions and sectors, without ŌĆŗundermining longer-term climate laws meant to shift Europe to a cheaper, low-carbon energy ŌĆŗsystem.
"Any proposal for legislative change will not deliver immediately and a bridge solution may be ŌĆīneeded ŌüĀto reduce energy prices in the next 2-5 years until the clean transition eases pressure on power prices as already seen in some regions," said the document, seen by Reuters.
The paper said the Commission would look at network charges - which make ŌĆŗup about 18% ŌĆŗof industrial power ŌüĀbills - and national taxes and levies, as well as carbon costs, which account for around 11% of bills.
It noted that ŌĆŗgovernments are underusing existing tools to cut companies' energy bills, ŌĆŗincluding state ŌüĀaid to offset carbon costs and contracts for difference that guarantee industrial consumers a stable power price.
The document said that if energy supplies are disrupted further, Brussels must ŌüĀbe ŌĆŗready to introduce measures to encourage consumers to ŌĆŗuse less energy, as it did in 2022 when Russia slashed gas deliveries.
A Commission spokesperson did not ŌĆŗimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Kuwait cuts oil production as precaution amid Iran tensions, KPC says

March 7 (Reuters) - Kuwait said it had implemented a ŌĆŗprecautionary reduction in crude ŌĆīoil production and refining throughput following the ŌĆŗongoing attacks by ŌĆŗIran against Kuwait and "Iranian threats ŌüĀto safe passage ŌĆŗof ships through the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz," Kuwait Petroleum Corporation (KPC) said in a ŌĆŗstatement on Saturday.
The ŌĆŗstate oil company said the ŌĆīmove ŌüĀwas part of its "risk management and business continuity strategy."
It said ŌĆŗthe adjustment ŌĆŗwas ŌüĀstrictly precautionary and would be ŌĆŗreviewed as the ŌĆŗsituation ŌüĀdevelops, and it remained ready to restore ŌüĀproduction ŌĆŗlevels once conditions ŌĆŗallow.

item.title: Trump announces new military coalition to 'eradicate cartels' in Western Hemisphere
item.description: March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. President ŌĆŗDonald ŌĆīTrump on Saturday ŌĆŗannounced ŌĆŗa new ŌüĀmilitary ŌĆŗcoalition ŌĆŗto "eradicate cartels" in the ŌĆŗWestern ŌĆŗHemisphere and ŌĆīsaid ŌüĀ17 nations had formally ŌĆŗentered ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀalliance.

item.title: Trump says Cuba negotiating deal with him and Rubio
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. ŌĆŗPresident Donald Trump said ŌĆīon Saturday that Cuba wants to make a ŌĆŗdeal and is ŌĆŗnegotiating with him and ŌüĀSecretary of State Marco ŌĆŗRubio.
Speaking at the "Shield ŌĆŗof the Americas" gathering of Latin American leaders in Miami, ŌĆŗFlorida, Trump said ŌĆŗCuba was "at the end of ŌĆīthe ŌüĀline."
"They want to negotiate, and they are negotiating with Marco (Rubio) and ŌĆŗmyself ŌĆŗand ŌüĀsome others, and I would think ŌĆŗa deal would ŌĆŗbe ŌüĀmade very easily with Cuba," he said.

item.title: Sweden says ship┬Āseized in Baltic Sea had false flag, one crew member notified of crime
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 7 (Reuters) - A ship seized by Sweden in its Baltic Sea waters sailed ŌĆŗunder a false flag and was suspected ŌĆīof violating maritime law and the national ship safety act because of its lack of ŌĆŗseaworthiness, the Swedish police said on ŌĆŗSaturday.
One crew member was notified about the ŌüĀsuspected breach of the national and ŌĆŗinternational laws, Johan Andersson, deputy head of police's ŌĆŗNational Operations Department, told a press conference.
Police and coast guard on Friday took control of Guinea-flagged Caffa ŌĆŗoff southern Sweden, saying it had unclear ŌĆŗflag status and was thus suspected of being stateless, ŌĆīand ŌüĀthat they had launched a probe into suspected violation of maritime law.
"Our investigation so far strengthens our suspicions and our ŌĆŗopinion that this ŌĆŗship ŌüĀhas extensive sea safety deficiencies," Andersson said on Saturday.
"We also have ŌĆŗinformation that the vessel would be ŌĆŗon ŌüĀthe Ukraine sanctions list," he said.
Most of the 11 crew members on Caffa, which ŌüĀaccording ŌĆŗto ship-tracking service MarineTraffic ŌĆŗis a 96-meter general cargo ship, were Russian, Andersson said.

item.title: Flavio Bolsonaro draws even with Lula in Brazil election matchup, Datafolha shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 7 (Reuters) - A second-round runoff between Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva and Senator Flavio Bolsonaro would ŌĆŗresult in a technical tie, according to a Datafolha ŌĆīpoll released on Saturday.
The poll shows that if the runoff were held today, Lula would receive 46% of the vote, compared with ŌĆŗ43% for Flavio, the eldest son of former President ŌĆŗJair Bolsonaro.
If Lula were to face off against Tarcisio ŌüĀde Freitas, governor of the state of Sao Paulo, ŌĆŗ45% of respondents said they would vote for Lula in ŌĆŗthe second round, while 42% would vote for de Freitas, also a technical tie.
Brazil's general election is scheduled for October 4. If no presidential ŌĆŗcandidate receives a majority of the vote, the two ŌĆŗcandidates with the most votes will face off in a runoff on ŌĆīOctober ŌüĀ25.
According to the survey, Lula, 80, who will be vying for his fourth presidential term, leads in all firstŌĆæround scenarios. However, FlavioŌĆÖs candidacy has gained strength since his name was put ŌĆŗforward in December ŌĆŗby Jair ŌüĀBolsonaro, who is in jail.
Lula and Flavio, 44, also have the highest rejection rates in the ŌĆŗpoll. Some 46% of respondents said they ŌĆŗwould not ŌüĀvote for Lula under any circumstances, while 45% said the same about Flavio - another technical tie.
Datafolha interviewed 2,004 voters from ŌüĀTuesday to ŌĆŗThursday in 137 municipalities. The survey ŌĆŗhas a margin of error of two percentage points in either direction.

item.title: UAE president says country is well and is no easy prey in first public comments since Iran strikes
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - The president of the United Arab Emirates said ŌĆŗhis nation was in a ŌĆītime of war but was well and told his enemies it was ŌĆŗno easy prey, in his ŌĆŗfirst public comments since Iran ŌüĀlaunched missiles at its Gulf ŌĆŗneighbour amid U.S.-Israeli strikes.
"The UAE has ŌĆŗthick skin and bitter flesh - we are no easy prey," Mohammed bin ŌĆŗZayed Al Nahyan, who is ŌĆŗalso ruler of Abu Dhabi, said in ŌĆīcomments, ŌüĀmade on Friday when visiting those injured in strikes, aired on Abu Dhabi TV on Saturday.
"We ŌĆŗwill carry ŌĆŗout ŌüĀour duty towards our country, our people, and ŌĆŗour residents who are also ŌĆŗpart ŌüĀof our family," he said.
The UAE, which consists of seven ŌüĀemirates ŌĆŗincluding Dubai, would protect ŌĆŗeveryone in the country, he said.

item.title: Associate of liquidated Brazilian lender Banco Master's owner dies, lawyers say
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 7 (Reuters) - Luiz Phillipi Mourao, an associate of lender Banco Master's owner Daniel Vorcaro, died ŌĆŗon Friday night, according to a statement ŌĆīreleased by his attorneys on Saturday.
Mourao was declared brain dead after a suicide attempt, police said on Wednesday, when he ŌĆŗwas among those arrested in the latest ŌĆŗphase of the investigation involving the liquidated ŌüĀMaster.
Master, which held under 1% of Brazil's banking ŌĆŗassets, was liquidated in November due to what the ŌĆŗcentral bank called a severe liquidity crisis, sharp financial deterioration and serious rule violations.
The investigation into the lender's unraveling ŌĆŗhas exposed a network of influence and conflicts ŌĆŗof interest shaking trust in some of Brazil's most powerful ŌĆīinstitutions.
Vorcaro ŌüĀwas arrested on Wednesday, accused of bribing two central bank officials and also plotting to attack and intimidate people he perceived as acting against his interests ŌĆŗwith Mourao, ŌĆŗwhom he ŌüĀcalled "Sicario" after the hit men employed by Mexican cartels.
Vorcaro's lawyers said they denied ŌĆŗthe allegations, and they expected to ŌĆŗdemonstrate "the ŌüĀregularity of his conduct."
Minas Gerais state civil police said on Saturday that MouraoŌĆÖs body was sent to ŌüĀforensic ŌĆŗscientists for examination and would ŌĆŗbe released to his family after tests are completed.

item.title: Israeli settler fatally shoots Palestinian man in West Bank, health ministry says

RAMALLAH, West Bank March 7 (Reuters) - An Israeli settler shot dead a 27-year-old ŌĆŗPalestinian man in the occupied ŌĆīWest Bank on Saturday, the Palestinian Health Ministry said.
The man was killed in ŌĆŗthe village area of Masafer ŌĆŗYatta, near the Palestinian city of ŌüĀHebron, according to the Health ŌĆŗMinistry and the official Palestinian news ŌĆŗagency WAFA.
The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment on ŌĆŗthe incident.
Masafer Yatta is among ŌĆŗthe sites where Palestinians have been increasingly displaced ŌĆīby ŌüĀsettler violence that has risen sharply over the past two years, according to the United Nations.
Two Palestinian brothers ŌĆŗwere shot ŌĆŗdead ŌüĀby an Israeli on Monday near the city of ŌĆŗNablus in the northern West ŌĆŗBank, ŌüĀthe Palestinian Health Ministry said.
Israeli newspaper Haaretz said the military police ŌüĀwere ŌĆŗinvestigating a reserve soldier ŌĆŗfor Monday's shooting.

item.title: Indian Oil Corp books some oil cargoes from Red Sea, source says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Top Indian refiner, Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , has booked some oil cargoes for loading ŌĆŗfrom the Red Sea port of Yanbu, ŌĆīa company source said on Saturday.
The U.S.-Israel war with Iran has disrupted oil and natural gas ŌĆŗexports from the Middle East, while ŌĆŗtop oil exporter Saudi Arabia is increasing shipments ŌüĀfrom the Red Sea as an alternative.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.5 strikes Indonesia's southern Sumatra region, GFZ says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - An earthquake ŌĆŗof magnitude ŌĆī5.5 struck ŌĆŗIndonesia's ŌĆŗsouthern Sumatra region ŌüĀon ŌĆŗSunday, the ŌĆŗGerman Research Center ŌĆŗfor ŌĆŗGeosciences (GFZ) said.
The quake ŌĆīwas ŌüĀat a depth of ŌĆŗ101 ŌĆŗkm (62.76 ŌüĀmiles), ŌĆŗGFZ said.

item.title: Over 150 Iranian nationals leave Lebanon including diplomats, Lebanese security source says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - More than 150 Iranian nationals, including ŌĆŗdiplomats and their families, ŌĆīleft Lebanon on Saturday, a senior Lebanese security ŌĆŗsource told Reuters, ŌĆŗafter an Israeli military ŌüĀspokesperson threatened "representatives" of Iran in ŌĆŗLebanon.
The security source ŌĆŗsaid they were being flown to Russia on a Russian ŌĆŗplane, and that ŌĆŗanother 20 Iranians had left ŌĆīon ŌüĀFriday following the start of a new war between Lebanese armed ŌĆŗgroup ŌĆŗHezbollah ŌüĀand Israel. An Israeli military spokesperson ŌĆŗon Tuesday told ŌĆŗrepresentatives ŌüĀof the Iranian government "still in Lebanon to ŌüĀleave ŌĆŗimmediately before ŌĆŗthey are targeted."

item.title: Venezuela's Rodriguez commits to dialogue in message to Trump
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Venezuela's acting president, Delcy Rodriguez, said on Saturday that "diplomatic dialogue" ŌĆŗwith the U.S. was the way ŌĆīto resolve the two countries' differences, after both governments agreed on diplomatic and consular relations.
"We reiterate our willingness ŌĆŗto build long-term relations based on ŌĆŗmutual respect, equality, and international law, with ŌüĀa view to promoting a work agenda ŌĆŗthat strengthens cooperation for the benefit of both ŌĆŗcountries," Rodriguez said in a post on X addressed to U.S. President Donald Trump.
The U.S. State Department announced ŌĆŗon Thursday that the two countries would formally ŌĆŗre-establish diplomatic ties.
Caracas severed diplomatic relations with Washington in 2019 ŌĆīafter ŌüĀthe first Trump administration refused to recognize Venezuela's then-President Nicolas Maduro as the country's legitimate leader, following a disputed election, and instead recognized ŌĆŗan opposition ŌĆŗlawmaker as ŌüĀthe country's president.
U.S. forces captured Maduro in January after months of ŌĆŗheightened tensions between the two countries, ŌĆŗsetting ŌüĀoff a chain of changes in Venezuela, including the swearing-in of interim President Delcy Rodriguez.
The two ŌüĀhave ŌĆŗsince gradually resumed bilateral relations, ŌĆŗafter Rodriguez's interim government expressed interest in rebuilding ties with ŌĆŗWashington.

item.title: ADNOC says it is managing offshore output, onshore operations continue
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Abu Dhabi National Oil Company said on Saturday it is actively managing offshore output levels to ŌĆŗaddress storage requirements amid the U.S.-Isaeli war on Iran, ŌĆīwhile its onshore operations are continuing.
"This approach preserves operational flexibility and will enable the company to resume normal operations without prolonged delay," ADNOC said in ŌĆŗa statement.
The war, now in its eighth day, has ŌĆŗblocked shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial waterway ŌüĀresponsible for roughly 20% of global oil and LNG supply.
Analysts ŌĆŗhave predicted the UAE and Saudi Arabia would soon have to cut ŌĆŗoutput as their oil storage fills up.
ADNOC said its operations are continuing, and that it is using export capacity that bypasses the strait as well ŌĆŗas international storage facilities to ensure supply continuity to global markets.
The ŌĆŗUAE can bypass the strait using the Abu Dhabi Crude Oil Pipeline, also ŌĆīknown ŌüĀas the Habshan-Fujairah Pipeline.
The pipeline, which has a capacity of 1.5 million to 1.8 million barrels per day, transports oil from Abu Dhabi's fields directly to the port of Fujairah on the ŌĆŗGulf of Oman, ŌĆŗfeeding its storage ŌüĀterminals and refining facilities.
"Business units are assessing the situation on a product-by-product and transaction-by-transaction basis, considering ŌĆŗthe ongoing disruption that is affecting shipping through the ŌĆŗStrait ŌüĀof Hormuz," it said.
ADNOC has activated well-established protocols and is working closely with authorities to protect its people, assets and operations, it added.
Kuwait ŌüĀPetroleum ŌĆŗCorporation began cutting oil output on Saturday ŌĆŗand declared force majeure, adding to earlier oil and gas reductions from Iraq and ŌĆŗQatar.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.2 strikes Afghanistan's Hindu Kush region, GFZ says
item.description: March 7 (Reuters) - An earthquake ŌĆŗof magnitude ŌĆī5.2 struck ŌĆŗAfghanistan's ŌĆŗHindu Kush region ŌüĀon ŌĆŗSaturday, the ŌĆŗGerman Research Center for ŌĆŗGeosciences (GFZ) ŌĆŗsaid. The quake ŌĆīwas ŌüĀat a depth ŌĆŗof ŌĆŗ206 ŌüĀkm (128 miles), ŌĆŗGFZ ŌĆŗsaid.

item.title: Cracks emerge in Iran's leadership
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Iran's hierarchy is showing signs of fracturing over a war its leaders see as existential, with angry divisions between hardliners and more pragmatic factions laid bare by a row over President Masoud Pezeshkian's promise not to strike Gulf states.
Fissures within Iran's ruling elite were ŌĆŗlong suppressed under the iron rule of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, but his killing a week ago has allowed them to spill out into the open as ŌĆīU.S. and Israeli strikes pile pressure on Tehran.
The unrelenting bombardment mortally imperils the Islamic Republic and has prompted its fiercest acolytes, the Revolutionary Guards , to seize a bigger role in strategy despite a decapitation campaign that has killed many top commanders.
Sources close to Iran's leadership, speaking from inside the country, told Reuters the strains were starting to show among leading figures still alive after a series of killings in the U.S.-Israeli strikes. They spoke anonymously due to the ŌĆŗsensitivity of the matter.
In a sign of the growing stresses to the system, clerics are accelerating the appointment of a new supreme leader with a decision possible on Sunday - though ŌĆŗit is far from clear if Khamenei's successor will wield enough authority to stamp out factional disputes.
While his son, Mojtaba Khamenei , is seen as a frontrunner ŌüĀbacked by the Guards and his father's powerful office, he is untested, junior to most of Iran's senior ayatollahs, and has alienated moderates within the system.
Other potential candidates could struggle to uphold ŌĆŗthe unquestioning obedience of the Guards required to maintain discipline within the system.
"Wartime tends to clarify power structures, and in this case the decisive voice is not that of the civilian leadership but of ŌĆŗthe IRGC," said Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, using an abbreviation of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.
Pezeshkian's apology to Gulf states for a week-long blitz of their territory - and his pledge to rein in such attacks - quickly prompted pushback from hardliners in the Revolutionary Guards and clerical elite, forcing him into a partial climbdown.
In one of the most open criticisms of Pezeshkian - and a sign of ŌĆŗinternal division, hardline cleric and lawmaker Hamid Rasai addressed the president on social media, saying: "your stance was unprofessional, weak and unacceptable."
When the president later repeated his earlier statement on social media, he ŌĆŗleft out the apology that had so angered the Guards and other hardliners - an embarrassing retreat.
To be sure, all senior figures within the hierarchy are steadfast in their commitment to defending the Islamic Republic and its revolutionary ŌĆītheocracy from ŌüĀU.S. and Israeli attacks, but there are clear splits over their strategic approach.
Iran's leadership has sometimes played up differences between hardliners and moderates as a tactic in negotiations with the West, but the dispute over Pezeshkian's statement on Saturday revealed genuine divisions, two senior sources said.
A hardliner close to Khamenei's office, which remains a central node in the hierarchy, told Reuters that Pezeshkian's comments had angered many senior commanders in the Guards.
Another senior Iranian source, a moderate former official, said nobody would be able to fill Khamenei's shoes, describing the late leader as a formidable strategist who had led Iran through ŌĆŗmany difficult periods.
With anxiety increasing in Iran's top ŌĆŗranks, senior ayatollahs began to publicly urge that ŌüĀthe clerical body responsible for appointing a supreme leader accelerate its work.
"It should expedite the process so that it leads to the disappointment of the enemy and the preservation of the unity and solidarity of the nation," Ayatollah Nouri Hamedani said in a statement carried by the semi-official Fars ŌĆŗNews Agency.
In Iran's unusual system, an elected president, government and parliament are subservient to a clerically appointed ayatollah ŌĆŗwho wields ultimate authority as ŌüĀsupreme leader and personally oversees the Revolutionary Guards and other powerful bodies of state.
As leader for 36 years, Khamenei often played hardline and moderate factions within the ruling system against each other while retaining the ultimate say, allowing them to voice disagreements so long as they bowed to his writ.
When he died, leadership formally passed to a constitutionally mandated interim council that included Pezeshkian, the clerical head of the judiciary ŌüĀand another cleric ŌĆŗfrom a hardline body called the Guardian Council.
In Khamenei's absence, strains are showing even inside that tight body, with ŌĆŗthe judiciary chief, noted hardliner Ayatollah Gholamhossein Mohseni-Ejei, saying some regional states had allowed their territory to be used for attacks.
"Heavy strikes on those targets will continue," he said, contradicting Pezeshkian's more conciliatory statement.
Still, even though Khamenei did sometimes allow moderate ŌĆŗor reformist voices to carry the day in disputes with hardliners, they were usually overruled when the system seemed to come under threat.

item.title: As Iran's retaliation began, US officials scrambled to arrange evacuations
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 7 (Reuters) - As the first explosions from Iran's retaliatory attack sounded across the United Arab Emirates last Saturday, the State Department was still scrambling ŌĆŗto finalize a key bureaucratic task - securing approvals for at least three U.S. embassies in the region to evacuate non-essential personnel.
Memos asking State Department leadership to approve evacuations for U.S. missions in Bahrain, ŌĆīKuwait and Qatar, all of which were already coming under fire from Tehran on Saturday, were not sent up for clearance and approved until hours after the United States and Israel launched their war on Iran , and in several cases not until the following day, according to two sources familiar with the matter and half a dozen internal State Department cables seen by Reuters.
The release of public announcements that the U.S. was starting to pull out non-essential staff from Gulf Arab countries began Monday, three days into the war. For the U.S. ŌĆŗembassy in Riyadh, the approval for authorized departure came on Tuesday, four days into the war and on the same day that it was struck by Iranian drones that led to a fire that ŌĆŗdamaged the mission's facilities.
The delay was unusual. Typically, the United States starts evacuations for a planned military action well before the event itself.
When the United States invaded Iraq in ŌüĀ2003, staff and U.S. citizens in the region had weeks to prepare, and at least two evacuations began more than a week before the actual operation started. Ahead of last week's strikes, Israel and Lebanon were the only regional embassies with orders for ŌĆŗnon-essential personnel to depart.
The attack on Iran - the biggest U.S. military operation in the Middle East since 2003 - has put enormous strain on officials from the U.S. and other countries with citizens in the region. But lawmakers, former diplomats ŌĆŗand sources involved in the process said the United States was unusually slow in activating contingency plans both for its own personnel and for thousands of stranded Americans.
The State Department's principal deputy spokesperson, Tommy Pigott, said hundreds of people were involved in the effort to help Americans return.
"We are working 24/7 and have contingency plans ready to go and implement when needed, including the ability to immediately activate the task force, which was done here," Pigott said in a statement to Reuters.
One factor underlying the uneven ŌĆŗapproach, said sources familiar with the matter, was that ahead of the war's start, Trump officials kept contingency planning to a small group of officials.
In one case, officials involved in helping Americans get home had found out from a social ŌĆŗmedia post from a senior Trump official that Washington was now offering charter flights to U.S. citizens, according to two people familiar with the situation.
"No directive came from anywhere," one of the people said.
Pigott said announcements on the task force that the ŌĆīDepartment created specifically ŌüĀto tackle the fallout from the crisis and charter flights were coordinated with relevant officials.
In another instance, the alert for Americans to leave the region came not through normal State Department channels but again via a social media post.
The top State Department official for consular affairs, Mora Namdar, posted on X on Monday urging Americans across 14 countries in the Middle East to leave and said the U.S. was working to facilitate charter flights for U.S. citizens.
But because that message was drafted outside normal channels, State Department staff were surprised and had to update the department's formal travel advisory system that American companies and others rely on for guidance for their overseas personnel, according to two people familiar with ŌĆŗthe situation.
As of Saturday, the U.S. State Department said ŌĆŗit has completed "over a dozen charter flights and has ŌüĀsafely evacuated thousands of Americans" from the Middle East. It did not say from which locations exactly the charter flights arrived.
One flight that departed Dubai bound for Washington on Friday carrying 182 embassy personnel and their families, and 51 private U.S. citizens, was only the second charter to depart from that country, according to a March 6 cable reviewed by ŌĆŗReuters. Since then, additional flights were set to take place.
When asked to confirm if specific plans were finalized ahead of the war to help evacuate American citizens from ŌĆŗthe 14 countries, a State Department ŌüĀofficial briefing reporters earlier this week gave a broad response.
"I wouldn't say that specifically. What I would say is, we always have contingency plans, and we're always ready to assist Americans. That's what I would say to that question," the official, speaking on the condition of anonymity, said.
The Department on Friday said it had assisted 13,000 Americans who reached out seeking help to depart.
As the Department rushed to implement plans to help Americans, novelist and filmmaker Mohana Rajakumar in Doha dialed the hotline that top ŌüĀU.S. officials said ŌĆŗstranded U.S. citizens should call for help. The recorded audio told her not to rely on government help to depart even as the ŌĆŗU.S. government repeatedly says the safety and security of U.S. citizens around the world is its top priority.
"I can tell you every WhatsApp group that I'm in with Americans, nobody feels that way," Rajakumar said, speaking to Reuters from Doha. "Everyone is asking why didn't they tell us to leave ŌĆŗgiven they knew they were going to do this? Why didn't we have the option to leave?"
Officials said that recorded message was later updated.

item.title: Over 30 hurt in Peru after nightclub explosion causes chaos
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - An early morning explosion at a nightclub in northwestern Peru injured at ŌĆŗleast 31 people, authorities said on ŌĆīSaturday.
The government of Peru's La Libertad region said hospitals were treating individuals after the blast ŌĆŗat the nightclub in Trujillo, ŌĆŗwhich authorities are investigating.
Four people are in ŌüĀgrave condition, the newspaper La Republica ŌĆŗsaid, citing the regional health authority, which ŌĆŗsaid minors were among those injured.
It is unclear what caused the incident. Peru has experienced a ŌĆŗsurge in violence related to organized ŌĆŗcrime, and Saturday's incident follows smaller attacks on ŌĆīnightclubs ŌüĀin Trujillo, according to local media reports.
The explosion occurred during a show by a Peruvian musical group and sent revelers ŌĆŗrunning for ŌĆŗsafety.
Members of ŌüĀthe Amor Rebelde group were unhurt after what they said ŌĆŗwere "unfortunate events" that occurred at ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀnightclub where they performed, according to a statement from the group on Facebook.
Video shared ŌüĀon ŌĆŗa Peruvian news program ŌĆŗshowed scenes of chaos after a loud boom was ŌĆŗheard.

item.title: Exclusive: Saudi has told Iran not to attack it, warns of possible retaliation, sources say
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia has told Tehran that while it favours a diplomatic settlement to Iran's conflict with the United States, continued attacks on the kingdom and ŌĆŗits energy sector could push Riyadh to respond in kind, four sources familiar with the matter told Reuters.
The message was conveyed before a speech on ŌĆīSaturday in which Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian apologised to neighbouring Gulf states for TehranŌĆÖs actions -- an apparent attempt to defuse regional anger over Iranian strikes that hit civilian targets.
Two days earlier, Saudi Foreign Minister Prince Faisal bin Farhan spoke to Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi and set out RiyadhŌĆÖs position with clarity, the sources said.
Saudi Arabia is open to any form of mediation aimed at deŌĆæescalation and a negotiated settlement, the sources quoted ŌĆŗthe minister as saying, underlining that neither Riyadh nor other Gulf states had let the U.S. use their airspace or territory to launch airstrikes on Iran.
But Prince ŌĆŗFaisal was also quoted by the sources as saying that if Iranian attacks persisted against Saudi territory or energy infrastructure, Saudi Arabia would ŌüĀbe forced to permit U.S. forces to use their bases there for military operations. Riyadh would retaliate if attacks on the kingdom's critical energy facilities continued, he said.
The sources said ŌĆŗthe kingdom had remained in regular contact with Tehran through its ambassador since the U.S. and Israeli military campaign against Iran began on February 28 following the collapse of talks on ŌĆŗIran's nuclear programme.
The Saudi and Iranian foreign ministries did not respond to requests for comment.
The United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia have all come under heavy drone and missile fire from Iran over the past week.
Iran's Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei , was killed on the first day of the war. Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf Arab states hosting U.S. military installations, ŌĆŗand Israel has attacked Lebanon's Iran-backed Hezbollah armed group.
Araqchi said in an interview on Saturday that he remained in constant contact with his Saudi counterpart and other Saudi officials, adding ŌĆŗthat Riyadh had assured Tehran it was fully committed to not allowing its territory, waters or airspace to be used for attacks against Iran.
Pezeshkian said Iran's temporary leadership council had approved suspending attacks on ŌĆīnearby countries - unless ŌüĀan attack on Iran came from those nations.
"I personally apologise to neighbouring countries that were affected by Iran's actions," he said.
To what extent Pezeshkian's remarks signal a change is unclear. There were further reports of strikes directed at Gulf states on Saturday.
Also, in a sign of possible divisions within IranŌĆÖs leadership, Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters - the unified combatant command of the Iranian armed forces - said in a statement afterwards that U.S. and Israeli bases and interests across the region would remain targets.
The command said Iran's armed forces respected the sovereignty and interests of ŌĆŗneighbouring states and had not taken action against ŌĆŗthem so far. But it said U.S. ŌüĀand Israeli military bases and assets on land, at sea and in the air across the region would be treated as primary targets and face "powerful and heavy" strikes by IranŌĆÖs forces.
U.S. President Donald Trump said in a social media post that Iran had "apologized and surrendered to its Middle ŌĆŗEast neighbours, and promised that it will not shoot at them anymore. This promise was only made because of the relentless U.S. ŌĆŗand Israeli attack."
Two Iranian ŌüĀsources confirmed that a call had taken place in which Riyadh warned Tehran to halt attacks on Saudi Arabia and neighbouring Gulf states. Iran, they said, reiterated its position that the strikes were not aimed at Gulf countries themselves but at U.S. interests and military bases hosted on their territory.
One Iranian source said that Tehran had in response demanded that U.S. bases in the region be ŌüĀclosed and ŌĆŗsome Gulf states stop sharing intelligence with Washington that Iran believes is being used to carry out attacks against it.
Another ŌĆŗIranian source said some military commanders were pressing to continue the strikes, accusing the U.S. of using bases in Gulf states and these countries' airspace to conduct operations against Iran.
Iran had in recent years mended fences with its ŌĆŗGulf neighbours, including former regional arch-rival Saudi Arabia. The diplomatic campaign imploded in the blitz of drones and missiles launched by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in the past week.

item.title: Katyusha rockets target US embassy in Baghdad, security sources say
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Katyusha rockets targeted the U.S. embassy in Baghdad on Saturday ŌĆŗand sirens sounded, security sources and witnesses said, though ŌĆīit was not immediately clear whether the rockets caused any damage or casualties.
The attack was the first time the embassy had been fired ŌĆŗon in more than two years. The last ŌĆŗrecorded strike was in late 2023, when around seven ŌüĀmortar rounds landed in the compound amid a wave ŌĆŗof attacks by IranŌĆæaligned militias on U.S. assets in Iraq ŌĆŗand Syria over WashingtonŌĆÖs support for Israel in its war against Hamas in Gaza.
Explosions were heard near the embassy in central Baghdad around ŌĆŗ9 p.m. (1800 GMT), and sirens warning people to take cover ŌĆŗsounded, witnesses said.
A senior Iraqi security official told Reuters the CŌĆæRAM defence ŌĆīsystem ŌüĀbrought down one of the rockets and that none landed inside the embassy. The official said there were no American casualties.
The attack indicates that IranŌĆæaligned Iraqi militias ŌĆö who have ŌĆŗvowed to retaliate ŌĆŗfor the ŌüĀkilling of IranŌĆÖs supreme leader ŌĆö have widened their targets beyond U.S. military bases in Iraqi ŌĆŗKurdistan and U.S. energy interests to now include ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀU.S. Embassy.
Iraqi Prime Minister Mohammed Shia alŌĆæSudani ordered security forces to track down those who launched the projectiles, calling them ŌĆ£rogue groups ŌüĀoperating ŌĆŗoutside the law that do not ŌĆŗrepresent the will of the Iraqi people,ŌĆØ a statement from his office said.
